chunks.text: Disclaimer. The MILA Country Model (CM) is a JWC product issued solely to support NATO exercises and therefor this product will be used by SHAPE approved exercise participants. For others, no part of this product may be reproduced or used in any manner without written permission by the JWC. This document is a document that contains a combination of fictitious and generic information extracted from publicly available open sources and adapted to the NATO exercises. Pictures, names and supporting info rmation used for biographies will not portray/depict/evoke a real character (historical/religious/political etc.). This to ensure integration of national caveats and sensitivities into these products. This setting document is the foundation for RED and GREEN ORBAT’s and Battle Space Objects (BSO’s) established in INTEL FS and TOPFAS, and the foundation for scenario related products developed for a specific exercise. The Setting and the exercise specific scenario products will integrate real world events, PMESII, actors, locations and organizations. For each exercise two RFI processes exist – INTEL FS and TOPFAS. The reader is kindly asked to use these processes for clarification on the documentation. While JWC try to keep the information accurate, JWC mak e no guarantees. JWC will make an effort to correct errors brought to our attention. MILA Country Model 1. Geostrategic and regional context Historical timeline BEFORE 1960 1960-1991 1992-1999 2000-2012 2013-2020 2021-PRESENT Despite decentralization and regional autonomy, a renewed – socio-economically and criminally motivated Agamen rebellion erupted in 2006-2007 over the not fully implemented 1991-1992 peace agreement .

regional context Historical timeline BEFORE 1960 1960-1991 1992-1999 2000-2012 2013-2020 2021-PRESENT Despite decentralization and regional autonomy, a renewed – socio-economically and criminally motivated Agamen rebellion erupted in 2006-2007 over the not fully implemented 1991-1992 peace agreement . The Agamen remain however largely fragmented with only a small group supporti ng the rebels as the Agamen remained included the nationwide power sharing arrangement. Since independence in 1960, the “ black majority ” enjoyed a monopoly of power through military, one-political rule . The “white minority” of Agamen, Arabs/Moors of northern MILA were largely being discriminated in the economic (e.g. property rights) and cultural space . With their region under military rule and “black majority” appointed governors, the Agamen, Arabs/Moo rs were politically powerless as only a limited number of “white” officials were appointed in official positions. The history of MILA includes medieval empires which thrived on trans-Saharan trade . The legacy of the seempires allowed MILA to create a national identity and ethnic harmony except for the northern nomadic groups . Colonized by FRANCE in the late 19th century, it became part of French Sudan. Growing economic marginalization and the call for regional autonomy resulted in an Agamen uprising and a coup in 1991 . A Transitional Government and agreements allowed the Agamen inclusion in government; the civil service and the army; and, partial autonomy to the northern regions populated by the Agamen. Autonomy would only become effective in 1999 with local elections in the northern regions for the first time.

in government; the civil service and the army; and, partial autonomy to the northern regions populated by the Agamen. Autonomy would only become effective in 1999 with local elections in the northern regions for the first time. Despite attempts to pacify the situation, interethnic violence escalated in to civil war between 1994-1995 . Further concessions prevented a northern cessation. MILA is a country in transition, facing significant challenges. It continues to grapple with severe political instability, terrorism, violent extremism, ongoing insurgencies, and governance issues. The transitional government'sreliance on military support from MURINUS and strained int ernational relations with FRANCE and the West highlight its precarious situation and the uncertain path towards stability and effective governance . 2012 saw a new coup and the northern MNLA (Azawad People’s Movement) declaring the independence of Azawad . Shortly after however due to the RLF taking control of northern MILA, the MNLA had to make an agreement with the government and the French contingent which was requested to support MILA. The remainder of the period saw continued political turmoil, cabin etreshuffles, ongoing conflicts with northern rebels, increased insecurity in northern and central MILA and the UN, ECOWAS, EU, and FRANCE interventions aimed to stabilize MILA and to support the government in restoring security and control over its terri tory. Widespread protests against corruption, insecurity, and economic hardship triggered a new coup in 2020 ousting the elected President . Following a cabinet reshuffle issue in 2021, a new coup occurred . Amidst deteriorating relations with

terri tory. Widespread protests against corruption, insecurity, and economic hardship triggered a new coup in 2020 ousting the elected President . Following a cabinet reshuffle issue in 2021, a new coup occurred . Amidst deteriorating relations with FRANCE and the Wes t and persistent political instability, the transitional government sought military support from MURINUS . MILA continues to grapple with internal conflict . Geostrategic interests and foreign policy The strategic outlook of MILA is predominantly responsive to internal considerations . It needs to consider factors as the level of instability and insecurity, inclusive governance and development, in particular for the northern regions and volatile civil-military relations . These considerations are set within a context of the impact of climate change, migration and a renewed interest of MURINUS in sub-Saharan Africa in particular, strained relations with FRANCE and the West which risk to isolate MILA even further regionally and internationally . The most significant strategic and Foreign Policy objectives of MILA include: Strengthen internal security by enhancing the capacity of the MILA National Defence and Security Forces by seeking foreign military assis tance from non-Western countries. Sustain the political, institutional, administrative and electoral reform process . Continue national reconciliation by implementing peace agreements with northern rebel groups in particular which address fundamental grieva nces, and fostering inclusive governance. Economic development through a diversification of its economy beyond agriculture and mining, and improving the business environment to attract foreign investment. Enhance diplomatic relations to prevent regional and international isolation to reduce overreliance

and fostering inclusive governance. Economic development through a diversification of its economy beyond agriculture and mining, and improving the business environment to attract foreign investment. Enhance diplomatic relations to prevent regional and international isolation to reduce overreliance on FRANCE and Western countries and to address regional cross-border security issues and economic integration. 2. Socio-economic and physical environment Overview of society Population More than 2 0 million. Official language French but recognizes several national languages of which Bambara serves as a lingua franca for daily communica tion, especially in urban areas . Other langua ges: Fula (Felingué ), Songhai (Zakan a-Sandu), Tamasheq ( Agamen ) and Soninke. Currency West African CFA franc (XOF) (1 XOF = 0, 0016 USD) Demographics Annual population growth is 2.95% . MILA has 41.07 births per 1, 000 population and 8.53 deaths per 1, 000 population . Life expectancy is 62.41 years overall (60.19 years for males and 64.7 years for females ). Infant mortality rate is 60.64 infant deaths per 1, 000 live births . 66.69 % of the population is under the age of 25. The median age of the population is 18 years. The total fertility rate of MILA is 5.54 children per woman . 45.4% of total population lives in urban areas . People and ethnicity A black majority of Velaphi, Owochi, Felingué and Zakana-Sandu ethnic groups constitutes 90% of the population. Agamen and Arab/Moors in northern MILA constit ute 10% of the population. Religion Approximately 94% of the population is Muslim,

ethnicity A black majority of Velaphi, Owochi, Felingué and Zakana-Sandu ethnic groups constitutes 90% of the population. Agamen and Arab/Moors in northern MILA constit ute 10% of the population. Religion Approximately 94% of the population is Muslim, practising Sunni Islam, often blending it with local traditions and beliefs . There are also small communities of Christians and practitioners of indigenous African religions, who maintain their traditions mainly in rural areas. Family and gender Traditional views on gender roles remain deeply ingrained, despite constitutional provisions guaranteeing equality between men and women. Women are often responsible for managing households and childcare, while men are seen as the primary breadwinners. Women play a crucial role in agriculture, yet they face challenges in accessing land and financial resources, particu larly in rural communities, which limits their economic autonomy. Education The education system is structured into primary, secondary, and higher education levels. Major institutions of higher learning include the University of KOMBIYE and several techn ical and professional institutes. Despite progress to expand access to education, the literacy rate remains low, particularly among women. Efforts to improve the education sector are ongoing, with initiatives aimed at increasing school enrolment and reducing dropout rates, especially in rural areas. Culture and arts MILA is known for its rich cultural heritage, which includes traditional music, dance, and art . The historic cities of Timbuktu, Djenné, and Gao are famed for their ancient mosques and mud-brick architecture, with several UNESCO World Heritage sites standing as testaments to MILA’spast as

which includes traditional music, dance, and art . The historic cities of Timbuktu, Djenné, and Gao are famed for their ancient mosques and mud-brick architecture, with several UNESCO World Heritage sites standing as testaments to MILA’spast as a hub of Islamic scholarship and trade. MILA consists of about 60 ethnic groups. The Velaphi groups in the south constitute almost 50% of MILA’spopulation; the majority of them are Bambara living in and around KOMBIYE. Together with the Owochi-Senoufo, the Dogon, the Felingué and the Zakana-Sandu, they constitute the black majority of almost 90% of MILA’spopulation. Northern MILA is inhabited by a “white” minority of Agamen and Arab/Moors. The different groups often engage in specific economic activities. The Bambara, Dogon, and Senoufo are mainly farmers, while the Bozo specialize in fishing. Other Velaphi groups such as the Maninka and Soninke are predominantly traders who mostly live in urban areas. Nomadic groups like the Felingué, the Agamen and the Arab-Moors are primarily involved in herding. Despite the ethnic diversity, there are no significant ethno-political cleavages with regards to national politics, except for the enduring crisis between the state and the Agamen and Arab/Moor. Moreover the enduring conflict with TGs, VEOs and other armed groups exposed an ethnic fault line leading to widespread persecution of the Felingué community by MILA’sauthorities and their foreign backers, fuelling resentment and mistrust among communities. Sizes of MILA’smajor ethnic group and subgroup: Velaph i-Bambara: 25% Other Velaphi groups: Maninka (8-10%), Soninke (8%), Bozo (5%), Bobo (2%), Khasonke (2 %).

community by MILA’sauthorities and their foreign backers, fuelling resentment and mistrust among communities. Sizes of MILA’smajor ethnic group and subgroup: Velaph i-Bambara: 25% Other Velaphi groups: Maninka (8-10%), Soninke (8%), Bozo (5%), Bobo (2%), Khasonke (2 %). Owochi-Senoufo: 12% Zakana-Sandu: 10% Felingué: 9-10% Dogon: 6% Agamen: 5% Arab/Moors: 2-3% Economy MILA is one of the poorest countries in the world. An undiversified exports basket dominated by gold and cotton-which exposes the economy to commodity price and climatic shocks-energy shortages and a large informal sector are only some of the structural weaknesses of its economy. In addition to these problems, the country has suffered severely from resource mismanagement, and the national debt has grown rapidly because of its dependency on foreign aid. MILA ’seconomy is overwhelmingly agricultural. With the northern half of the country occupied by the Sahara, most human activity is concentrated in the southern regions, in particular in the valleys of the Niger and Senegal rivers and their tributaries. The economy ’sprimary drivers are cotton production, agricultural goods and gold extraction; subsistenc eagriculture and livestock raising characterize domestic activities, although many people supplement their income by growing cash crops such as cotton. Poverty is as much a rural as it is an urban problem, although there are significant regional variation s, with the north facing much more difficulties than other zones. Over the last decade, the crisis in central MILA has limited the presence of the state in central and northern parts of the country and have had a

the north facing much more difficulties than other zones. Over the last decade, the crisis in central MILA has limited the presence of the state in central and northern parts of the country and have had a significant impact on the delivery of socia lservices in those regions, further exacerbating poverty and inequality. Under investment, corruption, multifaceted insecuri ty and climate change continue to threaten lives in MILA, accentuating pre-existing vulnerabilities and hindering population access to vital social services. Following the coups, MILA'seconomic relationships with regional and international organizations faced significant disruption, particularly due to san ctions and financial restrictions imposed by ECOWAS, WAEMU, and the EU. Although these sanctions are expected to impact trade flows, foreign investment, and government financing capabilities, it is still too early to quantify the effects. Commercially, MILA has returned to more isolationist policies under military rule, turni ng its back on its traditional allies (notably FRANCE and the UNITED STATES) while aligning with MURINUS . However, KOMBIYE ’srapprochement with FANBO – which also endured coups – is not believed to have replaced MILA ’scooperative and committed partners, with which the country shares a common currency and a customs union, both of paramount importance given MILA ’slandlocked position. Overall, MILA ’seconomy has remained resilient despite a decade of conflict, primarily because its core economic activities a re located in the south, far from the crisis in the central and northern regions. Nevertheless, MILA ’seconomic performance has shown signs of lagging due to an inadequate policy

conflict, primarily because its core economic activities a re located in the south, far from the crisis in the central and northern regions. Nevertheless, MILA ’seconomic performance has shown signs of lagging due to an inadequate policy planning and a degraded security context, which both have gradually weakened the economy. Today, the country faces an acute political and economic crisis, which re-enforces short-term interests and authoritarianism over sustainability and a strategic agenda. Economic sectors Agriculture is the primary driver of economic activity in MILA, accounting for approximately 40% of the country’s GDP and employing 60% of the working-age population. A vast majority of the population is engaged in subsistence agriculture1, but the government actively supports the development of commercial products. Millet, rice, wheat, and corn, as well as yams and cassava, are the main subsistence crops, while cotton is importa nt for commercial purposes; peanuts, sugarcane, tobacco, and tea are also grown for market. Livestock (cattle, sheep, and goats) and fishing are of economic significance, although the latter has slightly declined since the 1980s. 1 An agricultural area of major importance is the inland Niger delta. The industrial and natural resources sector has not been developed fully owing to the country’slimited infrastructure: its contribution to GDP is assessed around 25% and its employment share to 15%. The industrial sector faces several constraints, such as unfair competition from importe dproducts, lack of modern production tools, limited access to electricity and water, low technical capacity, and high cost of access to credit.

share to 15%. The industrial sector faces several constraints, such as unfair competition from importe dproducts, lack of modern production tools, limited access to electricity and water, low technical capacity, and high cost of access to credit. Mining is the largest source of Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) especially in the gold sector2, contributing around 70% of the state’srevenue. A part from gold, other exploited deposits include salt (Taoudenni), marble and kaolin (Bafoulabé), and limestone (Diamou). Though the mining industry remains vital in generating revenue, it also has social con sequences for communities, especially those reliant on artisanal mining . Artisanal mining, particularly in central and northern MILA has developed a nexus with the ongoing Agamen insurgency and other forms of radical extremism. Armed groups exploit artisanal mining operations by imposing taxes or taki ngcontrol of the mining sites to finance their campaigns. Industry concentrates largely on food processing for domestic use, and many people are involved in small-scale commerce. Most manufacturing enterprises process agricultural products or make construction materials and consumer goods, the bulk of production being for the domestic market. Products include cotton fibre, printed cloth, and blankets. There are also shops for the construction of motorcycles and repair of machinery. Services contribute to 35% of MILA ’s GDP, with an employment share of 25%. Trade contributes most to growth in the tertiary sector, followed by telecommunications; non-financial market services and non-market services, instead, make small contributions. Business climate Weak governance makes the business climate in MILA very fragile.

share of 25%. Trade contributes most to growth in the tertiary sector, followed by telecommunications; non-financial market services and non-market services, instead, make small contributions. Business climate Weak governance makes the business climate in MILA very fragile. Economic policies do not encourage the development of small and medium enterprises and hamper the development of a value chain since government budgetary efforts are mobilized first toward th edefence sector, with limited funds to support other areas. MILA ’seconomy is heavily reliant on the informal sector – approximately 94% of employed people in the country work informally. The government has taken steps to improve the business environment by implementing new investment policies, but these reforms do not really demonstrate a commitment to a liberal economy: instead, they signal to the business community a willingness to renegotiate deals based on political objectives. Besides insecurity, th emain barriers to doing business are corruption, administrative inefficiency, and lack of skilled labour. 2 Gold is mined primarily in the south-western areas of the country, on the Mandingue Plateau . However there also artisanal mining sites in the vicinity of Gao, Mopti and Timbuktu in areas which are control of the armed groups links to the insurgency and radical extremism. OVERVIEW: MILA has a traditional economic system in which the majority of the population engages in subsistence agriculture in the riverine area irrigated by the Niger River. MILA is number 113 in total exports worldwide, number 138 in total imports, and number 133 in terms of most

majority of the population engages in subsistence agriculture in the riverine area irrigated by the Niger River. MILA is number 113 in total exports worldwide, number 138 in total imports, and number 133 in terms of most complex economy . MILA major industries: food processing, construction, phosphate and gold mining. MILA major exports: gold ($7.26B), raw cotton ($105M), oily seeds ($39.7M), and rough wood ($30.4M) exporting mostly to UNITED ARAB EMIRATES ($5.57B), SWITZERLAND ($1.26B), AUSTRALIA ($405M), and DUSHMAN ($77.5M). MILA major imports: refined petroleum ($1. 69B), light pure woven cotton ($215M), and broadcasting equipment ($211M), importing mostly from CERO ($1.46B), SENEGAL ($1.13B), DUSHMAN ($581M), and FANBO ($304M). GNI explained: Because development encompasses many factors-economic, environmental, cultural, educational, and institutional-no single measure gives a complete picture. However, the total earnings of the residents of an economy, measured by its Gross National Income (GNI), is a good measure of its capacity to provide for the well-being of its people. Figure 1: Economic indicators of XXXX (year 2021 ). Table 1 – Macroeconomic indicators of MILA (2022) National budget MILA national budget remains highly dependent on international aid. The country relies on bilateral donors and multilateral financial institutions to fund major development projects. In addition to the very fragile security situation and delays related to the adoption of necessary institutional reforms, significant unc ertainty around the intricate socio-political environment clouds the appeal of much needed investment, particularly in agricultural value chains. Last year revised budget was 2, 994 billion CFA franc. Financial sector

adoption of necessary institutional reforms, significant unc ertainty around the intricate socio-political environment clouds the appeal of much needed investment, particularly in agricultural value chains. Last year revised budget was 2, 994 billion CFA franc. Financial sector Strengths: Substantial natural resources in terms of agriculture (tobacco, cotton) and mining (gold, bauxite, iron); International assistance; Young population; Large expats remittances. Weaknesses: Economy vulnerable to weather and commodity price fluctuations; Extreme poverty combined with poor security situation; Geographically isolated (landlocked); Worsening of governance and corruption; Weak business environment. Table 2-Economic indicators of MILA (202 3) MILA, along with seven other French-speaking countries in western Africa, is a member of the West African Economic and Monetary Union (WAEMU). These countries share a common bank, the Central Bank of the West African States (Banque Centrale des États de l’ Afrique de l’Ouest), which is responsible for setting banking standards and ensuring financial flow in the region. MILA’scurrency is the Communauté Financière Africaine franc (CFA) which is pegged to the euro; its stabilization is the resp onsibility of t he BCEAO. Although the BCEAO is fully independent, its policies are influenced by the European Central Bank’santi-inflation policy: this means that the BCEAO establishes regional objectives which may not always be the most optimal course of action for MIL A. The banking system in MILA has seventeen credit institutions (14 banks and 3 banking financial institutions). Most commercial banks have direct investments in western capital markets. Trade MILA is a member of both the West African

A. The banking system in MILA has seventeen credit institutions (14 banks and 3 banking financial institutions). Most commercial banks have direct investments in western capital markets. Trade MILA is a member of both the West African Economic and Moneta ry Union (WAEMU) and the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS): as a result, it has long enjoyed internal and external monetary stability characterized by typically low inflation, an exchange rate pegged to the euro and a set of Common Externa l Tariffs (CET) to regulate internal trade. However, sanctions and financial restrictions imposed by ECOWAS, WAEMU, and the EU have raised concerns over potential economic constraints. As a landlocked country with sea access corridors constricted, trading in MILA results in transportation costs sensibly high, with access to international markets further complicated by poor road infrastructure where goods travel slowly, and limiting trade connections. MILA economy continues to face multidimensional fragilit y, with limited diversification due to the absence of agricultural value chains, poor development of transport and energy infrastructures, low private sector contribution to the economy, high vulnerability to climate change, and the worsening security cris is. Moreover, recent diplomatic tensions, particularly with FRANCE, have led to a diversification of international partners but a lso created uncertainty in established trade relationships. The priority areas of MILA trade policy are the development of its industrial sector as well as the value chain (livestock meat, agro-food processing, etc) and the overall improvement of the business climate. International donors MILA relies

The priority areas of MILA trade policy are the development of its industrial sector as well as the value chain (livestock meat, agro-food processing, etc) and the overall improvement of the business climate. International donors MILA relies heavily on donor-supported development projects, as these offer additional oppor tunities in a variety of sectors, including hydroelectric, solar power generation and distribution, irrigation, telecommunications, public health, and agriculture. The country welcomes foreign investment and, to this end, has implemented policies to attra ctpartners that could help in diversifying beyond gold into other minerals like uranium, bauxite and lithium. MILA is also leveraging its climate conditions to attract investment in renewabl eenergy, particularly hydroelectric and solar power projects. Over the past decade, KOMBIYE has maintained robust cooperation with the International Monetary Fund, World Bank and other int ernational donors; however, the coups challenged these relationships and many, included the World Bank, have suspended operation. A ggressive anti-Western rhetoric from the junta has also caused several donors to reduce their support, like FRANCE, which suspended public development aid to the country in 202 2. The transitional government has struggled to maintain economic stability, lea ving international fiscal organizations worried about the sustainability of MILA’spublic finances. Diaspora and remittances Diaspora is one of the pillars of development in MILA. Migrant remittances-defined as transfers of funds from migrants to rela tives or friends in their country of origin-are an important source of global development finance. They are the second largest source, behind Foreign Direct Investment, of

MILA. Migrant remittances-defined as transfers of funds from migrants to rela tives or friends in their country of origin-are an important source of global development finance. They are the second largest source, behind Foreign Direct Investment, of external funding for developing countries, supplementing the domestic incomes of millions of poor families across the world. The contribution of MILA diaspora to the country’soverall economy is quite substantial, with remittances alone estimated at $964bn USD in 2019, confirming a trend of upward growth since 2016. Given the growing importance of remittances, MILA has set the mobilisation and valorisation of diaspora contributions to reduce poverty and support development as a strategic objective of the country'sdevelopment policies and strategies at both national and sectoral levels. Members of MILA diaspora have historically engaged in social development through their organizations. However, with new gener ations, new practices are now at work and the diaspora has turned more towards investment in business, including investment by indi viduals. The goal is both lasting societal impact and financial return. There are two major groups for MILA in terms of diaspora engagement: the MILA diaspora in neighbouring countries in West Africa and the MILA dias pora in Europe and North America. MIL A diaspora engagement in Europe and America presents a great potential, especially in relation to skills transfer and scienti fic and technical innovation. The social remittances that the African diaspora possess and can transfer to Africa include valuable transnational networks, knowledge, sound political contributions, democratic habits and attitudes,

especially in relation to skills transfer and scienti fic and technical innovation. The social remittances that the African diaspora possess and can transfer to Africa include valuable transnational networks, knowledge, sound political contributions, democratic habits and attitudes, as well as peace-making ideas and practices, new technological skills, norms of behaviour, work ethics, social values and cultural influence. Thus, these non-material social remittances are equally as significant as financial remittances-or perhaps even more. Socio-economic challenges MILA faces a range of socio-economic challenges that threaten to varying extents its internal security and long term economic growth and prosperity . Chief amongst these are: Enduring socio-economic vulnerabilities Political instability Frequent changes in administration have undermined policy agendas (MILA has had ten different prime ministers in the last decade and two coups since 2020); The security situation has also compromised the government’scapacity to exert control over MILA’sterritory in its enti rety. Corruption Poor transparency and accountability have led to episodes of mismanagement and corruption and to growing public discontent with government institutions; The legal framework to prevent corruption remains weak; moreover, the fight against corruption appears, in some respects, to be used as a means for the junta to remove all actors who may constitute threats to the longevity of its rule. Poverty and food insecurity More than 42% of MILA’spopulation lives in extreme poverty; Rural poverty is a major issue, with 90% of the country’spoverty located in southern rural areas; The number of people in need of humanitarian aid has risen and the

than 42% of MILA’spopulation lives in extreme poverty; Rural poverty is a major issue, with 90% of the country’spoverty located in southern rural areas; The number of people in need of humanitarian aid has risen and the country has been placed on maximum alert for food security (1 million children under the age of five fac e the imminent threat of acute malnutrition). Social protection MILA social safety nets are limited by the government’slow revenues and resources management capacities; 10 years of conflict have limited the presence of the state in central and northern MILA and have had a significant impact on the delivery of social services in those regions. Public education The post-2012 crisis further weakened an already-fragile school system, resulting in the closure of thousands of schools and a shortage of qualified teachers3; A lack of educational facilities in rural regions exacerbates the urban-rural divide. Population displacement MILA’scomplex crisis is marked by insecurity resulting in population displacement and people stranded in conflict-affected or in blockaded areas; Of over 7 million people in need, more than 1.3 million people are IDPs and returnees. Gender There are several barriers to equality of opportunity in MILA. These have multiplied rapidly during the ongoing conflict, whi ch has left a significant share of the population to fend for themselves; Women face significant obstacles to education, public office, employment and full civic participation, with one of the world’ slowest literacy rates (22.1% compared to 31.3% for men). Climate change Like all the Sahel

to fend for themselves; Women face significant obstacles to education, public office, employment and full civic participation, with one of the world’ slowest literacy rates (22.1% compared to 31.3% for men). Climate change Like all the Sahel countries, MILA is severely threatened by climate change and environmental degradation. Rising temperatures, droughts, unstable rainfalls and floods are substantial threats, particularly because 80% of the population lives off agriculture, and 95% of that population depends on rainfall for their livelihood. 3 Mainta ining regular school attendance remains a major challenge due to girls being taken out of school for early marriage and boys needed in the field . This trend is further exacerbated by the lack of trained professional teachers and regular teacher strikes dema nding better work conditions. Physical environment MILA is a landlocked country in West Africa, situated mostly in the Saharan and Sahelian regions. Th eterritory spans an area of approximately 1, 241, 238 square kilometres, making it the eighth-largest country on the continent. MILA shares a land borderline of approximately 7, 2 50 kilometres with its neighbours . It borders by ALGERIA (1, 376 km) to the north, REGIN (821 km) to the east, FANBO (1, 000 km) and CERO (532 km) to the south, GUINEA (858 km) and SENEGAL (419 km) to the west, and MAURETANIA (2, 236 km) to the northwest. KOMBIYE is the capital city of MILA, with a population of 3.1 million inhabitants . It is located on the Niger River, near the rapids

km) to the west, and MAURETANIA (2, 236 km) to the northwest. KOMBIYE is the capital city of MILA, with a population of 3.1 million inhabitants . It is located on the Niger River, near the rapids that divide the upper and middle Niger River valleys in the south-western part of the country. KOMBIYE is both the capital, by far the largest city, the main port, and the commercial and financial centre. Approximately 65 percent of the total land area is desert or semi-desert and about 60 percent of the population lives in rural areas and works mostly within the agricultural sector. Most of MILA ’sinhabitants are concentrated in the south-western part, where more than half the country ’spopulation lives. Terrain and climate Topography and key features MILA is extending south-west from the southern Sahara Desert through the Sahel to the Sudanian savannah zone. Desert or semi-desert covers about 65 % of its total area, and the Niger River creates a large and fertile inland delta as it arcs northeast throu gh MILA from GUINEA before turning southwards. The terrain is primarily savannahs in the south and flat to rolling plains or high plateau at 200 –500 meters in elevation in the north. There are rugged hills in the northeast, with elevations of up to 1, 000 meters. The Niger and the Senegal rivers are generally described as MILA'slifeblood, a source of food, drinking water, irrigation, and transportation. The country'slowest point is 23 meters on the Senegal River at the

up to 1, 000 meters. The Niger and the Senegal rivers are generally described as MILA'slifeblood, a source of food, drinking water, irrigation, and transportation. The country'slowest point is 23 meters on the Senegal River at the border to SENEGAL, and its highest point is Mount Hombori at 1, 155 meters. Map 1 – MILA overview map Lakes, rivers, river valleys and river delta MILA is home to more than 80 lakes, several of them of significance for the population. They are vital for the livelihoods of the surrounding communities, providing water for irrigation and fishing, as well as habitats for wildlife, particularly during the wet season when the Niger River floods the inner delta region. Lake Debo is the largest lake in MILA, located in the Inner Niger River Delta, formed by seasonal flooding of the Niger River and is crucial for local biodiversity and agriculture. During hig hwater stages, it can expand significantly, although its size diminishes during the dry season. Lake Faguibine used to be considered as MILA'slargest permanent lake, Lake Faguibine has experienced significant shrinkage due to prolonged droughts since the 1990s. Today, it covers an area of approximately 590 square kilometres during the rainy season. Another important lake in the region is Lake Horo, which plays a role in the local ecosystem and supports fishing and agriculture. Lake Niamana is located near the capital, and is regarded as significant for the local communities. Lake Talo is part of the Inner Niger River Delta and contributes

in the local ecosystem and supports fishing and agriculture. Lake Niamana is located near the capital, and is regarded as significant for the local communities. Lake Talo is part of the Inner Niger River Delta and contributes to the region'swetland ecosystem. MILA is home to several important rivers that play crucial roles in the country'sge ography, ecology, culture and economy. Niger River is the most significant river in MILA. From its source in the Guinea Highlands, it is traversing MILA for approximately 1, 700 km. It crosses the border with GUINEA and runs towards northeast into the deser t, before it turns southeast in a crescent shape towards the border with REGIN in southeast MILA, then continuing towards the Niger River Delta at the Gulf of Guinea. The Niger River also forms a vast inland delta in MILA, vital for agriculture, transporta tion, and fishing. The Bani River is the principal tributary of the Niger River within MILA. It is also the largest tributary outside IGAINER, and merges with the Niger River near Mopti in the inland delta. Other main tributaries of the Niger River in MILA are the Sankarani River, the Diaka River, and the Faya River. Located in western MILA, the Senegal River serves as an important water source for the region. It is formed by the confluence of the Bafing and the Bakoye Rivers at Bafoulabe, both entering from GUINEA where they originate from. While the main course of the Senegal River forms the border between SENEGAL

region. It is formed by the confluence of the Bafing and the Bakoye Rivers at Bafoulabe, both entering from GUINEA where they originate from. While the main course of the Senegal River forms the border between SENEGAL and MAURITANIA, its headwaters and upper tributaries play an important role in MILA'shydrology and economy. The Manantali Dam, a major dam on the Senegal River system, is located in MILA and is significant for irrigation and hydroelectric power generation. Coastline As a landlocked country, MILA has no coastline. Climate MILA experiences a predominantly hot and dry climate, characterized by clear seasonal variations and considerable geographical diversity. The climate can be divided into three main zones. In the north, the Sahara desert dominates the climate, while the Sah elregion in the centre and the more tropical zone in the south make the country further south more habitable. The Harmattan and Alize winds, both blowing from east, play significant roles in shaping MILA'sclimate, particularly during the dry season. Thes ewind patterns, especially the Harmattan, create the hot, dry conditions that characterize much of MILA'sclimate, particularly in the central and northern regions. In the desert zone the climate is arid, with annual rainfall less than 200 mm and extreme temperatures that can exceed 60 °Cduring the day. Night temperatures can drop to around 4 to 5 °C. The central semi-arid Sahelian zone experiences moderate rainfall between 200 to 600 mmannually. Temperatures averaging between 23 °C and 36 °C. This zone is particularly sensitive to climate variability

can drop to around 4 to 5 °C. The central semi-arid Sahelian zone experiences moderate rainfall between 200 to 600 mmannually. Temperatures averaging between 23 °C and 36 °C. This zone is particularly sensitive to climate variability and has seen increased drought conditions. Southernmost parts with a more tropical climate, receive 600 to 1, 400 mm of annual rainfall, and experience average temperatures ranging from 24 to 30 °C. This reg ion supports more diverse agricultural activities due to its higher moisture levels. MILA has two primary seasons. The dry season (November to June) is marked by low humidity and high temperatures. The northeast trade winds, known as the Alize, bring coole rweather from November to January, with average temperatures around 25 °C. From March to June, the Harmattan, a hot, dry wind from the Sahara, raises temperatures significantly, often reaching between 40 to 45°C. During the rain yseason (June to October), monsoon winds from the southwest bring heavy rainfall, particularly in August, which is the wettest month. During the rainy season, temperatures are generally lower compared to the dry season, with annual rainfall varying significantly across the country, from 20 mm in the desert areas to over 1, 100 mm in some southern areas. The main climatic conditions affecting military operations are heavy rainfall, high temperatures, high levels of humidity, an d the occurrence of sandstorms. Climate change As a country located in the Sahel region, MILA experiences significant climatic variations, ranging from severe flooding to extreme drought. Overall,

heavy rainfall, high temperatures, high levels of humidity, an d the occurrence of sandstorms. Climate change As a country located in the Sahel region, MILA experiences significant climatic variations, ranging from severe flooding to extreme drought. Overall, the climate is shaped by its geographical features and seasonal winds, leading to a complex interplay of temperature and precipitation that significantly influences the livelihoods and agricultural practices of its population. MILA is facing significant climate challenges, including rising temperatures projected to increase by 2 to 4.6 °C by 2080, and even more in northern MILA. Droughts have become more frequent, especially in northern areas, and frequency of hot nights has increased significantly. Projections, with some uncertainty, suggest that this warming trend is accompanied by a further decline in rai nfall, particularly affecting the dry northern regions. A significant reduction of more than 30% in water levels of rivers, especially the Niger River is projected. These factors are expected to become even more severe in the future. These factors, which a ll also negatively affect socio-economic environment, and overall, MILA faces significant climate risks that are expected to exacerbate existing challenges related to food security, water resources, economic and social stability, particularly affecting its agriculture-dependent population. Further exacerbation of drought conditions will lead to increased migration as communities seek more viable living conditions. Map 2 – MILA climate zones. as communities seek more viable living conditions. Map 2 – MILA climate zones. Environmental issues The environment of MILA is stressed by the impact of climate change with challenges as rising temperatures and decreasing and erratic rainfall leading to a continuous desertification, reduced water levels, and agricultural vulnerability (significant reduced food production). MILA is also fac ing related impacts as pollution, deforestation and waste management challenges. Though the mining industry remains vital in generating revenue, it has also raised concerns about environmental degradation and the social con sequences for communities relia nt on artisanal mining practices . Air pollution from dust storms, vehicle emissions, and industrial activities is rising. Additional contamination of water and land areas is expected, especially from agriculture, industries, mining, and household waste. Deforestation is a result, not only from climate changes, but also from artisan gold and mining activities. Especially in urban areas, solid and liquid waste handling challenges poses a significant environmental threat. These environmental issues are interco nnected and exacerbated by factors such as rapid population growth, urbanization, and limited resources for environmental protection and management. The government has implemented various policies and initiatives to address environme ntal challenges, but significant work remains to mitigate and adapt to environmental problems. The country has made some progress in developing its infrastructure, however, it still faces significant challenges, particularly in rural and conflict-affected areas. Its landlocked position makes regional land transport corridors and international cooperation crucial for its development. Upgrades of power generation and grid, maintenance and rehabilitation of roads and railways, improvements of water an dsanitation systems, improvements of ICT infrastructure and expansion of irrigation are the most urgent areas for investment and maintenance efforts . Road network: Despite a deteriorated network due to a lack of investment and maintenance, MILA has a broa d-reaching national road backbone . The paved road between KOMBIYE and Idaville in CERO is the main economic link to the coast and world markets. Railroad: One railway connects MILA with the port city of DAKAR . SENEGAL . The line is in poor condition and pro vides limited capacity for freight transportation. Planned future upgrades should increase capacity and the relevance of the railroad infrastructure significantly. Airports: T he Modibo Keita International Airport in KOMBIYE with direct lines to other West African and some European countries is paramount for MILA ’sconnecti vity. Communication network: MILA'scommunications sector has seen significant growth, especially in mobile services . It however still faces challenges in expanding coverage and improvin ginternet access, particu larly in rural and remote areas. Transport infrastructure Despite some improvements through the last two decades, MILA'stransport infrastructure is still considered poor by regiona lstandards. MILA is heavily depend ent on regional corridors and infrastructure for transport. There are three international trade corridors that are linking MILA to West-African seaports: the Tema (NAGA) - OWOTSHA (FANBO) - KOMBIYE corridor, the DAKAR (SENEGAL) - KOMBIYE corridor, and the Idaville (CERO) – Ferkesessedougou (CERO) -

There are three international trade corridors that are linking MILA to West-African seaports: the Tema (NAGA) - OWOTSHA (FANBO) - KOMBIYE corridor, the DAKAR (SENEGAL) - KOMBIYE corridor, and the Idaville (CERO) – Ferkesessedougou (CERO) - KOMBIYE corridor. Air transportation system MILA has 29 airports, including international, domestic, military and private airports. Only 8 of them have paved runways. Th emain a irport is Modibo Keita International Airport, located 15 kilometres south of KOMBIYE. It serves as the MILA hub for international travel, offering flights to neighbouring West African countries and Europe. The national airline, Compagnie Aerienne du MILA (CAM), operates a limited network both to domestic and international flights, connecting major cities within the country, as well as some regional destinations. A few international carriers provide servi ces from KOMBIYE, with Air France as the leading one. MILA'sair transport system provides crucial links both within the country and to international destinations, supporting trade, tourism, and economic development. A limited number of international connections compared to larger regional hubs and the limited Infrastructure at smaller airports are the main chal lenges for the air trans portation system of MILA. Road network system The density of MILA'sroad network is among the lowest on the continent, partially due to its geography with half the territory being arid or desert. The total road network spans out over an approximated 20, 000 km of which around 5, 000 km (25%) is paved. The remaining part is unpaved dirt road . The northern region is largely inaccessible desert, except for some roads connecting scattered mining sites and irrigation area. Outside of the urban areas, road conditions a re generally hazardous . MILA depends heavily on regional corridors towards coastal states for international transport and trade. Hence, it has focused on maintaining and rehabilitating its portions of regional road

the urban areas, road conditions a re generally hazardous . MILA depends heavily on regional corridors towards coastal states for international transport and trade. Hence, it has focused on maintaining and rehabilitating its portions of regional road corridors, ensuring major urban areas an dproduction zones are connected to deep water seaports. The primary economic coastal link is the paved road between KOMBIYE and Idaville in CERO. There are plans for future improved roads linking KOMBIYE to the port cities of DAKAR (Senegal) and Conakry via Kankan (Guinea). In urban centres, particularly KOMBIYE, informal buses and taxis are prevalent due to low rates of private vehicle ownership. Motorcycles have become increasingly popular . Road safety is poor and motor vehicle accidents are common. The government has pledged funds to improve road safety and create a national road security agency. Map 3 – MILA road network. Railway network system The once historically significant rail way network of MILA is limited, consis ting primarily of one main railway line, the DAKAR-Niger River Railway which spans 729 kilometers in MILA. It runs from the port of Koulikoro via KOMBIYE to Kidira at the border of SENEGAL and links MILA with the important port of DAKAR (SENEGAL) and conne ctivity to world markets for trade . In addition to freight, the line offers passenger services. MILA'srailway sector, while currently limited and facing challenges, remains an important part of the country'stransportation infrastructure. Sea Ports MILA i slandlocked and has no seaports. Sea Ports MILA i slandlocked and has no seaports. Inland waterway system While inland waterway transportation is operated on a small scale, it plays a role in MILA'stransport network. MILA'stwo major rivers, the Niger and the Senegal, serve as important transportation links. The Niger River is navigable year-round for small boats and from July to January for larger vessels. Koulikoro, northeast of KOMBIYE, is the country'sprimary river port. The Senegal River is navigable year-round from the north-western town Ambided i (near Kayes) to its mouth in SENEGAL . Health Infrastructure MILA'shealthcare system is a largely decentralized, three-tiered structure. At the local level, community health centers provide primary care with limited government support. The district level features referral health centers, while the regional/national level consists of hospitals offering specialized care. A significant disparity exists between urban and rural health infrastructure, with rural areas experiencing severe shortages of hospitals and medical staff. Key characteristics of MILA'shealthcare system include its decentralized nature with a focus on community involvement, cost recovery mechanisms requiring patients to pay for consultations and medications, limited government funding (jus t 3% of GDP allocated to healthcare), and a heavy reliance on international aid and NGOs. The healthcare workforce is sparse, with only 1.23 doctors per 10, 000 people, compared to the global average of 17.2 per 10, 000. The country has approximately 2, 700 medical facilities,

reliance on international aid and NGOs. The healthcare workforce is sparse, with only 1.23 doctors per 10, 000 people, compared to the global average of 17.2 per 10, 000. The country has approximately 2, 700 medical facilities, yet many lack necessary technology, resulting in a standard of care well below Western levels. For some specialist care, patients often need to seek treatment abroad due to the lack of advanced medical technology. The government has esta blished a National Development Plan aimed at improving healthcare infrastructure, resulting in modest progress. However, the plan is underfunded, and significant challenges persist, including a severe shortage of healthcare workers, inequitable resource distribution, limited rural access, high maternal and child mortality rates, low immunization coverage, a high burden of infectious and rising non-communicable diseases, and fragmented health insurance coverage. Patients face high out-of-pocket expenses due to these systemic issues. Overall, MILA'shealthcare system struggles with numerous challenges, including limited access, insufficient workforce, and poor quality of care, especially in rural and conflict-affected areas. National health expenditure remains low, and the country has one of the lowest densities of healthcare staff worldwide. Map 4 – MILA railroad network. MILA has, however, despite its electricity production continuing to rely on fossil fuels and imports, MILA has a significant unde veloped potential for renewable energy, especially solar and hydropower . Power system (general) MILA'senergy mix relies heavily on traditional biomass and imported fossil fuels. In rural areas, wood and charcoal are esse ntial for cooking and heating, making uproughly three-quarters of primary energy consumption. Oil imports also play a growing role, particularly for electricity generation and tra nsportation. Currently, hydropower and diesel together account for around half of the country'selectricity generation, though hydropower ’scontribution is only 4%. Despite these fossil fuel dependencies, MILA has significant untapped renewable energy potential, particularly in solar power, given the high solar irradiation across the country. There are also via ble opportunities in hydropower, wind, and biomass that, if developed, could greatly enhance the renewable energy landscape and help diversify the energy mix. Electricity access remains limited in MILA, with only about one-quarter of the population connected to the grid. In urban areas, access stands at around 60%, but rural electrification is low, with just 15% coverage. Demand for electricity is growing at an annual rate of around 10%, putting pressure on the existing supply, which has not kept pace with consumption needs. Expanding electricity access in MILA faces significant challenges, particularly limited domestic financial resources, which i mpede efforts to improve the electrification rate and modernize infrastructure. However, this gap

However, this gap presents consi derable opportunities for foreign investors to support renewable energy projects and infrastructure expansion, addressing both immediate needs and long-term sustainable growth in the energy sector. Nuclear power MILA currently does not have any operational nuclear power plants . Since the coup however, MILA has taken significant steps towards developing a nuclear energy program in cooperation with MURINUS. MURINUS and MILA signed a memorandum of understanding (MOU) on cooperation in the peaceful use of nucle arenergy and both countries agreed on a roadmap for cooperation in civil nuclear energy, outlining steps to develop MILA'snuclear infrastructure . MILA and MURINUS'sstate nuclear company Muratom signed three cooperation agreements which include nuclear knowledge transfer and the construction of a low-power nuclear power plant. According to sources, construction of three nuclear reactors could potentially start within two years. The first could be completed by 2030. Though the development of a nuclear powe rsector represents a significant shift in MILA’senergy strategy, plans are still in the early stages and the programme will likely face various technical, financial, political and regulatory challenges . Natural resources MILA is rich in natural resources, particularly mineral resources. Significant deposits of various mineral include gold, iron ore, uranium, manganese, lithium, bauxite, phosphate, diamonds, copper, zinc, and lead. The country has also deposits

challenges . Natural resources MILA is rich in natural resources, particularly mineral resources. Significant deposits of various mineral include gold, iron ore, uranium, manganese, lithium, bauxite, phosphate, diamonds, copper, zinc, and lead. The country has also deposits of limestone, marble, gypsum, kaolin, and rock salt. Despite this, its mineral extraction remains low . Many areas remain unexplored due to lack of infrastructure and funding. Gold is by far th emost important mineral. MILA is one of Africa'slargest gold producers and gold accounts for more than 80% of its total exports. MILA does not have any domestic crude oil, gas production or refineries . It imports all of its oil products . Total lyrelying on the import of fossil fuels, MIL A remains vulnerable to supply disruptions and price fluctuations. SENEGAL and CERO, for one quarter are the main sources of fossil fuels. MILA has a substantial amount of untapped renewable energy resources such as biomass, biofuel, based on a well-devel oped agricultural sector, hydroelectric power and solar energy. The government is actively seeking partnerships and foreign investments especially in the mining and energy sectors which could in turn be exploited by foreign companies or countrie s. Water Supply, wastewater and sewerage MILA has significant water resources, though unevenly distributed across the country. Despite this, the country faces challenges in water distribution, management, and quality, especially in rural and in the northern areas a way from the main water sources. These areas in particular face significant water stress as these are

this, the country faces challenges in water distribution, management, and quality, especially in rural and in the northern areas a way from the main water sources. These areas in particular face significant water stress as these are faced with lower rainfall is lower and a more severe impact of the climate change. MILA faces i ssues with water infrastructure with an estimated 30% of water facilities being non-operational due to poor construction, age, lack of maintenance, difficulties in harnessing water resources, and lack of investment and human capacity. Despite water being abundant, MILA is characterized by an "economic water scarc ity”. Surface water pollution is a concern for water quality, leading to the proliferation of invasive aquatic plants and water-borne diseases. Groundwater quality is at risk due to insufficient monitoring and protection schemes, particularly in urban areas. Wastewater management systems, particularly in urban areas, is inadequate with small sew age systems and small open-air sewage canals in some urban centres. There is low access to sanitation with just around half of the population having access to ba sic sanitation . Open defecation is common, especially in rural areas. Poorly-constructed and unprotected wells, as well as infiltration of latrine waste, cause pathogenic contamination in shallow aquife rs, increasing public health risks. Communication infrastructure MILA’scommunications infrastructure faces significant challenges, leading to a substantial digital divide between urban and rural areas. With low fixed-line penetration and limited mobile coverage, rural and remote areas are often left with out high-speed internet access and rely

infrastructure faces significant challenges, leading to a substantial digital divide between urban and rural areas. With low fixed-line penetration and limited mobile coverage, rural and remote areas are often left with out high-speed internet access and rely more heavily on traditional media. Internet penetration is gradually growing, reaching approximately 7.91 million users (34.5% of the population); however, acce ssremains restricted compared to global standards. The urban-rural disparity in telecommunications access is stark: urban areas have around 60% coverage, while rural areas lag at only 15 %. This uneven distribution is exacerbated by MILA’stopography and security issues, which complicate the development of a co mprehensive national network. MILA’smobile market is dominated by major operators Orange MILA (privately owned) and Milatel (a subsidiary of state-owned Sotelma), along with Telecel, which has limited reach. Ikatel, a subsidiary of Senegal's Sonatel, furt her supplements coverage in certain regions, though many areas still lack reliable signal, and connectivity disturbances are frequent. To address these gaps, MILA is expanding its reliance on satellite and mobile technologies. VSAT services are currently being deployed to connect isolated communities and show promise for supporting essential services in education, healthcare, and other sectors. Additionally, the seinitiatives could drive future increases in internet penetration as infrastructure develops. As a landlocked country, MILA depends on neighbouring countries for international bandwidth, which keeps internet prices high. The country connects to the SAT-3/WASC (West Africa Cable System) through two primary corridors: KOMBIYE-DAKAR (Senegal) and KOMBIYE-Idaville (CERO), which provide dual access and help maintain some

neighbouring countries for international bandwidth, which keeps internet prices high. The country connects to the SAT-3/WASC (West Africa Cable System) through two primary corridors: KOMBIYE-DAKAR (Senegal) and KOMBIYE-Idaville (CERO), which provide dual access and help maintain some network reliability and competition. Nonetheless, the lack of direct access to submarine cables remains a barrier to robust connectivity. Space MILA does not have a formal space program or space agency . It is however part of broader African initiatives and regional cooperation related to space. MILA is one of the signatories of the African Space Policy and Strategy . It continues to benefit from regional space efforts, such as the African Union Space Agency . It continues to use satellite technologies such as remote sensing applications, for environmental monitoring, agriculture, and natural resource management ), satellite imagery and data from different sources and for various purposes, such as the World Food Programme (WFP) supporting emergency response efforts in some of MILA’sconflict-affected areas. The usage of satellite data and imagery will likely continue. Though there are no concrete pla ns for a national space program, there is room for a further development of space technologies. MILA could potentially leverage small satellite technologies, or partnerships, to access space-based services. However, efforts will likely be affected many of the other challenges with which MILA is faced . Political and Key Actor Landscape Political system The Republic of MILA is a republic of the People, governed by the People and for the People. MILA applies a three-tier system of government with an executive branch, a legislative branch, and a judicial branch. The High Council of Local Authorities (HCCT) and the Economic, Social an d Cultural Council are other institutions of the republic. Following the 2020 cou p, the junta, calling itself the National Committee for the Salvation of the People (CNSP) established a Transitional Presidency, a Transitional Government and a Transitional Council . Within a Transitional Char ter, these bodies are to re-establish security and the foundations of the state; promote social stability and good governance, conduct political, institutional, electoral,

Transitional Presidency, a Transitional Government and a Transitional Council . Within a Transitional Char ter, these bodies are to re-establish security and the foundations of the state; promote social stability and good governance, conduct political, institutional, electoral, and administrative and education reforms. By the end of the transition period and general elections, these bodies are to hand over power to elected civilians. Their current mandates fit within the Transitional Charter and the 1992 Constitution. National government Executive branch The executive power is vested in the President as the Hea d of State. One of the coup leaders who meanwhile resigned from the Armed Forces, Suma BOMISILLAH was sworn in as Transitional President. The President is elected by direct universal suffrage. The presidential mandate is five years and limited to two conse cutive periods. The President appoints the Prime Minister and the other members of Government, and he/she presides the Council of Ministers. The President can dissolve the National Assembly. The President is Supreme Commander of the Armed Forces. He/she presides the Superior Council for National Defence and the Defence Committee of National Defence. He/she declares the state of siege and the state of emergency after deliberation with the Coun cil of Ministers. Legislative branch The primary legislative authority is exercised through a unicameral National Assembly. The National Assembly has 141 members, elected by direct universal suffrage with an election cycle of four years. However, following the coup, the 147-member Transitional National Council (CNT) comprises representatives of 19 organizations, a majority

National Assembly. The National Assembly has 141 members, elected by direct universal suffrage with an election cycle of four years. However, following the coup, the 147-member Transitional National Council (CNT) comprises representatives of 19 organizations, a majority of which come from the National Defence and Security Forces, the 5 June Movement (M5-RFP) and the political parties. Other groups such as the traditional chieftaincy, diverse civil society organiza tions (women’s, young people’s, human right groups, trade unions and press) and inhabitants living abroad are equally represented. The Transitional President appoints all members of the Tran sitional National Council. The CNT has a law-making initiative within the framework established by the Transitional Charter. The CNT has established normal parliamentarian commissions and groups as for the suspended National Assembly. As a priority, special enquiry commissions hav ebeen established in order to tackle corruption and impunity. The National Assembly declares the state of war. The extension of the state of siege and the state of emergency, declared by t he Council of Ministers, beyond a period of 10 days is the prerogative of the National Assembly. Judicia lbranch The legal system is strongly influenced by the French colonial period and combines modern written law and customary law under pinned by traditional customary rules. Despite attempts to codify particular provisions such as personal and family laws, customary law remains the main instrument for legal issues over land tenure. particular provisions such as personal and family laws, customary law remains the main instrument for legal issues over land tenure. By virtue of the Constitution, the court system in MILA is established as follows. At the highest level, there is the Supreme Court and the High Court of Justice. The Supreme Cour t is the highest court and all decisions from the court are binding for the executive power, the legislative power, as well as for all courts of law. The High Court of Justice is a special court competent to judge the Pres ident and other members of Governm ent for charges of high treason and/or infractions committed in the exercise of their duties. Justice at a lower level is delivered by a system of trial courts and courts of appeal. Relevant Executive and Advisory bodies The Superior Council for National Defence (SCND) falls under the direction of the President. The Superior Council is a general policy body for matters of defence in its broadest sense. It is composed of the President (chairing), the Prime Minister; Minister of Defence and Veterans Affairs; Minister of Security and Civil Protection; Minister of Foreign Affairs and International Cooperation; Minister of Territorial Administration and Decentralisation; the Minister of Economy and Finance; the Minister of Transport and Infrastructure; the Minis ter of Mines; the Minister of Health and Social Development; the Secretary General of the Presidency of the Republic; the Chief of Staff of the President (secretary); and, the Chief of General Staff of

Infrastructure; the Minis ter of Mines; the Minister of Health and Social Development; the Secretary General of the Presidency of the Republic; the Chief of Staff of the President (secretary); and, the Chief of General Staff of the Armed Forces. The Defence Committee for National Defence (DCND) also falls under the direction of the President. The Committee is a body for overall military direction and guidance and crisis management. It is composed of the President (chairing), the Prime Minister; Minister of Defence and Veterans Affa irs; Minister of Security and Civil Protection; Minister of Foreign Affairs and International Cooperation; Minister of Territorial Administration and Decentralisation; the Minister of Economy and Finance; the Minister of Transport and Infrastructure; the Minister of Public Works; the Secretary General of the Presidency of the Republic; the Chief of Staff of the President (secretary); the Inspector General of the Armed Forces; the Chief of General Staff of the Armed Forces; the Director General of State Secu rity; the Director General of the National Police and the Chiefs of Staff of the Armed Forces’ Services. The High Council of Local Authorities (HCCT) . The High Council is a policy body established in response to the 1991 national conference with the intent to empower local communities and their elected authorities though regional and local development. The High Council is compose d of permanent (the Presidency and Secretariat of the High Council) and non-permanent members representing municipal councils, tra ditional chieftaincy, representatives for the different ministries, NGOs, etc.).

Governance and administration at the sub-national level Governance is organised around a hierarchical structure with the District of KOMBIYE, an area around the capital city of KOMBIYE, further comprising regions, cercles, and communes. MILA currently counts nine regions (outside the District of KOMBIYE ) representing the first-tier administrative units. Each region is divided into (on average 6) cercles, the second-tier administrative units. Cercles are subdivided into (on average 15) communes. Each commune consolidates a set of adjacent villages, urban neighbourhoods, or rural fractions. Each region is headed by a governor and each cercle has a prefect both appointed by the central government. Central line ministries are represented at regional level. They could however be represented down to commune level for specific issues like health or education. These centrally appointed people are respon sible for enforcing national laws and coordinating administrative

line ministries are represented at regional level. They could however be represented down to commune level for specific issues like health or education. These centrally appointed people are respon sible for enforcing national laws and coordinating administrative functions. Communes are meant to shift public-service provision closer to both rural and urban citizens. All communes have elected councils which elect the commune mayor. The communes elect representatives to the cercle council, which in turn selects a council head (president). Finally, the cercles select represen tatives to the regional council, which selects a president. This structure reflects a largely decentralised and devolved system, which should enable more autonomy for local communities. However, the effectiveness of local governance is often hindered by limited technical expertise, political interference and corruption which raises questio nsabout the value of centrally appointed line administrators. Moreover, frequent tensions occur between commune officials and hometown associations linked with a commune. Remittances sent home and used to pool funds to finance infrastructure facilities often put commune officials against diaspora hom etown associations. Political parties and interest groups Political power. Political power in MILA is shaped by a complex interplay of a predominant ethnic black majority dominance, civil-military relations, regional tensions, and historical power structures. Since independence, the Velaphi ethnic group and the Bambara subgroup in particular have largely dominated the political sphere, often side-lining other ethnic communities, espec ially those in the north like the Agamen. This has created a power imbalance that fuels regional and ethnic divisions, making governance

subgroup in particular have largely dominated the political sphere, often side-lining other ethnic communities, espec ially those in the north like the Agamen. This has created a power imbalance that fuels regional and ethnic divisions, making governance challenging. Civil-military relations play a significant role in this mix, as the military has frequently intervened with coups during periods of perceived instability . Western influence-particularly from FRANCE and the USA-has long shaped MILA’spolitical landscape, but recently, MURINUS’ influence has grown resulting in a shift away from traditional Western alliance s. This pivot intensified internal political dynamics and is also reshaping MILA’sforeign policy orientation as it seeks to redefine its alliances and assert greater autonomy. Combined, all of these elements contribute to a volatile political environment . Political parties. MILA has a multi-party system with numerous active parties with at least 20-30 parties actively participating in elections and the political process. A large number of several smaller parties (more than 200) fragments the political landscape. Many political parties tend to rely more on political personalities rather than explicit ideologies. Election victories often depend on elite bargaining, clientelism, and coalition-making, resulting in a highly volatile political system. A limited number of political parties (see below) have been able to be systematically represented in the National Assembly. The larger and the smaller political parties form temporary coalitions to constitute parliamentary groups in the National Assembly. Due to this shifting nature of political alliances between the parties, political ideologies have become increasingly fluid. The ongoing conflict

larger and the smaller political parties form temporary coalitions to constitute parliamentary groups in the National Assembly. Due to this shifting nature of political alliances between the parties, political ideologies have become increasingly fluid. The ongoing conflict in northern MILA led to the closure of several state administrations in central and northern regions, affecting the a bility of political parties to operate effectively. Strong support for the military government among a large part of the population poses challenges for the traditional political parties and attempts to curtail their activities complicates things even more .
