MILA has a long tradition of civil society as the individual belongs to at least one community-based organization (CBO), whether it is an age group, an association as a larger CBO, such as one for hunters, women, etc. MILA has developed a vibrant network o finterest groups, including unions, business-oriented groups, and religious organizations, which often play significant roles in shaping political discourse and policy. The Government of MILA tried to incorporate this tradition of community spirit in orde r to mobilize people around development programs through membership organizations such as village cooperatives or village associations. Associations of the traditional chieftaincy continue to enjoy a significant degree of respect, enabling them to play

spirit in orde r to mobilize people around development programs through membership organizations such as village cooperatives or village associations. Associations of the traditional chieftaincy continue to enjoy a significant degree of respect, enabling them to play a major role in conflict management and post-conflict transformation. One of the most import ant civic movement is the June 5 Movement – Rally of Patriotic Forces (M5-RFP, or the "Mouvement du 5 Juin – Rassemblement des Forces Patriotiques"). The movement is a coalition of opposition groups, religious leaders, and civil society representatives. The M5-RFP was instrumental in organizing mass protests, which ultimately led to the military coup that ousted the seated President. Though initially suppor tive of the junta, M5-RFP gradually became critical, particularly with regards to the Transition Charter which some within M5-RFP saw as an attempt to monopolize power. Despite being plagued by internal divisions, M5-RFP remains a significant political force in MILA. Non-state actors Main Terrorist Groups and Violent Extremist Organisations Resistance and Liberation Front (RLF) The RLF originated in BUFRO, where it emerged as a splinter group of the BUFRO Liberation Front (BLF). The RLF seeks to establish a new regional, highly conservative order spanning from the Middle East, through West Africa and the Sahara and the Horn of Africa. The ultimate goal of the RLF is to establish a pan-national entity large enough and capable enough to attack the West from a position of power, spreading their ideology to all of humanity. Macina Liberation Front (FLM, or

ultimate goal of the RLF is to establish a pan-national entity large enough and capable enough to attack the West from a position of power, spreading their ideology to all of humanity. Macina Liberation Front (FLM, or Front de Liberation du Macina) Macina is the name given to the Niger River'sinland delta in the Mopti region of MILA, which is FLM'score area. The FLM is a large rebel movement in central MILA. After initially being repulsed in the Mopti region in 2013, small FLM armed groups established themselves as of mid-2016. The FLM gradually took control of new areas by killing village chiefs and others who cooperate with the authorities, causing the army and the state apparatus to retreat to the cities. This tactic combined with exploiting the people'sdissatisfaction with the authorities, the FLM has been able to gain some support locally, particularly among the Felingué pastoralists, many of them feeling marginalized for long. The group was formed and led by Hamadoun Komifa. National Movement for the Liberation of Azawad (MNLA, or Mouvement National pour la Liberation d’Azawad) The MNLA is an ethnologically driven insurgency which mainly fights for the rights of MILA’sminority Agamen. The MNLA – a successor to previous rebel groups – was formed in 2011. Many Agamen serving in the YABIL Army returned to MILA after the killing of Colonel Gammalar-YABIL’sleader-reinforced the MNLA as it spearheaded an uprising against the MILA A rmy. Th is allowed the MNLA to become the political-military platform to continue their fight

YABIL Army returned to MILA after the killing of Colonel Gammalar-YABIL’sleader-reinforced the MNLA as it spearheaded an uprising against the MILA A rmy. Th is allowed the MNLA to become the political-military platform to continue their fight for self-rule of the Agamen in northern MILA . Militant Groups MILA, as other countries in the Sahel region has a history of community-based militia. These predominantly ethnically based groups are deeply rooted in their territories. With large parts of the MILA Armed Forces having been non-effective in countering terroris m and insurgencies, particularly in northern MILA community-based militia launched their re-conquest of regions controlled by Agamen rebels and the RLF. Though there is little known on the umbrella organization of the Patriotic Resistance Forces (FPR, or Front Patriotique de Résistance), militia are dominated by sedentary communities, predominantly from the Zakana-Sandu ethnic group who have been in conflict for several decades with the more nomadic populations of the semi-desert region around Gao. The most prominent militia are the approximated 2, 000 strong Ganda Koy (Lords of the Land), and the 1, 300 strong Ganda Iso (Sons of the Land). The employment of these militia as part of CT and counter-insurgency efforts has however not always improved security. Allegations have been made of these groups, sometimes in collaboration with the national defence and security forces, having carried out kidnappings, property destruction, sexual abuse, involvement in drug trafficking, the recruitment of children, thus often seeking their own enrichment. 4. Levers of power Security sector and Armed Forces The security sector including the National Defence and Security Forces including home-grown militia groups and backed by MURINUS serves as a critical lever of power for internal control and stability. The Armed Forces play a prominent role, not only in defending a gainst external threats but also in managing internal security challenges stemming from TGs, VEOs, armed and criminal gro ups, particularly in the northern and central regions. The other security forces such as the National Guard and the National Gendarmerie operate as extensions of the Armed Forces, focusing on law enforcement and stability in rural and urban areas. The National Police, distinct in its civil mandate, maintains public order and manages internal security within urban centres . Together, these entities function as tools for the Transitional Government to exercise control, uphold law and order, and res pond to dom estic crises. Since the recent coups and political upheaval, the military’sinfluence has deepened, positioning it as a key actor in MILA’spower structure. Their use is closely coordinated with a MURINUS-supported information campaign to consolidate the Transitional Authorities’ political control and sustain public support, while also reinforcing reliance on MURINUS and, particularl y, the Private Military Company Messner Group. Command and control The Minister of Defence and Veterans Affairs is tasked with the implementation of defence policies and managing veteran and war victim affairs. This includes overseeing military forces and their security, ensuring national defines, managing force readiness and infrastructure, planning personnel and material needs, exercising military judicial powers, participating in international defines negotiations, overseeing peacekeeping missions, and developing protective measures for veterans and war victims. The minister also regularly updates the government on national security in collaboration with relevant departments. The General Staff of the Armed Forces of MILA is a joint organisation with responsibility for concept and doctrine developmen t, planning, decision-making and the conduct of military operations through a Joint Operations Centre. The General Staff falls unde r the command of the

The General Staff falls unde r the command of the Chief of the General Staff, and comprises the Army, the Air Force. In the event of the operational defence of the territory of MILA, the Chief of the General Staff has operational control over the Director General of the National Genda rmerie and the Chief of Staff of the National Guard. A Special Forces battalion and the hardening centres (specialised traini ng) depend directly from the Chief of the General Staff of the Armed Forces. The preparation, conduct, and coordination of civil and military defence efforts are carried out within a defence zone. A Presidential decree determines adhoc the geographical boundaries of the defence zone . A defence zone includes: a military region; an air region or units of the Air Force; one or more groups from the National Gendarmerie and the National Guard and the civil services contributing to national defence. The representative of the State in the region, the chief administrative officer of the defence zone, is responsible for coordinating civil defence activities. A Defence Zone committe eassists the representative of the State in the region. The defence zone is overseen by a Defence Zone Commander, a senior officer appointed by the President of the Republic. This commander is also the military region commander and represents the Chief of General Staff

The Defence Zone Commander has authority over all military units and services (to include Nationale Gendarmerie and National Guard) in the zone, ensuring joint coordination and consist ency of defence plans. Armed Forces military doctrine The military doctrine is purely defensive and predominantly geared towards in ternal stability. Military service Land Forces-Army With a strength of 19, 000 the Army is the largest of the country’s Armed Forces. The Army is responsible for recruiting personnel and the overall readiness of the land forces. The Army is charged with preserving the integrity of the national territory and contributing to its operational defence. It participates in peacekeeping missions and provides humanitarian assistance, thus reflecting the commitment of MILA to national, regional and international stability. Size and structure The MILA Army comprises a Chief of the Staff of the Army, an Army Staff, the Inspector of the Army, Military Regions and two functional commands . The latter commands are the Army Education, Training and Experimentation Command and the Army Support Command, which next to the general support to the Army provides the honor ary services and the security of the Chief of Staff of the Army. The Chief of the Staff of the Army (COSA) designs, directs, coordinates,

which next to the general support to the Army provides the honor ary services and the security of the Chief of Staff of the Army. The Chief of the Staff of the Army (COSA) designs, directs, coordinates, and controls the activit ies of the Army components. The COSA is responsible for the overall organization and operational doctrine, training and operational readiness of personnel and equipment, resource alloca tion and mobilization plans. Army elements that assist the COSA include the Cabinet, special advisors and the Army Staff branches for Operations, Logistics, Human Resources and Finance. The Army Headquarters is situated in KOMBIYE . The Army is structured a long the following branches and specialties: infantry, armour and caval ry, artillery, engineers, signals, logistics, and administration . Each of these has a proper education and training centre and a proper inspector operating under the control of the Inspector of the Army. The MILA Army is in transformation in order to cope with the enduring operations against TGs, VEOs and other armed groups. The organization and operating procedures of the Rapid Intervention Force of the Army have not been disclosed. Air Force Next to IGAINER and NAGA, the MILA Air Force is one of the most developed Air Forces in sub-Saharan West Africa. Strengthening the capabilities of the MILA Air Force is a priority for the General Staff for its operations against TGs, VEOs, other insurgencies and armed groups. The 2, 000 strong Air Force is tasked with the preservation of the integrity of the national airspace and contr ibutes to the operational defence of the territory. The Air Force participates in the search and rescue operations and supports MILA’ssocial, economic, and cultural development. Moreover, it engages in peacekeeping operations and provides humanitarian ass istance. The MILA Air Force has and continues to develop the following air capabilities: fighter, air surveillance, ground based air defence, fixed and rotary wing attack/transport and training, ISR/attack, communication and liaison. Size and structure The area of responsibility for the Air Force is divided into three Air Regions, largely in line with the administrative repartition of MILA. MILA. Air Region 1 with its HQ in Sénou covers the administrative regions of the District of KOMBIYE, Kayes, Koulikoro, Sik asso and the San circle from the Ségou administrative region which is a part of the Air Region 2. Air Region 2 with its HQ in Sévaré covers the administrative regions of Mopti, Timbuktu, Taoudéni, and the San circle from the Ségou administrative region . Air Region 3 with its HQ in Gao covers the administrative regions of Kidal, and Gao. Each Air Region disposes of one or more Air Bases and the Air Detachments within its regional boundaries, an Air Defence Grou p and a Defence and Protection Group. There ar e 3 main air bases in MILA ( KOMBIYE, Sénou, and Sévaré) and 3 secondary ones (Gao, Tessalit, Timbuktu ). Training and Readiness The MILA Air Force has the ability to provide for basic, technical and specialized, and advanced training and development. So me A ir Force personnel receive training abroad, facilitated by international military partnerships. International training missions, such as those provided by the European Union Training Mission (EUTM) and other partners, enhanced the capabilities of the Air Force personnel. However, resource constraints have an impact on the availability and quality of training programs. Efforts are ongoing to improve training infrastructure. Navy As a landlocked country, MILA precludes the necessity for a proper naval force. However, MILA operates a limited number of vessels for patrol and security operations along the Niger River, a vital transport route in the region. There have been incidents involving attacks on passenger boats and military positions along the Niger River, underscoring the ongoing security challenges in th is particular area . International military relations Defence cooperation agreements. Since its independence, MILA has engaged in various defence cooperation agreements, most notably with France. A Defence Coope ration Treaty, a key part of MILA’ssecurity guarantees existed between MILA and FRANCE has been in place for several decades. Treaty, a key part of MILA’ssecurity guarantees existed between MILA and FRANCE has been in place for several decades. MILA’sunilateral decision to terminate this treaty, due to growing unpopularity of the French military intervention, marked a significant shift in the region for FRANCE and Euro pean partner forces. MILA'sshift away from traditional Western partners towards new arrangements, notably MURINUS has been accompanied by tensions with former partners and changes in regional security dynamics. MILA is actively seeking more military and defence cooperation within the sub-Saharan region, notabl y with FANBO and GUINEA. Regional security architecture ECOWAS . ECOWAS member states agreed to coordinate efforts through national policies in their ambition to create stability across We stern Africa. This entails coordination and pooling of counter-terrorist efforts, including tackling the financing of violent extremism, and promoting intercommunal dialogue. However, in view of the coups, MILA has been suspended from ECOWAS and its activities. The LITTERAE7 initiative . The LITTERAE initiative is a coordinating forum between EBEN, FANBO, NAGA, CERO and GOOT (with MILA and REGIN as observers) to stop violent extremism, especially in the border areas. The initiative has three pillars: information and intel ligence capacity sharing; common training; and joint military operations. As this initiative falls under the ECOWAS architecture and MILA being suspended from ECOWAS, MILA has been suspended from this initiative as well. G5 SAHEL is a counterterrorism partnership among CHAD, FANBO, MAURITANIA, MILA and REGIN to address common challenges to peace and security by fighting terrorism in partnership

from ECOWAS, MILA has been suspended from this initiative as well. G5 SAHEL is a counterterrorism partnership among CHAD, FANBO, MAURITANIA, MILA and REGIN to address common challenges to peace and security by fighting terrorism in partnership with regional actors, conducting combined operations and preventing fighters and traffic kers from evading pursuit across national borders. However, in view of the recent coups, MILA left the G5 Sahel security partnership. Foreign military presence. FRANCE had a substantial military presence in MILA as part of its counter-terrorism mission in the Sahel region. However, relations between MILA and FRANCE deter iorated following the coups, leading to the withdrawal of French troops which is still ongoing. The United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in MILA (MINUSMI) involving various international troops continues to be active in MILA. However, as the mission starts to face increasing restrictions imposed by the Transitional Government, the future mandate and strength of MINUSMI are subject of ongoing discussions in MILA and among international partners. 7 The capitol of NAGA. Military-industrial complex (MIC) MILA is almost exclusively relia nt on arms imports to equip its armed forces. The lack of domestic production and limited financial resources for maintaining weapon arsenals, purchasing spare parts, and providing expertise to military personnel are crucial factors for the country'slow military capacity. There is only one local factory for ammunition production, which in recent years has primarily supplied neighbouring FANBO with ammunition for small arms and light weapons, in addition to equipping its own armed forces. Otherwise, MILA ha sonly limited maintenance capabilities. Major challenges A continuous cycle of violence MILA continues to struggle with violence posed by the RLF and various militant groups, predominantly in northern and central regions. This situation is however further complicated by ethnic tensions, particularly between the Fulani herders and more sedentary ethnic groups and the subsequent exploitation of this divide by TGs and VEOs. Weak state presence Weak control by the central government over large parts of MILA created a power vacuum continuously exploited by malign actors. Situation is exacerbated by porous borders with ALGERIA, FANBO and REGIN facilitating the movement of arms, fighters, and illicit goods. Enduring foreign military presence amidst a complex geo political landscape Inadequate resources reducing the National Defence and Security Forces’

exacerbated by porous borders with ALGERIA, FANBO and REGIN facilitating the movement of arms, fighters, and illicit goods. Enduring foreign military presence amidst a complex geo political landscape Inadequate resources reducing the National Defence and Security Forces’ effectiveness necessitate structural foreign military presence. Popular resentment against Western military support in view of the deteriorating security situation in MILA provided MURINUS with an opportunity to extend its influence across Africa. MILA’srealignment in favour of MURINUS continues to raise concerns, regarding the country’shuman rights record, the position of ECOWAS and the long-term stability of the Sahel region. The increased presence of MURINUS introduced new geopolitical complexities, as MILA needs to navigate relations hips with both Western and non-Western powers. Police and paramilitary forces NATIONAL GENDARMERIE The approximated 4, 000 strong National Gendarmerie falls under the authority of the Ministry of Defence and Veterans Affairs. Operational control of the National Gendarmerie is however shared with the Ministry of Security and Civil Protection. The National Gendarmerie is responsible for maintaining public order, collecting intelligence, protecting private property, and contributing to territorial defence due to its military stat us. The National Gendarmerie acts as the military police of the Armed Forces of MILA and participates in peacekeeping operations of the Armed Forces of MILA abroad. Though responsibilities overlap with those of the National Police, the National Gendarmerie is predominantly responsible for security in rural areas, while t he National Police covers the urban areas in MILA. The National Gendarmerie is under the command of a

with those of the National Police, the National Gendarmerie is predominantly responsible for security in rural areas, while t he National Police covers the urban areas in MILA. The National Gendarmerie is under the command of a Director General of the National Gendarmerie. It is organised with a numbe r of Gendarmerie Regions in accordance with the country’sadministrative repartition. These Gendarmerie Regions dispose of a variable number of National Gendarmerie legions, territorial groups, companies and brigades and security posts. The national Gendarmerie also includes a number of specialized units such as a National Gendarmerie Intervention Platoon (PIGN), a cavalry units, river units, etc. NATIONAL GUARD The National Guard is tasked with ensuring the security of institutions and authorities, protecting high-ranking individuals, maintaining public order, executing judicial and administrative decisions, defending the territory, combating terrorism and major crime, escorting equipment and delegations, border surveill ance, and supporting peace and humanitarian efforts. The National Guard plays an important role the security architecture of MILA, focusing on both internal security and territorial defence, while also engaging in community relations and development activities. While administratively under the Ministry of Defense and Veterans Affairs, operational control of the National Guard is share d with the Ministry of Security and Civil Protection. The approximately 10, 000 strong National Guard is composed of a Division-sized formation with a HQ and one National Guards Brigade in KOMBIYE and a number of regionally dispersed National Guard Groups in accordance with the administrative repartition of MILA. The latter peripheral units

is composed of a Division-sized formation with a HQ and one National Guards Brigade in KOMBIYE and a number of regionally dispersed National Guard Groups in accordance with the administrative repartition of MILA. The latter peripheral units have differing strengths from a Battalion-to a Company-sized formation and operate independently. Except for the National Guard in KOMBIYE equipment with relatively mode rnequipment of predominant MURINUS and DUSHMAN origin, the equipment of the other National Guard formations in the periphery is largely outdated with light armament, light reconnaissance vehicles, light cargo trucks, camels or horses. NATIONAL POLICE The National Police with an estimated strength between 5, 000 and 7, 000 is subject to the authority of the Minister of the Securit y and Civil Protection. The National Police is a largely decentralised organisation under the responsibility of a Directorate General of National Police. The latter has a number of special services such as a service for public security, judiciary police, general intelligence and surveillance of the territory, bor der police, the National Police Academy, and an Anti-Terrorist Group (und erdirect control of the Minister of Security and Civil Protection). National Police responsibilities are concentrated exclus ively in urban areas. Each administrative region and the capital district of KOMBIYE has a Regional Directorate of National Police. In the urban areas of each Region, the National Police has established territorial public security services, responsible for administrative and/or judicial police within the irterritorial jurisdiction. According to the Global Organized Crime Index, the National Police face

In the urban areas of each Region, the National Police has established territorial public security services, responsible for administrative and/or judicial police within the irterritorial jurisdiction. According to the Global Organized Crime Index, the National Police face corruption, lack of resources, and lack of training. There have been allegations that some National Police employees were involved in cases of forced labour and sex work. The public confidence in the National Police among society is low. CIVIL PROTECTION The Ministry of Security and Civil Protection established a territorial network of Civil Protection Directorates in each of t he regional capitals. Firefighter units, an integral part of the Civil Protection architecture, have been established in economically significant localities and along major roads and waterways. Rescue centres and rescue posts provide permanent assistance and relief to victims of accidents, fires, drownings, and other disaste rs. Following the recent coups, the Transitional Government decided to militarise the National Police and the Civil Protection. The implications of the militaris ation on structural and operational changes or cultural and attitudinal shifts are yet to be discerned. Intelligence and security services There is little information available on the current organisation and the command and control of the Intelligence and Securit y Services’ architecture in MILA since the coups. It is highly likely that a restructuring of the intelligence and security architecture is ongoing as the Transitional Government continues to establish itself. Presidential Office The former Direction Général dela Sûreté de l’Etat (DGSE or Directorate General of State

highly likely that a restructuring of the intelligence and security architecture is ongoing as the Transitional Government continues to establish itself. Presidential Office The former Direction Général dela Sûreté de l’Etat (DGSE or Directorate General of State Security), under direct control of the Prime Minister has been replaced by an Agence National de Securité de l’Etat (ANSE) under the direct authority of the Transitional President. The National Agency for State Security is responsible for ensuring the security of the State and its institutions both domest ic as abroad. It serves as a decision-making aid for the President in all national matters. The ANSE conducts research on threats to national integrity and securit y, such as terrorism, violent extremism, espionage, misinformation, and political interference. It develops strategies to anticipate and counter threats, emanating from individuals, groups, organizations, and foreign services using various means, including information technology. There is no information to assert whether this agency is intended to opera te as a managerial body for the intelligence services of the entire country. Ministry of Security and Civil Protection After the coup, the Ministry of Security and Civil Protection established an Intelligence Analysis and Fusion Centre. This Centre that me ets on a weekly basis appears to be operating as a coordination body for all intelligence related activities by the different Ministries and Servic essuch as the ANSE, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MoFA), the National Police, the National Gendarmerie, National Guard and Civil Protection and the Direction of Military Security of

intelligence related activities by the different Ministries and Servic essuch as the ANSE, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MoFA), the National Police, the National Gendarmerie, National Guard and Civil Protection and the Direction of Military Security of the Ministry of Defence and Veterans Affairs. Border security forces The Directorate of Border Police is one of the central services of the National Police. The Directorate of Border Police is the primary agency dedicated to border security and border management. It enforces entry and stay laws for foreigners, manages cross-border movement information, is sues travel and residence documents, handles immigration and emigration policy, and provides guidance for border control units and services. MILA has created a National Directorate of Borders, which is a governmental position under the Ministry of Territor ial Administration and Decentralization. The National Director is responsible for the management of MILA’sborders, the coordination with other government agencies an dinternational organizations on border-related issues, the implementation of national pol icies related to border security and management and the monitoring and controlling cross-border movements to prevent illegal activities such as smuggling and trafficking. Private security companies (PSC) Private security companies (PSCs) in MILA are vital, especially in view of the significant raise of terrorism and organized crime. Several hundred PSCs handle surveillance, guarding, fund transport, and protection providing services for government agencies, private firms, banks, dipl omatic missions, and individuals. However, PSCs face significant challenges. Though there is a legal framework, it is considered to be outdated. Inadequate training and oversight

transport, and protection providing services for government agencies, private firms, banks, dipl omatic missions, and individuals. However, PSCs face significant challenges. Though there is a legal framework, it is considered to be outdated. Inadequate training and oversight result in staff misuse of force and human rights issues. The difficult security environment and substandard working conditions also obstruct their operations as PSC personnel are not allowed to carry firearms, relying on armed police escorts in high-risk areas. Information and cyber Information Political instability and security challenges have considerably affected the information environment in MILA, where narrative sremain highly contested. The presence of MUR, TGs, VEOs and multiple armed groups complicate the information landscape, with various actors engaging to in fluence perceptions and assert legitimacy. The battle for hearts and minds is a critical aspect within the information environme nt with information used as a weapon to recruit, radicalize, and intimidate. One of the major challenges in the information environment is the spread of misinformation and rumours, which exacerbate tens ions and violence. Social Media. Social media is the most popular form of communication among the youth with Facebook being by far the most popular (19.6% of internet users). The re are an estimated 2.1 million social media users ( 9.1% of the population) in MILA, with an estimated 24. 2% of these being female and 75. 8% being male. Social media platforms are increasingly used for information sharing and mobilization, yet they also present challenges related to misinformation and hate speech,

MILA, with an estimated 24. 2% of these being female and 75. 8% being male. Social media platforms are increasingly used for information sharing and mobilization, yet they also present challenges related to misinformation and hate speech, especially in the context of ethnic tensions and political divide. MURINUS, the Messner Group and i nsurgent groups are using social media to disseminate propaganda, thus deepening the crisis. Government influence and media freedom. In theory, the media and journalists are free to cover the government, and the privately owned media are relatively independe nt. However, in practice, journalism has been made much more difficult by the political situation and the tougher stance taken by t he transitional authorities . The High Authority for Communication (HAC), MILA’smedia regulator, and the state-owned media do as they are told by government officials. The pressure for “patriotic” news coverage is growing. The accreditation process for for eign journalists is very intrusive and threatens the confidentiality of their sources. The HAC decided to ban Radio France International (RFI) and France 24 from broadcasting in MILA. The media’sactivity is governed by the press law, which determines the conditions in which they operate. But the press law is vague, does not define media offences and contains no provisions about online media. A revision of this obsolete legal framework has been under way for several years. Journalists are awaiting new laws that will abolish prison sentences for press offences, institutionalise state aid for the media, and improve access to inform

revision of this obsolete legal framework has been under way for several years. Journalists are awaiting new laws that will abolish prison sentences for press offences, institutionalise state aid for the media, and improve access to inform ation and the identification of journalists and media professionals. The state-owned media continue to have easier access to state-held information than privately owned media. Disinformation and misinformation. MILA has become one of the African states most influenced by MURINUS . Its media is saturated with MUR bots, proxies, and Messner Group affiliates, pushing narratives that under mine trust in international institutions and promote cooperation with MUR over Western alliances. The Messner Group and MURINUS have fueled anti-French sentiments, leveraging historical grievances and perceived failures of military interventions. They exploit dissatisfaction with security, particularly anger at FRANCE, to justify the need for PMC suppo rt. The transitional government equally uses coordinated disinformation to foster support for Messner and MUR while blaming international forces for the worsening security situation, thus hindering their mission and increasing risks to their security . Othe rgroups in MILA such as the RLF, FLM and MLA also use a range of means of communication (such as social media, word of mouth, encrypted messaging apps) for recruitment, training, and propaganda purposes, which continues to be a significant challenge for t he government . The Africa Initiative, a MURINUS news agency, based in REGIN but with active offices in MILA and FANBO is positioning itself as the new bridgehead for MURINUS'spropaganda in

be a significant challenge for t he government . The Africa Initiative, a MURINUS news agency, based in REGIN but with active offices in MILA and FANBO is positioning itself as the new bridgehead for MURINUS'spropaganda in Africa. Local initiatives and civic engagement. Traditional communication is a vital components of MILA’sinformation environment . This not only serves as a means of communication and dissemination of information; it also strengthen scommunity bonds. Traditional communication networks are particularly important in areas where formal media coverage is limited or in communities with high illiteracy rates. Information security and cyber threats. The most common cyber threats in MILA include challenges related to cybersecurity (similar to most cyber threats across Africa, these include: online scams, digital extortion, business email compromise, ransomware, and botnets ), disinformation, and privacy rights. MILA has enacted a cybercrime law that has raised concerns about potential threats to privacy and freed om of expression online. These factors highlight the crucial need to strengthen information security and combat cyber threats in MILA to protect digital rights and lessen the impact of ongoing conflicts on the country'sdigital environment. Media landscape The media landscape in MILA consists of both state-owned and private entities, including radio, television, print, and online outlets. Among these, radio stands out as the most accessible and prominent medium, especially in rural areas, due to its extensive reach and multilingual content. However, the dominance of state-run media, combined with the self-censorship practiced by private outlets, creates a complex and constrained media

most accessible and prominent medium, especially in rural areas, due to its extensive reach and multilingual content. However, the dominance of state-run media, combined with the self-censorship practiced by private outlets, creates a complex and constrained media environment. This situation is further complicated by political instabili ty and threats from extremist factions. MILA has two public state media outlets: the MILA Office of Radio and Television Broadcasting (ORTM), established in 1992. OR TM operates one national TV channel, one local TV channel in KOMBIYE, Radio MILA (a nationa lradio station), and eight regional radio stations. Print media, on the other hand, is largely limited to urban centers like KOMBIYE and other large cities. According to the Reporters Without Borders World Press Freedom Index, MILA ranks 113th out of 180 countries. Journalists and media outlets in MILA face economic hardships, making them vulnerable to influence and corruption. These challenges have been exacerbated by a decline i nadvertising revenue due to the pandemic and the cessation of government aid over the past years. Journalistic freedom in MILA is further restricted by intercommunal conflicts, extremism, and the presence of armed groups, particularly in the northern and central regions. The media are subject to attacks based on gender, class, and ethnicity, which leads to self-censorship and hinders the free flow of information. This environment also contributes to the spread of misinformation. The quality of journalism in MILA is affected by several factors, including poor qualifications of journ alists, low wages, lack of job security,

the free flow of information. This environment also contributes to the spread of misinformation. The quality of journalism in MILA is affected by several factors, including poor qualifications of journ alists, low wages, lack of job security, and the absence of collective agreements. DUSHMAN (DUS) has maintained a long-standing relationship with MILA since its independence. Recently, DUS has increased its investment in MILA’smedia infrastructure, journa list training, and cultural exchange programs. Some journalists have participated in training courses in DUS and shared their positive experiences in their newspapers, enhancing DUS’simage in MILA. However, other reports offer a more critical view, contra sting the rhetoric of brotherly cooperation with the realities of DUS'sactivities in the country. RADIO The number of private radio stations grew exponentially since the liberalization of the airwaves in the early 1990s. A naapproximated 500 radio stations reach 80% of the population. Due to the conflict in northern and central regions, the private and public radio stations operate almost solely in the south. Some radio stations in the north have been taken over or co-opted by some of the TGs, VEOs or rebel groups. There is a wide network of community radios divided in several associations. MILA also relays foreign radio content (Radio France Internationale-RFI, BBC Africa, Deutsche Welle and Voice of America-VOA) without being covered by any type of regulation. NEWS AGENCY AMAP Agence Milanienne de Presse – Press Agency of MILA Diplomacy MILA'sdiplomatic relations have undergone significant shifts, as it pivoted away from traditional Western alliances towards stronger ties with MURINUS, facilitated by the presence of the Messner Group. This realignment has strained MILA’srelationships with several sub-Saharan African nations, especially within the ECOWAS, which has expressed concern over the erosion of

Western alliances towards stronger ties with MURINUS, facilitated by the presence of the Messner Group. This realignment has strained MILA’srelationships with several sub-Saharan African nations, especially within the ECOWAS, which has expressed concern over the erosion of democratic processes and human rights under its military-led Transitional Government. The AU shares similar concerns, leading to tensions between MILA and these regional bodies. Moreover, MILA’scloser ties with MURINUS have complicated its role within the Sahel region, where cooperation on counterterrorism efforts is critical. The divergence from Western-backed initiatives has created challenges for regional cohesion, leaving MILA somewhat isolated diplomatically within both ECOWAS and the AU, while fostering new, yet more controversial partnerships with other military-led governmen ts in the region such as FANBO and GUINEA. ALGERIA The diplomatic relations between ALGERIA and MILA are multifaceted, involving cooperation in security, economic development, and cultural exchange. Both countries face significant security challenges, part icularly from terrorist groups operating in the Sahel region. ALGERIA has been actively involved in supporting MILA'sefforts to combat terrorism. ALGERIA has played a pivotal role in mediating peace processes between the government of MILA and various nor thern rebel groups resulting in the Algiers Accord signed in 2015. REGIN Both countries were part of French West Africa, influencing political and cultural ties leading to a close partnership. Cultu ral connections are significant including ethnic groups th at cross the border that fostered large-scale trade links and population movements between MILA and REGIN. Both countries have and continue to face similar

to a close partnership. Cultu ral connections are significant including ethnic groups th at cross the border that fostered large-scale trade links and population movements between MILA and REGIN. Both countries have and continue to face similar security challenges, particularly related to Agamen and in combating terrorism and organized crime, in particular in the Liptako-Gourma area, which encompasses the tri-border region between MILA, REGIN and FANBO. This area has become a key zone of conflict and criminal activity in recent year s. The political developments in MILA have not significantly strained relations with REGIN. FANBO A territorial border dispute between MILA and FANBO over the Agacher Strip in the 1980s triggered a short war in 1983. Since the border was demarcated after a ruling of the International Court of Justice in 1986, relation s have been generally stable. The two countries have conducted combined operations against violent extremists in the Sahel through the G5 Sahel initiative. However, the worsening situation in northern and central MIL A has spilled over into FANBO. The tri-border area between FANBO, MILA and REGIN is now one of the epicentres of violence and instability in sub-Saharan West Africa. Transhumance and sedentary farmers, forced south by conflict or desertification, continue to put pressure on land water resources in the borderlands, leading to tensions between farmers and pastoralists in MILA and FANBO. Following the coups in MILA and FANBO which left the two countries suspended from ECOWAS and the AU, diplomatic relations with FANBO have deepened in

borderlands, leading to tensions between farmers and pastoralists in MILA and FANBO. Following the coups in MILA and FANBO which left the two countries suspended from ECOWAS and the AU, diplomatic relations with FANBO have deepened in order to circu mvent their isolated position. GUINEA A shared understanding of their political contexts marks diplomatic relations between MILA and GUINEA. There is a historical notion of a political association between MILA and GUINEA. Both have experienced military coups, leading to a mutual understanding of their political situations. The recent political developments in MILA have strengthened ties. This generated collaborative efforts in security, regional solidarity in a conte xt of forming alliances with other count ries facing similar challenges, such as FANBO. CERO The diplomatic relations between MILA and CERO have been characterized by both cooperation and tension, particularly in light of the recent political developments in MILA. Relations have become tense as CERO condemned the coups and supported ECOWAS’ stance for a return to constitutional order. CERO unilaterally withdrew its peacekeepers from MINUSMI following the arrest of 49 its soldiers in MILA, whom KOMBIYE accuse d of being mercenaries. Despite the stra ined diplomatic relations, both countries recognize the importance of addressing security challenges, as TGs and VEOs are slo wly making inroad from the Sikasso region into northern CERO. SENEGAL Relations between MILA and SENEGAL have been characterized by cooperation and shared interests. Both countries have similar ethnic groups and share deep cultural, linguistic, and historical ties. MILA and MAURITANIA

the Sikasso region into northern CERO. SENEGAL Relations between MILA and SENEGAL have been characterized by cooperation and shared interests. Both countries have similar ethnic groups and share deep cultural, linguistic, and historical ties. MILA and MAURITANIA collaborate on several development initiatives, such as t he Organisation pour la Mise en Valeur du Fleuve Sénégal (OMVS), which focuses on the management and development of the Senegal River basin. However, in light of the rec ent political developments in MILA, SENEGAL has become more cautious in its diplomatic approach. SENEGAL condemned the coup and sup ported ECOWAS’ stance against military takeovers. It has also expressed concerns about the security situation in MILA and its potential spill over effects. Despite these challenges, both countries recognize the importance of cooperation in addressing regional security threats and issues such as smuggling, human trafficking, and illegal migration, especially in the Kayes region and the city of Kidira, one of the main transit points between SENEGAL and MILA and transport hub for contraband pharmaceuticals, narcotics, and cattle rustling. MAURITANIA MALI and MAURITANIA have maintained cordial diplomatic relations since they established ties they negotiated a boundary dispu te in 1963. Both countries face similar security challenges, particularly from TGs and VEO soperating in the Sahel region. The two countries work together to secure their shared border, addressing issues such as smuggling, human trafficking, and illegal migration, especially in the Kayes region and Nioro du Sahel which is a transport hub for contraband consumer goods and narcotics. together to secure their shared border, addressing issues such as smuggling, human trafficking, and illegal migration, especially in the Kayes region and Nioro du Sahel which is a transport hub for contraband consumer goods and narcotics. Guidance and caveats: The CERO Country Model (CM) is a Joint Warfare Centre product issued solely to support NATO exercises. No part of this product may be reproduced or used in any manner without written permission by the JWC. This document is a document that contains a combination of fictitious and generic information extracted from publicly available open sources and adapted to the NATO exercises. Although the CM draws on real world events and data, the actors and dynamics portrayed have been adapted in order to meet NATO Training Audience (TA) needs. For this reason, some actors are given a more prominent role. Furthermore, images, maps, names and other supporting information such as that used for biographies do not always describe a real character, location, or social characteristic (historical/religious/political etc.). This to ensure, inter alia, that the CM adheres to national caveats and sensitivities. Any remaining identified sensitivities should be communicated to JWC so that these can be addressed. User Guide: This setting document is the foundation for RED and GREEN ORBATs and Battle Space Objects (BSO’s) established in INTEL FS and TOPFAS, and the foundation for scenario related products developed for specific exercises. While it aims to ensure coherence with real world events and dynamics, the content has been adapted to meet exercise and scenario needs. The CM is not exhaustive and therefore allows for additional details to be obtained by the TA via the RFI process. The structure of the CM is designed to avoid repetition and to

exercise and scenario needs. The CM is not exhaustive and therefore allows for additional details to be obtained by the TA via the RFI process. The structure of the CM is designed to avoid repetition and to cover the domains that are most relevant to the TA. Drawing on PMESII and other frameworks it categorises the analysis by way of four chapters: the geostrategic and regional context, the socioeconomic and physical environment, the political and key actors’ landscape, and levers of power (military, information, etc) available to these actors. Geostrategic and regional context Historical timeline 1842 1893 1944 1960 1999 2002 2010 2011 2020 CERO in the making: For more than three decades after its independence from FRANCE in 1960, CERO was known for i ts religious and ethnic harmony as well as its well-developed economy. An armed rebellion in 2002 split the nation in two . Subsequent deals alternated with outbursts of violence as the country slowly edged its way towards a political resolution of the conflict. Despite these challenges, CERO today is the world'slargest exporter of cocoa beans, and its citizens enjoy a relatively high level of income compared to other countries in the region. President Henri COULIBALY, in power since 1993, is overthrown in a military coup . France grants the country independence under President Antoine BACEYOKO . He holds power until he dies in 1993. Long-delayed presidential elections . Election commission declares Moussa

in power since 1993, is overthrown in a military coup . France grants the country independence under President Antoine BACEYOKO . He holds power until he dies in 1993. Long-delayed presidential elections . Election commission declares Moussa DIACERATE the winner . FRANCE imposes protectorate over the coastal zone and begins a gradual colonization of CERO. First CERO Civil War splits country into Muslim rebel-held north and government-controlled Christian south . Up to 1, 700 people are killed and 750, 000 people displaced. Second CERO Civil War: post-election violence leaves 3, 000 people dead and 500, 000 displaced. CERO become sa French colony . Antoine BACEYOKO, later to become CERO’sfirst President, founds a union of African farmers, which develops into the African Democratic Rally . DIACERATE wins a controversial third presidential term in an election boycotted by the opposition. Foreign policy objectives Emerging from years of conflict and political violence, CERO continues to work on strengthening the stability of its institutions in order to improve governance and service delivery . The West African country is still struggling to overcome the polarizing ethnic politics and conflicting internal interests that undermined the government ’slegitimacy and contributed to a spiral of coups and, ultimately, civil war in the 2000s. Today, CERO’sforeign policy revolve saround four sectors of operations, namely sovereignty, security, sustainable development and human development . Its main strategic objectives are listed as follows: Ensuring peace, safety and national reconciliation by practising democratic governance, respect of human rights, and support to the rules-based

operations, namely sovereignty, security, sustainable development and human development . Its main strategic objectives are listed as follows: Ensuring peace, safety and national reconciliation by practising democratic governance, respect of human rights, and support to the rules-based international order; Securing its territory against internal and external threats, while serving as a stabilizing partner in the region; Encouraging a more evenly distributed economic growth, resultin g in a stronger, climate-adaptive, and transparent economy; Diversifying its diplomatic partnerships by nurturing its historical relations with Europe while fostering cooperation with Asia, North America and South America; Reinforcing its sub-regional rol e of economic power house of Francophone West Africa by integrating neighbouring countries into its national policy. The external action of the Republic of CERO is determined by a number of geographic, historical, economic, social and cultural factors, all arising from the complexity of its surrounding environment. Well aware of its geographic location, CERO takes advantages of its coastline not only to serve the interest s of its own population, but also of those in neighbouring landlocked countries, who a ll depend on the country for supply chain connectivity to ports and overseas markets. Linked by roads and railways with interior francophone countries, CERO plays an outsized regional economic role as a major hub and host country for many nationals from t he Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) and elsewhere . It has long been an immigration destination from other-much poorer-West African francophone countries . CERO is now on track to

country for many nationals from t he Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) and elsewhere . It has long been an immigration destination from other-much poorer-West African francophone countries . CERO is now on track to become the region’ssecond-biggest economy after IGAINER.1 1 The country Gross Domestic Product (GDP) rose from less than $43 billion in 2013 to $70 billion today. 2. CERO ’spopulation is likely to continue growing for the foreseeable future as almost 60% of the population is younger than 25. The total fertility rate of CERO is 3.4 children per woman. People and ethnicity More than 60 indigenous groups . Main groups are the Kawe (42%), the Dioula or Northerners (34%), and the Kora (11%) . Religion Primary: Muslim ( 42%) Other s: Christians ( 34%); traditional animist religions (24%) Family and gender According to CERO tradition, marriage s are often determined by the extended family, which constitutes the basic social unit. Men are generally seen as the head of the household, while women traditionally tend to domestic needs and childrearing. Multiple factors contribute to gender inequality in the country. For example, subsequent conflicts have impacted family structures in CERO, resulting in a significant increase of gender inequality. More than one-third of CERO girls are forced into marriage before the age of 18 . Girls also have fewer education opportunities. Education Educational services expanded considerably after independence and primary education is free of charge and officially compulsory for six years. Secondary schooling is provided in two cycles of three years and two years, respectively. The main universities in CERO include the University of Abobo-Adjamé, and the University of Cocody, both in IDAVILLE, and the University of Bouaké. There are also several colleges in the country, primarily located in IDAVILLE and TOUBA . About half of CERO ’spopulation age d 15 and older can read and write. Culture and arts The cultural scene is split between a maze of ethnic-based cultures and a foreign influence that is almost exclusively French. Music is a vital part of CERO culture, as testified by a strong tradition of griots who use music to help tell historical stories. The national library is located in IDAVILLE, as is a museum that houses a variety of artistic, ethnographic, and scientific collections. Our Lady of Peace of TOUBA Basilica (clearly emulating St. Peter ’s Basilica in ROME ) was built by former president Antoine BACEYOKO in TOUBA, his hometown; upon completion in 1989, it was the largest Christian church in the world. The country also features a number of UNESCO World Heritage

in ROME ) was built by former president Antoine BACEYOKO in TOUBA, his hometown; upon completion in 1989, it was the largest Christian church in the world. The country also features a number of UNESCO World Heritage sites: the Historic Town of Grand-Bassam, the Mount Nimba Strict Nature Reserve, as well as Tai and Comoé National Parks. 2 However, approximately 60 dialects (of which the Dioula is the most widely spoken) are represented in CERO. CERO consists of over 60 ethnic groups. The north-western Velaphi group features Maninka and Jula as its most important subgroups, while the north-eastern Owochi include Senoufo, Lobi, Mossi and other s; combined into one single category, they are known as the “Northerners ”. The term “Dioula” is commonly used to refer to the “Northerners” in general. The southern Velaphi peoples (Dan and Gouro) are culturally distinct from the northern Velaphi groups. The southeast is predominantly populated by Kawe peoples, of which the Baule are the most important group in political terms. The other most significant Kawe groups are the Abron, Agni, and th e Lagoon peoples . Finally, the Kora are composed of the Bete, Dida, Guere and other sub-groups, of which the Bete are the largest and politically most relevant subgroup . Close ties to FRANCE, diversification of agricultural

Economy One of West Africa’smost influential, stable, and rapidly developing countries, CERO is the largest economy in the West African Economic and Monetary Union, constituting 40% of the bloc'stotal GDP. Close ties to FRANCE, diversification of agricultural exports, and promotion of foreign investment are all factors behind its spectacular economic growth. CERO’ssuccess as an exporter of cocoa in particular (the top producer worldwide) and coffee has been a major factor contributing to its stability, as these exports quickly enabled the country to achieve comparatively high level of prosperity and social development. CERO has recovered well from the period of political-military instability and division that characterized the early 2000s . The country benefited from the reasonably solid structural base built up in the three decades after independence, primarily thanks to high cocoa prices and oil exploration in the Gulf of Guinea – sectors that remained largely untouched by the conflict. A sa major regional hub, CERO attracts significant trade and investment: the cocoa and coffee markets, largely controlled by multinational companies, make the country’seconomy deeply integrated into the global system. The government is actively pursuing a private sector-led growth model, welcoming Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) and offering favourable tax incentives to companies in accordance with priorities identified in its 2021-2025 National Development Plan ( PND – Plan National de Développement ). Elites in IDAVILLE are paying very close attention to international rankings to increase CERO attractiveness, focusing in particular on the digital economy as well as hydro-electric, solar, and thermal electricity

PND – Plan National de Développement ). Elites in IDAVILLE are paying very close attention to international rankings to increase CERO attractiveness, focusing in particular on the digital economy as well as hydro-electric, solar, and thermal electricity generati on capacity, and youth employment opportunities throughout the country .3 However, despite strong economic performance, the poverty has grown sharply compared to its level three decades ago. More than 40% of the population lives under the poverty threshol d, and around a quarter of the working population remains unemployed, lacking the skills and education needed to be competitive in a modernizing economy. A wide income disparity exists between IDAVILLE and the rest of the country, revealing a model of grow th not necessarily inclusive and sustainable. Economic growth has also come at the cost of environmental degradation: the country has pledged to reach net zero emissions by 2030 mainly through reforestation and land-use changes, but reaching that goal seems difficult as small-scale farmers need land to grow crops and often cut down trees to make space for agriculture. Moreover, CERO has recently faced a security threat from the expansion of terrorism affecting the Sahel in the north and maritime piracy in the south. This threat hinders economic development, especially as the port of IDAVILLE, the third largest port in Sub-Saharan Africa, handles 75% of the country’strade and accounts for 70% of the country's GDP . 3 The country’syoung population is an asset, comprising the majority of the available workforce: about 3/4 of

OVERVIEW: CERO features a traditional economic system heavily dependent on agriculture activities. Strategically located on the southern coast of West Africa, CERO plays a key role in transit trade for neighbouring landlocked countries. CERO is number 82 in total exports worldwide, number 90 in total imports, and number 117 in terms of most complex economy. CERO major industries: Foodstuffs, beverages, wood products, oil refining. CERO major exports: cocoa beans ($3.33B), gold ($2.12B ), rubber ($2.11B ), refined petroleum ($1.88B ), and cocoa paste ($1.08B ) exporting mostly to SWITZERLAND ($1.6B), MILA ($1.4B), USA ($1.08B ), and FRANCE ($940M ). CERO major imports: crude petroleum ($2.28B ), refined petroleum ($1.39B ), rice ($821M ), and special purpose ships ($637M ), importing mostly from DUSHMAN ($3.31B ), IGAINER ($1.97B ), FRANCE ($1.44B ), DRENUR ($965M), and BELGIUM ($812M). Gross National Income explained: Because development encompasses many factors including economic, environmental, cultural, educational, and institutional, no single measure gives a complete picture. However, the total earnings of the residents of an economy, measured by its Gross National Income (GNI), is a good indicator of its capacity to provide for the well-being of its people. Economy sectors The country’seconomy is mainly based on agriculture, with the primary sector contributing to 17% of the

Gross National Income (GNI), is a good indicator of its capacity to provide for the well-being of its people. Economy sectors The country’seconomy is mainly based on agriculture, with the primary sector contributing to 17% of the GDP and employing 46% of the country'sactive population. The government is trying to maximise agricultural output by developing raw material processing units. The oil sector has also been gaining weight, leveraging a steady growth rate and major investments. Moreover, the country has some mining activities, particularly of precious minerals, such as gold and diamonds, but also oth ers like nickel. Agriculture provides a livelihood for more than half the labour force, with locally grown subsistence crops meeting most rural domestic needs. Urbanization and the growing use of hired labour throughout the country created a demand for foodstuffs other than yams, cass ava, plantains, and corn. The industrial sector contributes to 22% of the GDP and employs only 10% of the active population. Key sectors include agro-processing, petroleum refining, and manufacturing. Agro-processing is a cornerstone of CERO industrial landscape, with cocoa processing being particularly significant as the world'slargest producer of cocoa beans. Similarly, the country'soil refining industry also benefits from its status of regional hub for petroleum products. Manufacturing, including textiles, chemicals, and food processing, is another important sector, driven by government efforts to diver sify the economy and promote industrialization. CERO industrial sector retains much of the legacy of a colonial policy founded on export rather than the more desirable expansion of the

important sector, driven by government efforts to diver sify the economy and promote industrialization. CERO industrial sector retains much of the legacy of a colonial policy founded on export rather than the more desirable expansion of the loca lmarket. The services sector contributes to 54% of the GDP and employs 44% of the workforce. Like in many other African countries, the tertiary sector has grown at relatively rapid rate in the last several years, with both the telecommunications and banking sector sbooming as key drivers of services. The informal secto rdominates the economy ( more than 90 %), creating a burden of higher taxation on the formal sector . Business climate CERO has managed to attract private capital, indicating international confidence in the country’smid to long-term economic prospects. Private companies represent the backbone of CERO economy: policy makers in IDAVILLE firmly believe that a dynamic private sector is a powerful engine for job creation and a pillar of sustainable and inclusive economic growth and poverty reduction. However, entrepreneur associations continue to complain about high domestic production costs, especially those related to electricit y and poor transportation infrastructure. The International Monetary Fund (IMF) has repeatedly expressed concern about the poor ope rating environment for business in CERO, to which the country has reacted by improving administrative procedures, creating a new investment code, and concentrating competencies in a single authority. However, other aspects of business activity, such as dea ling with construction permits or paying taxes, continue to pose greater challenges. Although

improving administrative procedures, creating a new investment code, and concentrating competencies in a single authority. However, other aspects of business activity, such as dea ling with construction permits or paying taxes, continue to pose greater challenges. Although the country used to be hailed as a capitalist model in Africa, the institutional setting for free markets and compet ition has never been fully developed. This is particularly true in the vital cocoa sector, where uncertainty over economic fundamentals and regulation has persisted. Strengths: Variety of resources: agricultural wealth (world’slargest producer of cocoa), hydrocarbons and ore (gold, copper, iron, manganese, bauxite); Infrastructure undergoing modernization; Expanding middle class; Access to international capital markets. Weaknesses: Vulnerable to climatic hazards; Deficiencies in the management of public finance combined with low government revenues and ineffective governance system; Massive informal economy; Large wealth gap between IDAVILLE and the rest of the country. National budget The government of CERO has recently announced a XOF 15, 301 billion (US$ 25.1 billion) budget for the current fiscal year to provide support to crippled economic sectors and contain the inflationary crisis that has affected the whole West African region for months now. Support from public authorities has been a key factor in the good growth figures seen in recent years. CERO was, indeed, relatively protected from shock s to global commodity prices thanks to the strong credibility ( in fiscal policy terms) of its government enjoys . The country’sprimacy in production of cocoa, its lar geuntapped mining resources, and a relatively

protected from shock s to global commodity prices thanks to the strong credibility ( in fiscal policy terms) of its government enjoys . The country’sprimacy in production of cocoa, its lar geuntapped mining resources, and a relatively diversified industrial base by regional standards are all factors explaining firm confidence from international investors and donors, which ultimately enabled the measures put in place by the authorities . Above all, investment posted an increase of almost 20%, more than half of which came from the continuation of large public infrastructure projects, all soundly supported by a clear roadmap believed to ensure large-scale investment also in the coming years. Financial sector CERO is a member of the West African Monetary Union ( Union Monétaire Ouest Africaine ) which provides for centralized foreign currency reserves, a single currency issued by a common central bank, the Central Bank of West African States ( Banque Centrale des États de l’Aique de l’Ouest-BCEAO), a common interest rate structure, free transfer of funds within the Union, and common banking legislation. The banking sector in CERO is highly competitive and IDAVILLE’simportance as a financial centre in West Africa is growing. Besides the BCEAO, there are 30 banks operating in CERO, many with branches throughout the country . However, with an economy strongly based on the informal sector, approximately 80 % of the population is reported to not be using banking facilities, having therefore no access to these formal financing methods. based on the informal sector, approximately 80 % of the population is reported to not be using banking facilities, having therefore no access to these formal financing methods. Trade CERO provides vital supply lines to the Sahel and wider Francophone region. Its geographic position makes the country a gateway and central hub into the West African regional market. Despite a largely perceived European domination of the market, CERO’sdependence upon international trade for its well-being goes well beyond a preferential relation with FRANCE, reflecting some change of its geographic pattern o ftrade, as demonstrated by some opening to other international players like DUSHMAN. CERO is part of the trade agreements between the African Union (AU) and the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA), and a member of regional and sub-regional economic unions such as the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) and the West African Economic and Mon etary Union (WAEMU). The country is also member of the World Trade Organization (WTO) since 1963 . Enduring socio-economic vulnerabilities Political instability The country’sstrong economic performance has not been matched by political progress in overcoming the divides that have driven CERO into civil war twice in the last twenty years. Corruption High levels of corruption-both petty and bureaucratic corruption – in virtually every office of the public administration, especially in judicial proceedings, contract awards, and customs and tax issues; There is a long tradition of financial malpractices in the country, encouraged by the absence of auditing; The effectiveness of the government’santi-corruption policies

the public administration, especially in judicial proceedings, contract awards, and customs and tax issues; There is a long tradition of financial malpractices in the country, encouraged by the absence of auditing; The effectiveness of the government’santi-corruption policies remains unclear, as the very institutions meant to oversee the use of public funds are understaffed and therefore ineffective in preventing abuses. Unemployment Unemployment and underemployment are major sources of discontent with national authorities in CERO, especially among the country’sgrowing youth population; Disillusioned local youth s in CERO are potentially vulnerable to recruitment by armed groups in rural areas, especially given the socioeconomic conditions of the northern regions of the country, which are far worse than in the south; Exclusion from the benefits of patronage also serve as a pretext for using violence to gain economic or political access. Social protection The government has committed itself to poverty alleviation, but social protection remains incomplete; CERO’slevel of health performance – incidence of malaria, tuberculosis, and HIV prevalence – remained similar to that of the least developed countries, despite its higher level of development. Statelessness For years, important elements of citizenship have been denied to migrant workers who have resided in CERO for decades; As a consequence, many people in CERO lack documentation proving their nationality, which prevent them from accessing essential services; Disputes over citizenship and the associated rights of the large population descending from migrants from neighbouring countries are an ongoing source of tension . Terrorism and piracy As coastal a country on the Gulf of Guinea, CERO has been faced with a security threat from terrorism affecting the Sahel in the north and maritime piracy in the south. This threat hinders economic development, especially as the port of IDAVILLE, the third largest port in Sub-Saharan Africa, handles 75% of the country’strade and accounts for over 70% of the country's GDP . Human rights Mass murder and serious violations of human rights have been committed since 1999, with perpetrators of violence not always been brought to justice . Gender Women lack adequate access to education, as shown by a lower female literacy rate (86.7% for women, 93.1% for men); The security sector regularly witnesses gender-specific rights violations and sexual violence; Inadequate enforcement of legal provisions to suppress female genital mutilation, despite its inclusion in the 2016 Constitution. Rural poverty and climate change Rural poverty is driving migration to urban areas. Subsistence agriculture and cash crop cultivation are subject to the erratic whims of nature and yield result insufficient to meet basic needs for health, shelter, education, and transportation. Physical environment CERO is located on the coast of the Gulf of Guinea in West Africa. The territory encompasses a total land area of 322, 462 km², which is slightly bigger than POLAND and a bit smaller that FINLAND. CERO borders five countries: LIBERIA (778 km) to southwest, GUINEA (816 km) to northwest, MILA (559 km) and FANBO (545 km) to north and NAGA (720 km) to the east. The coastline of CE RO stretches for approximately 515 kmalong the Gulf of Guinea in the Atlantic Ocean. The country is geographically biodiverse, with resources including diamonds, gold, iron ore, bauxite and manganese, in addition to offshore reserves of petroleum and natu ral gas. The administrative capital (since 1983) is TOUBA (225, 0 00), which is located centrally just south of Lake Kossou. However, the defacto capital is IDAVILLE (5.6 million), which is by far the country'slargest city, the main air-and seaport, and the commercial and financial centre. Most of CERO’spopulation is concentrated in the south-eastern quarter, where half the country’spopulation lives. Terrain and climate Topography and key features CERO'sterrain predominantly consists of a large plateau that gradually rises from sea level in the south to about 500 meters in the north. North

quarter, where half the country’spopulation lives. Terrain and climate Topography and key features CERO'sterrain predominantly consists of a large plateau that gradually rises from sea level in the south to about 500 meters in the north. North of the up to 60 kmwide coast, especially the southwest, is covered with dense tropical moist forest. This includes the Eastern Guinean forests and We stern Guinean lowland forests. The northern half of the country is dominated by savannah, a large plateau with rolling hills, low-lying vegetation, and scattered trees. Between the forest and savannah regions lies the Guinean forest-savannah mosaic belt, which is described as a transition zone interlacing forest, savannah, and grassland habitats. Most of the western border with Liberia and Guinea is shaped by mountain ranges, with their highest point consisting of Mount Nimba (1, 752 metres), which is situa ted in the Mount Nimba Strict Nature Reserve, where the borders of the three countries meet. The reserve park was designated a UNESCO World Heritage site back in 1982. Map 2, CERO overview map Coastline This diverse coastal landscape plays a crucial role in CERO geography, economy, and ecosystems, supporting various activities from fishing and tourism to international trade through its ports. The coastal region stretches up to 60 kminland, but seldom rises more than 30 meters above sea level, making it prone to flooding during rai nyseasons. The coastline features a strip of low, sandy islands and sandbars called the cordon littoral, hampering commercial shipping. Some parts of the

more than 30 meters above sea level, making it prone to flooding during rai nyseasons. The coastline features a strip of low, sandy islands and sandbars called the cordon littoral, hampering commercial shipping. Some parts of the coastline feature rocky shores interspersed with sandy beaches. The south-eastern region is marked by coastal inland lagoons that start at the NAGA border and stretch for about 300 kmalong the eastern half of the coa st. A series of lagoons runs parallel to the coastline, up to 7-8 kilometres wide. These lagoons are typically narrow, salty, and shallow, and interconnected by small watercourses or canals. The important port city of IDAVILLE is built around one of the lagoons. Lakes and rivers There are four major lakes in CERO, all man-made: Kossou Lake, the largest covering an area of about 1, 600 square kilometres and located in central CERO; the artificial Taabo Lake, downstream from Kossou Lake; Buyo Lake in the southwest part of the country; and Ayame Lake in the southeast near the border with NAGA. Four major rive rsystems flow southward, forming parallel drainage basins. Apart from the Cavally River, major rivers from west to east are the Sassandra, the Bandama, and the Komoe, all of which drain southward into the Gulf of Guinea. Because all are broken by numerous falls and rapids and seasonal variations in water levels, their value for long-distance transportation is minimal. Their hydroelectric potential is being tapped, however. Climate The prevailing climate in CERO is the tropical
