Climate The prevailing climate in CERO is the tropical savannah. Precipitation is the key climatic variable, especially in the southern half of the country. In the northern half, the southern savannah climate type dominates with its parching wind known as the Harmattan which blows form north and northeast towards south/southwest from December to February. Map 3 – CERO climate zones The dry season lasts from about November to March. A single rainy season from April to October produces annual precipitation totals ranging from around 1, 100 mm in the northeast and centre to approximately 1, 500 mm in the northwest. The northern region is drier than the rest of the cou ntry and, because of the elevation, somewhat cooler. In the southern half of the country two rainy seasons occur: rain falls larg ely from May through July and to a lesser extent in October and November on the coastal fringe. IDAVILLE receives approximately 2, 000 mm of precipitation annually, although considerable variations are experienced at different places along the coast. Temperatures range from around the low 20s C to the mid-30s C. In the forest zones and in the southern part of the savannah region the rainy seasons are less pronounced. Temperatures vary between mid-10s and upper 30s C, and the relative humidity is often high . On the mountains farther

forest zones and in the southern part of the savannah region the rainy seasons are less pronounced. Temperatures vary between mid-10s and upper 30s C, and the relative humidity is often high . On the mountains farther west there is no dry season, and precipitation amounts to about 2, 000 mm. The main climatic conditions affecting military operations are heavy rainfall, high temperatures, high levels of humidity, an d the occurrence of sandstorms . Climate change As a country located in the Sahel region, CERO experiences some of the most dramatic climatic variations in the world, rangin g from severe flooding to extreme drought. The country has already experienced a temperature rise of 0.5°Cover the last five decades, with further warming expected in coming years. Climate change affects the population in various ways and exacerbates social discontent, adding to root causes such as famine and con flicts. Moreover, excess rain and extreme droug htassoc iated with climate change affect the natural distribution of crops and reduce agricultural production, making it hard to meet the population’sdemand. These factors, which all negatively affect the socio-economic environment of the country, are exp ected to become even more severe in the future. Environmental issues Air pollution has negative impacts on public health in CERO. Main pollution sources are burning of wood and biomass for cooki ng, burning of waste and crop residues, as well as industrial and vehicle emissions (exacerbated by poor quality fuels and an aging vehicle fleet). Most of the population is

are burning of wood and biomass for cooki ng, burning of waste and crop residues, as well as industrial and vehicle emissions (exacerbated by poor quality fuels and an aging vehicle fleet). Most of the population is exposed to air pollution levels exceeding World Health Organization (WHO) guidelines, as proved by the spread of respiratory diseases like asthma and pneumonia. The government has made some commitments, such as jo ining the Coalition for Climate and Clean Air, but significant challenges remain to protecting public health from air polluti on. Water pollution is another environmental issue associated with the spread of diseases like diarrhoea and cholera affecting fishing, tourism, and property values5. 5 The Ebrie Lagoon, West Africa'slargest lagoon system, is seriousl ypolluted with heavy metals and organic matter. Critical National Infrastructure (CNI)6 Critical National Infrastructure (CNI) plays a key role in the social and economic development of CERO; however, a significan tpart of the country’sinfrastructure is ageing and some is in need of extensive maintenance. Upgrades of power generation, rehabilitation of roads and railways, improvement s of sanitation systems, of ICT infrastructure and expansion of irrigation are the most urgent areas for investment efforts to supp ort economic growth and welfare for the population. Road network: CERO has a broad-reaching national road backbone that serves the country'spopulation, accommodating both domestic transportation and cross-border regional transit traffic. that serves the country'spopulation, accommodating both domestic transportation and cross-border regional transit traffic. Communication network: According to the World Bank, the country has a national ICT backbone and a competitive mobile market with high penetration rates, ensuring a national fibre network that connects local communities internally, and also provides connectivity to neighbouring countries and global internet. Ports: The Port of IDAVILLE, CERO’smain port, has the potential to become a regional maritime hub. Energy infrastructure: The electricity sector is considered critical for production, transport, and distribution of electricity, but also production and distribution of petroleum products. Access has significantly improved last years, reaching now 70% of the population. Water and sanitation: In CERO, access to water is approxi mately 71%, while access to sanitation is limited at 35%, indicating an urgent need for further development and investment in this critical sector. Health infrastructure: CERO’shealth system, which includes around 1, 800 health centres, is considered as on e of the weakest in Africa. Shortage of hospitals and medical staff is a major challenge. Transport infrastructure Even if CERO has made significant investments in its transport infrastructure ( particularly in roads and urban transport ) there are still challenges to overcome in meeting the growing demands of urbanization, especially in rural areas . Road network CERO has invested significantly in developing its road transportation infrastructure, which is considered one of the best in West Africa as it plays a crucial role in regional connectivity and economic development. However, ongoing maintenance efforts are still necessary to address remai ning challenges, particularly in secondary and rural road networks. The total road network spans approximately 82, 000 km. Around 10% of roads are paved, including around 8, 000 km of national roads and 370 km of highways. The remaining 74, 000 km are unpave ddirt roads. CERO has two 4-lane motorways: a 224 kmhighway connecting IDAVILLE to TOUBA and a 30 kmhighway linking IDAVILLE to Grand-Bassam. The trunk roads connecting major regions and townships are generally in good condition. Over the last 10 years CERO has undertaken a major program of road construction and maintenance. As of now, the paved road network condition appears to be as follows: 42% in good condition, 24% in average condition, 29% in poor condition, and 5% under construction . Vehicle weight control measures have been implemented since 2015 on the main highway and at borders to prevent overloading. The Trans-West African Coastal Highway provides paved connections to neighbouring countries like NAGA, GOOT, EBEN, and IGAINER. Paved highways also connect to landlocked countries like MILA and FANBO. Railway network CERO'srailway network is limited to one main international line, which plays a crucial role in regional transportation and trade. The line is known as the IDAVILLE-OWOTSHA railway. Other larger cities on the route are Bouake (CERO), Bobo-Dioulassso (FANBO ) and Kaya (FANBO). The railw ay, which serves as a crucial link between the Sahel region and the Atlantic coast, is jointly owned by CERO and FANBO governments, and is operated by SITARAIL, a transnational railway company .9 The railway is un-electrified an duses a single-track system including numerous stations in both countries along its 1, 260 kmroute. The railway'smain role is servicing international freight traffic, including petroleum products, containers, fertilizers, grain, clinker, cement, and cot ton. including petroleum products, containers, fertilizers, grain, clinker, cement, and cot ton. Passenger service runs three times a week, with the journey taking between 36 to 48 hours. CERO is investing in upgrading its existing infrastructure and developing new urban transit solutions to meet growing transportation needs. A major renovation project announced in 2023 to overhaul and expand the existing line aims to improve speed, comfort, and capacity. Plans include relaying 853 km of track, refurbishing 31 stations, and adding new trains. IDAVILLE city is also developing a 37 km-long rapid transit network, the IDAVILLE Metro, considered the most ambitious mass transit project in sub-Saharan Africa. The project, started in January 2023, aims to address traffic congestion in IDAVILLE and is expected to carry over 500, 000 passengers daily upon completion after 5 years . The new infrastructure includes 18 stations, 24 bridges, 34 pedestrian footbridges, 1 viaduct over the lagoon, and 8 subways, including connection to the IDAVILLE Airport. Once operational in 2028-29, the IDAVILLE metro-project will provide a sustainable transit option for its growing population and boost the local economy alleviating traffic congestion. 9 Bollore, a French transportation company, holds a 67% stake in SITARAIL, while CERO and FANBO governments each have 15%. The remaining 3% is owned by the workforce. 4-CERO railway network Seaports CERO aims to be come the major hub of the West African coast, with modern and competitive port platforms supporting its national economy. CERO'ssea trade is centred on its two main ports, with IDAVILLE serving as the primary hub, and San-Pedro providing the second seaport. Both ports, especially IDAVILLE, serve as transhipment and transit points also for landlocked cou ntries like FANBO, MILA, REGIN and others. The Port of IDAVILLE handles nearly 80% of CERO'scommercial exchanges with the outside world, accounting for 70% of the country's GDP. Annually, it ha ndles about 650, 000 containers of over 22.5 million tons of cargo, and provides modern port facilities such as: Fishing terminal (600, 000 tonnes annually-9 mdraft for accommodating 15 vessels at once-1, 550 metres of wharves-7500 m2 auction hall-numerous seafood processing plant s-ice-production factory-storage capacity of 87, 000 tonnes-and a fish market). 47 hectares container terminal (five berthing facilities, advanced and automated operating systems-container scanning equipment-seven shore side gantries-two mobile cranes-storage of 40, 000 TEUs (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit). Fruit and Grain terminal ( annually processing 250, 000 tonnes of fruits and 300, 000 tonnes of grains-bagging machines for cereal, rice, flour and fertilisers-packaging capacity 5000 tonnes/ 24 hours). Oil terminal located on a floating buoy (receive maximum 250, 000-tonne ships-maximum 255 metres tankers-five piers-11 mdraught-network of pipelines connecting the terminal to the processing plants-1700 vessels a year-five storage tanks for keeping oil and petroleum). Roll On Roll Off (RORO) Terminal (two berths-inbuilt ramps, spanning 300 metres-13.5

ships-maximum 255 metres tankers-five piers-11 mdraught-network of pipelines connecting the terminal to the processing plants-1700 vessels a year-five storage tanks for keeping oil and petroleum). Roll On Roll Off (RORO) Terminal (two berths-inbuilt ramps, spanning 300 metres-13.5 mdraft-200-hectare industrial zone-9-hectare open storage area). Mineral terminal (13 mdraft of marine vessels-Vridi canal is dredged and widened to enable large vessels with a maximum draft of 16 m to enter the port). The port of San Pedro is located 350 kmsouthwest of IDAVILLE, and is the second major port in CERO. It is the world'sleadin gcocoa export port, handling ove r 1 million tons annually. A new multipurpose industrial terminal was recently inaugurated with 11 million tons/year capacity, and further expansion is expected to increase port capacity and traffic significantly. The navigation channel is 13.5 metres deep, 600 mlong and 145 mwide without draft restrictions. The main port covers 10 hectares and comprises several multipurpose wharves handling containers, breakbulk and miscellaneous cargo. Ample storage space is available in 10 warehouses, with two addition albonded warehouses covering 13, 000 m2 attached to cold storage. The port also has a specialised cereals berth and an underground pipeline network supplying palm oil to the nearby oil refinery. The container freight station has 145 reefer conn ections. The general cargo terminal has four berths for accommodating bulkers, container ships and RORO carriers, four mobile cranes, six reach stackers, nine forklifts and several conveyors. This terminal also handles timber exports, fertilisers and imported rice, and adjacent to

cargo terminal has four berths for accommodating bulkers, container ships and RORO carriers, four mobile cranes, six reach stackers, nine forklifts and several conveyors. This terminal also handles timber exports, fertilisers and imported rice, and adjacent to it is a cement berth which receives vessels laden with clinker, stones, gypsum and gravel. Apart from these two major ports, there are also small ports at Sassandra, Aboisso, and Dabou, and the Espoir Marine Terminal, 10 nautical miles off shore form Jacqueville. Inland waterways Inland waterway transportation is operated on a small scale in CERO . In IDAVILLE water buses connect neighbourhoods and suburbs across the Ebrié Lagoon, where many small informal operators use small boats called "pinasses". These also connect some southern IDAVILLE neighbourhoods via the Azagny River. Water transport is cost-effective in CERO: affordable fares, with cheap rides for the population, help alleviate road traffic congestion in IDAVILLE . However, despite a dense network of rivers and lagoons, CERO has focused more on expanding coastal transport and road, rather than developing its inland water transport system. Health Infrastructure CERO' shealth system is one of the weakest in Africa, with personnel and services negatively affected by the socio-political disturbances occurring during the last 20 years. With no more than 1, 800 medical facilities, CERO witnesses a severe shortage of healthcare workforce and medical professionals (one medical doctor per 10, 000 inhabitants, and around 0.016 physicians per 1, 000 people ). Medical facilities often lack necessary medical technology, and the standard of

witnesses a severe shortage of healthcare workforce and medical professionals (one medical doctor per 10, 000 inhabitants, and around 0.016 physicians per 1, 000 people ). Medical facilities often lack necessary medical technology, and the standard of care is below th at of Western medical centres. For certain speci fic health problems, it may be necessary to seek treatment abroad due to the lack of advanced medical technology locally. Hence, there is a significant disparity between urban and rural healthcare infrastruct ure. The social protection system in CERO does not offer comprehensive health insurance, covering only work accidents and illnesses. Care in public health facilities incurs a cost, while admission to private facilities is subject to prior insurance payme nts. Organizations like Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) are active in the country, providing support in mental healthcare, epilepsy treatment, and malaria screening. The government has been working on improving the health system and infrastructure, and has established a National Development Plan which has led to some improvements. However, significant chal lenges remain, primarily a shortage of healthcare professionals, in adequate facilities (especially in rural areas), and limited access to specialized care. Energy, Mining, Water Supply and Communications Energy situation CERO is working towards becoming a West African electricity hub and is investing in new power genera tion projects, including thermal10, solar, and hydropower plants. The country’senergy system is evolving, with efforts to increase electricity access, boost renewable energy capacity, and improve energy efficiency while maintaining its position as a region alelectricity

power genera tion projects, including thermal10, solar, and hydropower plants. The country’senergy system is evolving, with efforts to increase electricity access, boost renewable energy capacity, and improve energy efficiency while maintaining its position as a region alelectricity exporter. 10 The country relies heavily on thermal power plants using natural gas as the main fuel source. Electrification CERO has four primary energy sources: hydropower, oil, natural gas, and biomass. The energy mix is dominated by biomass, whic haccounts for about 56% of total energy consumption. Natural gas also plays a significant role in electricity generation: around 80% of it is produced by Foxtrot11, while CNR12 accounts for around 90% of crude oil output. CERO has a total installed electricity capacity of 2, 548 MW, divided between thermal power plants (65% - 1, 669 MW) and hydroelectric plants (35% - 879 MW). Planned solar power plants will have a significant impact on CERO energy independence in several ways: Increased domestic generation capacity: CERO is rapidly expanding its solar power capacity, and aims to reach 400 MW by 2030. Solar power, which is expected to account for 9% of the energy mix within five years, will help diversify CERO'selectricity mix, ultimately reduci ngreliance on imported fuels for electricity production. A more diverse energy portfolio enhances energy independence, as it reduces price volatility and supply disruptions. Enhanced grid stability and rural electrification: Distributed solar projects can improve grid stability and facilitate rural electrification, reducing the need for costly grid expansions and enhancing energy access

independence, as it reduces price volatility and supply disruptions. Enhanced grid stability and rural electrification: Distributed solar projects can improve grid stability and facilitate rural electrification, reducing the need for costly grid expansions and enhancing energy access across the country. Economic benefits: Investing in domestic solar resources will create local jobs, stimulate economic growth, and reduce spending on fuel imports, all of which contribute to greater energy and economic independence. CERO aims to strengthen its position as an energy hub and increase it sability to meet growing domestic demand i ndependently. CERO currently does not have any nuclear power plants, nor are there any concrete plans to add nuclear power into the country 'senergy mix. Mining Production of gold has been increasing significantly over the past decades, making this commod ity the primary mineral resource of CERO. Gold output reached a record 51 metric tons in 2023, up from 48 tons in 2022, indicating around 5% annual production growth rate. In addition to gold, several manganese mines are in operation. Manganese production passed 2.5 million tons in 2023. The number of manganese mining permits and projects has tripled last decade from 9 to 28. Finally, the country has one nickel mine and one bauxite mine operational. Other minerals as iron ore, diamonds, cobalt, copper, and colombo-tantalite, but the irproduction is not sufficiently industrialised. 11 An offshore petroleum company, operator of one of the Joint Venture sexploiting blocks in CERO. 12 Canadian Natural Resources, one of the largest natural gas producer

Water supply CERO has abundant water resources, but faces several issues. Southern parts of the country are well-watered, while the north experiences chronic water shor tages, resulting in uneven distribution throughout. Population growth and economic development are driving upwater demand, but climate change impacts poses new distribution challenges. The drastic decline in the flows of main watersheds, with deficits var ying between 30-60% across the hydrographic network of rivers, streams and lakes is further complicated by a lack of sufficient infrastructure development . Wastewater and sewerage infrastructure is suffering from under investment (especially in rural areas ) and is even less developed compared to water supply. Sanitation services have limited coverage and are primarily focused on urban areas, particularly IDAVILLE. Communication infrastructure The telecom sector in CERO is one of the most advanced in West Africa, with a stron gfocus on mobile services and internet penetration. The government is promoting a converged services approach, allowing operators to offer both fixed-line and mobile services under universal licenses. The country has more than 11 million fixed-line internet users, 53% living in urban areas. Mobile internet remains the main mode of access to the web . Nearly 90% of banks, insurances, telecom and construction companies in CERO have become victims of cyber-attacks. Space CERO has a small and nascent space program, 13 as announced by the creation of a national space agency in 2021 . The country wants to acquire a remote sensing (RS) satellite with main purposes to detect illegal gold mining, facilitate access to drinking water, map deforestati on, and addressing national security issues. 13 The space program is still in its early stages, with no operational satellites or major space infrastructure installed. Neither is any specific timeline or budget details provided for the development of their space agency or the acquisition of th eplanned satellite. Under-water communication infrastructure CERO uses underwater communications infrastructure to be connected to the rest of the world. Political and key actor landscape Political system CERO is a republic with strong executive power vested in its President: the government consists of an executive branch, a legislative branch and a judicial branch. The Constitution highlights the special role of traditional chieftaincy, represented by the National House of Kings and Traditional Chiefs, an institution responsible for the protection of habits and customs, the promotion of the ideals of peace, devel opment and social cohesion, and the non-judicial settlement of conflicts in villages and between communities. Under specific conditions determined by law, the traditional chieftaincy can participate in the administration of the territory. No elite actor in CERO questions the legitimacy of the democratic framework per se . Nevertheless, the extent to which the behaviour of political actors is shaped by the ins titutions can be questioned, especially in light of constitutional amendments to prolong presidential tenure. While elections are regularly held, it is also unclear to what extent they truly determine the ultimate government, especially if political compet ition is severely curtailed through the disqualification of competitors. In absolute terms, democratic institutions in CERO appear fragile, lacking significant vertical and horizontal accountability, with neither Parliament nor the courts holding much influence. Executive branch Executive power is vested in the President, who is directly elected for a five-year term and, from 2020, can only be re-elected once. The President, who serves as

Parliament nor the courts holding much influence. Executive branch Executive power is vested in the President, who is directly elected for a five-year term and, from 2020, can only be re-elected once. The President, who serves as the head of state, is assisted by a vice president, who is chosen by the elected president and approved by the Parliament. The President is also assisted by a Prime Minister, who serves as the Head of Government . The President appoints the Prime Minister and, with the Prime Minister’srecommendations, the Council of Ministers. In addition, there are two other advisory bodies: the Economic, Social, Environmental and Cultural Council and the Constitutional Council. Wheth ergovernmental actors are subject to judicial and parliamentary oversight is a matter of debate. There is no effective check on the government, with the judiciary poorly equipped to balance the hegemony of the executive. Legislative branch Legislative power is vested in a bicameral Parliament, consisting of the National Assembly and the Senate. The 255 members of the National Assembly are directly elected for a five-year terms. The Senate has 99 members, of which 66 are indirectly elected by local and regional councils and 33 are appointed by the President. Senators serve a five-year term. The Parliament enacts legislation, approves taxes, monitors the government’saction and assesses publi cpolicy, with members enjoying law-making initiative through proposed bills. Judicial branch The three highest bodies of the judiciary are the Court of Cassation, which deals with criminal and civil matters, the Council of

government’saction and assesses publi cpolicy, with members enjoying law-making initiative through proposed bills. Judicial branch The three highest bodies of the judiciary are the Court of Cassation, which deals with criminal and civil matters, the Council of State, which handles administra tive disputes, and the Court of Auditors, which oversees matters pertaining to public finances and accounts. There are also subordinate Courts of Appeal and Courts of First Instance. The Superior Council of Magistracy is the body that oversees matters per taining to the employment and disciplinary matters of judges. The High Court of Justice is a court of exceptional jurisdiction that judges the President, vice president, and members of the government. The Constitutional Council is concerned with electoral issues and the constitutionality of laws. The judiciary is forma lly distinct, but its decisions and doctrine have always been vulnerable to executive intervention . Local governance and administration at the subnational level For administrative purposes, CERO is divided into 2 autonomous districts (IDAVILLE and TOUBA) and 12 districts, which are further divided into régions and then départements . There are also smaller administrative units of sous-préfectures, communes, and villages, each with an elected council. Despite its nature of unitary state, central powers in CERO have been decentralised in line with the country’sadministrative partition (“ circonscription administrative ”). The prefect is the major representative of the central government, in charge of government policies implementation and coordination at district level. He/she plays a key role in maintaining the rule of law in

(“ circonscription administrative ”). The prefect is the major representative of the central government, in charge of government policies implementation and coordination at district level. He/she plays a key role in maintaining the rule of law in the district and works closely with the police and gendarmerie to ensure security and stability. The prefect is also respo nsible for overseeing the administration of justice and ensuring the protection of citizens' rights and freedoms. At the region and department level, sub-prefects are the government officials, appointed by the prefect within their respective district. At the commune level, the lowest administrative subdivision in CERO, the mayor is the elected official responsible for the administration, i mplementation of government policies, management of local services such as schools and hospitals, and the rule of law. TG's in neighbouring MILA and FANBO often conduct attacks on both civilian and military targets, approaching the CERO border.14 None of the terrorists involved in major attacks in the country where CERO nationals. Although CERO nationals are unlikely to appeal to the use of regional extremism-motivated violence, low support for the TG has been documented. The RLF has no established infrastructure in CERO and it is not considered to be within one of its provinces. However, this could change in the future as it expands its geographical outreach given the push south of t he Sahel of the RLF. of the RLF. Community protection groups Traditions of civil society in CERO have historically been weak and weakened further over the course of the protracted civil conflict from 2002 to 2011, when the climate of intimidation and the militarization of public life undermined the potential for stronger civic participation in public life. Today t he landscape of voluntary organizations is concentrated within the economic secto r, with independent planter associations active since colonial times. However, these activities generally lack a component of civic engagement, and themselves became, instead, drivers of polarization and confrontation. Throughout the last decade, the framework conditions for public activism have clearly improved, with a more vibrant civil society sector reinforcing engagement and citizen participation in matters of public interest. Many interest groups in CERO – from cocoa planters’ associations to student unions – continued to exist even throughout the decade of violent conflict between 2000 and 2010; however, they cooperate only rarely and have very little influence on political processes. The country lacks a tradition of constructive relationships between the state and intere stgroups. Additionally, there is little faith among social actors in the state’swillingness to listen to them, as showed by advocacy groups try ing in vain to convince the government about the virtues of a more inclusive style of policymaking . In terms of governance, t he dominance of the Presidency reduces the functional quality of other institutions (such as the Parliamen t) and more broadly, horizontal and vertical accountability. Local government has very

style of policymaking . In terms of governance, t he dominance of the Presidency reduces the functional quality of other institutions (such as the Parliamen t) and more broadly, horizontal and vertical accountability. Local government has very limited resources and it is not a meaningful dime nsion of political participation or vertical accountability. Such a lack of accountability is believed to have diminished the legitimacy of institutions in CERO in the eyes of significant por tions of the population. Other forms of social contracts based on kinship, custom, commerce, religious and traditional authority, as well as local notions of justice do exist and play an important role in sustaining social cohesion despite the state’slimitations. However, these contracts are fragile and prone to collap se when manipulated or contested by violent non-state actors. 14 CERO troops have faced several setbacks since 2020 as groups linked to the RLF have grown more active in MILA and FANBO. In June 2021, three CERO soldiers were killed when one of their vehicles struck an improvised explosive device (IED) and their convoy was ambushed near Tehini, a northeastern town near CERO'sborder with FANBO. 4. Levers of power Security sector and Armed Forces CERO is still regenerating its National Defence and Security Forces after more than a decade of civil conflict. The governmen t has stepped upefforts to rebuild the military to address the security situation in the north and the threat from TGs and VEOs, a sproved by the adoption of a Security sector reforms

civil conflict. The governmen t has stepped upefforts to rebuild the military to address the security situation in the north and the threat from TGs and VEOs, a sproved by the adoption of a Security sector reforms intended to boost professionalism of the personnel, its operational capabilities and the improvement of military infrastructure. CERO faces considerable corruption across its defence institutions, with no controls in operations and weak transparency in finances and procurement. Corruption fuels conflict as it diminishes the effectiveness of national institutions, undermining the legitimacy of the state, and empo wering armed groups who present themselves as the only viable alternative to corrupt governments. Moreover, the existence of the northern and southern Joint Military Zones-largely along the formed separation of the two factions during the prolonged civil war-are indicative of a dormant paralle lcommand structure and of a lack of political will to restructure the Army. A clear strategy for the reconstruction of the Armed Forces of CERO would transform these forces into a unified, professional entity capable of ensuring national security, upholding the rule of law, and supporting the country'stransition to lasting peace and stability. However, the government seems to have failed to develop a solid strategy for the Armed Forces; mutinies within some segments have been reoccurring events, temporar ily pacified by paying special bonuses. The current budget estimate is approximately 387Bn CFA (about 6 57 million current US$). ily pacified by paying special bonuses. The current budget estimate is approximately 387Bn CFA (about 6 57 million current US$). Command & Control The President, as the Supreme Commander of the Armed Forces, ensures national independence, territorial integrity, and adherence to international treaties. He directs national and defence policy and oversees the preparation and conduct of military operations, and, in major crises, ca nconvene a Restricted Defence Council to make defence-related decisions. The Minister of Defence, under the President'sauthority, executes defence policy, managing the Armed Forces and their infrastr ucture. The Minister assists the President and ensures the security of the Armed Forces. He oversees a Central Administration, the General Staff of the Armed Forces, and the Superior Command of the National Gendarmerie. The Minister also presides over the Defence Coordination Committee, which monitors the implement ation of defence measures decided by the Government and the Restricted Defence Council. The Committee includes high-ranking officials from the military and defence sectors, ensuring comprehensive oversight and coordination. The command of the Armed Forces is left with the Chief of General Staff of the Armed Forces who coordin ates actions, prepares the Armed Forces for deployment, directs intelligence efforts, participates in international negotiations, and develops budgetary directives. The Chief is sup ported by the General Staff and the Commanders of Joint Defence Zones North (JDZ-N) and South (JDZ-S), who handle mobilization, protection plans, and military preparations. The military regions north-west and northeast (see below) constitute Joint Defence Zone-North. The two other military regions

and the Commanders of Joint Defence Zones North (JDZ-N) and South (JDZ-S), who handle mobilization, protection plans, and military preparations. The military regions north-west and northeast (see below) constitute Joint Defence Zone-North. The two other military regions const itute Joint Defence Zone-South. The preparation, conduct, and coordination of civil and military territorial defence operations are carried out within a JDZ. The JDZ Commande r has authority over all military units and services in the zone, ensuring joint coordination and consistency of defence plans. The JDZ commander reports directly to the Chief of General Staff of the Armed Forces. Command and control relationships between services of the Armed Forces and the National Gendarmerie will depend on the type o foperations that are to be conducted. Policing or law and order activities alongside units of the National Gendarmerie will normally be overseen by a co mmander appointed by the Superior Commander of the National Gendarmerie, while territorial defence operations involving units of the National Gendarmerie, will usually be overseen by a commander appointed by the Chief of General Staff of the Armed Forces. Armed Forces military doctrine CERO military doctrine is purely defensive and predominantly geared towards internal stability. The country has taken the nec essary measures to integrate human rights, public freedoms anti-corruption policy and programmes into the Armed Forces doctrine. Army With 14, 000 active duty personnel, the Army is the largest service, charged with preserving the territorial integrity of CERO against any aggression. It complements the actions of the National Gendarmerie and other internal security forces for internal security operations. It participates in peacekeeping missions, as well as multinational military exercise spart of US AFRICOM’scapacity-building efforts where Special Forces exercise along other African and European partners. Size and structure The Chief of Staff of the Army ensures the general direction and management of all its components. It includes the Army Staff, the Army Inspection, the Directorate of Human Resources, the forces, a territorial organization with four military regions, functional comm and and services, and the education and training organizations. He/she designs, directs, coordinates, and controls the

Army Staff, the Army Inspection, the Directorate of Human Resources, the forces, a territorial organization with four military regions, functional comm and and services, and the education and training organizations. He/she designs, directs, coordinates, and controls the activities of the Army components and is responsible for the overall organiza tion and operational doctrine, training and operational rea diness of personnel and equipment, resource allocation and mobilization plans. The Army Headquarters is located in IDAVILLE. The area of responsibility for the Army is divided into 4 military regions that largely correspond with the administrative repartition of the country. Each commander of a military region disposes of a staff and a corps that comprises one or more administrative units. The regional commander is responsible for the administration, resource allocation, and implementation of the training directives and mobilization plan for the units under his command. Military Region South-East with its HQ in IDAVILLE covers the capital and the surrounding departments Géh-Djiboua, Lagunes, and Comoé. This region is home to an airborne battalion, an infant rybattalion, an armoured battalion, an air defence artillery battalion, and the security (light infantry) battalion S-E. Military Region South-West with its HQ in Daloa covers the departments of Bas-Sassandra, Sassandra-Marahoué, and Montagnes. It is home to one infantry battalion and the security (light infantry) battalion S-W. Air Force The 2, 000 strong Air Force of CERO ensures the surveillance and defence of the national airspace, participating in both internal and external defence tasks through observation, transport, support, communication and liaison missions. It assists the other services of the Armed Forces, the National Gendarmerie, and other internal security forces in executing their missions. In the absence of combat aircraft-therefore com bat experience-the ability of CERO Air Force pilots to respond to changing threats and opposing tactics is negligible. The area of responsibility for the Air Force is divided into two Air Regions: Air Region North with its HQ in IDAVILLE covering the territory of the Joint Defence Zone-North. Air Region South with its HQ in Bouaké covering the territory of the Joint Defence Zone-South. There are three main air bases in CERO ( IDAVILLE, TOUBA and Bouaké ). Equipment and Weapons The Air Force equipment is an eclectic mix of older and more recent equipment from MURINUS, FRANCE, USA and some other European countries. It ensures the surveillance, control, and defence of the lagoons, rivers, coastline, and the maritime spaces under national jurisdiction, and also participates in the communal surveillance of the Gulf of Guinea. The Navy contributes to inte rnal and externa ldefence through observation, transport, support, supply, and interception missions. With the rising threat of maritime crime in its waters, CERO has significantly increased its maritime patrol capability, by developing a maritime air surveillance capabi lity using Unmanned Aerial Systems such as drones to operate in pair with the maritime patrol crafts. CERO has a company-sized maritime infantry capability which disposes of one landing craft. The Navy disposes of a limited number of ocean-going (blue wate r) and coastal patrol (brown water) craft s and some recent acquired fast patrol boats. It also has numerous smaller vessels used primarily for traffic, immigration, and contraband control within the lagoon system. The majori ty of the CERO Navy equipment ha sbeen provided by FRANCE. While there is no information on coastal radar installations, CERO uses satellite-based radar technology for maritime surveillance to detect potential illegal activities, particularly in the fishing sector. The Navy of CERO participates in multinational training events organised by US AFRICOM such as Obangame Express. Alongside the Navies of the Gulf of GUINEA littoral states, African and European countries, CERO Navy exercises on combating maritime threats, improving interop erability, and capacity building to ensure a maritime safe and secure environment. Special Forces A Special Forces battalion, under the direct authority of the President, is composed of personnel from the Army, Air Force, Navy and National Gendarmerie . National Gendarmerie The almost 20, 000 strong National Gendarmerie falls under the authority of the Ministry of Defence. Operational control of th e National Gendarmerie is however shared with the Ministry of Interior and Security. The National Gendarmerie is responsible for internal security (administrative and judicial police), military duties (military police and territorial defence), and other tasks upon request. The National Gendarmerie participates in peacekeepin goperations abroad. The Gendarmerie has a vertical organization with two levels of responsibility, central and territorial. The National Gendarmerie is organised along regions located in IDAVILLE, Bouaké, Korhogo and Daloa. The regional commanders exercise operational command over all National Gendarmerie units within their jurisdiction. Each region encompasses one or

responsibility, central and territorial. The National Gendarmerie is organised along regions located in IDAVILLE, Bouaké, Korhogo and Daloa. The regional commanders exercise operational command over all National Gendarmerie units within their jurisdiction. Each region encompasses one or more legions of mobile gendarmerie and territorial gendarmerie. There are several specialized formations such as an armoured group, an intervention unit, a high-profile personalities ’ protection group, as well as a security group for the airport and the two harbours. Presidential Guard The Presidential Guard-also often referred to as the Republican Guard-is a 2, 500 strong elite formation under the command of a 3-star general. Though an integral part of the Armed Forces, the Presidential Guard falls under the direct authority of the Military Cabinet of the Pre sident. The Presidential Guard is responsible for security and honour duties for the high authorities of the State and its institutions. It is the only armed force authorized in the national palaces (Presidential Palace in IDAVILLE, Senate and National Assembly in TOUBA, Palace of Justice, Prime Minister's Office, and the main ministries). It is composed of two groups located in IDAVILLE (Group No. 1) and TOUBA (Group No. 2). National Police The National Police, with an estimated strength between 5, 000 and 7, 000, is subject to the authority of the Mini ster of the Interior and Security. It collaborates with municipal police and the National Gendarmerie in fighting major economic and financial delinquency, protecting people and pro perty, and maintaining public order. The organisation of the National Polic efollows a more centralized structure, allowing for a unified command and control system; however, there are regional and local branches to handle day-to-day policing and address specific local issues. The National Police has a number of special services such as a Directorate of Territorial Surveillance handling intelligence gathering, counter-terrorism efforts, and monitoring activities that could threaten national security, a Criminal Investigation Department, and Special Forces and Intervention Units such as the Rapid Intervention Brigade and the Special Anti-Terrorist Unit. Forest Guards The Forest Guards is a paramilitary organization under the Minister of Environment, Water and Sanitation . Their structure includes regional and local offices that mana gespecific protected areas and forest reserves, and natural resources. They work to combat illegal logging, poaching, and ot her environmental crimes. Within the context of the Security Sector Reform and the DDR process, former combatants have offered the opportunity to reintegrate the Forest Guards as part of the reintegration process. Private Security Companies (PSC) The private security sector in CERO has become a major industry with a variety of actors in different roles. The number of private security companies exploded during and after the conflict, many operating illegally. This proliferation was partly due to the population’slack of confid ence in the National Defence

a variety of actors in different roles. The number of private security companies exploded during and after the conflict, many operating illegally. This proliferation was partly due to the population’slack of confid ence in the National Defence and Security Forces, as well as their lack of efficiency. Tasks vary across PSCs and even across personnel: some PSCs offer personal protection and security services, while others are directly involved in conflicts and undertake various functions such as surveillance, transportation, or even, in some cases, direct participation in hosti lities. International military relations Peacekeeping missions CERO has actively contributed to UN, AU and ECOWAS peacekeeping missions. Contingents from the CERO National Defence and Secu rity Forces are currently participating in peacekeeping missions in in CENTRAL AFRICAN REPUBLIC, DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC OF CONGO, HAITI, MILA and Western Sahara. However, CERO unilaterally withdrew its peacekeepers from MINUSMI (MILA) following the arrest of 49 of its soldiers. Defence cooperation agreements International military cooperation plays a vital role in the overall policy objectives of CERO to increase the operational capabilities of its National Defence and Security Forces. The country enjoys close defence ties with FRANCE, which keeps a training mission in the country and helped build a training facility. The armed-forces school at Zambakro runs courses for personnel from CERO as well as form the sub-Saharan West-African region. With French assistance, CERO opened the International Academy for the Fight against Terrorism in IDAVILLE to help develop counter-terrorist capability for CERO and the sub-Saharan West-African region. The following countries have been accredited and have a military attaché: DUSHMAN, FANBO, FRANCE, GERMANY, MILA, MOROCCO, NAGA, SENEGAL, SOUTH AFRICA, USA, and UNITED KINGDOM. The AU and ECOWAS have a military representation in country. Regional security organizations ECOWAS: ECOWAS member states agreed to coordinate efforts through national policies in their ambition to create stability across Western Africa. This entails coordination and pooling of counter-terrorist efforts, including tackling the financing of violent extremism, and promoting intercommunal dialogue. The LITTE RAE15 initiative: The LITTERAE initiative is a coordinating forum between EBEN, FANBO, NAGA, CERO and GOOT (with MILA and REGIN as observers) to stop violent extremism, especially in the border areas. The initiative has three pillars: information and intel ligence capacity sharing, common training, and joint military operations. Intelligence and security services The intelligence and security services of CERO experienced two key phases until the reform process of 2013. From 1960 until 1993 services were used for personal political gains, but after a period of instability which lasted until 2011, new intelligence services were created to address trust issues with existing services. As part of the broader Security Sector Reform process, politi cal authorities recogni sed the need for urgent and bold actions to address effectively political destabilization and an incremental terrorism. CERO is part of regional security and intelligence sharing platforms. The Nouakchott Process16, initiated by the AU in 2013, aims to enhance security cooperation and intelligence sharing among Sahel-Saharan countries. It is part of the African Peace and Security Architecture (APSA) and focuses on political, security, and development aspects. The security component emphasi ses coordination of efforts against conflicts, terrorism, and organized crime. 15 The capital of NAGA. 16: The Nouakchott Process includes eleven countries: ALGERIA, FANBO, CERO, GUINEA, YABIL, MILA, MAURITANIA, REGIN, IGAINER, SENEGAL, and CHAD. The Committee of Intelligence and Security Services of Africa (CISSA) is a body established by the AU to facilitate cooperation and coordination among African intelligence and security services. It aims to enhance the collective security of African countries through the sharing of intelligence, fostering mutual support, and addressing common security challenges. Presidential Office The National Security Council of CERO operates under the Presidency. It is responsible for continuously advising the Head of State on security matters, overseeing national intelligence, and directing intelligence activities. It has a dual role: it monitors and improves the functioning of intelligence services and acts as a coordinating and action body. The Direction of External Surveillance (DSE) monitors foreign activities that may affect domestic events, complementing the internal intelligence system. Ministry of the Interior and Security Under the Ministry of the Interior and Security, the Direction of General I ntelligence and the Direction of Territorial Surveillance play key roles. The first focuses on gathering, centralizing, and utilizing intelligence to inform the Government and conducting administrative investigations. Ministry of Finance and National Budget The National Financial Information Processing Unit operates under the Ministry to protect the national economy and to fight against illicit financial activities, money laundering, and terrorist financing. The Customs Investigations Directorate deals with customs administration, and is focused on investigation, intelligence gathering, and international cooperation in maintaining the integrity of CERO’sborders and trade activities. Information CERO'smedia landscape is among the most politicised and polarised in West Africa, with long-running political divisions continuing to be reflected in its media landscape .17 The media were accused of inflaming violence following a coup in 1999 and during civil wars in 2002-2007 and 2010-2011; in 2020 many journalists were detained while covering protests and violence during and after the presidential election in October of that year. CERO was among the last countries in West Africa to liberalise it sbroadca stmedia. The advent of the first privately owned TV channels in 2019 ended a more than 50-year monopoly of Radiodiffusion Télévision CERO (RTC ): the country now has three private TV channels, all owned by supporters of the ruling party and all relying on advertising for their survival . Most of the main commercial TV channels have owners with links to the ruling coalition and online news sources are becoming increasingly popular. The opposition has limited access to official outlets, eve nduring election campaigns when it is entitled by law to "equitable media access". The print and online media are regulated by the National Press Authority, while radio and television are regulated by the Hig h Authority for Audiovisual Communication. In a highly polarised media landscape, investigative journalism is a minor presence next to “opinion journalism”. BBC World Service in French is available on FM, including

television are regulated by the Hig h Authority for Audiovisual Communication. In a highly polarised media landscape, investigative journalism is a minor presence next to “opinion journalism”. BBC World Service in French is available on FM, including in IDAVILLE (94.3), as well as Radio France Internationale and Voice of America. Press freedom is still closely tie d to the political environment, with RTC, the state-owned broadcaster, practically acting as the public relations branch of the government and the President . Under CERO’slaw, press offences are not punishable by imprisonment but it is still a crime to insult the President. The Constitution guarantees freedom of expression, and the 2017 press law protects journalists’ sources. However, investigative reporters are often the target of bribery, intimidation, and arrests, and their offices may be attacked. In general, all journalists face security problems in connection with their work, from both political party activists and law enforcement. In summary, while CERO'smedia landscape is heavily influenced by government control and regul ation, it also showcases a diverse array of print and broadcast outlets alongside a burgeoning digital presence that reflects changing consumer behaviours. The traditional and community-based forms of communication in CERO reflect a rich tapestry of cultur alpractices that emphasize oral traditions, music, dance, art, and communal interactions. These methods not only serve functional purposes but also play a crucial role in preserving cultural identity and fostering community cohesion amidst the country's diverse ethnic landscape. methods not only serve functional purposes but also play a crucial role in preserving cultural identity and fostering community cohesion amidst the country's diverse ethnic landscape. Oral storytelling is a vital means of communication, where elders share legends that educate younger generations about their herit age and values. Disinformation has become a significant issue in CERO, particularly in the context of political events. The rise of misinform ation is intertwined with the country'scomplex political landscape, especially during election periods when it creates an environm ent of fear and confusion among voters, significantly impacting electoral participation. Misinformation campaigns often exploit ethnic tensions, further polarizing society. MURINUS information operations in CERO are more limited than MILA and FANBO, but on the rise and centre on the dissemination of misleading narratives that favour MURINUS’sinterests or undermine their adversaries common to many countries in the region. Several MURINUS-backed disinformation campaigns were identified last year, many of tho sebelieved to come from the “Murosphere” network which claims no direct link to MURINUS. During the last Presidential elections, a network of inauthentic content was spread through fake accounts posing as locals and independent news organisations. 17 Most of the country’sapproximately 100 newspapers and news sites have a strong political bias, which is signalled on newssta nds by the colour of the publications: green for those that support the ruling party, and blue for those that support the opposition. Model of governance The trajectories of political and economic transformation in CERO have taken different paths. After independence in 1960, the country’sfirst president, Antoine BACEYOKO dominated CERO political life and decision-making thanks to a broadly liberal economic system actively integrated into the world market, but essentially combined with an authoritarian mode of political regulation. Such style of leadership inhibited the development of a clear notion of citizenship and social political inclusion, as BACEYOKO exploited ethnicity to maintain power. Citizenship rights were one of the root causes of the prolonged civil conflict .18 For many years, important elements of citizenship have been den ied to both the “northerners” and migrant workers who have resided in CERO for decades. Politician’sfuelled nationalistic narratives, promoting the belief that southerners were the true people of CERO, while xenophobic sentiments and discriminatory action stargeted northerners, regardless of their CERO nationality. In other words, the debate over nationalism started from the clash between two different models of citizenship: on one side, there was a narrow and exclusionary notion based on descent, the righ ts of autochthonous people of CERO; on the other side, there was a more tolerant and inclusionary notion of citizenship, essentially conceived to grant “foreigners ” full rights as CERO nationals .

based on descent, the righ ts of autochthonous people of CERO; on the other side, there was a more tolerant and inclusionary notion of citizenship, essentially conceived to grant “foreigners ” full rights as CERO nationals . For years, elites in IDAVILLE have been so eager to drive toward a more prosperous future that they have shown little interest in a genuine social reckoning with this notion of identity . All of the old triggers for conflict-frustration around presidentia lsuccession, questions of nationality, toxic regional divides, and agitation for generational change-persist in the current political climate. Layered atop the fact that CERO’sgrowth has not been particularly inclusive, these flashpoints can be used to manipulate and mobilize struggling citizens. Diplomacy Worldwide, CERO diplomacy is committed to the promotion of peaceful relations with all countries, with special emphasis on fair trade and economic connections . The government has managed its position within the international community well, prioritizing good relations with donor organizations – dominated by Western actors – while considering other international players, such as DUSHMAN, more important as trade or investment partners. This strategy has contributed toward reaching the objectives set by the government in the National Development Strategy (20 21 – 2025). A key player in both ECOWAS and the African Union, CERO needs to maintain a delicate balance between supporting multilateral efforts and managing bilateral tensions, as spill over of insecurity continues to pose challenges, especially with regards to its cooperation in regional counterterrorism efforts as 18 Closely associated

needs to maintain a delicate balance between supporting multilateral efforts and managing bilateral tensions, as spill over of insecurity continues to pose challenges, especially with regards to its cooperation in regional counterterrorism efforts as 18 Closely associated is the question of land rights. First, BACEYOKO policy that “the land belongs to those who develop it” bred resentment among local communities. A 1998 land law therefore did forbid non-CERO nationals from owning land. As such, the prospect of land-ownership has created a clear incentive to formally possess CERO nationality today in a way that it never did before. LIBERIA The diplomatic relations between CERO and LIBERIA have been characterized by cooperation and occasional challenges. In the aftermath of the LIBERIA civils wars, with significant cross-border impac tincluding an influx of refugees, both countries have been working closely to promote stability. CERO has actively collaborated with LIBERIA on border security initiatives to prevent the movement of armed groups and to curb illegal activities, such as smu ggling and trafficking. Despite occasional tensions, often over cross-border security or lingering issues of refugee repatriation, diplomatic relations remained constructive. GUINEA Diplomatic relations between CERO and GUINEA have been characterized by co ncerns over political instability in GUINEA, following the 2021 military coup. As GUINEA resisted ECOWAS’scalls for a return to constitutional governance, which CERO actively supports, bilateral relations got strained. However, both CERO and GUINEA recogn ize the importance of regional stability and have, therefore, engaged in joint efforts to monitor and secure their shared

return to constitutional governance, which CERO actively supports, bilateral relations got strained. However, both CERO and GUINEA recogn ize the importance of regional stability and have, therefore, engaged in joint efforts to monitor and secure their shared boundary. MILA Relations between CERO and MILA have been characterized by both cooperation and tension, particularly in light of the recent political developments in MILA. CERO condemned the coups in MILA and supported ECOWAS’sstance for a return to constitutional order. In response to the arres t of 49 CERO soldiers in MILA — accused by MILA of being mercenaries —CERO unilaterally withdrew its peacekeepers from MINUSMI. Despite these tensions, both countries understand the importance of addressing shared security challenges, as TGs and VEOs are making gradual inroads from the Sikasso region of MILA into northern CERO. FANBO Bilateral relati ons between the two countries have been strained at several occasions. A 2008 treaty of friendship and cooperation marked an important step towards stronger ties; however, after the 2022 coup in FANBO, relations faced renewed tensions. As CERO aligns itsel f with ECOWAS'scondemnation of unconstitutional changes in government and expressed concern over the instability in FANBO, frictions with CERO have arisen. Rising violent extremism in the Sahel further complicates bilateral relations, with increasing pres sure on land and water resources, and a growing influx of refugees from FANBO that could potentially influence future relations. CERO maintains a cooperative approach, focusing on dialogue and joint initiatives to address mutual concerns, while navigating the difficult diplomatic

and water resources, and a growing influx of refugees from FANBO that could potentially influence future relations. CERO maintains a cooperative approach, focusing on dialogue and joint initiatives to address mutual concerns, while navigating the difficult diplomatic landscape created by the political changes in FANBO. NAGA CERO and NAGA enjoy strong economic and cultural ties. Both countries actively collaborate within ECOWAS and the African Unio n to promote regional stability and economic integration. As two major cocoa producers, CERO and NAGA work closely to stabilize glo bal cocoa prices and advocate for fair pricing to benefit local farmers. Occasional issues have however arisen such as the maritime border dispute around the determination of rights to offshore oil reserves in the Gulf of GUINEA or over cross-border migra tion, as communities along the shared border frequently move for economic opportunities. Yet, these have been managed through diplomatic channels, as both countries maintain an overall positive bilateral relationship.

The Republic of EBEN Country Model (CM) Guidance and caveats: The EBEN Country Model (CM) is a Joint Warfare Centre product issued solely to support NATO exercises. No part of this product may be reproduced or used in any manner without written permission by the JWC. This document is a document that contains a combination of fictitious and generic information extracted from publicly available open sources and adapted to the NATO exercises. Although the CM draws on real world events and data, the actors and dynamics portrayed have been adapted in order to meet NATO Training Audience (TA) needs. For this reason, some actors are given a more prominent role. Furthermore, images, maps, names and other supporting information such as that used for biographies do not always describe a real character, location, or social characteristic (historical/religious/political etc.). This to ensure, inter alia, that the CM adheres to national caveats and sensitivities. Any remaining identified sensitivities should be communicated to JWC so that these can be addressed. User Guide: This setting document is the foundation for RED and GREEN ORBATs and Battle Space Objects (BSO’s) established in INTEL FS and TOPFAS, and the foundation for scenario related products developed for specific exercises. While it aims to ensure coherence with real world events and dynamics, the content has been adapted to meet exercise and scenario needs. The CM is not exhaustive and therefore allows for additional details to be obtained by the TA via the RFI process. The structure of the CM

the content has been adapted to meet exercise and scenario needs. The CM is not exhaustive and therefore allows for additional details to be obtained by the TA via the RFI process. The structure of the CM is designed to avoid repetition and to cover the domains that are most relevant to the TA. Drawing on PMESII and other frameworks it categorises the analysis by way of four chapters: the geostrategic and regional context, the socioeconomic and physical environment, the political and key actors’ landscape, and levers of power (military, information, etc.) available to these actors. 1. Geostrategic and regional context Historical timeline BEFORE 1899 1899-1960 1960-1972 1972-1990 1990-PRESENT The kingdom of EBEN began to develop around 900 CE. After the rise, the golden age (1300-1700 CE), the international trade period, the fall of one of the most significant and long-lasting kingdoms in West African history is dated to 1897, after decades of civil wars and power struggles. 1485: First Portuguese ships arrived and Portuguese involvement. 1553: First British ships arrived and involvement. 1704: First port build by France. 1889: Last independent ruler was defeated. 1899-1960: The Colonial period of EBEN. The country gradually was granted more autonomy, especially after WW-II. 1958: Became an autonomous republic. 1960: Gained full independence . This marked the end of the colonial period and the beginning of the era as a sovereign nation. A succession of military coups brought many changes of government. The last of these brought a regime professing strict Marxist-Leninist principles.

the end of the colonial period and the beginning of the era as a sovereign nation. A succession of military coups brought many changes of government. The last of these brought a regime professing strict Marxist-Leninist principles. The 1972 coup: Military officers of northern ethnic background ended the rotational presidential system. The country applied a Marxist-Leninist policy . In 1975 the name EBEN was introduced (the People's Republic of EBEN). The People's Revolutionary Party remained in complete power until 1990. 1989: The abolition of Marxism-Leninism. 1989-90: A new Constitution established with a multiparty system and democracy. EBEN was thus the first African country to successfully complete the transition from a dictatorship to a pluralistic political system. 1990: Beginning of a politically relatively stable period with democratic governance being gradually established. All major ethnic groups are represented in a power sharing agreement. 2002: First municipal elections since before the institution of Marxism-Leninism. Since the late 2010s: The majority factions have been increasingly assertive in the political arena, excluding political opposition from elections. This has at times sparked viole ntprotest across the country. Current: EBEN is currently facing increasing challenges. The first two decade sfollowing EBEN independence in 1960, saw civil rule interrupted by successive coups, military rule, and then the Marxist-Leninist period between 1972 and 1989. Since 1990 has EBEN been relatively stable, experiencing democratization and the introduction of a multiparty political system where power is shared between the four most significant ethnic groups. However, recent restricting of political opposition has

and 1989. Since 1990 has EBEN been relatively stable, experiencing democratization and the introduction of a multiparty political system where power is shared between the four most significant ethnic groups. However, recent restricting of political opposition has at times sparked violent protests. Climate changes and rapidly growing population is adding to polit ical and socio-economic challenges . Geostrategic interests and foreign policy EBEN is one of the better example s of democratic progress and stability in sub-Saharan West Africa since the 1990s . EBEN has focused on sustaining internal stability, thus it does also need to do more to address threats with a regional dimension such as that of Terror Groups (TGs) and Violent Extremist Organisations (VEOs). Along with other armed groups, TGs and VEOs continue to exploit ungoverned spaces in the region and are gradua lly penetrating southwards and expanding from the SAHEL into the southern states along the Gulf of GUINEA (GoG), and further towards western states of Senegal, Guinea and especially CERO. EBEN has had to respond to such threats without disrupting its internal political and socio-cultural equilibrium, whilst also facing the additional challenges brought about by globalization, climate change, demographics migration and an evolving international system. The strategic outlook demonstrates EBEN'scommitment to comprehensive economic and social development, with a focus on industrialization, infrastructure improvement, fiscal responsibility, and adaptation to climate change challenges. The strategic and foreign policy objectives of EBEN include: Economic Growth and Development by achieving a strong, inclusive, sustained, and durable economic growth, focusing

focus on industrialization, infrastructure improvement, fiscal responsibility, and adaptation to climate change challenges. The strategic and foreign policy objectives of EBEN include: Economic Growth and Development by achieving a strong, inclusive, sustained, and durable economic growth, focusing on economic diversification and modernization of key sectors as agriculture, transportation, infrastructure, and services (logistics, digital, tourism, and financial). Industrial Development by encouraging local processing of agriculture products (as cashew nuts), and by bolstering manufacturing output and exports. Private Sector Development by prioritizing private sector partnerships and financing for climat einvestments and overall economic development. Fiscal consolidation by pursuing update of national fiscal policy, aiming to increase tax revenues and rationalize public expenditure. Infrastructure upgrade by improving the competit iveness of the Port of Cotonou, including extension of the port basin and building a new terminal. Regional Integration and Security by leveraging its strategic position for transit trade and regional integration. The country is addressing security challenges, particularly in the north, through military expansion and international partnerships. Strengthen diplomatic relations to enhance regional and international cooperation and to address regional cross-border security issues and regional economic collaboration and integration. Enhance Climate Change Adaptation by adapting agricultural practices, restoring and protecting forests, investing in water resources, and strengthening resilience to urban flooding. (see map 1, for the most significant ) Religion 52% Christians, 25%. Muslims, 18% traditional indigenous religions, and 5% with no religious affiliation . The secular constitution provides for freedom of religious thought, expression, and practice. Family and gender EBEN is a predominantly patriarchal society. While EBEN is a signatory to the Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women (CEDAW), women are prevented from reaching their full potential because of discriminatory social norms, incentives and legal institutions. In most ethnic groups, however, mothers and sisters have influence in family life. Education The literacy rate is around 38.4% (males 49.9%, females: 27.3%). have influence in family life. Education The literacy rate is around 38.4% (males 49.9%, females: 27.3%). EBEN has achieved near universal primary education, while around 54% of children are enrolled in secondary education. Tertiary level enrolment is around 12% of the 18-25-year age cohort. State primary and secondary level education is free. Culture and arts In line with its ethnic diversity and century-old traditio ns, EBEN has wide-ranging cultures and traditions. These are however predominantly clustered in two distinct regions, the largely Muslim north and the largely animist and Christian south. French influence has been most significant among the educated segmen ts and the elite of EBEN. Ethnicity The Tehoue and the Ifu, two closely related Guinean ethnic groups, make up the largest ethnic groups in the southern part of EBEN, accounting for respectively around 36% and 11% of the population of EBEN. The Owochi and Baruba concentrated in the northern part of the country account for a further a 20% of the EBEN population. The Bayougi, comprising about 9% of the population and with a large community in neighbouring IGAINER, dominate the southeast (see map below). Economy EBEN’seconomy is reliant on agricultural production-particularly cotton-as well as formal and informal re-export and transit trade with IGAINER. The country’sgeographical position at the juncture of two major regional corridors, the IDAVILLE-Lagos and Cotonou-Gouncoro corridors, makes this West African country an important commercial and tourism hub. Officially, EBEN has a mixed economic system, however, subsistence farming and its underdeveloped industries are more akin

two major regional corridors, the IDAVILLE-Lagos and Cotonou-Gouncoro corridors, makes this West African country an important commercial and tourism hub. Officially, EBEN has a mixed economic system, however, subsistence farming and its underdeveloped industries are more akin to those of a traditional lower income economy. The country’sprimary source of revenue is the port of Cotonou, which accounts for as much as 40% of EBEN’snational budget. EBEN leverages its port as a platform to reach over 100 million consumers in neighbouring landlocked countries such as FANBO, MILA, REGIN, and CHAD, and 155 million in IGAINER. A large informal sector, estimated at over 48% of GDP, is based primarily on unregu lated trade with IGAINER. Sharing a long border with its neighbouring economic powerhouse, EBEN’seconomy is highly dependent on serving the IGAINER market, almost exclusively informally. High levels of trade protection in IGAINER have created profitable opportunities for EBEN’sinformal traders who import goods for re-export to IGAINER across a porous border. EBEN is also positioned as a transit corridor for the landlocked countries to its north . It represents the shortest and most competitive transport route to Gouncoro and is a competitive option for goods destined for OWOTSHA, northern IGAINER and parts of MILA and CHAD. The transport industry and the port of Cotonou, referred to as the ‘lungs of t he economy’, play a primary role in EBEN’seconomy. Public debt is equivalent to 54.6% of GDP, which is acceptable for a developing country, but masks EBEN’sdependency on trade taxes. In close collaboration with the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank (WB), the government of EBEN has committed to structurally transform the country’seconomy by implementing a rigid public expenditure policy. Ongoing reforms have created fiscal space by stimulating growth in domestic revenue. Despite several efforts to improve internal tax collection, the predominance of the informal sector poses a challenge to longer-term economic growth. Overall, the main economic vulnerabilities in EBEN consist of global cotton and import prices and climat echange. Furthermore, the worsening security situation in EBEN’snorthern areas could further compromise its economic outlook. EBEN has experienced sustained economic growth over the last few years, ranking for the first time as a lower middle-income econ omy. However, the country is still a long way from achieving sustainable poverty reduction . Social protection programs-essential to strengthening social inclusion, such as health and retirement insurance-still need to be implemented. Development is not geographically uniform, with urban and coastal areas benefiting significantly more than rural areas. Rising inequality has encouraged internal migration and deepened dissatisfaction among underprivileged people and unemployed young university graduates.
