has encouraged internal migration and deepened dissatisfaction among underprivileged people and unemployed young university graduates. Economic sectors Port activity and the cotton sector are the largest drivers of economic growth in EBEN. President NEEBOBY’srule is problematic given his conflict of interests as a major businessperson involved in the strategic sectors of the economy. NEEBOBY and his family have established complete control of the cotton sector, owning 51% of the Society for the Development of Cotton (SODECO) through his private companies and controlling the remaining 49% of shares held by the state as President. He als o has business interests relating to the port of Cotonou . The country’s GDP composition by sector consists of 51% services, 26% agriculture, and 23% manufacturing. The primary sector plays an important role in EBEN economy, generating 70% of employment. Since the mid-1980s, EBEN has produced yams, cassava, corn, millet, and beans to achieve self-sufficiency in staple foods. Among cash crops, the formerly predominant palm product output declined considerably in the 1980 seven though cotton output rose. The output of karite, peanuts, cacao beans, and coffee also has increased. Livestock include cattle, goats, pigs, horses, and poultry. Cotton is the primary export commodity providing nearly 40% of EBEN ’s GDP and roughly 80% of official export receipts .1 The uptrend in cotton and food production has not however translated into better yields and an increase in per capita income because it stems primarily from an increase in land area under cultivation. Steady output growth has

The uptrend in cotton and food production has not however translated into better yields and an increase in per capita income because it stems primarily from an increase in land area under cultivation. Steady output growth has been accompanied by a deforestatio nrate that is more than twice the Sub-Saharan average. Sustainability issues arise in both agriculture and forestry due to trends in resource degradation, unclear tenure rights, and water stresses exacerbated by climate change. Substantial quantities of fish are caught annually in lagoons and rivers, while coastal fishing produces a smaller, but growing, amount of catch. Most of the fish is exported to IGAINER and GOOT. A modest fishing fleet provides fish and shrimp for local subsistence and export to Europe. Formerly government-owned commercial activities are now privatized, which makes the private commercial and agricultural sectors the principal contributors to growth in the primary sector. The manufacturing sector is confined to some light industry, mainly involved in processing primary products and the cattle production of consumer goods. It has, however, been improving slightly since 2012, propelled by construction (itself driven by the rehabilitation of various roads and the construction of hotels in Cotonou). In addition, as a leading cotton producer, EBEN is moving into processing finished goods (from the cotton industry) as a route to prosperity. The country’sagricultural centre, which used to produce high volumes of agricultural commodities (with most of the crops used to be sold raw without any transformation), is now considering processing those items on its

a route to prosperity. The country’sagricultural centre, which used to produce high volumes of agricultural commodities (with most of the crops used to be sold raw without any transformation), is now considering processing those items on its own national soil, instead of selling them raw. An initiative between EBEN and DUSHMAN, known as Integrated Industrial Platforms, is unde rway to create jobs and revenue. It aims to process cotton (as well as other agricultural products) locally, with the goal o fexporting finished goods, such as T-shirts and underwear, to Europe, Asia, Africa and the US A2. Lastly, the service sector has grown quickly, stimulated by economic liberalization and fiscal reform, but also the use of modern technology such as automobiles and computers. 1 In the past few years, EBEN has become Africa’sleading cotton producer . 2 The African Union has selected the textile industry as a priority because it has been, and still is in many countries, the starting point for industrialization, therefore a sector in which job creation is encouraged. Moreover, the country’sposition on the west coast of Africa is a logistical advantage for buyers in the US, Europe and Africa, as products will not have to be shipped so far. The country is a member of various trade organizations including the World Trade Organization (WTO) and the West African Economic and Monetary Union (WAEMU). The vast majority of its trade passes through the port of Cotonou, the capacity of which the government wants to develop. While EBEN’sdomestic market remains small, the port of Cotonou allows it to re-export goods to landlocked neighbours and IGAINER (to which it also serves as secondary port, thus holding a potential to earn lucrative customs duties) reaching markets with an estimated 255 million consumers. The long border between EBEN with IGAINER, the economic powerhouse of West Africa, heavily influences the economic and social landscape of the country. Economic and social developments in IGAINE Rexert a decisive influence on formal and informal sectors of EBEN, with traders rapidly exploiting differences in trade barriers and regulatio nsbetween IGAINER and EBEN, and fluctuations in the IGAINER Naira against the West African CFA Franc. Immense transit and informal trade flow at the Dantokpa market in Cotonou generate enormous economic benefits. With hundreds of thousa nds of daily clients, 20, 000 vendors renting permanent shops and 25, 000 itinerant vendors, Dantokpa market is the largest workplace in EBEN. Beyond its economic role, Dantokpa is a social and cultural hub, and it has also become an important site of political competition between national, regional and local networks. Beyond its economic role, Dantokpa is a social and cultural hub, and it has also become an important site of political competition between national, regional and local networks. Overall, EBEN’sstrategic geographical location, relatively calm soc ial climate and political stability are conducive to export growth geared towards IGAINER, as well as regional and global markets. However, the business climate needs to improve through, inter alia, more effective trade facilitation and tra desupport institutions, better governance, and better delivery of public services. Private sector and business climate Not all major economic players in EBEN agree on the goal of building a liberal market economic system. Many political actors hesitate to support privatization and a substantial reform of the civil service, adhering instead to the idea that crucial industries should be in the hands of the state. Some entrepreneurs are not eager to create more competition, as they enjoy the benefits of near monopolie s or oligopolies . Moreover, a segment of the population relies on the income from illegal smuggling activities. Overall, private companies are formally free to operate, although facing a number of institutional diff iculties. In addition to the privatizati on of the ports and the cotton sector, several other state companies and public services have seen their management entrusted to private parties (e.g., airports, school canteens, health insurance). EBEN continues efforts to attract private investment in su pport of economic growth despite reports of high-level corruption. There is an evident conflict of interest

There is an evident conflict of interest between the President’srole and his business interests. His priva tecompanies have regained control of key sectors of the economy, including the port and market of Cotonou, and the cotton sector. Considering that cotton and the import and re-export commodities to neighbouring countries are the main drivers of the EBEN economy, these sectors yield significant socio-political power and influence. In order to implement its development strategy and avoid dependency on international donors, the government has announced plans to mobilise resources from the private sector. The World Bank judges that EBEN has improved the reliability and transparency of its land administration as well as the speed of procedures related to the transfer of property. However, EBEN’sbusiness environment remains poor and still faces structural problems, such as access to credit, corruption and unfair tax rates. Despite the progre ssmade, poor energy and transport infrastructure and a dominant informal sector constitute major challenges. EBEN major industries: Textiles, agro-processing industry, light engineering, telecommunication, cement production. EBEN major exports: gold ($849M), raw cotton ($625 M), nuts and cashews ($275M), other oily seeds ($43.6M), refined petroleum ($39.8M), exporting mostly to UNITED ARAB EMIRATES ($854M), BANGLADESH ($427M), DRENUR ($341M), and DUSHMAN ($128M). EBEN major imports: rice ($690M), cars ($343M), palm oil ($249M) and light pure woven cotton ($121M), importing mostly from DUSHMAN ($1.06B), DRENUR ($603M), USA ($317M), and FRANCE ($287M). Gross National Income explained: Because development encompasses many factors-economic, environmental, cultural, educational, and institutional-no single measure gives a complete picture. However, the total earnings of the residents of an economy, measured by its Gross National Income (GNI), is a good measure of its capacity to pr ovide for the well-being of its people. Financial sector The financial sector in EBEN has developed considerably from a very low starting point. A series of reforms undertaken in the 1990s resulted in the consolidation of the banking sector and the privat ization of all state banks. However, the sector, aligned (in principle) with international standards, suffers from institutional weaknesses. Due to a large informal economy and widespread poverty, relatively few enterprises have access to the banking sys tem. The predominance of the informal and subsistence economies has encouraged the diffusion of microfinance institutions (MFIs). However, most of

to a large informal economy and widespread poverty, relatively few enterprises have access to the banking sys tem. The predominance of the informal and subsistence economies has encouraged the diffusion of microfinance institutions (MFIs). However, most of EBEN’s MFIs are not legally authorized, and the irexistence increases the risk of instability. EBEN is a membe r of the West African Economic and Monetary Union (WAEMU), as such, the country cannot pursue an independent currency and foreign exchange rate policy. The currency is pegged to the euro, therefore heavily influenced by the European Central Bank’santi-inflation policy. Economic foreign relations and international cooperation EBEN has a liberal institutional trade framework, but commercial exchanges are hampered by a lack of modern technical equipme nt and bureaucratic barriers. The country is a signatory to all the relevant international trade conventions of the World Trade Organization and the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development. EBEN is part of the trade agreements between the European Union, African Union, Caribbean and Pacifi c (ACP) countries, and a member of regional and sub-regional economic unions such as ECOWAS and WAEMU .3 Trade liberalization in EBEN has increased due to progress toward the adoption of an Economic Partnership Agreement (EPA) with the European Union, EBEN’slargest trading partner, and the launch of the Economic Community of the West African States (ECOWAS) Common External Tariff (CET). However, given its dependence on foreign assistance as a developing country without substantial natural resources, EBEN has very little negotiation power vis-àvis international players

the Economic Community of the West African States (ECOWAS) Common External Tariff (CET). However, given its dependence on foreign assistance as a developing country without substantial natural resources, EBEN has very little negotiation power vis-àvis international players over accepting or refusing international aid. Development partners have faith in EBEN’sattempts to advance democratic governance but are increasingly worried by the prevalence of corruption and mismanagement in the state administration. This means that, although the government has nominally relative discretion on the allocation of aid, international partners are able to strongly influence the country’spolicies an dpriorities. Property rights Property rights are formally defined in EBEN but cannot be considered fully safeguarded because of shortcomings in the rule o flaw, particularly in terms of corruption. Most land in EBEN is customary land and the occupants lack formal documents to pr ove their ownership. The conventional process for land registration is too expensive and complex for most rural landholders. Moreover, customary practice excludes or limits access to land owner ship to vulnerable groups (women, migrants, pastoralists). 3 West African Economic and Monetary Union (WAEMU). Socio-economic challenges Governance: The state’sbasic administrative structures extend throughout the country’sterritory, but are centrally controlled. In rural areas, however, the performance of political and judicial decision-making is substandard, and the allocation of public goods is i nefficient. The largely donor-driven decentralization process has slowly started to improve socioeconomic conditions and strengthen the presence of basic administrative infrastructure. Still, legal standards are not enforced, and corruption remains a syste mic problem. Poverty: A significant increase in extreme poverty and undernourishment is observable in EBEN, with the majority of people not benefiting from the minimum wage due to their informal employment or dependence on subsistence agriculture; Conflic ts of interest: President NEEBOBY status as a major businessperson potentially conflicts with his commitment to advance economic transformati on. Terror Groups: The country faces is affected by a complex crisis and instability coming from the cross-border region of the Liptako Gourma with TGs influx in the northern border areas from neighbouring FANBO and REGIN. Both the government of EBEN and external donors are considering preventive measures to avoid the spread of violence in the country. Smuggling and trafficking: EBEN faces a range of criminal market challenges, including human trafficking, arms trafficking, environmental crimes, and drug trafficking. Socio-cultural and ethnic similarities among border communities, mainly the Bayougi and the Felingu é, facilitate cooperation in illicit cross-border activities, supporting markets for smuggled fuel, drugs and weapons as well as environmental crimes. For all those, EBEN primarily serves as a transit country rather than a consumption market. Human trafficki nginvolves primarily women and children from low-income families who are exploited in forced labour or sex trafficking in GABON, IGAINER, and the DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC OF THE CONGO (DRC). Foreign actors have a significant presence in the criminal markets of EBEN, with groups from both the DRC and from DUSHMAN operating in EBEN. Maritime crime: The IMB Piracy Reporting Centre4 recorded a significant increase in attempted and successful attacks in EBEN’swaters over the last years. Most attacks involved attempts at kidnapping crews in exchange for ransom. With the rise of maritime crime in the Gulf of GUINEA, the region is the location of the vast majority of reported kidnappings at sea worldwide . Social volatility: Despite its relatively stable democrati ctradition, EBEN has experienced episodes of social volatility. High levels of youth unemployment and poverty, coupled with inadequate access to education and healthcare, contribute

reported kidnappings at sea worldwide . Social volatility: Despite its relatively stable democrati ctradition, EBEN has experienced episodes of social volatility. High levels of youth unemployment and poverty, coupled with inadequate access to education and healthcare, contribute to discontent among the population, particula rly in the remote areas. Political tensions, occasional protests, and labour strikes have arisen, reflecting public dissatisfaction. The regional division betwe en the north and the south as well as the struggle between nomadic Filengue herders and sedentary ethnic groups over access to grazing lands and water, land tenure, and crop damage and access to water have also led to localized confrontations. Moreover, the vulnerability of EBEN to external shocks, such as climate change, desertification and economic fluctuations in neighbouring IGAINER bring further social volatility risks. 4: International Maritime Bureau Electricity: Only 29% of EBEN’shouseholds have access to electricity. This low rate, which is below Sub-Saharan Africa’saverage electrification rate of 35%, is compounded by deficiencies in qual ity of service. Water and sanitation: Access to improved sanitation facilities remains low, particularly in rural areas. Another major issue related to water and sanitation is flooding, particularly in Cotonou and other coastal cities. Transport infrastru cture: Infrastructure design and investments in EBEN need to be considered with increased awareness of future climate change challen ges. Transport infrastructure and related services are key to EBEN’sgoals of improved trade and competitiveness, especially on its transit corridors to the hinterland and neighbouring countries. Refugees and Internal Displaced Persons (IDP): According to the UNHCR and World Bank data, EBEN has around 2, 700 persons with refugee status and almost 500 asylum seekers. Most refuge escome from the CENTRAL AFRICAN REPUBLIC (around 75%) . The rest comes from CAMEROON, CERO, FANBO and MILA. There are an estimated 6, 900 IDPs in EBEN who have been forced to move by violence, conflict, or climate change impacts. Migration: Population movement sacross the region occur due to conflict and local instability, and in search of better living standards. Internal migration. Areas with over 75% informal non-agricultural job opportunities increase the risk of migration . Due to the high population density in the coastal municipalities, parts of the population migrate from the cities and rural coastal departments to the less densely populated Northern provinces to work in the agricultur alsector . Inward migration: Since the mid-1990s EBEN has increasingly become a destination country for labour migration, mostly from ECOWAS countries, particularly CERO, GOOT, IGAINER, NAGA and REGIN. Outward migration: Economic factors such as poverty, unemployment, increased living cost and small purchasing power drive outward migration. Further drivers include the non-recognition of merit, administrative blockages, and lack of enforcement of legislation. An estimated 4 million of EBEN nationals live abroad. Physical environment General overview EBEN, is one of the smaller countries in West Africa with a total land area of 114, 763 km², which is about the size of Bulgaria and Ohio State (USA), slightly larger than Iceland and Hungary, and smaller that Greece and Pennsylvania State . Its southern bor der is a 121 kmlong coastline along the Gulf of GUINEA (GoG) on the Atlantic Ocean. EBEN borders four countries: GOOT (651 km) to its west, FANBO (386 km) to the northwest, REGIN (277 km) in the northeast and IGAINER (773 km) to the east. The country’scapital is KOANDA, but Cotonou is EBEN’slargest city . Cotonou is also the main seaport, and EBEN’sadministrative and economic centre . KOANDA is located on a coastal lagoon at the extreme south-eastern part of the country, a few kilometres offshore from the GoG and next to the border with IGAINER. Cotonou is the defacto port city of KOANDA and controls the naval entry to KOANDA from the GoG via Lake Nokoue. The capital city has approximately 300, 000 inhabitants . The population of Cotonou is officially ca 700, 000, however over 2 million people live in the larger urban area. The 3rd

The population of Cotonou is officially ca 700, 000, however over 2 million people live in the larger urban area. The 3rd largest city, Parakou (275, 000), is located inland halfway towards REGIN. The majority of EBEN'spopulation resides in southern coastal cities along the Gulf of Guinea. Map 2-General overview of EBEN Terrain and climate The country includes a hilly region in the northwest. There are plains in the east and north and a marshy region in the south, towards the coastline. This varied topography contributes to EBEN 'sdiverse ecosystems and climatic zones, ranging from the humid coastal areas to the drier northern regions bordering the Sahel. Topography and key features The distance from the northern border to the Atlantic Ocean in the south is about 650 km. Although the coastline measures 121 km, the country spans approximately 325 km at its widest point. Its diverse topography spans several distinct geographical regions; the coastal region, the southern pla teaus, the central region, the At acora Mountains and the northern plains. From south, the low, flat, and sandy Coastal Area, backed by tidal marshes and lagoons, is composed of a long sandbar on which grow clumps of coconut palms. The lagoons are narrower in the western part, where many have transformed into marshes because of silting. In the west, the Grand-Popo Lagoon extends into neighbouring GOOT, while in the east, the KOANDA Lagoon provides a natural

The lagoons are narrower in the western part, where many have transformed into marshes because of silting. In the west, the Grand-Popo Lagoon extends into neighbouring GOOT, while in the east, the KOANDA Lagoon provides a natural waterway to the port of Lagos in IGAINER. Moving further inland from the coast, you encounter the Southern Plateaus, before the Central A rea. The plateaus elevation ranges from 20 to 200 meters, split by rivers and partly covered by the Guinean forest-savannah mosaic ecoregion, while the central region consist of flatlands dotted with rocky hills, and altitudes rarely exceeding 400 meters. While the country is mostly flat, there are some hills and low mountai nstowards the north and west. T he highest point is Mont Sokbaro at 675 meters located in the Atacora Mountains . The Northern Plain consists of plains that slope down to the Niger River valley to the far northeast where the river forms most of the border towa rds REGIN . Coastline EBEN’scoast is 121 kmlong along the GoG. Sandbanks create difficult access to a coast with no natural harbours, river mouth s, or islands. The low-lying, sandy, coastal plain, which has a highest elevation of 10m is, at most, 10 kmwide. The coast is marshy and dotted with lakes and lagoons connected to the ocean. The only practicable access for ships is Cotonou’sharbour, or the capital KOANDA, via Lake Nokoue and the Yewa River. Only at Grand-Popo and at Cotonou do the lagoons have navigable outlets to

lagoons connected to the ocean. The only practicable access for ships is Cotonou’sharbour, or the capital KOANDA, via Lake Nokoue and the Yewa River. Only at Grand-Popo and at Cotonou do the lagoons have navigable outlets to the sea. The country'smain port, the Port of Cotonou, is a crucial source of trade, transport and revenue. However, it'sworth noting that EBEN, like many West African coastal coun tries, faces challenges related to coastal erosion, which can impact its maritime resources and coastal communities. Map 3-EBEN topographic overview EBEN'smaritime claims are significant for its economy, particularly for sectors such as fishing and maritime trade, and the maritime eco nomic zone extends significantly into the sea. The Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) covers an area of 35, 648 km2. The territorial Sea of 200 nautical miles is claimed by EBEN. However, it'simportant to note that this claim is not universa lly recognized. The United States, for instance, does not recognize this 200 nmterritorial sea claim. Continental Shelf: EBEN claims a continental shelf extending to 200 nautical miles. Exclusive Fishing Zone: EBEN also claims an exclusive fishing zone of 200 nautical miles . Lakes and rivers EBEN'smost prominent lake, Lake Nokoue, in large part a lagoon, is a 20 km by11 kmlake in southern EBEN. The lake is partly fed by the Oueme River and the So River, both of which deposit sediments carried from across the region into t he lake. The city of Cotonou sits on the southern border of the lake and

partly fed by the Oueme River and the So River, both of which deposit sediments carried from across the region into t he lake. The city of Cotonou sits on the southern border of the lake and the town of Ganvie on the northern edge. It also has a canal connecting to the capital city, KOANDA. Parts of the surrounding population have been displaced by coastal and lake floodi ng. Because of its diverse wildlife, the lake provides an important source of food and economic activity. Fishing is typically artisanal, with small crew dugout canoes catching small batches of fish. With the surrounding low-lying topography, the lake is expected to double in size and flood as the current global climate change gradually affects the sea level. This development carries a risk of future salinization, which would make the freshwater lake more brackish and potentially change its ecology. Different sections of the lake currently alternate between freshwater and brackish ecosystems. EBEN’ssecond largest lake is Lake Aheme, also in the coastal region . It varies betwe en 78 and 100 square kilometres depending on the season. It is mainly fed by the Couffo River, while the 10 km-long Aho Channel connects the lake'ssouthern end to the Grand-Popo Lagoon on the Atlantic coast. The region’smost prominent river, the Niger, does not flow through EBEN but forms the main part of the border between EBEN and REGIN. EBEN has several smaller rivers. In the northwest, the Pendjari, also called Mekrou, eventually forms parts of

river, the Niger, does not flow through EBEN but forms the main part of the border between EBEN and REGIN. EBEN has several smaller rivers. In the northwest, the Pendjari, also called Mekrou, eventually forms parts of the EBEN/FANBO and EBEN/REGIN borders before it empties into the Niger River. The Couffo to the southwest, the Mono to the far west, and Oueme to the centre and south empty into the Gulf of GUINEA . Climate Short description: Tropical, hot, humid in south and semiarid in north. EBEN'sclimate is hot and humid and divide sinto two climatic zones: the north and the south. Annual rainfall in the southern coastal area averages 1, 360 mm, which is not particularly high for coastal West Africa. Coastal EBEN has two rainy and two dry seasons. The principal rainy season is from April to late July, with a shorter, less intense rainy period from late September to November. The main dry season is from December to April, with a short cooler dry season from late July to early September. Temperatures and humidity are high along the tropical coast. In Cotonou, th eaverage maximum temperature is 31 °C, while the minimum is 24 °C. Variations in temperature increase when moving north, through the savannah and plateau towards the Sahel. A dry wind from the Sahara, called the Harmattan, blows towards the south and west from December to March. Grass and vegetation turn dry with a veil of fine dust hanging over the country, causing the skies

A dry wind from the Sahara, called the Harmattan, blows towards the south and west from December to March. Grass and vegetation turn dry with a veil of fine dust hanging over the country, causing the skies to be overcast. Farmers burn brush in the fields during this season . Precipitation is the key climatic variable, with most areas exp eriencing a clear alternation between wet and dry seasons. The length of the rainy season generally decreases moving northwards, with fewer rainy days and total annual precipitation. Also a verage relative humidity tends to decrease significantly from south to north. Military implications: The main climatic conditions that impact military operations are heavy rainfall, high temperatures, high levels of humidity, and the occurrence of sandstorms. Climate change Climate change affects the population of EBEN in various ways and threatens increases social discontent, thus also providing extra fuel for TGs and VEOs to increase recruitment. A rise in temperature and rainfall is likely to compound these challenges, esp ecially in the agriculture and forestry sectors. Excess rain and extreme drought associated with climate change are generally affecting the natural distribution of crops in EBEN and reducing production, making it hard to meet the population’sdemand. Low yields, reduced livestock production, food shortages and loss of income are further consequences of these irregular climatic conditions, which are expected to worsen in the future. In the long term, the coastal area of EBEN is expected to experience a shar prise in sea levels. This will

are further consequences of these irregular climatic conditions, which are expected to worsen in the future. In the long term, the coastal area of EBEN is expected to experience a shar prise in sea levels. This will threaten half of the population living along the coast, where both incomes and population density are higher than in other parts of EBEN. Biodiversity is also at risk, with habitat loss threatening various wildlife and plant species. The health of the population is jeopardized as well, with an inc reased risk of waterborne disease outbreaks linked to climate-induced changes in water systems and flooding. Environmental issues EBEN faces several significant environmental issues that impact its ecosystems and communities. One of the most pressing conc erns is deforestation, driven by illegal logging and agricultural expansion, which threatens biodiversity. Water pollution is another critical issue, as industrial and agricu ltural activities contaminate rivers and lakes, adversely affecting water quality and aquatic life. Soil degradation is prevalent due to poor farming practices and land overuse, leading to erosion and reduced agricultural productivity. Additionally, coastal areas are experiencing erosion exacerbated by sea-level rise and human activities. The impacts of climate change are increasingl yevident in EBEN, manifesting as droughts, floods, and rising temperatures that pose risks to agriculture, water resources, and coastal stability. Biodiversity loss is a significant concern as well, with wildlife and plant species facing threats from habi tat destruction, poaching, and unsustainable resource use. Mining activities contribute to land degradation in certain regions, while waste management remains a challenge due to improp erdisposal of chemical waste and pollutants. Urban areas likely experie nce air quality issues typical of developing countries. Overexploitation of natural resources is another critical issue, particularly in fisheries due to overfishing. Despite having abundant water resources, many people in EBEN still struggle to access cle an water. Cross-border transhumance corridors Cross-border transhumance is a longstanding traditional pastoralist practice across West Africa where herders migrate seasonally with their livestock in search of pasture s and water, to avoid areas affected by livestock disease or in order to engage in trade . The selivestock movements take place along regulated transhumance corridors ( including by ECOWAS ). In recent years, conflicts between pastoralists and sedentary farmers have increased . The Pendjari, Arly and W National Park s, together form the W-Arly-Pendjari ( WAP ) complex-a protected area which stretches across the borders of EBEN, FANBO and REGIN. The WAP complex Map 5: EBEN, W, Arly and Pendjari (WAP) National Parks cover most of the unstable and fragile cross-border Liptako Gourma tribes region. The WAP

which stretches across the borders of EBEN, FANBO and REGIN. The WAP complex Map 5: EBEN, W, Arly and Pendjari (WAP) National Parks cover most of the unstable and fragile cross-border Liptako Gourma tribes region. The WAP complex is managed by the African Parks Network5. The WAP protected area in itself and the transhumance corridors through the WAP complex have become some thing of a ‘safe haven’ for TGs and VEOs as well as for any local sedentary communities and pastoralists seeking to avoid law enforcement agencies . Railway network After its independence, the railway network of EBEN has deteriorated due to neglect of the infrastructure and the rolling sto ck. EBEN railways is currently in a state of limited functionality and in need of significant modernization. The network was constructed before 1936 and consists of a single-track, narrow gauge (1, 000 mm) system totalling 578 km. The three main lines are the central line from Cotonou to Parakou (438 km), the western line from Parakou to Segbohoue via Ouidah (32 km), and the eastern line from Cotonou to KOANDA (107 km). EBEN has no international rail connections. Only the central line (Cotonou to Parakou) is currently operational, however, the track and rolling stock are in poor conditi on. The western and eastern lines have been put out of operation. Attempts to revive and expand the railway system, have met challenges. Limited investment, legal disputes over concession contracts have stalled development projects. There are several ambiti ous plans for the future of EBEN railways. Seaports EBEN'ssea transportation sector, centred on the Port of Cotonou, plays a vital role in the country'seconomy. The maritim esector serves not only EBEN, but also neighbouring landlocked nations. Over 90% of its foreign trade is conducted by sea. EBEN has two main seaports: Port of Cotonou The Port of Cotonou is EBEN'sprimary seaport and a crucial hub for regional and international trade. It is signifi cant to the economic growth, and to the development of the country, but also a vital import and export harbour and transit hub for the neighbouring landlocked

crucial hub for regional and international trade. It is signifi cant to the economic growth, and to the development of the country, but also a vital import and export harbour and transit hub for the neighbouring landlocked countries of FANBO, REGIN, MILA, CHAD and, to a lesser degree, parts of IGAINER. The seaport of Cotonou, exercise extensive trading relations with North and South America, Asia and Europe, has transformed the port into a major trading hub facilitating its growth and helping to attract foreign direct investment. The construction of a seco nddeep terminal, intended to operate as a link between Cotonou and IGAINER, is underway. A brief description of the port and its main equipment, include: Port Infrastructure: The port covers an area of approximately 400, 000 m2. It has a 1, 300-meter long commercial quay with six conventional berths and a container terminal. The port includes 5 conventional berths (150-300 meters long) and 3 container berths (500 meters long). It can handle vessels with drafts up to 15 meters at conventional berths and 10 meters at contai ner berths. Main Equipment: Main equipment includes 4 dockside cranes, 2 container gantries, 4 mobile cranes with a capacity of 140 metric tonnes, 7 reach stackers with a capacity of 45 metric tonnes, 13 ro-rotug trailers, 11 metric tonne grain elevator s, 7 forklifts with capacities ranging from 4 to 16 me tric tonnes, and 45 port tractors. Healthcare system EBEN'snational health system has a pyramid structure with three levels, the central, the intermediate and the peripheral. There is a good distribution of health facilities across the country. Almost 80 % of the population lives within 5 km of a health establishment, however, only 44% of the population actually uses these health services. Community health workers serve at the peri pheral level and are trained in malaria treatment and other high-impact interventions. There is an unequal distribution of staff across the country, and there is a shortage of specialists. Only a small part of the general state budget is allocated to the health sector, and more than half of it is financed by households. EBEN'smain health sector challenges high out-of-pocket payments, disparities in care between rural and urban areas, insufficient government spending on healthcare, and need for better quality services, especially in maternal and child health. Energy situation Like most countries in Sub-Saharan Africa, the energy sector of EBEN is largely dominated by the use of biomass-based energy sources (60%), followed by petroleum products (38%) and, to a lesser degree, electricity (2%). The country entirely depends on imports to satisfy its petroleum product dem and, while 85% of its electricity demand is provided by NAGA, CERO and IGAINER. The country is 100% dependent on petroleum imports. EBEN has a huge, untapped, renewable energy

on imports to satisfy its petroleum product dem and, while 85% of its electricity demand is provided by NAGA, CERO and IGAINER. The country is 100% dependent on petroleum imports. EBEN has a huge, untapped, renewable energy potential due to a lack of domestic or foreign capital. Inadequate hazard prevention safeguards have resulted in EBEN'senergy infrastructure being extremely vulnerable to expected future climate stresses. The main challenges in the energy sector are the non-sustainable use of biomass and low access rate to electricity in rural areas. Electricity generation is constrained due to occasionally curtailed supply from neighbouring countries. Although EBEN has develope da rural electrification strategy, the objective is likely too ambitious due to insufficient grid network and funding issues. Power system and electrification Overall access to electricity in EBEN remains limited, and around one third of the total popu lation has access to electricity, leaving the rural access rate to 15% and the urban to 60%. Approximately 1.5 million households lack power. EBEN heavily relies on electricity imports. About 85% of electricity consumption is met through imports from NAGA, CERO, and IGAINER, while the remaining 15% is provided by domestic generation. The energy mix of domestic production mainly comes from biomas s (60%), Petroleum products (38%). Of total installed power generation in EBEN is approximately 350 MW, of which 250 MW is from diesel and heavy fuel oil and the rest from hydroelectric sources (approximately 100 MW). EBEN'senergy sector faces significant challenges, including low domestic generation capacity, high dependence on imports, and limited rural electrification. The country is working towards improving its energy situation by increasing and diversifying its electric power generation capaci ty, wit ha focus on leveraging renewable energy sources, particularly solar power. Nuclear power EBEN does not have a nuclear power program, nor are plans to develop one in the near future. The country seems to be prioriti zing other forms of energy development to meet its power needs. Water supply, wastewater and sewerage About 1.8% of the total surface area of EBEN is covered by water. It is estimated that the available water resources are suff icient to meet current and anticipated future demand. The current water usage is not larger than about 1% of the available resource s. The drinking water supply and sanitation in EBEN has improved over the last years, particularly in rural areas where coverage is higher than in many other African countries. However, challenges remain. Sanitation receives less attention and wastewater treatment hardly exists. Moreover, there are significant differences in water supply coverage among localities. Around 90% the urban and 75% of the rural population have access to "improved" water. Still, around 2 million people lack access to "improved" water. 90% the urban and 75% of the rural population have access to "improved" water. Still, around 2 million people lack access to "improved" water. Heavy rains and subsequent widespread flooding continue to cause significant damage to water resources. Additionally, polluti on caused by large volumes of plastics and other debris being released into Lake Nokoue and the GoG via open drains, make wat erresources scarce. In 2021, the European Investment Bank provided a €50 million loan to help Cotonou in improving its storm-water management, specifically by upgrading storm-water drainage to capture plastic. The initiative will lessen floods near buildi ngs, benefiting people in and around Cotonou, while also reducing plastic and other pollutants in the Gulf of GUINEA. Natural resources EBEN has a variety of natural resources. It has fertile agricultural land, particularly in the southern regions and its economy relies heavily on agriculture, particularly cotton production. After cotton, the main agricultural resources are cashews, yams, cassava, corn, millet, beans, and rice. EBEN also possesses a variety o fminerals as limestone, marble, gold, iron ore, phosphates, clay, and granite. The country also exploit its forests, mainly by timber production, including mahogany, iroko, teak, and samba, as well as its salt and fresh water resources (fish and shrimp from coastal waters and rivers). EBEN has also discovered some offshore oil deposits, though production has been limited in recent years, due to underinvestment and lack of exploratio n. Finally, the country has a large renewable energy potential linked to solar and wind

has also discovered some offshore oil deposits, though production has been limited in recent years, due to underinvestment and lack of exploratio n. Finally, the country has a large renewable energy potential linked to solar and wind energy resources, though largely untapped currently. The country faces challenges in fully developing and utilizing many of its mineral and energy resources due to limited infras tructure and investment. Efforts are being made to diversify th eeconomy and better leverage these resources for economic development. Petroleum and oil EBEN currently has very little domestic oil production. The country ceased petroleum production from its Seme oilfield in 199 8 due to unprofitable operations. Histor ically, oil was first discovered in EBEN in the 1980s in offshore fields. Production peaked in 1986 at about 8, 000 barrels per day, but declined thereafter. EBEN is situated in a region known to have significant offshore oil deposits, and the country'soff shore sites are believed to have potential, attracting int erest from major oil companies. The petroleum sector of EBEN is currently underdeveloped but with potential for growth, particularly in offshore areas. The country is actively seeking to attract investment and expertise to revitalize its oil industry. The main challenges are insufficient infrastructure, limited domestic expertise, and the need for significant investment to develop resources. EBEN lacks a functional oil refinery, requiring the country to import refined oil products to meet domestic demand. EBEN is currently involved in regional cooperation on oil infrastructure projects, such as the REGIN-EBEN

investment to develop resources. EBEN lacks a functional oil refinery, requiring the country to import refined oil products to meet domestic demand. EBEN is currently involved in regional cooperation on oil infrastructure projects, such as the REGIN-EBEN Oil Pipeline, which probably will create economic opportunities for the country. Mining Mining is considered a significant sector in EBEN, with a rich history and promising future. It plays a crucial role in the count ry'seconomic development, contributing to GDP and creating employment opportunities. EBEN has a diverse mining industry, and both artisanal miners and large-scale industrial operations are involved in mineral extraction. Foreign investment plays a significant role in the sector, driving growth and development. The main extracted minerals are gold, limestone, phosphates, clay, marble, iron ore, chromite, manganese, and bauxite. Mining ha a huge economic impact in EBEN, contributing to GDP, ensuring export earnings, creating employment opportunities, and stimulating growth in other related industries. However, the mining sector in EBEN is considered largely underdeveloped compared to its potential. Main challenges are inadeq uate infrastructure, environmental concerns, need for sustainable mineral extraction practices, limited investment in some areas, and lack of skilled labour in certain aspects. Loans for Infrastructure: Since year 2000, DUSHMAN has advanced $536 millio n in loans to EBEN, primarily for building roads, communications, and water infrastructure. DUSHMAN investments and initiatives under the BRI framework have clearly contributed to EBEN'seconomic growth and infrastruc ture development, while also creating a level of economic dependence on DUSHMAN. This aligns with DUSHMAN'sbroader strategy in Africa, where it aims to secure long-term influence through economic ties and infrastructure development. In 2023, DUSH MAN and EBEN established a strategic partnership, which includes renewed commitments toward developing EBEN'sinfrastructure for healthcare and education. DUSH MAN 'sinvestments in EBEN, particularly in the Port of Cotonou and railway system, aim to create a hub for DUSHMAN business activities

a strategic partnership, which includes renewed commitments toward developing EBEN'sinfrastructure for healthcare and education. DUSH MAN 'sinvestments in EBEN, particularly in the Port of Cotonou and railway system, aim to create a hub for DUSHMAN business activities not just in EBEN, but throughout West Africa, especially targeting the large IGAINER market. DUSHMAN'sstrategy involves inte grating its economic interests deeply into EBEN'sdevelopment, making it challenging for EBEN to disengage from this relationship in the future. 3. Political and Key Actor Landscape Political system In the 1990's, The Republic of EBEN (EBEN) transitioned to a multiparty democracy with the adoption of a new constitution tha tguarantees human rights and political freedoms. The Republic of EBEN is a presidential republic with a three-tier system of gove rnment with an executive branch, a legislative branch, and a judicial branch. Traditional chieftaincy, which remains relevant, is constitutionally recognised as a guardian of habits and customs. Discussi ons regarding its legal framework are ongoing. Moreover, especially across the border with IGAINER, traditional leaders have maintained an informal system of agreements to assist each other with respect to various social and cultural issues Political power The Tehoue, the Bayougi and the Owochi alongside other ethnic groups in the north such as the Harou and Felingu é in northern EBEN, constitute the centres of social and political power in EBEN. These groups constitute the three pre-colonial ancient kingdoms of south-centre Dahomey, south-eastern Hogbonou and northern Nikki respectively. Centred on the Owochi, these northern ethnic groups remain political significant

the centres of social and political power in EBEN. These groups constitute the three pre-colonial ancient kingdoms of south-centre Dahomey, south-eastern Hogbonou and northern Nikki respectively. Centred on the Owochi, these northern ethnic groups remain political significant in their struggle for national political power. Executive branch Executive power is vested in the President as the Chief of State and the Head of the Government. He/she appoints, after an advisory opinion of the National Assembly, the members of the Government who remain answerable to the President. The President is elected by direct universal suffrage. The presidential mandate is five years, with a maximum of two consecutive presidential mandates. The President presides over the Council of Ministers. This Council deliberates and decides on general policies of the State, Government bills, ordnances and decrees. The President also signs laws passed to him/her by the National Assembly. The President, as the Supreme Commander of the Armed forces, is responsible for National Defence. He/she presides the Nationa l Council on Defence and Security. Legislative branch The primary legislative authority is exercised through a unicameral National Assembly . The National Assembly has 109 members, elected by direct universal suffrage with an election cycle of four years. Members of the National Assembly can be re-elected. Members of the National Assembly have the law-making initiative through initiating draft bills on a wide range of topics. They are also responsible for approving the national budget and overseeing the activities of the executive branch. Parliamentary overs ight is conducted through

have the law-making initiative through initiating draft bills on a wide range of topics. They are also responsible for approving the national budget and overseeing the activities of the executive branch. Parliamentary overs ight is conducted through several mechanisms of which the Parliamentary committee, examining government policies, programmes, and activities is the most relevant one. These committees have the power to summon the President and other member s of the Governme nt to answer questions and provide information. The National Assembly also provides oversight over the budget allocation proc ess, approving or rejecting the national budget proposed by the Government. The National Assembly has the power to initiate impe achment procedure against the President, which are then led by the High Court of Justice. Despite the legislative power being an important source of checks and balances over the executive branch, the National Assembly has often been ineffective in providi ng this function. Moreover, corruption remains a substantial problem, and many National Assembly members have been accused of using their position for personal gain, especially those with significant business interests in the commercially developed souther nhub of Cotonou. Judicial branch The legal system is strongly influenced by the French colonial period and combines modern written law and customary law under pinned by traditional customary rules. Despite attempts to codify particular provisions such as personal and family laws, customary law remains the main instrument for legal issues over land tenure. By virtue of the Constitution, the court system in EBEN is established

rules. Despite attempts to codify particular provisions such as personal and family laws, customary law remains the main instrument for legal issues over land tenure. By virtue of the Constitution, the court system in EBEN is established as follows. At the highest level, there is the Supreme Court and the High Court of Justice. The Supreme Court is the highest court and all decisions from the court are binding for the executive power, the legislative power, as well as for all courts of law. The High Court of Justice is a special court competent to judge the Pres ident and other members of Government for charges of high treason and/or infractions committed in the exercise of their duties. Justice at a lower level is delivered by a system of trial courts and courts of appeal. The judicial system is generally weak, ineffective or burdened by insufficient funding. Political interference is rife and includes the appointment of judges close to the executive branch for the purpose of neutralising political opposition. Corruption within the judicial apparatus is also widespread and includes instances of bribery, nepotism, and political influence, which have eroded the credibility of judicial decisions. Relevant Federal Executive bodies National Council on Defence and Security: this falls under the direction of the President. It brings together different ministe rs that are involved in defence: the Minister of National Defence, Minister of the Interior and Public Security, Minister of Foreign Affairs and Cooperation, Mini ster of Justice and Legislation, Minister of Water

brings together different ministe rs that are involved in defence: the Minister of National Defence, Minister of the Interior and Public Security, Minister of Foreign Affairs and Cooperation, Mini ster of Justice and Legislation, Minister of Water and Mines, Minister of Communication and Post, and Minister of Infrastructure and Transport. However, its role is predominantly consultative and its influence generally limited. The Economic and Social Council: this is an advisory body to the President, the council of Ministers and the National Ass embly on issues of economic, social, cultural, scientific, and technical nature. Government bills with an economic or social character are submitted to the Commis sion for advice. The High Commission of Audio-Visuals and Communications: this was established to guarantee and assure the freedom and the protection of the press and other mass communication means. Challenges While EBEN'spolitical system is structured around democratic principles, recent developments indicate significant challenges to its democratic integrity and institutional independence. Despite its initial reputation as a beacon of democracy in West Africa, EBEN faces challenges such as: Erosion of judicial independence. Restrictions o npress freedom and civil liberties. Increased political repression against opposition parties. Since 2016, there have been concerns regarding democratic backsliding under the President’sadministration. Electoral reforms introduced in 2018 have been criticized for restricting opposition participation, leading to a parliament dominated by the President'ssupporters. Governance and administration at the sub-national level Governance is organised around a hierarchical structure that is comprised of departments, communes, and local councils. EBEN counts twelve departments,

opposition participation, leading to a parliament dominated by the President'ssupporters. Governance and administration at the sub-national level Governance is organised around a hierarchical structure that is comprised of departments, communes, and local councils. EBEN counts twelve departments, each further divided into communes, which are the second-tier administrative units. The communes are subdivided into arrondissements, providing a more localized level of administration. At the lowest level, local councils represent the smallest administrative entities and are responsible for local governance and decision-making. This structure reflects a largely decentralised and devolved system, enabling more autonomy for local comm unities. Map 9-EBEN administration at subnational level Each department is headed by a prefect appointed by the central government, responsible for enforcing national laws and coord inating administrative functions within the department. Communes elect a mayor as executive head. Local councils are the bodies responsible for local planning and development of projects. However, challenges persist, including disparities in the allocation of resources and capacities across departments. Effectiv eness of local governance is often hindered by insufficient fundi ng, limited technical expertise, and political interference. Political parties and interest groups Political parties have since the 1990s presented themselves either independently or as part of often-changing political alliances. The political landscape i slargely fragmented by a large number of parties, many of which are very small . The majority of the parties in EBEN lack internal democratic rules and transparency. These are often considered to be the private property of the founder or financier and to predominantly

many of which are very small . The majority of the parties in EBEN lack internal democratic rules and transparency. These are often considered to be the private property of the founder or financier and to predominantly serve the latter or his family’sinterests. Most are anchored in one specific department and/or within one specific ethnic group or clan/tribe. A limited number of political parties (see below) have been able to tr anscend local or ethnic interests. Due to the shifting nature of political alliances between the parties, political ideologies have become increasingly fluid. T he most prominent form of political distinctio nremains the cleavage between the north and south. There is no Prime Minister in the EBEN Government. Non-state actors EBEN continues to grapple with and the threat of internal instability from non-state actors. The most prominent TGs and other VEOs have trans-national source s. Main Terrorist Groups and Violent Extremist Organisations. Resistance and Liberation Front (RLF) The RLF originated in BUFRO, where it emerged as a splinter group of the BUFRO Liberation Front (BLF). The RLF seeks to establish a new regional, hard-core anti-multicultural, anti-pluralist, anti-Western order spanning from West Africa to the Middle East, through the Sahara and the Horn of Africa. The RLF was able to, and continues to, secure strategic alliances with affiliated groups in different countries, creating a franchised operation. Felingu é Freedom Fighters (FFF or F3) The Felingu é Freedom Fighters are a guerrilla force consisting of IGA, EBE armed groups, and other

secure strategic alliances with affiliated groups in different countries, creating a franchised operation. Felingu é Freedom Fighters (FFF or F3) The Felingu é Freedom Fighters are a guerrilla force consisting of IGA, EBE armed groups, and other fighters who operate in small units of up to 25 personnel in cross-border area s, primarily concentrated on NWIGA. Their profile became more significant after 2005 when they began to attack IGADSF with increasing levels of success, gaining support from local population as they aspire d to counter all Western influence and presence in the region. Militant groups (Community protection groups) EBEN, as with other countries in the Sahel region, has a history of community-based self-defence entities. These groups are deeply rooted in their territories. During political rebellions across the Sahel region in the 1980s and 1990s, a second phase in these groups’ existence emerged. This partly stemmed from the state'sdesire to support and rely on these groups to counter rebel groups. Gradually however, the initial security-related role of these groups evolved: They have been used to protect political networks and have also sough t their own enrichment, especially in gold mining areas. The employment of these groups as part of CT and counter-insurgency efforts has however not always improved security. Allegations have been made of self-defence groups engaging in illegal militia-like activities, sometimes in collaboration with the national defence and security forces, such as kidnappings, property destruction, sexual abuse, involvement in drug trafficking and the recruitment of children . This has served

self-defence groups engaging in illegal militia-like activities, sometimes in collaboration with the national defence and security forces, such as kidnappings, property destruction, sexual abuse, involvement in drug trafficking and the recruitment of children . This has served to increase pressure on displaced populations. Moreover, in northern EBEN, the Felingué community is particularly vulnerable to violence from these groups. Violence perpetrated by self-defence groups often has a still more counterproductive effect in that it may drive targeted populations to seek protection from TGs and VEOs. 4. Levers of Power Security sector and Armed Forces General overview EBEN has over the last decade remained largely untouched by expanding extremist violence out of the Sahel . However it has increasingly found itself the target of TGs and VEOs which have been able to establish a foothold in the national parks of northern EBEN shared with REGIN, and FANBO (the Liptako Gourma region) . These groups have steadily expanded their activities and reach in both those Sahel countries . This reverberates throughout the country'sstructures and poses a risk to national governance. The shared border zone among these three countries has become a concern for the government and t he military, as it serves the expansionist intentions of TGs and VEOs into northern EBEN. In the south of EBEN, the security situation has also gradually deteriorated with increased maritime criminal activity along its coastline on the GoG . Though the Armed Forces do no thold the same values as their Western counterparts, civil-military relations in

EBEN, the security situation has also gradually deteriorated with increased maritime criminal activity along its coastline on the GoG . Though the Armed Forces do no thold the same values as their Western counterparts, civil-military relations in EBEN have evolved positively since the 1990s. Due to an active civil society, there have been few complaints of abusive behav iour by the Armed Forces, though corruption remains an issue. Ultimate authority over the security sector rests with the President as the Head of State and Head of Government. The Ministry of National Defence is responsible for the overall coordination and management of the countr y's Armed Forces, while the Ministry of Interior and Public Security oversees domestic security and law enforcement agencies. The Council on Defence and Security advises the President on matters relating to public security. However, its role is purely advi sory and its relevance is rather marginal. There is limited oversight by the legislative branch over the security sector through the Foreign Affairs, Cooperation to Dev elopment, Defence and Security Committee in the National Assembly. However, these proce edings are generally a formality despite efforts to improve democratic oversight and accountability. A Security Sector Reform (SSR) cell under the Ministry of the Interior and Public Security (MoIPS) coordinates all SSR relate dactivity in close cooperatio n with the Ministry of National Defence ( MoND ), Ministry of Justice and Legislation and administrative and institutional reforms. International military relations International military cooperation plays a vital role in the overall

dactivity in close cooperatio n with the Ministry of National Defence ( MoND ), Ministry of Justice and Legislation and administrative and institutional reforms. International military relations International military cooperation plays a vital role in the overall policy objectives of EBEN to increase the operational capabilities of its Armed Forces and to promote its diplomatic ties with other nations. Participation in and support to international or regional peacekeeping missions is considered by the EBEN Government to constitute a key means of bolstering regional and national stability. EBEN has forged a balanced set of strategic partnerships with different powers. BELGIUM, FRANCE, USA, DUSHMAN, MURINUS, and African (predominantly neighbouring) nations as well as SOUTH AFRICA are among the country’skey partners. These partnerships involve joint military exercises, intelligence sharing, and capacity-building initiatives within the fields of counte r-terrorism and maritime security. Peacekeeping missions. EBEN has actively contributed to UN, AU and ECOWAS peacekeeping missions. EBEN troops have been deployed to conflict zones in sub-Saharan African countries, such as LIBERIA, SIERRA LEONE, CENTRAL AFRICAN REPUBLIC, and RWANDA but also outside Africa in KOSOVO and EAST-TIMOR. There is currently no peace keeping mission deployed in EBEN. Defence cooperation agreements. EBEN has established and continues to expand its bi-lateral cooperation. Bi-lateral (technical) defence cooperation agreements with the following countries aim to improve the Armed Forces’ training or to support their operational capabilities: BELGIUM, FRANCE, GABON, LIBERIA, SOUTH AFRICA, MURINUS and the USA. EBEN has six military attaché posts: in FRANCE, BELGIUM, USA, UNITED NATIONS, IGAINER and

aim to improve the Armed Forces’ training or to support their operational capabilities: BELGIUM, FRANCE, GABON, LIBERIA, SOUTH AFRICA, MURINUS and the USA. EBEN has six military attaché posts: in FRANCE, BELGIUM, USA, UNITED NATIONS, IGAINER and DUSHMAN, with the intent of opening an additional one in GERMANY. The LITTERAE8 initiative . The LITTERAE initiative is a coordinating forum between EBEN, FANBO, NAGA, CERO and GOOT ( with MILA and REGIN as observers) to stop violent extremism, especially in the border areas. The initiative has three pillars: information and intelligence capacity sharing; commo ntraining; and joint military operations. Multinational Joint Task Force (MNJTF) . IGAINER initiated the MNJTF to quell trans-border armed banditry and the threat from TGs around the broader Lake Chad Basin. EBEN provides a company-sized formation although not in combat capacity. ECOWAS . ECOWAS-linked security initiatives include coordination and pooling of CT efforts, including countering the financing of violent extremism, and the promotion of intercommunal dialogue. Defence budget The current budget est imate is approximat ely 60 billion CFA (about US$1bn) . The Ministry of National Defence has announced a significant increase in the national defence budget of more than 25% for the next years to increase the Armed Forces’ operational capabilities, the efficiency of its services and to reinforce the bond between the Armed Forces and the nation. 8 The capitol of NAGA. Armed Forces structure Command & Control The Ministry of National Defence (MoND) is comprised of civilian and military components. Next to the standard directorates within each ministry in EBEN, the MoND has three special directorates. Directorate for Military Cooperation and Peace Operations . Directorate for Military Security . Direct orate for the Support to the Economic Development and other Tasks of Public Interest . Additionally, and given the increased terrorist threat, a special cell situated directly under the MoND has been established. The cell tracks terrorist related indicat or & warning analysis. It also takes preventive measures to prevent and respond to terrorist incidents. The General Staff of the armed forces of EBEN is a joint organisation with responsibility for concept development, planning, decision-making and the conduct of military operations. The General Staff falls under the command of the Chief of the General Staff, and comprises the EBEN Army, Navy, Air Force, National Guard, and the Gendarmerie. The Armed Forces, under the authority of the President, may, unde rexceptional circumstances be called as a back-up for conventional public security forces, the police and the gendarmerie. The subordination of the Armed Forces to civilian authority is guaranteed under the constitution. Armed Forces military doctrine EBEN has a selective compulsory military service (age 18 to 35 males and females who have passed the secondary school examina tion) mixed with a volunteer force. Compulsory military service lasts a minimum of 18 months for males and 12 months for females. The re-structuring of the Armed Forces and the establishment of the National Guard is ongoing and constitutes an integral part of the Security Sector Reform progra mme. The military doctrine is defensive in nature and predominantly geared towards internal stability. Army With an authorized strength of 8, 000 the Army is the largest of the three services. It has for decades been prioritized with respect to human resources and procurement. However, in view of an increase in maritime crime, there has been a slight shift toward supgrading the operational capabilities of the Navy. The primary mission of the Land Forces resides in defending the territorial integrity of EBEN. The

view of an increase in maritime crime, there has been a slight shift toward supgrading the operational capabilities of the Navy. The primary mission of the Land Forces resides in defending the territorial integrity of EBEN. The Army can however also be used for other tasks of public interest. The latter aspect allows the Army to be used to suppress insurrection and act in aid of civil authorities to restore order. To be able to accelerate the fight against TGs and VEOs, Army recruitment has been accelerated and the National Guard has been established. Size and structure The Chief of the Staff of the Army Staff (COSA) leads the EBEN Army and is responsible for the command, direction, and sustainment of t he Army. Staff elements that assist the COSA include Policy and Plans, Operations, Logistics, the Military Secretary, and the Army Headquarters Garrison in Cotonou. The Army’smain formation is one Motorised Infantry brigade, next to one Airborne and one Presidential Guard battalion. There are some Combat Support and Combat Service Support battalion-sized formation. With the rising threat of maritime crime in its waters and an expected revival of its oil sector, EBEN has increased its maritime patrol capabi lity and has developed a very small maritime air surveillance capability. The Maritime Prefect has use of the administrative police and is responsible for the implementation of emergency measures and controls the use of force at sea . The Maritime Prefect has use of the administrative police and is responsible for the implementation of emergency measures and controls the use of force at sea . His jurisdiction extend s to the shoreline of the sea, and encompasses the littoral zone, but does not encompass the administrative boundaries of the ports. Gendarmerie The almost 3, 000 strong Gendarmerie is subject to the authority of the Minister of National Defence. The Gendarmerie has authority and jurisdiction over 90% of the national territory of EBEN and 80% of its population. T he Gendarmerie has responsibilities in the domain of administrative policing, criminal investigations, and acts as the military police of the Armed Forces. Responsibilities often overlap with those of the National Police. The Gendarmerie is however predomi nantly responsible for security in rural areas and along the highways in EBEN. The National Police, meanwhile, covers urban areas in EBEN. Police and paramilitary forces The National Police is subject to the authority of the Minister of the Interior and Public Security. The National Police Directorate General is responsible for the coordination of National Police activities and functions to include: technical departments (police administration, public safety, judiciary police, general intelligence and sur veillance of the territory, and the National Police Academy), external departments (departmental/central/local police station s, special and border police units), republican security companies, crime brigade, the Research, Assistance, Interventions and Diss uasion units (RAID) and the brigade for the protection of the littoral and the fight against pollution. The National Police, predominantly active in the urban areas, has been accused of acts of violence against civilians in sever alurban centres. An incre asing number of civil society organisations have established police monitoring programmes monitoring police violence and abuses such as ar bitrary detentions. Reports suggest that the National Police is badly trained, equipped and in general prone to corrup tion. Intelligence and security services Immediately following independence, EBEN developed an intelligence and security services’ network within the state’smilitary and paramilitary organisations, i.e. the Armed Forces, the National Police, and the National Gendarmerie. The National Police collects, processes, analyses and disseminates public securi ty-related intelligence relevant to the national government decision making process. The Directorate of Territorial Surveillance (Direction dela Surveillance du Territoire (DST)) is an organisation which detects, prevents and addresses threats to the inte rnal and external security of the State. National Gendarmerie. The Gendarmerie has an organic intelligence service predominantly dedicated to delinquency and organised crime. Armed Forces. The Bureau of Intelligence and External Relations of the General Staff of the Armed Forces comprises three organisations: Directorate for Military Security (Direction de Securite Militaire (DSM)) under direct control of Minister of National Defence; Directorate for Military Intelligence (Direction de Renseignement (DRM)) under control of the Chief of the General Staff; Directorate for the Employment and Operational Preparation ( Direction Emploi et Preparation Operiotionelle (DEPO or). The DSLD also has counter-espionage responsibilities. Border security forces The Border Police Unit (BPU), falling under the National Police, is the primary agency dedicated to border security and border management. The BPU is responsible for border surveillance and preventing cross-border crimes such as human trafficking, drug trafficking, arms smuggling, and irregular migration. The BPU is the first responder in the event of a border incident given that it collaborates with law enforcement agencies in neig

preventing cross-border crimes such as human trafficking, drug trafficking, arms smuggling, and irregular migration. The BPU is the first responder in the event of a border incident given that it collaborates with law enforcement agencies in neig hbouring coun tries and international partners. The Directorate for Emigration and Immigration (Direction de l’Émigration etde l’Immigration (DIE)) is an integral part of the National Police which provides passport and visa control at border crossings. The Agency for the Integrated Management of Border Spaces further augments control of border areas where cross-border ethnic and family ties render proper border management and control difficult. This Agency improves living conditions, bringing central government and loc alcommunities together through infrastructure development, poverty reduction, border security (as an intelligence source to the EBEN Armed Forces) and cross-border cooperation. The Customs and Excise Department (Direction Générale des Douanes et Droits In directs) is responsible for overseeing trade, taxation, and customs-related matters, regulating and monitoring goods and people crossing the borders. As already highlighted, the northern borders of EBEN and those of its neighbouring countries have increasi ngly become areas of heightened security concern. These areas have witnessed an incremental infiltration of radical TGs and VEOs, resulting in increased violence and instabili ty. The already porous border departments which were already characterised by ill icit activities along historic trade routes have now become increasingly vulnerable due to limited state presence and socio-economic challenges that these groups and organisations continue to exploit for the purpose of recruitment and support. by ill icit activities along historic trade routes have now become increasingly vulnerable due to limited state presence and socio-economic challenges that these groups and organisations continue to exploit for the purpose of recruitment and support. Private sec urity companies Many private security companies have sprung up in EBEN since the beginning of the 2000s. A considerable number of licences have been awarded by the Ministry of Interior and Public Security to private security companies. Not all companies complete their mission to the highest standards even though they are supervised by the security forces. The management of the national parks in northern EBEN and the role of the SOUTH-AFRICAN private security company African Parks Network (APN) constitutes an EBEN-specific driver of political violence and radicalisation. The Government’sgrip on the parks through the APN to develop touri smclashes with the interests of both hunters, pastoralists, local farmers and villagers who have been denied access to th eparks. On the other hand, cooperation between the ANP and the EBEN Armed Forces where the former supported the latter has benefited the fight against violent extremism. Reports suggest that the APN h as in some cases assumed command and control of the EBE N Armed Forces in the border areas. However, the defacto privatisation of security provided by the APN and its privileged link with the EBEN Presidency suggest that commercial interests are being inserted into a complex problem set which could in turn spu rfurther conflict .
