At the national level, the departmental and mobile Gendarmerie constitute the main pillars of the force . Six Gendarmerie Legions located all over the national territory regroup the departmental and mobile Gendarmerie formations within the territorial boundaries of the Legion. There is one Special Gend armerie Legion under the direct control of the Chief of Staff of the National Gendarmerie. The Special Gendarmerie Legion includes the Gendarmerie National Intervention Special Unit (GNISU), as well as the honour and security squadron, and the Intervention an d Surveillance Quick Response Group (ISQRG) Officers are trained at the military academy in Pô (FANBO) or in gendarmerie officer schools abroad. Non-commissioned officers are trained at the national school for non-commissioned officers of the gendarmerie in Bobo Dioulas so. The Gendarmerie is a key contributor to the FANBO contingents in UN, AU and ECOWAS peacekeeping missions. Other forces Police and paramilitary forces The National Police of FANBO acts under the auspices of the Ministry of Territorial Administration, Decentralization and Security . The National Directorate coordinates National Police activities through several General Direct orates . The main Direct orates are Public Security, Border Police, and the Intervention Units . The National Police is in charge of the internal security and judicial police and is responsible for the security in

Direct orates . The main Direct orates are Public Security, Border Police, and the Intervention Units . The National Police is in charge of the internal security and judicial police and is responsible for the security in the urban areas, while the National Gendarmerie is in charge of the security in the rural areas and along the highways in FANBO. Below the national level, the organization of the National Police follows the FANBO administrative division with its 13 regions. Each of the 13 Regional Directorates has specific services; all regional offices are further organized into Provincial Directorates. Border Security forces The Border Police, which sits under the National Police, provides security at the border crossing points of FANBO. It is the first responder in the event of border security incidents . It is also the main entity dealing with law enforcement agencies in neighbouring countries. The Armed Forces of FANBO contribute rather indirectly through maintaining territorial integrity, public order and security in FANBO. Forest rangers The Forest rangers fall under the responsibility of the Minister of Environment, Energy, Water and Sanitation . They are in charge of protecti ng of FANBO 'sforests and natural resources. Private Security Companies (PSC) Events in FANBO have raised the level of insecurity of the country: weapons have proliferated as a legacy of past conflicts in the region, the territory has increasingly become vulnerable to threats posed by TGs and VEOs, and a rise in armed crime has been recorded. The deman d for private

proliferated as a legacy of past conflicts in the region, the territory has increasingly become vulnerable to threats posed by TGs and VEOs, and a rise in armed crime has been recorded. The deman d for private security services has greatly increased in the last few years due to a clear inability from the defence and security forces to cope with demand7. A considerable number of licences have been awarded to Private Security Companies (PSC) - some of which work in other sub-Saharan countries-like Global Guardian, ETS Risk Management, Titan Groupe, SafeLane, and the Messner Group. However, significant challenges derive from the activities of these security actors present in t he region. For example, training imposed by the PSC personnel often proves absent or insufficient, and next to alleged violations of labour laws, reports on the FANBO PSC sector mention the deliberate misconduct by employees, with cases of corruption or theft. 7 The country has historically maintained a relatively small and less developed military apparatus. Additionally, FANBO lacks a substantial industrial base; therefore, - while there are maintenance and repair facilities-FANBO does not have an arms producti on sector. The country'ssecurity forces rely on arms import to equip its Armed Forces ( before MURINUS became a major arm supplier, FANBO was primarily supplied with used military equipment by FRANCE). Of particular note, the success of the SOUTH-AFRICA Nprivate security company African Parks Network (APN) in EBEN has not gone unnoticed to leaders in FANBO. International military relations International military cooperation continues to play a vital role in the overall policy objectives of FANBO as it seeks to promote its diplomatic ties with other nations. However, while FANBO has historically shown a preference towards FRANCE, recent events suggest a significant shift towards MURINUS given the latter’sprofessed anti-colonial ist stance . Participation in international and regional peacekeeping missions is one of FANBO’smost important objective s as a means to bolster regional stability. Peacekeeping missions Since the late 1990s, FANBO has contributed to UN, AU and ECOWAS peacekeeping missions. Troops have been deployed to conflict zones, predominantly in sub-Saharan Africa. Currently, FANBO provides mission experts, police and military contingents and individual staff officers to missions in the CENTRAL AFRICAN REPUBLIC, the DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC of CONGO, MILA, and SOUTH-SUDAN. No peacekeeping mission is currently deployed in FANBO. Defence cooperation agreements Bilateral agreements with EGYPT, FRANCE, RWANDA, and the USA8 aim to improve FANBO Armed Forces’ training or to support their operational capabilities . Military cooperation with MURINUS, to include the training of FANBO

FANBO. Defence cooperation agreements Bilateral agreements with EGYPT, FRANCE, RWANDA, and the USA8 aim to improve FANBO Armed Forces’ training or to support their operational capabilities . Military cooperation with MURINUS, to include the training of FANBO Armed Forces officer cadets and officers at all levels, and pilots is being discussed. When it comes to regional agreements, t he EU has allocated funds to FANBO to support th e G5 Sahel joint counterterrorism force. FANBO has defence attaché s in ALGERIA, BELGIUM, CERO, EGYPT, FRANCE, GERMANY ( cross-accredited to other European countries including MURINUS), IGAINER, MILA, and the USA. Foreign military presence Based on a bilateral agreement between the two countries, in 2018 FRANCE had deployed troops to FANBO as part of a broader military presence to fight terrorism and extremism in the Sahel region. However, in the light of further instability and successful MURINUS messaging, these have been withdrawn, with no current plans to redeploy them. 8 However, since the coup, the bilateral defence cooperation with FRANCE has been suspended. The USA also applied coup-related restrictions affecting military aid. Similarly, Messner Group, a MURINUS Private Security Company, has increased its presence in FANBO. This deployment was authorized by the FANBO junta with the publicly-stated justification of fighting terrorists and extremist militants. security organizations The LITTERAE9 initiative: a forum between EBEN, FANBO, NAGA, CERO and GOOT (MILA and REGIN are observers) to stop violent extremists, especially in the border areas. However, in view of recent coup sactivity, FANBO has been suspended from this forum . ECOWAS: member states agreed to coordinate efforts to try to create stability across Western Africa . This entails pooling of counter-terrorist efforts, including tackling the financing of violent extremism, and promoti ngintercommunal dialogue. G5 Sahel Joint Force: a counterterrorism partnership between CHAD, FANBO, MAURITANIA, MILA and REGIN to address common challenges to peace and security . The Joint Force seeks to combat terrorism and prevent fighters and traffickers from evading pursuit across national borders. However, FANBO has also been suspended from G5 Sahel Joint Force activities due to the above-mentioned instability . Intelligence and security services The Agence National de Renseignement (ANR or National Agency of the Intelligence Services), which acts under direct control of the President, contributes to combating securit ychallenges such as cybercrime, while increasing the training and professionalization of personnel to im prove interpretation of intelligence threats by political leaders. Presidential Office The Agence National ede Renseignement, under direct authority of the President of FANBO, is an umbrella organization intended to be the managerial body for the intelligence services of the entire country, including the Gendarmerie, the National Police, and the Armed Forces. Its establishment was a response

the President of FANBO, is an umbrella organization intended to be the managerial body for the intelligence services of the entire country, including the Gendarmerie, the National Police, and the Armed Forces. Its establishment was a response to the absence of a formal entity responsible for coordinating and guiding the activities of all intelligence structures, both domes tic and foreign. Its main mission revolves around intelligence collection activities for the benefit of the President and the government, facilitating effective decision-making. No information is currently available on the status of the ANR, because of the 2022 coups. 9 The capital of NAGA Gendarmerie The Gendarmerie intelligence service was restructured in the 1980s, with the estab lishment of the Centre de Recherche et Traitement de l’Information (CRTI or Information Research and Processing Centre ). The CRTI collects analyses and disseminates national and international information via its special units working on defence and securit y, economic activities and education and training. The counter-intelligence service within the CRTI collects on cou nter espionage and subversive activities by foreign countries likely to threaten FANBO. National Police The Intelligence Services within the National Police are managed by the Division de Renseignements Généraux (DRG or Directorate for General Intelligence), which operates under the Direction dela Sûreté de l’Etat (DSE or Directorate of State Security). The DRG contains seve ral specialized services such as the protection of high-ranking individuals, oversight of civilian arms and ammunition, and special investigations. External entities like the Services Régionaux de Sûreté de l’Etat

Directorate of State Security). The DRG contains seve ral specialized services such as the protection of high-ranking individuals, oversight of civilian arms and ammunition, and special investigations. External entities like the Services Régionaux de Sûreté de l’Etat (SRSE or Regional State Security Services) and specialized police units are also responsible for overseeing border security at airports, land borders, and railways. Armed Forces of FANBO The Armed Forces' intelligence services are primarily responsible for processing and analysing military intelligence. This is coordinated at a strategic level by the Directorate of Strategic Analysis within the Ministry of Defence and Veterans Affairs . These services involve the Division de Renseignements Militaires (DRM or Military Intelligence Division) at the General Staff of the Armed Forces and the Centre de Recherche et Traitement de l’Information (CRTI ) of the National Gendarmerie. T he DRM has counterparts in each headquarters of the different services . Information The deterioration of the security environment in FANBO has also brought an increased risk for freedom of information . Military authorities have suspended media outlets and deported foreign journalists-including the correspondents of western newspapers-causing a rise in cases of intimidation of journalists. Aside from the threats against journalists, practitioners living in the red zones of Hauts-Bassins, Boucle du Mouhoun, Nord, Sahel, and Est still face serious challenges in accessing sources of information. Press freedom and the right to informati on have been guaranteed by the Constitution since 1991 and the Information Code does grant journalists free access to sources of

still face serious challenges in accessing sources of information. Press freedom and the right to informati on have been guaranteed by the Constitution since 1991 and the Information Code does grant journalists free access to sources of information; however, there are exceptions pertaining to the internal or external security of the state, military secrets, strategic economic interests, ongoing investigations and legal proceedings. Consequently, in 2 019 the criminal code was amended so as to criminalise the dissemination of information on military operations in order to “not undermine troop morale”, which became punishable by up to 10 years in prison, and heavy fines . In general, it is becoming increa singly difficult to access information on security and military matters, and the dangerous parts of the country are turning into “no-news zones”. In terms of external influence10, FANBO ’sinformation ecosystem has been targeted over recent years with increasing pro-MURINUS and anti-FRANCE propaganda11. Several orchestrated campaigns aimed at users of social networks have been identified, predominately on Facebook . Researchers also suggest that the highest volume of influencer actions take place on Whats App and Telegram. While it is difficult to assess and directly attribute, it is clear that pro-MURINUS message s are being heard and amplified in FANBO, as in the rest of the Sahel, and not just through social media. The Africa Initiative, created in 2023 is a MURINUS news agency, based in REGIN but with active offices in MILA and FANBO. The agency is positioning itself as the new

Sahel, and not just through social media. The Africa Initiative, created in 2023 is a MURINUS news agency, based in REGIN but with active offices in MILA and FANBO. The agency is positioning itself as the new bridgehead for MURINUS'spropaganda in Africa. There is no doubt that MURINUS information operations were parti ally responsible for protests in the country as well as fuelling unrest around recent military coup s. This has included numerous sightings of MURINUS flags at protests indicative of anti-FRANCE sentiment. These narratives, promoted by Messner Group, have also been evident in mainstream and local media outlets. Techniques have included copy-paste of pro-MURINUS narratives in adverts on Facebook to deliver anti-Western messages and calls to action, the use of sock-puppets and social media accounts from outside FANBO, as well as the use of pro-MURINUS testimonial videos by nano-influencers supporting Messner involvement in FANBO. Calls for MURINUS assistance in fighting VEOs in FANBO appeared to be heavily influenced by MURINUS ’ perceived military success in other African countries. Measures taken by FANBO to counter disinformation efforts coming from MURINUS include fact checking, awareness campaigns, improving media literacy and enhancing cybersecurity. However, it is difficul t to measure effectiveness and the on-going campaign by MURINUS remains a serious cause for concern. Terror Groups, particularly t he RLF, are actively using a range of means of communication ( such as social media, word of mouth, encrypted messaging apps) for recruitment, training, and propaganda purposes, including in FANBO . The Internet penetration rate is high in the main urban centres in FANBO, whereas rural areas tend to be more digitally isolated. In both urban and rural areas, there is unstable and poor-quality broadband, and high access costs. Last year only 19% of the population had access to electricity (60% of urban population and 3% of rural population is connected ). These factors severely hinder the greater use of information and communications technology in the economic and social life of many people in FANBO. Almost all news media (radio, television, printed press) have integrated digital media. In almost all media companies, there are two editorial offices: a traditional editorial office and a web editorial office. The 2.85 million social media users represents 12% of the population. Diplomacy The defining feature of FANBO ’sforeign policy is its dependence on external financial support, particularly foreign aid . Its “diplomacy of development” views external action as a practical tool to serve its nation-building process and the deepening of African unity, via mechanisms in support of economic and financial initiatives. This approach to diplomacy is rooted in the Constitution, as well as in its bilateral agreements and in national programmes . FANBO has regional and international commitments with organisations like the West African Economic and Monetary Union (WAEMU), the Economic Community of West Afri can States (ECOWAS), the African Union (AU), the Francophone International Organisation, the African Caribbean Pacific-European Union (ACP-EU), the United Nations (UN) and the World Trade Organisation (WTO). Given its fragile democracy and heavy dependen ce on international cooperation13, FANBO has sought to build a reputation for promoti ngpeace and security in West Africa. FANBO presents itself on the international stage as ready to share its experience in managing conflicts, and therefore calls for bilateral and multilateral actions to contain violence in the region. Most states in the industrialized world maintain diplomatic relationships with FANBO . As described, i ncreasing military cooperation is also an indicator of the country’sperceived credibili ty, not only in regional and international organizations, but

the region. Most states in the industrialized world maintain diplomatic relationships with FANBO . As described, i ncreasing military cooperation is also an indicator of the country’sperceived credibili ty, not only in regional and international organizations, but also bilaterally, as shown by a number of mil itary training and exchange programs (particularly on counter-terrorism ) with several western states . However, FANBO continues to face significant border security challenges with its neighbour s (primarily border-crossing criminal activities ) which exploit the institutional vacuum, and only adds to the challenges of combatting violent extremism . 13 External partners finance 80% of FANBO development programs. MILA A border dispute between MILA and FANBO over the Agacher Strip triggered a short war in 1983. Since the border was demarcated after a ruling of t he International Court of Justice in 1986, relations have been generally stable. The two countries have conducted combined operations against violent extremis ts in the Sahel through the G5 Sahel initiative. However, the worsening situation in MILA has fuelled violent extremism in FANBO, to the extent that the country, and in particular the tri-border area between FANBO, MILA and REGIN is now one of the epicentres of conflict in sub-Saharan West Africa . Following the series of coups, both countries have been suspended from ECOWAS and the AU . Transhumance and sedentary farmers, forced south by conflict or desertification, continue to put pressure on land water resources in the borderlands, leading to tensions between farmers and pastoralists in MILA

suspended from ECOWAS and the AU . Transhumance and sedentary farmers, forced south by conflict or desertification, continue to put pressure on land water resources in the borderlands, leading to tensions between farmers and pastoralists in MILA and FANBO. EBEN A border dispute between EBEN and FANBO arose in the early 2000s over an area around the village of Koualu near the tri-border with GOOT . After an agreement in 2009 declaring the area a neutral zone, there have been no further border disputes. As the northern EBEN national parks (transhumance corridors ) have become focal points for violent extremists’ attacks, both countries have agree d to cooperate in managing their common border to address insecurity in the region. REGIN Due to a vague colonial delimitation, REGIN and FANBO had difficulty in agreeing on the exact location of the boundary until a ruling from the ICJ resolved the dispute in 2013 . However, more stable bilateral relations have recently been disrupted by violent extremism in the tri-border area between FANBO, MILA and REGIN. Given their geographical position s, transnational criminal activit ies such as human trafficking and smuggling influence bilateral relation s. GOOT Without any border disputes between GOOT and FANBO, relati ons between the two countries have been stable. Recently violent extremism along the GOOT-EBEN border area has forced transhumance and sedentary farmers south, putting pressure on land and water resources . along the GOOT-EBEN border area has forced transhumance and sedentary farmers south, putting pressure on land and water resources . NAGA, GOOT and FANBO have developed the IDAVILLE Border Forum ( IBF), an EU-sponsored project part of the Strengthening Border Security in NAGA . CERO The border between the two countries has never been formally defined. Relations have evolved in a positive way with a “Treaty of Friendship and Cooperation” concluded in 2008; however, current relations are influenced by growing violent extremism, with its consequent influx of refugees from FANBO. Geostrategic and regional context Historical timeline 1800-1914 1914-1960 1960-1966 1966-1999 1999-PRESENT Period characterized by a series of military dictatorships, with coups and counter coups leading to long periods of military rule. Subsequent regimes engage in widespread human rights violations, corruption, and economic mismanagement, with the country'soil wealth providing the basis for political control and the personal enrichment of military leaders. IGAINER formally becomes a British colony in 1914. During the colonial period, GREAT BRITAIN asserted political and economic control over IGAINER, using the country as a source of raw materials and a market for British goods. Pre-colonial era characterized by a diverse array of ethnic groups and civilizations . These societies had their own cultures, religions, and systems of governance. Trade and commerce were important aspects of pre-colonial society in IGAINER, with the trans-Saharan trade routes and coastal trade with Europeans constituting key sources of wealth and power. Period marked by civil rule with elections at regular intervals with a handover of power between different political parties. IGAINER sees a growth of its civil society and a more active press, however corruption remains a major challenge. Period of the First Republic of IGAINER marked by a mix of hope and challenges. Ultimately, IGAINER struggle s to establish a stable and functioning democratic system, having to deal with complex social, economic, and political issues . This period is marked by several military coups . IGAINER in the making: Since its independence

struggle s to establish a stable and functioning democratic system, having to deal with complex social, economic, and political issues . This period is marked by several military coups . IGAINER in the making: Since its independence from GREAT BRITAIN in 1960, the history of IGAINER has been characteri sed by several periods of political, socio-cultural and ethnic instability and outbreaks of violence. While the government is currently primarily focused on the country’sstability and internal security, it nevertheless pursues ambitions to establish itself as a dominant regional power. The de-facto colonial period of IGAINER began in 1861 when British merchants established a protectorate over the coastal areas of the country. IGAINER’sstruggle for independence begins in the 1940s and culminate s in 1960 with the country'sindependence and the establishment of the Federal Republic of IGAINER. Despite the country’s 2000s oil boom creating a stronger middle class, IGAINER continues to grapple with security challenges, including insurgency and sectarian violence . Even then, IGAINER becomes increasingly aware of its important position in regional affairs as one of the most popul ated and economically influential countries in sub-Saharan Africa, also attracting the interest of DUSHMAN and MURINUS. Despite an eventual return to democratic rule, the legacy of military rule continues to shape the political and economic landscape of IGAINER . Geostrategic interests and foreign policy The strategic outlook of IGAINER balances internal and external considerations, reflecting factors such as its geo-strategic position, population size, the influence of the armed forces, and its natural resources

of IGAINER . Geostrategic interests and foreign policy The strategic outlook of IGAINER balances internal and external considerations, reflecting factors such as its geo-strategic position, population size, the influence of the armed forces, and its natural resources and wider economy. Its history has been characteri sed by alternating periods of civil and military rule, the product of deeply rooted, political, socio-economic and socio-cultural cleavages, diverse ethnic groups and high levels of criminality. Ungoverned spaces have been exploited by Terror Group s (TG), Violent Extremist Organisations (VEO) and other armed groups, threatening internal stability. This balancing act is further complicated within the context of globali sation, climate change, demographic challenges, migration and the rising influence of external actors with a renewed interest in the African continent. Its most significant strategic objectives include: Protecting its sovereignty, territorial integrity, and people predominantly by strengthening its security institutions. Promoting its prosperity and sustainable development agendas as well as economic diversification. The country continues to improve its busi ness environment and national infrastructure and attractiveness to foreign investment. Promoting internal stability and cohesion i nlight of the country’smultitude of ethnic, linguistic and religious groups. Tackling corruption, promoting transparency and accountability of the government and governance. Promoting its regional and international interests as an active member of the international community and through strong partnerships. Foreign policy objectives. IGAINER pursues its external interests predominantly through soft Ievers of power such as diplomacy and trade. IGAINER is member of several regional organi sations and engages in dialogue and

community and through strong partnerships. Foreign policy objectives. IGAINER pursues its external interests predominantly through soft Ievers of power such as diplomacy and trade. IGAINER is member of several regional organi sations and engages in dialogue and cooperation with a wide range of countries in Europe, America, Asia, and the Middle East, regardless of their political or ideological orientation. As a member of the Non-Aligned Movement (NAM), it seeks to promote developing countries’ interests as well as a balanced and more equitable international system. IGAINER continue s to play an active role in regional peacekeeping, conflict resolution initiatives and operations initiated by the Economic Com munity of West African States (ECOWAS), the African Union (AU) and the United Nations (UN). Additionally, IGAINER continues to seek a multilateral approach to counter regional security threats, such as piracy, terrorism, and organised crime through regional security partne rships and military cooperation programmes on a bi-or multilateral basis with a wide range of countries and parties. 2. However, three languages are considered to be dominant: Harou language in the north; Ikoku, commonly spoken in the southeast and Bayou gi in the southwes t; and Pidgin English, which is a mixture between English and African languages and is the fastest growing language in IGAINER. Percentage of total population per age group / gender: ag e 7 to 12: 16.2% (male), 16.04% (female); age 12 to 18: 16.3% (male), 16.05% (female); age 50 and over: 9.63% (male), 10.21% (female) years. Total net migration: minus 60, 000 per annum (approximated). The stimated annual growth rate of its population is approximately 2.4%. People and ethnicity IGAINER is known for its complex patchwork of over 250 ethnic groups. Religion IGAINER is characterised by a religious divide between Muslims in the north, estimated to be 50% of the population, and Christians, in the south, estimated to account for 40% of the population . 10% of the population adheres to various indigenous religions. Family and gender IGAINER is a patriarchal society where men are dominant over women in virtually all areas. While the country is a signatory to the International Convention on Equality for Women, women have fewer legal rights than men. In most ethnic groups, how ever, mothers and sisters have sway in family life. Women and children face a perilous life in the IGAINER society with widespread violence against on a regular basis. Incidents of abuse include rape, physical abuse, sexual exploitation, slavery, the execu tion

have sway in family life. Women and children face a perilous life in the IGAINER society with widespread violence against on a regular basis. Incidents of abuse include rape, physical abuse, sexual exploitation, slavery, the execu tion of children, and female genital mutilation. Education The literacy rate (age 15 and mor e, able to read and write) is 62 %. Most children start school at the age of 6 in primary school (for 6 years), which is compulsory and free. Overall enrollment rate is 83.2%. Those passing selection may start a 6 years secondary school (enrollment rate is 68%). Secondary education is provided in federal government-funded colleges (on average 2 per state) and state-owned and funded schools. There are private secon dary schools, but these are generally expensive by the country'sstandards. There are institutions of higher learning with federal and state universities, and numerous colleges. Higher education enrollment rate is 17%. Culture and arts In line with its ethnic diversity, IGAINER has wide-ranging cultures and traditions. Each individual state and region contains very different cultures . Traditionally, culture and art used to serve social or religious or educational purpose . Due to its storytelling tradition, IGAINER has a long and incredibly rich literary history. Dance and music are particularly vibrant . Economy IGAINER has emerged as Africa'slargest economy, with oil being a dominant source of government revenue since the 1970s. Alth ough regulatory constraints and security risks have limited new investment, the economy of IGAINER has continued to perform over

has emerged as Africa'slargest economy, with oil being a dominant source of government revenue since the 1970s. Alth ough regulatory constraints and security risks have limited new investment, the economy of IGAINER has continued to perform over th elast few years, driven by growth in non-oil sectors such as financial services, telecommunications, entertainment and agriculture. IGAINER is the largest producer and exporter of oil in Africa. Petroleum is also the most important source of government revenue and foreign exchange. Most of the oil output comes from onshore fields in the Niger River Delta, although an increasing portion of the crude is produced at offshore locations. In total, there are four refineries owned by the government of IGAINER, all controlled and managed by the country ’snational oil company, the IGAINER National Petroleum Corporation (INPC). The four refineries – with a combined plate capacity of 445, 000 bpsd (barrel per stream day) – are: the Warri Refinery and Petrochemical Company (WRPC), the Old Port Harcourt Refinery, the New Port Harcourt Refinery and Petrochemical Company (PHRPC), and the Kaduna Refinery and Petrochemical Company (KRPC). However, despite the country ’soil riches, the four state-owned refineries are unable to refine crude oil locally due to poor maintenance, general operational failure, and inconsistent supply of raw materials. Oil is therefore primarily refined abroad and imported back to the country, contributing to its high costs. Those costs are shouldered mostly by the government, which introduced a subsidy in order to reduce the price paid by consumers. This

therefore primarily refined abroad and imported back to the country, contributing to its high costs. Those costs are shouldered mostly by the government, which introduced a subsidy in order to reduce the price paid by consumers. This has been, and still is, the practice in IGAINER, and most residents have grown upisolate d from paying the actual price of petrol . The subsidy is hugely popular among citizens of IGAINER, many of whom consider it their major – if not the only – benefit from the government .1 However, as the cost of maintaining it has increased significantly ove r the years, today both experts and country elites agree that in light of IGAINER economic reality, the subsidy has become a huge financial burden which the state can no longer afford. Primary sector Given the resources at the country ’sdisposal, it is no surprise that resource extraction is the most important sector of the economy in IGAINER. Apart from oil, the most economically valuable minerals are natural gas, coal, tin, and columbite (an iron-bearing mineral that accompanies tin). There are vast reserves of natural gas, but most of the gas produced is a by-product of previously burned off crude oil. This has made IGAINER a globally ranked exporter of this commodity. The oil and gas industry in IGAINER has attracted significant foreign investment. Several multinational companies (Shell, ExxonMobile, Chevron, Total, Eni, Addax Petroleum, ConocoPhillips, etc.). are involved in oil and gas exploitation. Moreover, the INPC has established joint ventures with

and gas industry in IGAINER has attracted significant foreign investment. Several multinational companies (Shell, ExxonMobile, Chevron, Total, Eni, Addax Petroleum, ConocoPhillips, etc.). are involved in oil and gas exploitation. Moreover, the INPC has established joint ventures with com panies such as Chevron, ExxonMobil, Shell, and Total. However, while the petroleum sector is important-as government revenues still heavily rely on this domain-it remains a small part of the country'soverall economy. 1 The sensitivity of the subsidy has far-reaching repercussions in a country of more than 200 million people where 70 million liters of fuel are consumed daily, one i nthree people are unemployed and more than 100 milli on live below the poverty line. Agriculture accounts for nearly 25% of the GDP and provides employment (both formal and informal) for a large majority of the population. The sector is still dominated by traditional smallholders raising subsistence crops such as sorghum, maize, cassava, yams, millet, rice and incre asing quantities of wheat. Beans, melons, pepper and vegetables are also grown on chopping fields. Oil palms, rubber and bananas are grown for domestic consumption and export, with palm oil, particularly, playing a major role in IGAINER escalating personal care products industry. Arable potential has been put at 25 % of IGAINER ’stotal area, of which about 12% is cu ltivated; however, cultural traditions2-such as the prohibition against selling family land-have in part restricted access to farmland in localities that appear to have abundant cultivable land, and, in the far north, desertification has severely

12% is cu ltivated; however, cultural traditions2-such as the prohibition against selling family land-have in part restricted access to farmland in localities that appear to have abundant cultivable land, and, in the far north, desertification has severely limited the land area available for cultivation. Plantations and commercial agriculture owned by (or in partnership with) multinational corporations are gaining ground in producing viable crops and raw materials for company use ( e.g., grain for breweries). Irri gation schemes, higher producer prices, the expansion of credit and improvements in the rural infrastructure are beginning to show results. However, despite these improvements, the agricultural sector is currently experiencing extremely low productivity, reflecting reliance on traditional methods. This has resulted in a sharp decline in both the range and quantity of available products which, combined with the country ’sinabili ty to keep up with the rapid population growth, obliges IGAINER to import a significant amount of food to sustain itself. IGAINER is also the largest fish consumer in Africa and among the largest fish consumers in the world with about 3.2 million metric to ns of fish consumed annually. Its fisheries are among the fastest growing subsectors in the country. With a coastline of 853 km, total fish production per year is close to 1 million metric tons (313, 231 metric tons from aquaculture3 and 759, 828 metric tons from fisheries). Fishing constitutes a vital source of livelihood for poor communities as well as an important protein source at the household level in IGAINER.

Fishing constitutes a vital source of livelihood for poor communities as well as an important protein source at the household level in IGAINER. Secondary sector The secondary sector, which includes major industries performing in the manufacturing and processing of goods, accounts for 31 % of the GDP of the country. Goods manufactured in IGAINER include textiles, leather items, soaps and detergents, as well as processed food s. Of note, the pharmaceutical industry in IGAINER has headroo m for growth over the next 10 years, with NAGUI potentially hosting about 60% of the pharmaceutical production capacity in Africa and projected to grow further. Manufacturing has been the fastest growing sector of the economy due to a combination of cheap labour and a vast retail market (both within and beyond the country); however, it has been undermined by several factors, including the scarcity of foreign exchange to purchase imports of raw materials. Tertiary sector Like most thriving economies, the services sector is gaining momentum in IGAINER as a result of more people moving from the countryside to the cities to find employment . The country ’stertiary sector, which is mainly made up of telecommunications, financial services, and technology, offers great potential to drive the diversification agenda of the government. Currently praised as the most productive sector – contributing as high as 44% of GDP-the information and communications technology sector is a sunrise industry with significant poten tial. Of

to drive the diversification agenda of the government. Currently praised as the most productive sector – contributing as high as 44% of GDP-the information and communications technology sector is a sunrise industry with significant poten tial. IGAINER’seconomy is ranked number 52 globally in terms in of total exports, number 50 in terms of total imports, and number 126 in terms of most complex economy according to the Economic Complexity Index (ECI). IGAINER major i ndustries: Crude oil, coal, tin, columbite, palm oil, peanuts, cotton, rubber, wood, hides and skins, textiles, food products, footwear, chemicals, fertilizer, printing, ceramics, steel. IGAINER major exports: crude petroleum ($41.8B), petroleum gas ($8.52 B), special purpose ships ($1.25B), cocoa beans ($779M), refined petroleum ($667M), exporting mostly to DRENUR ($9.07B), SPAIN ($6.7B), USA ($3.69B), FRANCE ($3.32B), and DUSHMAN ($3.05B). IGAINER major imports: refined petroleum ($11.3B), wheat ($3.32B), cars ($2.42B), and packaged medicaments ($972M), importing mostly from DUSHMAN ($21.9B), DRENUR ($4.75B), NETHERLANDS ($4.58B), USA ($4.42B), and BELGIUM ($2.34B). GNI explained: Because developme ntencompasses many factors-economic, environmental, cultural, educational, and institutional-no single measure gives a complete picture. However, the total earnings of the residents of an economy, measured by its Gross National Income (GNI), is a good measure of its capacity to provide for the well-being of its people. Food security IGAINER is a huge consumer of wheat products, but its local production accounts for just 1% of the 6 million metric tons cons umed yearly. Hence, the enormous demand-supply gap is bridged with over USD2 billion spent annually on the import of wheat to meet its food and agricultural producti on shortfalls (mostly wheat, rice, poultry and fish). Food insecurity is an increasing policy concern in

gap is bridged with over USD2 billion spent annually on the import of wheat to meet its food and agricultural producti on shortfalls (mostly wheat, rice, poultry and fish). Food insecurity is an increasing policy concern in IGAINER. While the country is endowed with immense agricultural resources, dependence on food imports has remained high. This, combined with a global increase in food prices, is adversely affecting food security conditions all over the country. Acute food insecurity is not only driven by inflation and rising food prices, but also by continued conflict and climate change. The state of insecurity in northern IGAINER plays a major role in the projected rate of food insecurity in the country. Food access has been affected by pers istent violence in Borno, Adamawa and Yobe (BAY) States and armed banditry and kidnapping in states such as Katsina, Sokoto, Kaduna, Benue and Niger River . The northwest region, around Katsina, Zamfara and Sokoto States, is an increasing malnutrition hotspot affecting nearly 3 million people . Additionally, land ownership conflicts have the potential to undermine both environmental stability and food security in IGAI NER. Problems of scarcity of land, political issues encouraging illegal settlements amo ngvillagers in return for political favours, as well as marginalization of some social groups forced to defy certain rules and regulations are all factors further affecting human security in IGAINER. Increased flooding and drought make the situation even worse, degrading the environment and affecting crops, farm settlements, livestock, and seedling stores. These impact

defy certain rules and regulations are all factors further affecting human security in IGAINER. Increased flooding and drought make the situation even worse, degrading the environment and affecting crops, farm settlements, livestock, and seedling stores. These impact harvest s and subsequent planting season s, culminating in a food shortage crisis. Depending on the type of sediment deposited on farmlands during floods, some can not be cultivated for long periods of time, creating a cycle of food scarcity and hunger. Aquaculture and fish farming are not spared either: floods wash away fish stock, leading to a loss of income for farmers and a loss of valuable source of protein. Maritime crime and piracy The IGAINER maritime sector is affected by various crimes related activities. Shipping, one of the strong catalysts for socio-economic development in IGAINER, has been significantly damaged by insecurity in the Gulf of Guinea (GoG) region. This has resulted in lost revenue within the maritime industry sector alongside high freight charges and insurance premiums for IGAINER bound cargoes, sometimes resulting in a total boycott of the IGAINER ports by shipping lin es. Although piracy is portrayed as the main threat in the GoG, armed robbery against ships, illegal arms and drug trafficking, crude oil theft, human trafficking, as well as Illegal Unreported and Unregulated (IUU) fishing are other challenges which seve rely undermine socio-economic development and affect security in IGAINER. and Unregulated (IUU) fishing are other challenges which seve rely undermine socio-economic development and affect security in IGAINER. Piracy revenue in the GoG amounts to USD 4 million per year and, similarly, illegal fishing is so extensive that it amounts to 50 % of the legally reported catch in West Africa. Diaspora, remittances, and contribution to national development The number of people living outside IGAINER4, almost tripled between 1990 and 2020, constitute an important source of remittances. IGAINER has become the largest remittance recipient country in sub-Sahara Africa, receiving more than USD 24.3 billion in official remittances. However, since many transactions are unrecorded or take place through informal channels, the actual amount of remittance flows into the country is likely to be higher. Remittances include cash and noncash items that flow through formal channels such as electronic wire, or through informal cha nnels, such as money transfer services or in the form of goods carried across borders. These help poorer recipients to meet basic needs, fund cash and non-cash investments, finance education, establish new businesses, service debt and drive economic growth. The government of IGAINER is known to have developed a plan towards attracting investment from its diaspora community for nat ional development. The project revolves around a specific policy framework conceived to generate capital to invest in the development of small-and micro-enterprises, which will in turn, create employment. generate capital to invest in the development of small-and micro-enterprises, which will in turn, create employment. National budget Through its National Development Pla n 2021 to 2025, the government of IGAINER intends to achieve a number of strategic objectives including macroeconomic stability, human development, food security, energy sufficiency, improving transport infrastructure, and promoting industrial isation by focusing on Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs). The last approved national budget for IGAINER amounts to IGN 21.83 trillion. The budget is based on an IGN 10.49 trillion revenue and an IGN 11.34 trillion deficit, the latter to be financed mainly by borrowings made up of domestic and foreign sources. Banking system The banking system in IGAINER is regulated through the Central Bank of IGAINER. The major objectives of the bank are to main tain the external reserves of the country, promote a sound financial environment, and act as a financial adviser to the federal government. Other priorities include refocusing the Central Bank on its mandate to reduce inflation, increasing non-oil revenues, and, even more importantly, adopting a single and market-responsive exchange rate. IGAINER has opted for a multiple foreign exchange rate regime. This implies that the country has more than one rate at which its currency is exchanged, so different rates (fixed and floating) are used for the same currency during the same period. In a multiple exchange rate system, the fixed rat eonly applies to certain segments of the market-considered as essential-for which the price of transactions is determined by a market-driven

for the same currency during the same period. In a multiple exchange rate system, the fixed rat eonly applies to certain segments of the market-considered as essential-for which the price of transactions is determined by a market-driven exchange rate. All the other different segments have each its foreign exchange rate, which could be fixed or floating. Multiple exchange rates result in problems with the Central Bank and the federal budget. The different exchange rates pre sent a significant risk of losses in foreign currency transactions, which could force the Central Bank to issue more currency to make up for the loss resulting inflationary pressures. 4 Today millions of IGAINER nationals live abroad . The largest communities can be found in the United Kingdom (500, 000 –3, 000, 000) and the United States (600, 000 –1, 000, 000). There are also large groups in Ireland and Portugal . However, despite the mismanagement of the IGAINER naira-primarily cau sed by the unwillingness of the leadership in NAGUI to let markets decide the value of its currency-the Central Bank has been instrumental in the growth and financial credibility of national commercial banks. Today, IGAINER h as one of the most advanced financial sectors in Africa, with most of its commercial banks holding branches in other countries. Business climate and informal economy The foundations of market-economy based competition do exist in IGAINER. However, the informal sector represents nearly 60% of the economy of the count ry and involves activities that range from agricultural production

Business climate and informal economy The foundations of market-economy based competition do exist in IGAINER. However, the informal sector represents nearly 60% of the economy of the count ry and involves activities that range from agricultural production to mining and quarrying, small-scale building and construction and machine-shop manufacturing. Consequently, millions of people in IGAINER, particularly those residing in the economic centre of the country, Lagos State, live, work, and trade in this sector, employing the most vulnerable resid ents to whom informality provides crucial economic opportunities.5 The government of IGAINER has embarked on a series of measures to encourage the formalisation of businesses in the informal sector in order to sustain economic growth and development. Priv atization has also been accompanied by deregulation of various economic sectors to encourage private sector participation, no tably in telecommunications, power, and downstream petroleum sectors. Private companies are considered important engines of develop ment and growth. However, in general, the private sector encounters considerable economic, political and social barriers to development. Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) inflows have equally been strong, with over 70 % of them situated in the non-oil sector s. Deeper economic diversification in IGAINER is required to undertake structural transformation. Although the task of further diversify the economy is significant, IGAINER has significant advantages in terms of: a) strong demographics, with a large domest icmarket and labo urforce; b) abundant natural resources; c) a developing financial sector with strong management teams (and ability to partner with international banks); and d) growing

in terms of: a) strong demographics, with a large domest icmarket and labo urforce; b) abundant natural resources; c) a developing financial sector with strong management teams (and ability to partner with international banks); and d) growing democratic institutions with the political will to bu ild foundation s for the future. 5 When citizens cannot find job in traditional wage employ ment, the requirement for sustenance forces them to look for labour elsewhere. The alternative is mainly in the informal sector of the economy, where there is no minimum wage and workers are unlikely to pay taxes, have no holiday or labour rights, and freq uently work in unsafe conditions. Most of the time, it is difficult for them to obtain microcredit since they lack economic stability and concrete employer-employee ties. Socio-economic challenges IGAINER faces a range of socio-economic challenges that threaten to varying extents its internal security and long term economic growth and prosperity . Chief amongst these are: Governance and corruption challenges: Public policy in IGAINER is typiﬁed by a mixture of rent-seeking, patronage, clientelism and corruption resulting in a failure of good governance. Not surprisingly, the political system of the country still faces fundamental prob lems regarding state coherence, administrative and institutional efficiency, internal security, democratic representation and rule of law. Likewise, economic development is hindered by modest economic growth rates, poor handling of financial affairs and high unemployment rates. Low productivity in a griculture: The shortage of farmland in some localities and limited access to land in others are among the factors that restrict the size of farmland cultivated per family, alongside traditional confrontation between farmers and livestock breeders . Environ mental deterioration and climate change, poor storage facilities and transportation systems, and a lack of investment capital contribute to low productivity and general stagnation in the agricultural sector. With the population growing rapidly and urbanisa tion accelerating, the food deficit continues to worsen despite government efforts. Maritime crime

Maritime crime and piracy: There have been regional, national, and international efforts to stem the tide of maritime crime and piracy-related offenses committed in the Gulf of Guinea. IGAINER accounts for most of the vast resources and shipping traffic in the area and most of the attacks are traceable to its territorial waters. Poverty: 60% of the country’sestimated 220 million people live in extreme poverty: the burdens of aging, illness, underemployment and unemployment are carried mostly by extended family networks and the informal sector. This means that only civil servants and employees in higher education, state-owned companies and large international compan ies enjoy a certain level of social security. Extreme weather events: Floods, droughts and heat stress have become more severe and frequent, especially in the Sahelian northern part of the country bordering REGIN and the size-decreasing Lake Chad . These climate risks have already contributed to declining per-capita food production. The impact of climate change is seen in crop yields, which are projected to decline by 7% in the medium term (by 2035) and by 25% in the long term (by 2050). Social volatility: Violence can erupt as a result of disagreements over ethnicity, politics, resources, or civil rights. People identify strongl y with their ethnic group, which creates the potential for conflicts to become ethnic in nature, even if it did not begin as such. Political and/or economic dissatisfaction may also be perceived as intentional ethnic or religious marginalization. Violence is also frequently associated with elections . Crime: The kidnapping for ransom of expatriate oil workers are a major source of income for local criminal groups. IGAINER is a major hub in the trans-Atlantic narcotics trade . Significant drug-related activities, including production, sale, and shipment. Refugees and Internal Displaced Persons (IDPs): IGAINER has almost 90, 000 foreign refugees and asylum seekers, predominantly coming from the West African and sub-Saharan Afri can regions . The country also provides an important link between the coast of West Africa and the trans-Saharan routes through REGIN to North Africa – routes that are taken in attempts to travel to Europe. Migration: Population movement occurs due to conflict and political instability, desertification, natural disaster, economic opportunities or in search of better living standards. These movements have a permanent, semi-permanent or seasonal character such as temporar ylabour migration or nomadic pastoralists looking for seasonal feeding grounds to herd their animals. o Internal migration. The restructuring of the IGAINER territory into states initiated large population movements . Natural and social conditions (insecur ity, unfavourable climatic and economic conditions, desertification of once-arable land, etc) aff ect internal migration. The capital NAGUI is the main desti nation for internal migration . The well-developed city agg lomerations of Lagos and Ibadan continue to attract people from less-developed areas seeking employment opportunities and better education. o Inward migration. IGAINER is a major destination country for migrants from other West African countries. The porous nature of its borders and its economic prospect act as catalysts for inward migration . migration. IGAINER is a major destination country for migrants from other West African countries. The porous nature of its borders and its economic prospect act as catalysts for inward migration . The majority of inwards ’ migrants (74%) originate from other ECOWAS member states, in particular from EBEN (29%), NAGA (18%), MILA (13%), and GOOT (12%). o Outward migration. Outward migration (emigration) exce eds the inward migration (immigration) into the country. Sub-Saharan Africa is the main recipient of IGAINER emigrants. SUDAN has the largest IGAINER population among all African countries and constitutes an attractive destination for younger people in sea rch of work. The primary destinations in West and Central Africa are CAMEROON, NAGA and REGIN. Among OECD countries, the UNITED KINGDOM, USA and CANADA are the main countries of destination. During the past decade, a diversification of emigration to EU Mem ber States occurred, primarily towards ITALY and SPAIN. Beyond OECD countries, Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) Member States likely constitute the main destination countries, especially for individuals from the north of IGAINER. One of the positive impacts of outward migration is the economic benefit through remittances (see economic section). However, irregular migration commonly occurs across the country ’sborders, attracting human traffickers and smugglers who transport people to North African and Europea ncountries. Human rights issues: Based on reports from international and domestic observers, multiple human rights abuses by both the government and various extremist groups continue to occur throughout the country. Most reported abuses involve the denial of citizens’

Human rights issues: Based on reports from international and domestic observers, multiple human rights abuses by both the government and various extremist groups continue to occur throughout the country. Most reported abuses involve the denial of citizens’ rights by authorities: arbitrary arrest, politically motivated murder by security forces, summary executions, torture, rape, and degrading treatment and detention of prisoners, detainees, and suspects. Cultural property-IGAINER and large parts of sub-Saharan Africa have faced several incidents involving the destruction of cultural property, including its Sukur world heritage site . The Resistance Liberation Front (RLF) has been involved in attempts to erase the cultural identity and collecti vememory of some groups and communities. Civil Society and Interest Groups The civic space of IGAINER has expanded since the return of civilian authority in 1999. Today, the country ’scivil society comprises a broad range of advocacy groups, labour unio ns, government watchdogs, and ethnic, cultural, and religious associations. Civil society plays a crucial role in shaping the country'ssocial, political, and economic landscape. Comprising a diverse array of organizations and movements, civil society grou psactively engage with various stakeholders to address pressing issues and promote positive change. Their activities encompass human rights, social justice, transparency, a ccountability, and the advancement of democratic governance. Nonetheless, successiv eadministrations in IGAINER have curtailed freedoms of speech, assembly, and the press. Activists and journalists have faced interference or criminal charges when commenting on sensitive issues. Physical environment IGAINER a coastal country in West Africa. It is situated in the inner part of the Gulf of Guinea on the Atlantic Ocean, and encompasses a total land area of 923, 768 square kilometres ( km²), ranking 31 in the world . Its territory is slightly larger than FRANCE and GERMANY combined. IGAINER borders the four countries of EBEN (773 km) to its west, REGIN (1, 497 km) to the north, DACK (87 km) in the far north-east and CAMEROON (1, 690 km) to the east. Its approximately 853 kmlong

borders the four countries of EBEN (773 km) to its west, REGIN (1, 497 km) to the north, DACK (87 km) in the far north-east and CAMEROON (1, 690 km) to the east. Its approximately 853 kmlong coastlin elies on the Gulf of Guinea in the south and borders Lake Chad in the northeast. IGAINER makes maritime claims of 200 nautical miles as an exclusive economic zone, 12 nautical miles of territorial sea, and a 200-nautical-mile continental shelf. The count ry is geographically biodiverse, with resources that, apart from petroleum, include natural gas, tin, iron ore, coal, limestone, niobium, lead, zinc and arable land. The capital city is NAGUI while Lagos is the largest city. However, Lagos is by far the country'slargest city, the main air-and seaport, and the commercial and financial centre. Most of IGAINER’spopulation is concentrated in the southern part of the country, where especially the southwestern part is densely populated . Map 1: Map of IGAINER . Terrain and climate The country is made up of a wide variety of physical regions that can be broadly divided into three distinct categories: plains, highlands and river valleys. Northern IGAINER is dominated by extensive plains intersected with occasional granite outcrops. The Sahel belt extends across the most northerly parts of IGAINER. Lake Chad, within the larger Lake Chad Basin, lies to the east along the border with CHAD, REGIN and CAMEROON. The Niger River and its main tribute, the Benue River, effectively divides the country in a southern and

Chad, within the larger Lake Chad Basin, lies to the east along the border with CHAD, REGIN and CAMEROON. The Niger River and its main tribute, the Benue River, effectively divides the country in a southern and a northern part. The two rivers meet in the centre of IGAINER (at Lokoja), before emptying into the Gulf of Guinea via the Niger River Delta. In general, elevation gradually rises from the south of the country to the north. Topography and key features Plains extend across northern IGAINER. Elevation ranges from 250 to 1, 000 mabove sea level and is highest in the centre whilst gradu ally flattening towards the east, north and west, grading into the Lake Chad basin in the north east. In general, plains are unrestrictive to movement and are mostly flat with occasional granite outcrops and abruptly rising inselbergs. Plateaus and mountain sdominate the eastern part of IGAINER. The Gotel range contains the highest peaks. The Shebshi mountain range is approximately 160 kmlong, is orientated north to south, and runs along the IGAINER/CAMEROON border. The Benue River bisects the eastern plate aulandscape. The Yoruba highlands dominate the landscape in the west of the country and are separated from the Sokoto Plains in the north by the Niger River . The Yoruba highlands are characterised by a broad expanse of level sandy plains, interspersed with rocky dome outcrops. The eastern highlands are comprised of the Oban Hills and plateau. highlands are characterised by a broad expanse of level sandy plains, interspersed with rocky dome outcrops. The eastern highlands are comprised of the Oban Hills and plateau. The coastal plain extends inland for about 10 km and gradually ris es to 50 mabove sea level in the northern part. The coastal plain consists of general lyrolling lowland. The southernmost areas are comprised of sandy beaches and lagoons that stretch back into creeks and mangrove swamps. Coastline IGAINER ’scoastline is 853 kmlong and consists of narrow strips and can be divided into four littoral zones (see map). Sediments on beaches range from silt and clay (Mahin mud coast) to medium grained sand in west. Beach slopes are relatively flat. IGAINER experi ences tides with a range of 1 metre. There are many oil terminals/infrastructure off the coast which can be potentially hazardous to shipping. Additionally there are numerous shipwrecks littered along the coast presenting further hazards for shipping and a mphibious operations. The coast is divided in four littoral zones (see map) . From west to east they are the “ Barrier Lagoon coast ”, the “Mahin mud coast ”, the “River delta coast ”, and the “ Strand coast ”. mud coast ”, the “River delta coast ”, and the “ Strand coast ”. Lakes, rivers, and delta s IGAINER is divided into two main catchment areas; that of Lake Chad in northeast and that of the Niger River area, that covers about 63% of the entire country. The main lake, Lake Chad and the Chad Basin, in the dry north-east of IGAINER, is fed from the north-eastern quarter of IGAINER, through rivers flowing north-eastwards. Lake Chad is very shallow and provides surrounding savannahs, grasslands and woodlands with seasonal flooding. Lake Chad is economically importa nt, providing water to more than 30 million people living in the four countries around it. The lake has had a variable history over the las tmillion years, drying upseveral times and just as often growing to many times its current size. In recent decades, its surface area has been reduced considerably, which may partly be due to humans taking water from its inlets to irrigate agricultural land. IGAINER has constructed around 300 dams, providing the country with hydroelectric power and irrigation. The Kainj i Lake and Jebba reservoirs (both artificial) in the Niger River, are the second and third large stlakes after Lake Chad . The surface area of Kainji dam varies between 1280 km² and 660 km². August to December is the filling period of Kainji, whilst April to July is the period of emptying. Jebba has a maximum depth of 105 m and an estimated surface area of 303 km². The Niger River and the Benue River are central features of IGAINER and flow through lower river valleys as well as constituting a water barrier between the highland, plateaus and mountains in west, north and east and the lower areas and the river delta in the southern part of the country. The Niger River flows from the north west of IGAINER and bisects the Sokoto plains in the north and the Yoruba Highlands in the southwest. It merges with the Benue River in the centre of IGAINER where the two rivers form a “Y” and flow ssouth where it even tually empty into the Atlantic Ocean. Climate Precipitation is the key climatic variable, with most areas experiencing a clear alternation between wet and dry seasons. The length of the rainy season generally decreases moving northwards, as well as the number of rainy days and total annual precipitation. In the south, the long rainy season occurs from March until the end of July, peaking in June with humidity values above 85%, especially in the Niger River Delta and coastal lowlands. After a drier period through the "August Break", there is another, but shorter, rainy season from early September to mid-October, however not as heavy as the first period. In the north the wet season is shorter and normally lasts from mid-May to September, often with rain s that are heavy and short in duration, and peak in July and Augu st. These rains are often characterized by frequent storms, which can result in local flash floods. Average relative humidity tends to decrease from south to north, and east to west in some areas. There is a significant range in total annual rainfall acros s the country. Annual rainfall along the coast in the southeast can approach 4, 000mm, whereas in the north-east it is only up to 500mm. The main climatic conditions that impact military operations are the influence of heavy rainfall, high temperatures, hig hlevels of humidity, and the occurrence of sandstorms. Climate types include: Warm desert climate (red). Characterised by hot temperatures (monthly means 21°C to 32°C) and little cloud cover. Daily maximums can exceed 40°C, wit ha significant temperature drop at night. Precipitation is unreliable. Warm semi-arid climate (orange ). Annual precipitation totals (380-630mm) that is higher than that of desert climates. Whilst not as extreme as in the true deserts, variations in annual precipitation are still significant. Tropical Savannah climate (light blue ). Climate is relatively hot (mean temperatures above 18°C) and rainfall is high (800-1, 600mm of rain annually), with a distinct wet season (7 or more months) and a relatively short (5 or fewer months) dry season. Monsoon climate (dark blue ). Variation of the tropical monsoon climate with large amounts of rain during the wet season, usually in the form of frequent thunderstorms, and have a less pronounced dry season, during which a sizeable amount of precipitation also falls. Both temper atures and humidity are high. Map 4: Overview IGAINER'sclimat icregions (upon Köppen-Geiger classification). Climate change Climate change and environmental degradation are major challenges facing IGAINER, and by extension, sub-Saharan Africa. The area is particularly vulnerable to drought, flooding and the resulting decline in crops, livestock production, food supply and income, which can exacerbate pove rty and food insecurity . In the norther npart of IGAINER, the encroaching

The area is particularly vulnerable to drought, flooding and the resulting decline in crops, livestock production, food supply and income, which can exacerbate pove rty and food insecurity . In the norther npart of IGAINER, the encroaching desertification and subsequent land degradation in arid, semi-arid, and dry sub-humid regions is a significant issue, largely due to factors such as overgrazing, deforestation, and poor land management practices. Desertif ication is having a significant impact on herder and pastoralist communities. The increasing encroachment of deserts continue s to reduce available land for grazing, leading to conflict between sedentary farmers and pastoralists over access to resources. Th is in turn exacerbates poverty, undermines social stability, and exacerbates popular discontent. It also provides threat actors with recruitment narratives and increases their potential pool of recruits. Moreover, climate change increases the risk of a sig nificant public health crisis, decreases the output of hydroelectric power production, and reduces access to shelter and road networks due to flooding. It also threatens coastal cities in terms of sea-level rise and increased frequency of coastal floods. IGAINER is expecting increasing climate change effects in the near future, especially from excess flooding in the southeastern and central regions, as well as temperature increase in all regions of the country. These environmental issues are exacerbated by basic needs from IGAINER'shuge population, and its rapid growth rate of close to 3%. Environmental issues IGAINER faces several significant environmental challenges, including air-and water pollution, deforestation, land degradation, and waste manageme nt. These

are exacerbated by basic needs from IGAINER'shuge population, and its rapid growth rate of close to 3%. Environmental issues IGAINER faces several significant environmental challenges, including air-and water pollution, deforestation, land degradation, and waste manageme nt. These issues are exacerbated by rapid industrialization and urbanization that have led to increased vehicle emissions and industria lair pollution. Air quality in IGAINER has deteriorated significantly in recent years, particularly in urban and industrial areas, making respiratory infections the highest ranked cause of death in the country. Gas flaring from oil extraction in the Niger River Delta releases greenhouse gases and causes respiratory problems for local people. IGAINER'swater resources face severe contamination, as for instance oil spills in the Niger Delta have destroyed ecosystems. Toxic chemical discharge from unregulated industrial activity flows into drains and open sewers in cities, especially in Lagos and Kano. Inadequate sanitation and increased domestic and industrial waste have polluted watercourses in urban areas throughout the country. IGAINER is experiencing rapid deforestation, and land degradation is major issues. Up to 80% of forests have been lost and IGAINER has the highest rate of deforestation in the world, losing 3.7% of its forest cover every year, driven by poverty, agricultural expansion, commercial logging, and urbanization. Land degradation is caused by deforestation, intensive grazing, over-cultivation and inappropriate agricultural practices. Oil and gas exploration has aggravated ecological damage in the Niger River Delta. Illegal mining activities have left many areas severely degraded. Poor waste management is a significant challenge. over-cultivation and inappropriate agricultural practices. Oil and gas exploration has aggravated ecological damage in the Niger River Delta. Illegal mining activities have left many areas severely degraded. Poor waste management is a significant challenge. Indiscriminate dumping of household and industrial wastes on land, water, and air is prevalent in IGAINER. Controlling waste disposal remains a major environmental challenge that needs to be solved. A significant part of key infrastructure of IGAINER was built during the colonial period and is in heavy need of extensive maintenance and upgrading. Airports and air transportation The IGAINER aviation is presently one of the fastest growing sectors of the country’seconomy, and served by 22 foreign carriers . Both passenger traffic and aircraft movements have significantly increased over the latest years. IGAINER travellers can fly directly to many global business centres, including London, Paris, Frankfurt, New York, and Dubai . Total air freight vol umes are also increasing, but from very low levels. IGAINER has 32 airports, 26 of which are operated by the Federal Airports Authority of IGAINER (FAAI). In addition, there are airstrips or airfields scattered around the country, built mainly by the IGAI NER Air Force and multinational oil companies. The principal airports are Murtala Muhammed International Airport in Lagos and Namdi Azikiwe International Airport in the capital, NAGUI . Airports are strategically crucial for IGAINER, considered a critical element in its transportation system. While airports are undoubtedly critical national infrastructure, it'sworth noting that their full potential is not yet realized. To foster economic growth through aviation, IGAINER needs to address challenges such as aligning the sector with economic needs, improving passenger research, enhancing airport efficiency, and creating a more enabling environm ent for the industry. Railway network and rail transportation After IGAINER ’sindependence, the railway network degraded significantly . Years of neglect of both the rolling stock and the tracks seriously reduced the capacity and utility of the system. Restoration projects have been underway since 2009 to remedy the poor condition, efficiency, and profitability of the country’srailroads. The government has introduced two projects: a railway connecting Lagos in the south to Kano in the north; and a railway spanning the coast, from Lagos to Calabar, close to the CAMEROON border. Work on both projects is still ongoing . Construction has also begun on a new line linking Kano to Maradi, in neighbouring REGIN. These projects feature heavy DUSHMAN involvement . A 1 billion EUR, DUSHMAN-built railway line was, after several delays finally opened in Lagos. The line is 156 k mlong and stretches from the largest city, Lagos, to the metropolis Ibadan. Th is railway is eventually intended as a gateway to the rest of the country . The train way line was built by the sam ecofounded arrangement between IGAINER and DUSHMAN governments. Recent government actions underscore the railway'scritical status. Significant investments have been made in new railway projects and the rehabilitation of the existing ageing networks. In 2023, the government introduced a new law enabling states to build railway lines, indicating the importance placed on expanding the network . The government'songoing efforts to revitalize and expand the system further emphasize its strategic importance to the country'sdevelopment. The railway network con tributes to social cohesion and national development, promoting mobility and migration between regions, and contributing to social integration among different linguistic and ethnic groups. Improved railway infrastructure can attract investment to underdeveloped areas, potentially reducing regional economic disparities. Rail transportation is valued not only for its efficiency but also for its additional security benefits compared to road trave l, and could contribute to peacebuilding efforts by br inging economic opportunities. There are six major seapor tsalong the Atlantic coastline engaged in maritime global trade, and especially with DUSHMAN, DRENUR, JAPAN, MURINUS, the USA and the European Union .
