especially with DUSHMAN, DRENUR, JAPAN, MURINUS, the USA and the European Union . The 6 main seaports of IGAINER are: Port of Lagos / Apapa The Port of Lagos (also known as the Premiere port and Apapa port ). It is the oldest and the biggest port in the country, in terms of both land area and volumes of cargo handled. Presently, it is one of the most efficient ports in IGAINER, equipped with modern equipment and operating systems . More th an half of the IGAINER maritime trade passes through the Apapa quays, which are home to the port . The port is connected to other parts of the country and its immediate neighbours through railways, inland waterways and roads. Crude oil and petroleum product s are handled at the eight jetties. The port has four fishing wharves for handling fishing boats. Lagos port also has four tank farms for storing refined petroleum products. The port’scontainer terminal spans 55 hectares and has a total quay length of 10 05 m. It has large container capacity and is equipped with 13 mobile harbour cranes and 14 rubber-tired cranes used for loading and unloading operations. Storage space comprises 3 warehouses spanning 3025 m2 and an unpaved open yard for the temporary stora ge of goods. The port has implemented electronic procedures such as fixed berthing windows, real-time visibility of transactions, performance reviews, technical support, real-time invoicing and container tracking. Port of Tin Can Island The Tin Can Island port is situated

goods. The port has implemented electronic procedures such as fixed berthing windows, real-time visibility of transactions, performance reviews, technical support, real-time invoicing and container tracking. Port of Tin Can Island The Tin Can Island port is situated west of Apapa near the Lagos port complex and is the second busiest port in IGAINER. It was established in 1975 as a consequence of the oil boom in IGAINER. An additional facility was subsequently constructed to reduce cargo congestion. Th emain storage terminal has one of the biggest grain silos with a 28, 000-tonne capacity. The grain terminal handles wheat, maize, malt, and barley and transports 4000 tonnes of grains daily. It can accommodate ships weighing around 30, 000 tonnes and also features a grain-bagging facility. The container terminal is owned by a consortium that includes DUSHMAN ’s Africa Development Fund . The total berthing line is 770 m with an alongside depth of 13 m. Fully functional port equipment eases port operations. The Tin Can Container terminal has 8 mobile cranes, 15 rubber-tiered gantries, 14 reach stackers, 6 empty handlers, 4 forklifts and 45-yard trailers. Calabar port The Calabar port lies 45 nautical miles upstream and is a multi-purpose port that consists of thr eeterminals. It requires regular dredging to maintain a 7.5 mdraught. Mainly general cargo and crude oil are handled at the port. Calabar port has also traditionally served the northern states of IGAINER by handling containers, general cargo, bagged ceme nt, grains and seafood. Port of Onne Onne

Mainly general cargo and crude oil are handled at the port. Calabar port has also traditionally served the northern states of IGAINER by handling containers, general cargo, bagged ceme nt, grains and seafood. Port of Onne Onne port lies on the Bonny River along the creek of Ogu and was originally established as a free port zone to serve the oil and gas sector of West Africa. The refurbished port can accommodate the latest generation-ocean going vessels and consists of a Federal Ocean Terminal and the Federal Lighter Terminal. The former has four wharves, and the latter contains eleven berths. These specialised terminals handle bulk cargo, containers, raw materials, steel, ferti lisers, coal and finished products. The port can accommodate 90, 000 Deadweight Tonnage ( DWT ) vessels. It has three terminals opera ted by private companies. The first terminal has a 370 mlong linear quay with a depth of 9.5 m for receiving container ships and conventional cargo vessels. The second terminal is the biggest oil and gas free zone in Africa. The deep-water facility has a 15 mchannel draft and a warehouse area for keeping containers and breakbulk. The third terminal, named West Africa Container Terminal, accommodates the largest container vessels. It has a 25-hectare storage yard and a total quay length of 570 m with a dra ft of 12 m. length of 570 m with a dra ft of 12 m. Port of Harcourt The port is strategically positioned in one of the world’sbiggest crude oil production zones and has a 1300 mberthing line for accommodating eight sea-going vessels simultaneously. It is equipped with 16 tanks for storing oil and petroleum products, seven stacking areas and significant storage capacity. It also has a dockyard for electrical, marine and engineering works. Port of Warri The Warri port was constructed to serve the west delta region and attract oil companies. The port is divided into the Warri new port and the old port that deals with RORO, containers and general cargo. It has four container wharves and five general cargo berths covering 1600 m with a draft of 8 m. A 60, 000 m2 yard is used for storing containers and a 2500 m2 paved area for keeping loose cargo. It is equipped with the latest equipment such as cranes, forklifts, trailer s, port trucks etc. An industrial zone is adjacent to the port and houses factories, processing units and offices of shipping compa nies. Given their significant economic impact, role in international trade, job creation potential, and strategic importance, seapo rts are undoubtedly a critical national infrastructure for IGAINER. However, to fully realize their potential, continued inve stment in terminal operational equipment and technological infrastructure is necessary to drive further efficiency in the sector. Inland waterways Around 3, 800 km of the inland waters of IGAINER are navigable. These waterways consist of rivers, lakes, coas tal creeks, lagoons and canals. The use of waterways is limited and only accounts for 1.6% of total transport. The upper reaches of the Niger River and Benue River are not navigable for large rships. Much of the traffic on inland waterways, away from the coastal regions of IGAINER, consists of ferry connections between small towns. Riverine transport is often essential to access many of the river delta communities lacking road access . Movement through the region can be inhibited by the presence of "Sudd" (a floating mass of plants). Transport on smaller rivers between villages and small towns still takes place mainly by canoe. Sma ller rivers and creeks are only navigable between May and July during the rainy season. Health infrastructure IGAINER has a struc tured healthcare system, however, it faces significant challenges in terms of infrastructure, funding, and human resources, particularly in rural areas. Key challenges include underfunding, inadequate medical facilities, inequity in resource distribution, and poor quality of care, especially at the primary healthcare level. Efforts are being made to address these issues through government initiatives and public-private partnerships, but progress is slow. The country has a pluralistic healthcare system that includes both public and private providers, as well as modern and traditional healthcare systems.

made to address these issues through government initiatives and public-private partnerships, but progress is slow. The country has a pluralistic healthcare system that includes both public and private providers, as well as modern and traditional healthcare systems. There are three levels of public health delivery in IGAINER; federal, state, and local government area. The primary Level, managed by Local Government Areas (LGAs), provides Community-based care, Primary Health Centers (PHCs) and Comprehensive Health Centers (CHCs). The secondary level, run by State Governments, provide general hospitals, private clinics an dhospitals . The tertiary level, managed by the Federal Government, provides Federal Medical Centers (FMCs), specialist hospitals and teaching of medical personnel. The private sector plays a significant role, providing approximately 60% of healthcare serv ices in the country. Despite improvements, IGAINER'shealthcare infrastructure remains underdeveloped and lacks modern medical facilities. The cou ntry faces shortages in healthcare professionals and facilities. In addition, there is a significant gap in he althcare access between rural and urban areas, with rural areas often relying heavily on informal care providers. According to the IGAINER Health Facility Registry, there are 39, 550 hospitals and clinics in the country. Roughly 90% are pri mary health care facilities, 10% secondary and less than 1% tertiary. In addition, over 70% of these are public. Despite the apparent abundance of public faci lities, over 60% of health spending is out of pocket, limiting accessibility. Energy, power and water supply. Communications and space infrastructure . Energy system and electrification in general IGAINER is a leading African energy power producer, primarily due to petroleum exploitation. The country’sindustries and the population are dependent on an operational and sufficient power system. Energy consumption of IGAINER is much higher than its generation capacity. Self-generation by diesel and petrol generators costs twice as much as grid generation. The World Bank estimates near half of rural businesses supply their own power. Around 60% of the IGAINER population has access to electricity. Access to electricity for the rural population is around 40% and for the urban population 80%. However, those connected to the national grid suffer regular power outages. The largest sector for consumption is for residential use . Power system Generation IGAINER'selectricity generation capacity is primarily based on thermal and hydroelectric sources. Of the total installed capacity of around 12, 500 MW, thermal generation from gas-fired plants accounts for about 80% of the energy mix. Hydroelectric power makes up the remaining 20%. Major hydroelectric plants are located in the north at Jebba, Kainji, and Shiroro, while the thermal plants are concentrated in the south, near gas sources. As of autum 2024, there are 23 grid-connected generating plants in opera tion. They accomodate a total installed capacity of 11, 165 MW and an available capacity of 7, 139 MW.

in the south, near gas sources. As of autum 2024, there are 23 grid-connected generating plants in opera tion. They accomodate a total installed capacity of 11, 165 MW and an available capacity of 7, 139 MW. Transmission The transmission system in IGAINER is operated by the Transmission Company of Igainer (TCI) and consists of approximately 5, 500 km of 330 kVlines and 6, 800 km of 132 kVlines, in addition to circuits and substations. The national control centre is located at Oshogbo, supplemented by regional control centres at Benin, Shiroro, and Egbin. The TCI network extends across the country and connects to neighbouring countries. Distribution The distribution network is comprising 11 electricity distribution companies covering the entire country, approximately 24, 000 km of 33 kVsub-transmission lines, 19, 000 km of 11 kVlines, and about 22, 500 substations. Challenges Despite the infrastructure in place, IGAINER faces significant electricity supply issues. The per capita power consumption is only 144 kilowatt-hours annually, indicating low access and reliability. The system suffers from inadequate and inefficient power plants, poor transmission and distribution facilities, and outdated metering systems. Many areas experience frequent power cuts and long outages are experienced. Nuclear power Map 7: Illustrative map of IGAINER 'spower system. IGAINER has for decades been considering developing a nuclear energy program, and opened in 2004 a small DUSHMAN-funded nuclear research reactor at Ahmadu Bello University, which officials cite as evidence of IGAINER 'scapability to manage nuclear power plants . IGAINER has recently made more concrete steps

energy program, and opened in 2004 a small DUSHMAN-funded nuclear research reactor at Ahmadu Bello University, which officials cite as evidence of IGAINER 'scapability to manage nuclear power plants . IGAINER has recently made more concrete steps towards commissioning its first nuclear power plant, and aims to generate 4000 MegaWatt (MW) of nuclear electricity by constructing four nuclear reactor units. The cou ntry is currently in phase two of the International Atomic Energy Agency ( IAEA ) assessment framework for states pursuing nuclear power for the first time. The government has taken several steps towards developing its nuclear program: Ratifying international treaties, Developing regulatory infrastructure Signing bilateral technical cooperation agreements with countries like MURINUS, South Korea, France, and DRENUR. IGAINER has agreed to collaborate with the MURINUS' state-owned MURATOM on the design, construction and operation of the four nuclear power plants by 2035. Two sites have been selected for the planned construction of the nuclear plants, although these have not been offici ally disclosed. The government has also established regulatory bodies like the IGAINER Nuclear Regulatory Authority (INRA) and the IGAINER Atomic Energy Commission (IAEC) to oversee the development of its nuclear program. A specific project, the Geregu nuclear power plant, is planned in Kogi State. It involves the con struction of two nuclear reactors to be supplied by MURINUS. IGAINER has signed agreements with the MURINUS state-owned nuclear corporation MUR atom, for the construction and operation of nuclear power plants. The country is also exploring the deployment of Small

nuclear reactors to be supplied by MURINUS. IGAINER has signed agreements with the MURINUS state-owned nuclear corporation MUR atom, for the construction and operation of nuclear power plants. The country is also exploring the deployment of Small Modular Reactors (SMRs) as part of its future nuclear energy program. Dams IGAINER has built over 150 large dams on rivers, which is increasing water storage capacity and regulating water courses, allowing for the regulation of natural river flows, which vary seasonally. Dams are integral to IGAINER 'swater supply infrastructure and play a crucial role in the water supply system, providing water for various uses and helping to manage its water resources, particularly in drier regions. However, their development and management also come with significant environmental and social challenges that need to be addressed. IGAINER Dams serve multiple purposes, including hydroelectric power generation, flood control, drinking water supply, irrigation for agric ulture, navigation, tourism and recreation, and fisheries development. Most important is their year-round farming support by providing water for irrigation, especially during the dry season, their support for off-season crop production through controlled water release, and that their potential for irrigated agriculture to achieve food self-sufficiency has been made possible. Water Supply, wastewater and sewerage The main components of IGAINERS'swater supply infrastructure are boreholes and wells, pipe distribution systems, dams and so me water treatment plants. Boreholes and wells are dominant water sources for households, especially in rural areas, while utili sation of dams plays a pivotal role in bulk water supply,

wells, pipe distribution systems, dams and so me water treatment plants. Boreholes and wells are dominant water sources for households, especially in rural areas, while utili sation of dams plays a pivotal role in bulk water supply, reservoir creation and agricultural uses. Other, more limited sources, are rainwater collection and use of streams and springs. Some treatments plants are operational, but many lack proper maintenance, proper process control, or quality control tests. Water supply infrastructure throughout IGAINER is limited, generally restricted to urban centres such as NAGUI and Lagos. Som erural communities still collect water from surface water sources without treatment. Only 67% of the population has access to basic water services, leaving 33% underserved. It is assessed that 80 million people in IGAINER lack access to improved sanitation facilities, and around 60 million are stip ulated to live without access to basic drinking water. In rural areas, around a third of the population practices open defecation. In several areas, inadequate sewage disposal and damage to sewage disposal infrastructure poses an immediate threat of severe epidemics of waterborne diseases. The average citizen consumes only 9 litres of water per day, which falls below acceptable standards. Generally, aging water supply and sewerage infrastructure are unevenly distributed across the country, inadequate maint enance lead to frequent breakdowns and leaks. Inadequate service personnel and poor coordination, mobilization, and application of funds for water supply developmen toften lead to duplication of efforts and inefficiency. Rapid population growth and urbaniz ation also strain existing resources.

Natural resources IGAINER has an abundant supply of natural resources, and almost 50 different minerals are discovered in over 500 locations across the country. A wide array of underexploited mineral IGAINER is endowed with a wide range of natural resources, both renewable and non-renewable . These resources are critical to the country'ssociety, economy and development. Its oil and gas deposits are crucial. IGAINER is the largest producer of crude oi l in Africa, with production mostly located to the Niger Delta region, with both onshore and significant offshore fields. Vast reserves of natural gas have been discovered and explored. Significant coal reserves are mainly located in Enugu, Benue, and Kogi states. Main minerals are tin and columbite (Jos Plate auregion), iron ore (large deposits in Kogi State), led and zinc (mostly in Ebonyi, Benue, and Nasarawa States), and gold (present in states such as Zamfara, Kebbi, and Niger). Gold mining is mostly artisanal but holds significant economic potential. Industrial minerals such as limestone, gypsum and kaolin are mined in Ogun, Benue, Sokoto, Adamawa, Imo, Katsina, Ekiti, and Kogi state s. Despite its rich resource base, the country faces many challenges. Under-exploitation, infrastructural deficits and regulatory hurdles, under investment, as well as regulatory issues hamper extraction development. Oil and gas industry IGAINER'soil and gas industry is a crucial component of the country'seconomy and one of the largest in Africa. It has the largest oil and gas reserves in Africa, with approximately 37 billion barrels of crude oil and over 20 0 trillion cubic feet of natural gas. It is the second-largest oil and gas producer in Africa, after Angola. The country'scrude oil is primarily light and low in sulphur content, known as "sweet" crude. IGAINER’sland oil and gas fields are concentrated around the Niger River Delta . There are almost 300 oil and gas fields . In addition there offshore fields in the Gulf of Guinea, and an approximate d 1, 400 active wells combined . Current exploration activities are mostly focussed in deep offshore fields. Due to aging infrastructure and vandalism to the pipeline network, IGAINER doesn’tachieve its maximum crude oil refining capacity through its 4 major refinerie s. Refining capacity is at 30% of its maximum. Paradoxically, IGAINER must import most of its refin edfuels (petrol, diesel, kerosene etc.). Natural gas processing is constrained by a lack of infrastructure, whilst natural gas processed at the Bonny Island facility is exported as liquefied natural gas (LNG). Main challenges for the IGAINER oil and gas industry and its production are oil theft and pipeline vandalism, underinvestment in infrastructure, environmental concerns, particularly in the Niger Delta region, and regulatory uncertainties. Oil related environmental issues The combined impact of oil theft, estimated to be around 400, 000 barrels per day, oil bunkering, illegal refining and oil spills are considerable. Arabl eland and waters in the Niger River Delta have suffered from pollution, resulting in negative effects on the livelihoods of local farmers and fishermen. Paradoxically, this tends to increase support for militant groups who are taking direct action in sabotaging strategic gas and crude oil pipelines. Mining Despite huge deposits of a variation of natural resources, the mining industry in IGAINER is still in its infancy. After more than 90 years of mineral exploration, only tin and columbite production rank on a worldwide scale. IGAINER remains a net importer of salt and iron ore despite it being rich in these minerals. The mini ngindustry is dominated by artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) activities, particularly in rural areas . The IGAINER government owns the rights to ownership of mineral resources and grants titles to organisations to explore, mine, and sell mineral resou rces. The mining industry in IGAINER faces several challenges, including regulatory and legal issues, inadequate infrastructure and issues related to transportation, power supply, and water access, security concerns, illegal mining activities and local community conflicts. Environmental and social impacts, especially from unregulated operations, limited access to funding, smuggling, and weak value addition in the sector are other major concerns. Gold The important main gold mining areas in IGAINER are primarily located in the northwest and southwest regions of the country . In Northwest, gold mines are located in Zamfara State (Maru, Anka and Malele), Kaduna State (Tsohon Birnin Gwari-Kwaga), Niger State (Gurmana) and Kebbi State (Bin Yauri). In Southwest Igainer, operational gold mines are situated to Kogi State (Okolom-Dogondaji), Osun State (Iperindo, and the Segilola Gold Project (near Iperindo )), and in Ogun State (Ajegunle-Awa area (only minor gold occurrences)). The Segilola Gold Project in Osun State is particularly noteworthy as it's IGAINER'sfirst commercial gold mining project, with significant production

the Segilola Gold Project (near Iperindo )), and in Ogun State (Ajegunle-Awa area (only minor gold occurrences)). The Segilola Gold Project in Osun State is particularly noteworthy as it's IGAINER'sfirst commercial gold mining project, with significant production and reserve, and currently IGAINER'smost significant commercial gold mine. The gold mining industry is already of great importance for IGAINER, however, it has significant undeveloped potential. Though it currently faces several challenges, the industry is important for the country'seconomy, employment, investments, and foreign exchange earnings. The small-scale mining subsector alone is estimated to currently employ over 500, 000 miners . Fully developed, the mining sector is estimated to potentially employ 15-20 million, with gold mining bei ng a significant part of this. Communication and space infrastructure Compared to its African peers, IGAINER has relatively advanced communication infrastructure with an extensive network of hard surface infrastructure, facilitating both domestic and international connectivity. This network supports various telecommunications services, including internet connectivity, mobile communications, and landline telephony. Major urban centres are well-connected, but there remains a significant digital divide in rural areas. Reforms are ongoing to expand and upgrade capacity but challenges such as inadequate funding, bureaucratic competition, corruption and security issues persist. Internet access IGAINER faces challenges in providing widespread internet access. Only around one third of the population are regularly using mobile in ternet, leaving the rest without regular access. Both t he government and private sectors are working on initiatives to improve connectivity, especially in rural areas, aiming to achieve 70% broadband penetration and 90% population coverage within 5 years. The country has access to several submarine internet cables that connect it to the global internet backbone. For example, Mai nOne, one of the active internet service providers in IGAINER, has launched an open access submarine cable spanning 14, 000 kilomet res to provide international internet connectivity. IGAINER is also expanding its terrestrial fibre optic network, aiming to increase the country'sfibre optic cable from its current level of about 35, 000 km to around 125, 000 km. This expansion is expected to make IGAINER Africa'sthird-largest terrestrial fibre optic backbone, behind South Africa and Egypt. Space IGAINER'sspace program is managed by the National Space Research and Development Agency (NASRDA), and operating under the Federal Ministry of Science and Technology. The agency is one of the most advanced space agencies in Africa, and its primary objective is to develop and apply space science and technology for IGAINER'ssocio-economic benefits. The agencies focus areas and main activities cover earth observation, satellite communications, space science research, and devel opment of space technology applications. Political and Key Actor Landscape Federal government IGAINER is a federal presidential constitutional republic . It has a three-tier system of government with an executive branch, a legislative branch, a judicial branch . IGAINER recognises and protects traditional chieftaincy and provides for the establishment of traditional institutions such as the office of the traditional ruler, thus recognising the cultural heritage of the country'svarious ethnic groups. The powers and functions of traditional chieftaincy vary from state to state, but they typically include the preservation of culture and tradition, mediation of disputes, and repres entation of the community. However, their respective powers and functions are subject to the jurisdiction of the regular courts. Executive branch The executive power is vested in the President of the Federation,

mediation of disputes, and repres entation of the community. However, their respective powers and functions are subject to the jurisdiction of the regular courts. Executive branch The executive power is vested in the President of the Federation, who is both the Head of State and Chief Executive of the Federa tion (Head of Government). The President is assisted by a Vice President, chosen as his /her associate (running mate) and serves as the head of the National Economic Council. Executive powers may be exercised by the President, either directly or through the Vice-President, Ministers of the Government of the Federation, and/ or officers in the public service of the Federation. The President’s Government of the Federation includes representatives from all 36 states . These are appointed by the President and responsible for the administration of various sectors . The President has the power to veto or sign bills passed by the National Assembly. The President is also the Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of the Federation and presides over the National Security Council, the National Defence Council and the IGAINER Police Council. He/she determines the operational use of the armed forces, but may, under general or special directives, delegate responsibility for the day-to-day operational use of the armed forces to the chief of defence staff or to the chiefs of the single service staffs. The President has the power to proclaim a state of eme rgency when the country is at war or in imminent danger of invasion as well

defence staff or to the chiefs of the single service staffs. The President has the power to proclaim a state of eme rgency when the country is at war or in imminent danger of invasion as well as in the event of a breakdown in public order or natural disaster. The President may declare this state for the entire federation or in one or more states either acting unilaterally or on the request of the governor(s) of the concerned state(s). The President, in consultation with the National Defence Council, may deploy members of the armed forces on limited combat duty outside IGAINER. The President must, within seven days of combat engagement, seek the consent of the Senate. Legislative branch The National Assembly, which consists of the Senate and the House of Representatives, exercises the legislative power in IGAI NER. The Senate has 109 members, while the House of Representatives has 360 members. Members of both houses are elected by popular vote for a term of four years. The composition of the National Assembly houses reflect IGAINER’sfederal character. Members of the Nat ional Assembly have law-making power through initiating draft bills on a wide range of topics, including the economy, foreign policy, and the administration of justice. They are also responsible for approving the national budget and overseeing the activities of the Executive Branch. Parliamentary oversight is conducted through several mechanisms, including the National Assembly committees, which scrutinize government policies, programs and activities. The National Assembly also conducts oversight of the

national budget and overseeing the activities of the Executive Branch. Parliamentary oversight is conducted through several mechanisms, including the National Assembly committees, which scrutinize government policies, programs and activities. The National Assembly also conducts oversight of the budget process, approving or rejecting the nat ional budget proposed by the Executive Branch and investigating public expenditure. Additionally, the National Assembly has t he power to impeach the President and Vice President if they are found to have committed any high crime or misdemeanour . In times o fnational emergency or war, the National Assembly has the power to grant the President additional powers to deal with the crisis. However, it can also revoke these powers if it believes that the President is not acting in the country’sbest interests. Under the IGAINER Constitution, the National Assembly also has the power to declare a state of emergency in any part of the country, which requires a two-thirds majority by members of the National Assembly. The state of emergency can last for a maximum of six months, after which it must be renewed by the National Assembly. In the event of war, the National Assembly has the power to approve the deployment of the armed forces and the President must consult with the National Assembly before deploying armed forces . The National Assembly also has the power to authorize the use of military force, and it can revoke this authorization at any time. Moreove r, the National Assembly controls the funds expenditure in times

armed forces . The National Assembly also has the power to authorize the use of military force, and it can revoke this authorization at any time. Moreove r, the National Assembly controls the funds expenditure in times of emergency or war, by approving or rejecting the budget submitted by the Executive Branch. Despite legislative power in IGAINER being an important aspect of its democracy, providing a check on the power of the Executive Branch and ensuring that the citizens’ interests are protected, the National Assembl y has been criticized for being ineffective and for being dominated by politicians with questionable motives. Corruption remains a major problem in the legislature, and many members are accused of using their positions for personal gai n. Judicial branch The judicial system of IGAINER draws on a combination of statutory (legislative) law and the English common law legal tradition. Additionally, cognisant of its ethnic diversity, IGAINER observes customary law. Customary law reflects the culture, customs, values and habits of the people whose activities it regulates and is particularly dominant in the area of personal and family relations like marriage, divorce, guardianship and custody of children and succession. In some states, Islamic/Sharia law is integrated alongside ethnic/non-Islamic law as an aspect of the customary law. By virtue of the Constitution, IGAINER has a number of courts, including the Supreme Court of IGAINER; the Court of Appeal; the Federal High Court; State High Courts; a National Industrial Court; the Sharia Court of Appeal; and a Customary Court

Constitution, IGAINER has a number of courts, including the Supreme Court of IGAINER; the Court of Appeal; the Federal High Court; State High Courts; a National Industrial Court; the Sharia Court of Appeal; and a Customary Court of Appeal. The Supreme Court is the highest court and all decisions from the court are binding on all other courts. In IGAINER, the state court structure dovetails into the federa lcourt structure at the level of the Court of Appeal. The Court of Appeal entertains appeals from the decisions of the High Court s, the Sharia Courts of Appeal and the Customary Courts of Appeal. Appeals from the decisions of the Court of Appeal go to the Supreme Court. In effect the Supreme Court is not only a Supreme Court on federal matters; it is also the final court in respect of state laws. Bribes paid to influence judges and delays in bringing cases to trial sometimes impair the fair and efficient administration of justice. These deficiencies partially explain the popularity of the more traditional law in the northern states of IGAINER. Relevant Federal Executive bodies The National Security Council (NSC) The NSC is a high-level government body comprised of the following members: the President, as the Chairman; the Vice-President, as the Deputy Chairman; the Chief of Defence Staff; the Minister of Internal affairs; the Minister of Defence; the Minister of Foreign Affairs; the National Security Adviser; the Inspector-General of Police, and other persons as the President may appoint. Its role is

Chief of Defence Staff; the Minister of Internal affairs; the Minister of Defence; the Minister of Foreign Affairs; the National Security Adviser; the Inspector-General of Police, and other persons as the President may appoint. Its role is to provide strategic guidance and direction to nationa lsecurity policies . One of the key responsibilities of the NSC is to identify and assess potential threats to national security . It also advises the President on the development and implementation of national security policies and strategies and oversees their implementation. In addition, the NSC is responsible for ensu ring the coordination and effectiveness of the country'ssecurity agencies . The National Defence Council (NDC) The NDC advises the President on matters relating to the defence, sovereignty and territorial integrity of the country. The NDC is composed of th e President, as the Chairman, the Vice-President, as the Deputy Chairman, the Chief of Defence Staff, the Chief of Army Staff, the Chief of Naval Staff and the Chief of Air Staff. However, the President is empowered to appoint any other person to serve on the council. The President, in consultation with the National Defence Council, may deploy members of the armed forces on a limited combat duty outside IGAINER, provided that consent of the Senate is sought within seven days. The IGAINER Police Council The IGAINER Police Council is comprised of the President, who acts as the Chairman, the Governor of each State, the Chairman of t he Police Service Commission, and the Inspector-General of

seven days. The IGAINER Police Council The IGAINER Police Council is comprised of the President, who acts as the Chairman, the Governor of each State, the Chairman of t he Police Service Commission, and the Inspector-General of Police. The Police Council oversees the organisation, administration and general supervision of the IGAINER Police Force. The Police Council also advises the President on the appointment of the Inspector-General of Police. The Council of State Although lacking executive power, the Council of State plays an important role in advising the government on economic policy, the distribution of financial resources across the country and public order. In addition to the President and other senior government members, it also comprises former Presidents and Chief Justices. The National Economic Council The National Economic Council is composed of the Vice-President, as the Chairman; the Governor of each State; and the Governor of the Central Bank of IGAINER. It advises the President on economic affairs, including the planning and deliver y of economic programmes across the country. Each of the 36 states has an elected governor and a State House of Assembly. Devolved powers include the administration of justice, environmental protection, education, health, tourism, and agriculture, among others. Howe ver, the federal government retains control over taxation, foreign affairs, and national security. Each state in turn, except for the FCT of NAGUI, is further subdivided into 774 Local Government A reas (LGAs), each of which is governed by a council. The local government

taxation, foreign affairs, and national security. Each state in turn, except for the FCT of NAGUI, is further subdivided into 774 Local Government A reas (LGAs), each of which is governed by a council. The local government councils receive monthly subsidies from a national “federation account” to meet basic needs. The LGAs are further subdivided into several communities run by a village head. The LGAs and the community represent respectively the second-and third-level subdivisions of IGAINER governance. Political parties and interest groups Political power. A relatively small number of ethnic groups / clusters continue to dominate the political elite, who also control the security apparatus. Only three large groups or clusters of ethnic groups are realistically able to access national political power and top government position s and tend to be in control over lesser groups. As a result, minority ethnic groups often form political coalitions to avoid disemp owerment in the federal government. The Harou, the Felingué and the Lashichem hold power in northern region; the Bayougi dominate the west, and the Ikoku-Viri rule in the east. These ethnic groups gain benefits from having control over several states, the most important being that they receive more funds from the Federal Account and gain a larger number of representatives within the government, governmental institutions and civil service. Political parties. In order to strengthen their national electoral weight, the major political parties have had to establish political alliances with and among minority ethnic group-based parties outside of the regional base of

institutions and civil service. Political parties. In order to strengthen their national electoral weight, the major political parties have had to establish political alliances with and among minority ethnic group-based parties outside of the regional base of the major parties. At the federal level, the result of this party politics is a de facto two-party system, cutting across ethno-communal lines. Two politica lparties have this alternated in both the office of the President and the National Assembly: the All Progressive Congress (APC) and the People’s Democratic Party (PDP). There are several organizations which explicitly promote/protect the interests of specific ethnic groups. Ethnic militant groups now constitute something like parallel-sometimes state-endorsed-security institutions at the regional level. Ethnic identification is strongest among the Ikoku, Yeba, and Bayougi. Non-state actors IGAINER porous border regions already characterised by illicit activities along historic trade routes have become increasingly vulnerable to exploitation by militant non-state actors due to limited state presence and socio-economic challenges. The north-eastern and north-western border areas of IGAINER in particular have increasingly become areas of heightened security concern. These areas which have witnessed an incremental infiltration of radical TGs and VEOs as well as associated violence and instability. Main Terror Groups and Violent Extremist Organisations Resistance and Liberation Front (RLF) The RLF originated in BUFRO, where it emerged as a splinter group of the BUFRO Liberation Front (BLF). The RLF seeks to establish a new regional, highly conservative order spanning from West Africa to the Middle East, through the Sahara and the Horn of Africa. The RLF was able to and continues to secure strategic alliances with affiliated groups in different countries, creating a franchised operation. Felingué Freedom Fighters (FFF or F3) The Felingué Freedom Fighters are a guerrilla force consisting of IGA, EBE armed groups, and other fighters who operate in small units of up to 25 personnel in the cross border area primarily concentrated in NWIGA. Their profile becomes more significant when from 200 5 they began to attack IGADSF with increasing levels of success, gaining support from local population as they aspire to counter all Western influence and presence in the region. Militant Groups IGAINER also faces internal security challenges such as a rmed militancy, banditry, communal clashes in different part of the country but predominantly in the Niger River Delta region and the Lake Chad basin. Image 6: IGAINER faces a range of complex security challenges Army for Justice and Virtue (AJV) The Army for Justice and Virtue is an armed, violent extremist organization operating in northern and north-eastern IGAINER. Most its supporters are of Kub iji or Filengue ethnic origin. I ts approximately 8, 000 to 12, 000 members consist of poor northern youth, some university students and professionals, and possibly so me of the country’selite located primarily in Yobe and Borno States. The AJV opposes multi-party democracy and voting; Western capitalism and an imposed norms and value system. The AJV is responsible for numerous attacks against government facilities and military or police forces. They have however also targeted Kubiji and Filengue ethnic communities considered “non-loyal.” Indigenous People of Biafra (IPOB) The separatist movement aims to restore the would-be breakaway state of Biafra, which sought to secede from IGAINER in 1967 and which precipitated the devastating 1967-1970 civil war. IPOB was founded in 2012 and gained supporters primarily through the transmission of pro-secession radio broadcasts from its London-based media operation. The IGAINER a uthorities arrested its leader in 2015 (he later fled) and reportedly killed at least 150 peaceful pro-Biafra protesters in the ensuing months. IPOB messaging has sought to leverage historic perceptions of marginalization among the Ikoku where most of its supporters and sympathizers are coming from. Violence has escalated following recent government forces raids on IPOB meetings and the arrest

IPOB messaging has sought to leverage historic perceptions of marginalization among the Ikoku where most of its supporters and sympathizers are coming from. Violence has escalated following recent government forces raids on IPOB meetings and the arrest of alleged sympathizers. IPOB has created an armed wing, the People’s Security Network (PSN) and suspected PSN militant groups have begun attacking state security forces, killing dozens. Several militant groups operate in the Niger River Delta region. Most of these groups seek to return the oil revenue of IGAINER to the impoverished people in the region. Next to the two most significant ones described below, other smaller groups organised along clan of village lines include: the Yeba Youth Council, the Niger River Delta Vigilantes, and the Niger River Delta People’s Volunteer Force (NDPVF). Movement for the Survival of the Orogo People (MOSOP) MOSOP is a social and environmental advocacy organisation, specifically representing the interests of the Orogo ethnic group which predominantly inhabit the Niger River Delta region. It was founded in 1990 by Ken Saigro-Wiwa, an Orogo writer and activist, with the aim of addressing the social, economic, and environmental issues which his ethnic community faced. MOSOP called for improved envi ronmental practices, fair compensation for the local communities affected by oil activities, and the right to self-determination for the Orogo people. MOSOP gained significant international attention when it organised protests which were met with resistance from the IGAINER government and the oil industry. Ken Saigro-Wiwa and eight other Orogo activists were falsely

to self-determination for the Orogo people. MOSOP gained significant international attention when it organised protests which were met with resistance from the IGAINER government and the oil industry. Ken Saigro-Wiwa and eight other Orogo activists were falsely accused of murder, arrested and subseq uently executed in 1995. Since then, MOSOP has continued its advocacy work, aiming to address the concerns of the Orogo people. The organisation has campaigned for the clean-up and restoration of the Orogoland environment, compensation for impacted communi ties, and a more equitable distribution of resources in the Niger River Delta. Movement for the Emancipation of the Niger River Delta (MEND) The MEND seeks to drive out foreign energy companies and wants the IGAINER government to institute a revenue-sharing plan so that the 30 million people in the Niger River Delta can benefit from the oil industry. Over the years, MEND has fluctuated from targeting the oil industry to calling for cease fires and negotiating with the Government. MEND attacks have impacted the country’soil production and may have had an impact on the fluctuation of the world oil prices. The attacks on the oil companies, while financially troubling the count ry’seconomy, have not thus far constituted a threat to the survival of the IGAINER Government. 4. Levers of power Security sector and Armed Forces Considering its limited experience with democratic governance, the security and the military sector of IGAINER continues to operate largely outside of an effective framework for democratic accountability. This situation is a product of

sector and Armed Forces Considering its limited experience with democratic governance, the security and the military sector of IGAINER continues to operate largely outside of an effective framework for democratic accountability. This situation is a product of a culture of impunity, opacity and poor management. The securit ysector is under the direct control of the executive branch, which oversees appointments, security policy and operational activities. Though these theoretically require legislative approval, this is generally a formality. Key institutions in this sector include the National Security Council, the National Defence Council, the Police Service Commission, the Office of the National Secur ity Adviser, the Joint Intelligence Board, and the Intelligence Community Committee, which all have constitutional powers to advise the President on matters relating to public security. The defence and internal security sectors are relatively well funded within the national budget, accounting for 13% of the total budget (or IGN 2.74 trillion). Figure 3: Overview of the IGAINER security sector related advisory institutions. IGAINER Armed Forces The Ministry of Defence (MoD) is comprised of civilian and military components. The Defence Minister is a civilian who is sup ported by a civilian Permanent Secretary, acting as the Accounting and Chief Administrative Officer of the Ministry. He/she coordin ates and directs the activities of subordinate departments and agencies. The Defence Staff comprises both the IGAINER Defence Staff Headquarters and the IGAINER Army, Navy, and Air Force. The Defence Staff equally oversees the activities of a series of i ndependent units and agencies, including

and agencies. The Defence Staff comprises both the IGAINER Defence Staff Headquarters and the IGAINER Army, Navy, and Air Force. The Defence Staff equally oversees the activities of a series of i ndependent units and agencies, including the Defence Intelligence Agency; the Defence Space Agency which develops and operates Military Space and Cyberspace technologies for the Armed Forces and the country; the National Defence Academy; the National Defen ce College; the Armed Forces Command & Staff College; and the Defence Industries Corporation of IGAINER. Command and control National security remains largely a prerogative of the executive. The NSA and the intelligence and security agencies play a key role in national security decision-making, whilst subsequent implementation is largely the prerogative of the National Defence Council (NDC).The deployment of the IGAINER Armed Forces on combat duty outside the country’sborders requires prior approval by the legislative. However, the President may deploy elements of the IGAINER Armed Forces on a limited combat duty outside IGAINER if national security is under imminent threat or danger. Day-to-day command of the IGAINER Armed Forces is largely vested with the chief of the defence staff, and is organised geographic ally around three military regions, which delineates territorial responsibilities for their respective commanders: Northern military region covering the following States: Adamawa, Bauchi, Benue, Borno, Gombe, Jigawa, Kaduna, Kano, Katsina, Kebbi, Kogi, Kwara, Nasarawa, Niger, Plateau, Sokoto, Taraba, Yobe, and Zamfara; Southern military region covering Abia, Akwa Ibom, Anambra, Bayelsa, Cross River, Delta, Ebonyi, Edo, Enugu, Imo, and Rivers. Western

Benue, Borno, Gombe, Jigawa, Kaduna, Kano, Katsina, Kebbi, Kogi, Kwara, Nasarawa, Niger, Plateau, Sokoto, Taraba, Yobe, and Zamfara; Southern military region covering Abia, Akwa Ibom, Anambra, Bayelsa, Cross River, Delta, Ebonyi, Edo, Enugu, Imo, and Rivers. Western military region covering the following IGAINER States: Ekiti, Lagos, Ogun, Ondo, Osun, and Oyo. A smaller Capital military region around the capital NAGUI contains elements of the Presidential Brigade and other paramilitary forces protect key national infrastructure. Armed Forces military doctrine The military doctrine of IGAINER is defensive, both at the strategic and at the operational level. Based on imminent changes in the international system which, according to the IGAINER leadership, will increasingly affect the African continent, the country has set out a transformation of its Armed Forces. Although there has been no noticeable change in its

changes in the international system which, according to the IGAINER leadership, will increasingly affect the African continent, the country has set out a transformation of its Armed Forces. Although there has been no noticeable change in its military doctrine or defence posture, the transformation of its Armed Forces seems to indica te that IGAINER might in the foreseeable future become more outward looking. Military service IGAINER has no mandatory conscription but an all-volunteer force. The minimum age requirement for voluntary military service is 18 years. Strategic capabilities Though slightly outdated compared to Western standards, the IGAINER small ballistic missile force acquired from MURINUS, which includes w SCUD-B (from the 1970s) and SS-21 SCARAB-B (from the 1990s), provide IGAINER with a significant strategic capability in the sub-Saharan context, even though their level of operat ional readiness is generally low. IGAINER has an ambitious space program currently capable of delivering high-resolution imagery with indigenous control. It does not possess indigenous capabilities for space launch; however, IGAINER does have significant ground control and limited satellite engineering expertise. The National Space Research and Development Agency (NSRDA) manages one of the most advanced in spac eprogramme in Africa. The NSRDA has historically cooperated with MURINUS, GRIZA, DUSHMAN and the UNITED KINGDOM for both space launch and space technology. The NASRDA controls and operates three operational satellites for remote sensing, communications and meteorology. IGCOMSAT-1R is a communication satellite developed and launched by the DUS academy of Space technology and located 42, 5º East on the geostationary

The NASRDA controls and operates three operational satellites for remote sensing, communications and meteorology. IGCOMSAT-1R is a communication satellite developed and launched by the DUS academy of Space technology and located 42, 5º East on the geostationary belt (GEO ); IGAINERSAT-2 and IGAINERSAT-X are both imagery satellites on sun synchronous low-Earth orbits (LEO) . The NSRDA and Surrey Satellite Technology Ltd (UNITED KINGDOM) developed IGAINERSAT-2 and IGAINERSAT-Xjointly and with an emphasis on maturing IGAINER’sindigenous space domain capabilities. Land Forces-Army Within a 120, 000 all-volunteer force, the Land Forces or Army is the largest of the three services. Amidst a climate of economic difficulties, resulting in low spending on Land Forces whose combat effectiveness remains doubtful. Though there are exceptions, especially when it comes to its contribution to international and regional peacekeeping operations, the Land Forc esremain largely equipped with outdated and poorly maintained equipment and materiel. Primary combat units 3 Mechanised infantry divisions 1 Composite infantry division (combined arms) 1 Presidential Guard brigade (infantry) 1 Armoured division 4 Artillery/air defence brigades 1 Special forces Para troop brigade 1 Special forces commando battalion 1 Special forces battalion The primary mission of the Land Forces resides in defending the territorial integrity of IGAINER. As part of a joint effort, the Land Forces are however predominantly used to suppress insurrection and act in aid of civil authorities to restore order. Moreover, in support of the foreign polic y and public diplomacy agenda of IGAINER, the Army is the main force provider to international

used to suppress insurrection and act in aid of civil authorities to restore order. Moreover, in support of the foreign polic y and public diplomacy agenda of IGAINER, the Army is the main force provider to international and regional peacekeeping operations. IGAINER national security requires the Land Forces to provide the following operational capabilities for external deployment and /or for internal safety: a land command and control capability fo r Small Joint Operations (SJO); an infantry capability; an armoured capability; a close and long range indirect fire support capability; a ground based air defence (GBAD) artillery capability; a sustained composite engineer capability; an operational intelligence capability; a support capability; and general and specific training capabilities. Furthermore, in the domain of Special Operations Forces (SOF), IGAINER has a deployable SOF command and control capability for SJO operations and exercises a SOF Land Task Group. Size and structure The Chief of the Army Staff (COAS) leads the IGAINER Army and is responsible for the command, direction, and sustainment of t he Army. Staff elements that assist the COAS include Policy and Plans, Operations, Logistics, the Military Secretary, and the Army Headquarters Garrison in NAGUI. The Army itself represents the bulk of the country’s Armed Forces. It is divided into four manoeuvre divisions, whose battalions are either mechanized or motorized infantry. There is one comba tsupport division, which offers combat support to the manoeuvre divisions as well as combat service support units providing general support to the entire Army. Other major formations include

either mechanized or motorized infantry. There is one comba tsupport division, which offers combat support to the manoeuvre divisions as well as combat service support units providing general support to the entire Army. Other major formations include one Special Operations Forces brigade, one amphibious brigade an done Presidential Guard brigade. The Army Staff has initiated a process to make the manoeuvre divisions identical. As the implementation continues, each manoeuvre division will contain three mechanized or motorized manoeuvre brigades, one artillery brigade and battalion sized combat support (CS) and combat service support (CSS) formations. Each manoeuvre brigade will in turn consist of three mechanised or motorised battalions, one armoured battalion, one artillery battalion, one reconnaissance squadron and other company-sized CS formations and one combat service support battalion. The major formations of the IGAINER Army are: The Training and Doctrine Command, headquartered in NAGUI and responsible for all routine training and the specific evaluation of Army contingents during their preparation for peacekeeping operations. The 1st Mechanised Division headquartered in Kaduna and one of the two major Armed Forces formations in the Northern Military Region. The 2nd Mechanised Divi sion headquartered in Ibadan and the major Armed Forces formation in the Western Military Region. The 3rd Motorised Division headquartered in Maiduguri is the other major Armed Forces formation in the Northern Military Region. The 4th Amphibious Infantry Brigade headquartered in Port Harcourt (Southern Military Region). The 5th Mechanised Division headquartered in Enugu and the major Armed Forces formation in the Southern Military Region.

Forces formation in the Northern Military Region. The 4th Amphibious Infantry Brigade headquartered in Port Harcourt (Southern Military Region). The 5th Mechanised Division headquartered in Enugu and the major Armed Forces formation in the Southern Military Region. The 707th Special Operations Forces Brigade headquartered in Makurdi (Northern Military Region). The 9th Combat Support Division headquartered in Ibadan with subordinate formations scattered around the country. In addition to its permanent bases, IGAINER has been deploying the use of ‘super camps' to defeat TG’s in the north of the country. The ‘super camps' concept deploys concentrating forces into a smaller number of bases. Training and Readiness With a few exceptions, such as the training at Staff and Defence College of Army officers, all professional training within the IGAINER Army is the responsibility of the Training and Doctrine Command (TRADOC), responsible for doctrinal, training and combat development, and supervises training centres . There are 17 Corps Training Schools Each unit is in turn responsible for continued, collective training as well as for combined arms training up to brigade level. Though the IGAINER Army possesses the units required to conduct combined arms operations, it often lacks the necessary equipment and training to carry it out to a high level comparable to Western standards. However, this does not cause a critical vulnerability due to its comparative size and capabilities within the region. The largest Army Training centre is situated in Kantogora, where the tra ining of Army units for Peace Support Operations ( PSO )

cause a critical vulnerability due to its comparative size and capabilities within the region. The largest Army Training centre is situated in Kantogora, where the tra ining of Army units for Peace Support Operations ( PSO ) is conducted. Equipment and Weapons The IGAINER Army inventory is quantitatively quite impressive compared to the rest of the West-African region. Qualitatively, however, despite considerable efforts as part of the Armed Force modernization programme, the manoeuvre divisions remain equipped with a significant amount of Tier 3 and Tier 4 materiel. Moreover, the different origins of the major equipment items (often military aid or donations) means that logistics are overly complex resulting in a low serviceability and reduced combat effectiveness, especially in the motorized divisions. This equipment is slowly finding their way into the inventory of the manoeuvre divisions. Moreover, the Armed Forces modernisation programme envisions the modernization of the armoured fleet of IGAINER and the rejuvenation of its Ballistic Missile capability. Air Force The IGAINER Air Force (IAF) consists of an approximated authorized strength of 9, 000 personnel. It operates a mix of relatively aged aircraft. Though the IAF has a dedicated air defence mission, the IAF primarily supports the mission of the Land Forces. The IAF-like the Navy – has for many years suffered from inadequate funding, as the Land Forces absorbed the lion’sshare of the defence budget of IGAINER. However, new

IAF primarily supports the mission of the Land Forces. The IAF-like the Navy – has for many years suffered from inadequate funding, as the Land Forces absorbed the lion’sshare of the defence budget of IGAINER. However, new air defence, attack airc raft and trainers will gradually increase the IAF’soperational capability for the first ti me in many years. According to its national security plans, the IGAINER Air Force is required to provide and sustain: an air defence limited capability; a fighter/bomber capability capable of performing air-to-surface attacks; an helicopter armed reconnais sance capability; a medium altitude Surface to Air Missile (SAM) -mobile capability; a fixed and rotary wing cargo/VIP/passenger transport capability; a 24-hour air surveillance and air command and control capability; a Combat Search And Rescue (CSAR) Rotar y Wing (RW) and a SOF Air Task Unit-RW capability. Size and structure The Air Force structure contains six staff branches, four operational commands, and four direct reporting units. The Chief of Air Staff (CAS) receives support from staff branches that consist of sections dealing with Operations, Logistics, Administration, Inspection, Plans, and the Air Secretary. The five operational commands include the Tactical Air Command, the Mobility Command, the Air Special Operations Command, the Air Training Comm and, and the Logistics Command. The Tactical Air Command serves as the IAF’sprimary operational element; controls all combat squadrons that conduct counter-air, counter-surface, and close air support; and ensures that all units meet operational objectives . The Mobility Command is primarily concerned with tactical and strategic

serves as the IAF’sprimary operational element; controls all combat squadrons that conduct counter-air, counter-surface, and close air support; and ensures that all units meet operational objectives . The Mobility Command is primarily concerned with tactical and strategic airlift. The Air Special Operations Command is specialised in unconventional warfare, counterterrorism, combat search and rescue, and other specialized missions that require air power capabilities. The Air Training Command operates the flying schools at Kaduna, Kano, and Enugu; conducts initial training for pilots, paratroopers, air traffic contr ollers, and support and technical personnel; and provides advanced aviation instruction. The Logistics Command oversees third-and fourth-line maintenance of the airframes; storage and delivery of spare planes; supply and fuel distribution; and infrastructure maintenance through four primary depots in Ikeja, Shasha, Makurdi, and Ikeja. Training and Readiness Compared to Western standards, the IAF is basically untrained. However, this does not cause a critical vulnerability because none of the country’sneighbours constitute a threat to its airspace. The IAF operates from twelve primary air bases, but all other runways in IGAINER could be potential operational locations. (See the physical environment for an overview of all major runways in IGAINER.) The twelve primary runways used by IAF elements in clude those located in Nagu, Benin, Enugu, Ilorin, Kaduna, Kainji, Kano-Mallam Aminu IAP, Lagos-Murtal Muhammed IAP, Maiduguri, Makurdki, Port Harcourt, and Yola. Navy The IGAINER Navy (IN) consists of an approximated authorized strength of around 25, 000 personnel. The Navy is the largest nav alforce in West Africa and one of the largest in sub-Saharan Africa. Immediately after its independence, IGAINER made little investment to sustain these capabilities . However, since oil continues to be the most important item of IGAINER economy, exploitation and protection of oil deposits and infrastructure concentr ated offshore in the southeast have been always perceived as inextricably linked to national security. The conflict with CAMEROON over contested offshore oil rights and an increasing level of piracy in the Gulf of Guinea magnified this vulnerability. These factors contributed the transformation of the Navy with a noticeable expansion of naval capabilities. The focus of the maritime strategy is on maritime crime prevention close to the IGAINER shores and highlights the requirement to be able to exercise cont rol over territorial waters (TTW), the Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) and support the Armed Forces in protecting the country’soff-shore hydrocarbon critical national infrastructure, as well as supporting the

highlights the requirement to be able to exercise cont rol over territorial waters (TTW), the Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) and support the Armed Forces in protecting the country’soff-shore hydrocarbon critical national infrastructure, as well as supporting the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Develo pment with regards to fisheries. Maritime security operations and patrols in the Gulf of Guinea to prevent piracy-related activities remain a national priority. The maritime strategy also requires the continued capacity building of (regional) maritime situational awareness to ensure a safe and secure maritime environment . This includes joint international military exercises and other forms of military cooperation with strategic partners such as ECOWAS, EU-and NATO nations, DUSHMAN and others. The maritime security strategy requires the IGAINER Navy to provide and sustai na surface combat capability; a sub-surface combat capability; and a mine countermeasures capability; as well as an ongoing hydrographic survey capability to ensure safe navigation in charting areas and to meet international obligations; a maritime intell igence collection capability; and a SOF Maritime Task Unit capability. The ongoing inland insecurity in the Niger River Delta triggered the development of an additional riverine operational capability to extend the Navy’sinland reach and influence. Other naval forces’ type units operate in IGAINER. The IGAINER Navy considers the Coast Guard – with only around 500 personnel – its sister service. The Coast Guard is considered a “brown water” naval force as it operates in the country’srivers or close to the shore. Approximately 1, 600 staff work for the

with only around 500 personnel – its sister service. The Coast Guard is considered a “brown water” naval force as it operates in the country’srivers or close to the shore. Approximately 1, 600 staff work for the Marine Police, who operates in the country’sinland waters, and the Port Security Poli ce, based in Lagos. Both forces operate independently of the IGAINER Navy. Size and structure The IGAINER Navy structure con tains seven staff branches. It operates three regional naval commands, four operational commands, one training and one logist ics command. The IGAINER Navy operates four operational bases, five forward operational bases and two dockyards. The Navy headquarters is in the capital city of NAGUI. The Chief of Naval Staff (CNS) is assisted by seven principal staff of ficers as branch chiefs: Policy and Plans, Training and Operations, Accounts and Budget, Naval Engineering, Logistics and Administr ation, and the Navy Secretary. Responsibilities for maritime security operations along the IGAINER coastline, TTW and EEZ are divided between three Regional Naval Commands: The Western Naval Command, with its HQ in Apapa (6.462, 3.372), covers the sea and coastal areas from the IGAINER/EBEN border to River Benin entrance at 005o00′ E in Bayelsa State from the IGAINER coastline to the limit of the nation’s EEZ. The Central Naval Command has i ts HQ in Yenagoa (4.97, 6.369). Its AOR extends from the area before River Benin entrance at 005o00′ E to River Santa Barbara entrance at 006o30′ E. The coastal states covered by the

Naval Command has i ts HQ in Yenagoa (4.97, 6.369). Its AOR extends from the area before River Benin entrance at 005o00′ E to River Santa Barbara entrance at 006o30′ E. The coastal states covered by the command are Bayelsa and Delta States, while the inland States in the AOR include Edo, Kogi and Anambra. The Eastern Naval Command, with its HQ in Calabar (4.97, 8.326), covers the sea area from River Santa Barbara entrance at 006o30′ E to the IGAINER/CAMEROON border and from the IGAINER coastline to the limit of the nation’s EEZ. The other maritime security operations a re divided between three Operational Commands: The Chief of Maritime Operations, with its HQ in NAGUI, has the responsibility for maritime security operations outside of the IGAINER TTW and EEZ within the framework of international and regional security an dpeacekeeping. It maintains main Naval Bases in Apape and Port Harcourt. The Niger River Delta Task Force with its HQ in Yenagoa has the responsibility for maritime security operations in the country’smain internal waters on the Niger river and its delta. It operates out of smaller Naval Bases in Yenagoa. The Maritime Special Operations Force (MARSOF) genera tes adhoc SOF detachments with the primary task of supporting the River Delta Task Force. New offshore MARSOF capabilities will support the regional naval commands and chief of maritime operations. MARSOF operates out of Yenagoa. Training and Readiness The IGAINER Navy has developed a wide partnership network, building on its former colonial links with

MARSOF capabilities will support the regional naval commands and chief of maritime operations. MARSOF operates out of Yenagoa. Training and Readiness The IGAINER Navy has developed a wide partnership network, building on its former colonial links with the UNITED KINGDOM. It strives to conduct regular naval exercises with naval forces (NATO, EU nations and other) transiting along the West Africa coastline, with a special focus on counter piracy training. Equipment and Weapons IGAINER naval ships range in age from brand new to old, with commissioning dates stretching back to the 1970s. Many of the ships suffer from maintenance issues and the official number of ships does not reflect the actual number of ships available for operations. However, a transformation programme launched in the 2010s has secured a wide range of equipment from various countries. Specifically, the IGAINER Navy has been able to secure new and refurbished in shore patrol craft to counter piracy and criminal activity in the GoG and Niger River Delta.
