International military relations International military cooperation plays a vital role in strengthening IGAINER’ssecurity apparatus and promoting diplomatic ties with other nations. By actively participating in multinational military exercises, contributing to peacekeeping missions, and engaging in defence cooperation agreements, IGAINER strives to bolster regional and global stability. IGAINER initiated the Multinational Joint Task Force (MNJTF) in order to initially quell trans-border armed banditry around the broader Lake Chad Basin area and facilitate free movement along the IGAINER north-eastern border. As of the 2010s, the MNJTF changed tide with the uprising of the Army of Justice and Virtue. Four Troop Contributing Nations CAMEROON, DACK, IGAINER and REGIN have a Brigade-sized sector operating within its respective country providing a Safe and Secure Environment in the Lake Chad Basin area. EBEN only provides a company-sized formation although not in combat capacity. Key partnerships and collabora tive efforts. In line with its Non-Alignment Movement policy, IGAINER has forged a balanced set of strategic partnerships with different powers. The USA, UNITED KINGDOM (UK), FRANCE, DUSHMAN, MURINUS and African nations are among the country’skey partners. These partnerships involve joint military exercises, intelligence sharing, and capacity-building initia tives, fostering a robust security network. Peacekeeping missions. IGAINER actively contributes

USA, UNITED KINGDOM (UK), FRANCE, DUSHMAN, MURINUS and African nations are among the country’skey partners. These partnerships involve joint military exercises, intelligence sharing, and capacity-building initia tives, fostering a robust security network. Peacekeeping missions. IGAINER actively contributes to UN peacekeeping missio ns, reflecting its commitment to regional and global security. IGAINER troops have been deployed to conflict zones in predominantly sub-Saharan African countries, such as the DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC OF THE CONGO, LIBERIA, and MILA, but also in SOUTH-SUDAN. Defence cooperation agreements. IGAINER has signed defence cooperation agreements with several nations, including the USA, UK, FRANCE, GERMANY, DUSHMAN and MURINUS, among others. These enable mutual assistance in areas such as training (USA, UK, DUSHMAN), equ ipment procurement (USA, UK, GERMANY, DUSHMAN and MURINUS), and intelligence sharing (USA, UK, and FRANCE within the areas of counterterrorism, counterinsurgency, and maritime security). Foreign military presence. Except for the hybrid UN/AU peacekeeping force in north-western IGAINER, there is no permanent or semi-permanent foreign military presence in IGAINER. All bi-or multilateral assistance such as security force assistance by the UK or the USA has been conducted based on ad hoc basis an dtemporary bases. There are however permanent or semi-permanent military bases provided by FRANCE, GERMANY and the USA in all of its neighbouring countries. Military-industrial complex (MIC) Like most countries in the West-African sub-Saharan region, IGAINER largely depends on foreign sources for the procurement of its major weapon systems and has for decades opted for a ninternationally diversified procurement strategy. The Defence Industries Corporation of

most countries in the West-African sub-Saharan region, IGAINER largely depends on foreign sources for the procurement of its major weapon systems and has for decades opted for a ninternationally diversified procurement strategy. The Defence Industries Corporation of IGAINER (DICOI), established in the 1 960s, is an indigenous source of military materiel, particularly small arms and ammunition. However, lack of investment in research and development, as well as inadequate manpower to guarantee competitiveness in the global economy has affected the growth potential of its MIC. Since the early 2000s, DICOI, in technical partnership with Poly-Technologies Incorporated from DUSHMA N, has produced medium range weapons such as 60mm and 81 mmmortars and RPG-7. In the 2010s, IGAINER started the development of locally manufactured Armoure d Personnel Carriers which are gradually fielded in support of COIN efforts, including in the Lake Chad basin area. In the maritime sector, two naval dockyards and one shipya rd have been constructed. Due to a lack of funding, the dockyards focus mainly on ship repair. Major challenges Over the last decades the IGAINER Armed Forces have faced and will continue to face some major challenges. Most of these requ ire a multifaceted approach and foreign military assistance and cooperation. Corruption: When the IGAINER politics and governance were dominated by the military, regular abuse of power took place, including ethnic retaliati on, extortion and illegal tax collection. Instances of corruption within the Armed Forces have been reported, impacting efficiency, public confidence, internal morale9 and discipline. A lack of

military, regular abuse of power took place, including ethnic retaliati on, extortion and illegal tax collection. Instances of corruption within the Armed Forces have been reported, impacting efficiency, public confidence, internal morale9 and discipline. A lack of trust continues to create challenges for the government as well as for the Armed Forces. Progress has however been made to restore proper civil-military relations with civil society and larger segments of the population. Overstr etched Armed Forces: the IGAINER Armed Forces often perform duties more associated with policing than with those of an Army, particularly in conflict-prone localities. Human security shortfalls: IGAINER’s Armed Forces do not hold the same values as their Western counterparts. They do not necessarily abide by the Geneva Conventions in dealing with their own population. The Armed Forces and the IGAINER Police Force have committed human rights a buses and local communities have increasingly made arrangements to provide security for their communities through locally-raised militia or vigilantes. Police and paramilitary forces The Ministry of the Interior controls two subordinate elements that can be used as additional military forces: the IGAINER Police Force (I PF) and the IGAINER Security and Civil Defence Corps (ISCDC). Since there are no local or state police forces, the IGAINER Police Force (IPF) is the primary law enforcement agency to provide police services across the country. The IPF operates in twelve zones and possesses small arms weapons. The IPF has an estimated strength of over 370, 000 personnel. It is structured across the country

enforcement agency to provide police services across the country. The IPF operates in twelve zones and possesses small arms weapons. The IPF has an estimated strength of over 370, 000 personnel. It is structured across the country through a hierarchical organisation comp rising federal headquarters, located in NAGUI; 12 IPF zones headed by a zonal hea dquarters and 36 state commands in each of the 36 states of IGAINER, including the Federal Capital 9 The IGAINER Army has been historically plagued by poor morale and non ‐professionalism, leading to various incidents of insubordination and muti ny. Low morale in the northeast stems from the high reward‐to‐risk ratio of military service in the oil ‐rich REGIN Delta, from where some of the soldiers have been redeployed. Soldiers are said to be lobbying their senior commanders to be sent to the REGIN Delta. Territory (FCT) of NAGUI. Within this hierarchical structure, the IPF also has specialised units and departments, such as the Criminal Investigation Departme nt (CID), Special Anti-Robbery Squad (SARS), Counterterrorism Unit, and Mobile Police Force (MOPOL), which focus on specific policing areas. The IGAINER Security and Civil Defence Corps (ISCDC) is a paramilitary agency established to protect lives, property, and critica lnational infrastructure; to maintain peace; and, to provide civil defence services to citizens. The ISCDC has an estimated streng th of 150, 000 personnel. It is structured across the country through a hierarchical organisation comprising of national headquarters, located in NAGUI; 6 Zonal Commands and 36 State Commands in

citizens. The ISCDC has an estimated streng th of 150, 000 personnel. It is structured across the country through a hierarchical organisation comprising of national headquarters, located in NAGUI; 6 Zonal Commands and 36 State Commands in ea ch of the 36 states of IGAINER, including the Federal Capita l Territory (FCT) of NAGUI. Roles and functions of the ISCDC include: Security functions . The ISCDC plays a crucial role in maintaining national security and civil society. It is responsible for the protection of critical infrastruct ure, such as oil pipeli nes, power stations, and telecommunications facilities. The ISCDC also combats various forms of crime, including vandalism, oil theft, illegal bunkering, and cybercrime. Disaster Management and Response . The ISCDC is actively involved in disaster managemen t and response. It coordinates emergency responses to natural disasters, such as floods, earthquakes, and epidemics, by providing rescue operations, humanitarian aid, and post-disaster rehabilitation support . Peacekeeping and Conflict Resolution . The ISCDC contributes to peacekeeping efforts and conflict resolution within IGAINER. It collaborates with other security agencies to prevent and manage conflicts, mediate disputes, and promote peaceful coexistence. Intelligence and security services The responsibility for intelligence and security at the Federal level is split between: A central organ for coordination, control and supervision. The National Security Advisor (NSA), on behalf of the President thro ugh the National Security Council, the Joint Intelligence Board (JIB) and the Intelligence Community Committee (ICC); Specialist agencies for the collection of intelligence and security services. The State

Security Advisor (NSA), on behalf of the President thro ugh the National Security Council, the Joint Intelligence Board (JIB) and the Intelligence Community Committee (ICC); Specialist agencies for the collection of intelligence and security services. The State Security Service (SSS) collects local intelligence and provides internal security, the National Intelligence Agency (NIA) provides external intelligence and the Defence Intelligence Agency (DIA) for defence intelligence and security both within and outside IGAINER; The National Defence Forces, the Police and para-military organizations with responsi bilities for specific aspects of national security. These are tasked through the respective Ministries, Service Council or Boards and command and control chains. The NSA, member of the NSC, advises the President and directs the NIA and SSS on policies appr oved by the President. Border security forces There is no specific agency solely dedicated to border security. The IGAINER Immigration Service (IIS) and the IGAINER Customs Service (ICS) col laborate to ensure border security and control. The IGAINER Immigration Service (IIS), under the purview of the Ministry of Interior, is responsible for managing and regulating immigration activities, incl uding the control of its borders. The IIS deploys its personnel to border posts, checkpoints, and other strateg iclocations to monitor and manage the movement of people across the borders. It is responsible for enforcing immigration laws, conducting passport and visa checks, and preventing ill egal entry and exit. The IGAINER Customs Service (ICS), under the purview of the Ministry of Finance, is primarily responsible for customs and excise

responsible for enforcing immigration laws, conducting passport and visa checks, and preventing ill egal entry and exit. The IGAINER Customs Service (ICS), under the purview of the Ministry of Finance, is primarily responsible for customs and excise duties, including the enforcement of customs laws and regulations. The ICS plays a vital role in ensuring compliance with customs procedures at the borders, preventing smuggling and illicit trade, and facilitating legitimate trade. Information and cyber Information The IGAINER information environment is vivid and dynamic, reflecting the country’sdiversity and challenges. Each cultural community fosters a multit ude of narratives that shape the diverse identity of IGAINER. Traditional and modern communication channels result in a blend of oral tradition s, local, government-sponsored and private radio and television stations, newspapers and outlets, and a burgeoning social media lan dscape. IGAINER boasts a dynamic media landscape, where both state-controlled and private media coexist. Traditional media, such as radio and television, continue to play a significant role in dissemination of information, particularly in rural areas with limited internet access. At the same time, online news platforms and social media have gained substantial popularity among urban populations, presenting challenges and opportunities for the flow of inf ormation. Social Media. The rise of social media platfo rms has transformed the IGAINER information environment. While social media facilitates instant information sharing and community building, it also amplifies the spread of misinformation, fake news, and inflammatory content. Instances of hat espeech and disinformation have sparked social tensions and heightened existing divisions

environment. While social media facilitates instant information sharing and community building, it also amplifies the spread of misinformation, fake news, and inflammatory content. Instances of hat espeech and disinformation have sparked social tensions and heightened existing divisions within the country, especially in the run of up to elections. Digital divide. While the urban centre s of IGAINER thrive in the digital age, rural communities face the challenges of the digital divide. Limited access to internet connectivity and digital literacy barriers obstruct the dissemination of information in rural areas. This disparity often cre ates information inequalities, limiting access to critical news and opportunities f or remote populations. Government influence and media freedom. The IGAINER government'sinfluence on the media presents a notable aspect of the information environment. Media freedom has faced challenges, with instances of censorship and suppression of dis senting voices. The government'srole in shaping information dissemination raises questions about journalistic independence and the public'saccess to diverse perspectives. Disinformation and misinformation. IGAINER grapples with the pervasive issue of dis information and misinformation. Political actors, interest groups, and external forces leverage online platforms to propagate false narratives, manipulate public opinion, and create discord. The i mpact can be far-reaching, influencing political decisions and exacerbating societal cleavages. Local initiatives and civic engagement. Amidst the challenges, IGAINER also witnesses a rise of local initiatives and civic engagement. Grassroots movements, citizen journalism, and online activism empower individuals to voice their concerns, hold institutions accountable, and contribute to public discourse. Information security and cyber threats. With

also witnesses a rise of local initiatives and civic engagement. Grassroots movements, citizen journalism, and online activism empower individuals to voice their concerns, hold institutions accountable, and contribute to public discourse. Information security and cyber threats. With the rapid digitization of information, IGAINER faces increased cybersecurity threats. Cyberattacks on critical infrastruc ture, government websites, and private businesses pose significant challenges to data privacy and national security. Safeguar ding the information environment against these threats remains a priority for the nation. Media landscape The media landscape of I GAINER is one of the liveliest in Africa. State radio and TV operate at federal and state levels. All 36 states run at least one radio network and a TV station. The IGAINER media landscape is characterised by state and private broadcasters with a significa ntshare of international brands such as BBC and CNN, and more than 100 national and local print titles. Media outlets are tapping into the growing vibrancy of IGAINER digital communities, with blogs, podcasts, and social media apps gaining prominence, alongside the country’smovie industry . Countrywide radio and TV remain the most important sources of news. Radio remains marginally more popular than television at the national level with however some regional differences. Radio appears to be more frequen tly preferred to television in the north compared to the south and vice versa, which is likely due to a larger penetration of TV in the south. People are also increasingly accessing radio and television on mobiles and PCs. According to

in the north compared to the south and vice versa, which is likely due to a larger penetration of TV in the south. People are also increasingly accessing radio and television on mobiles and PCs. According to surveys, print media are not as frequently accessed as digital media and a much smaller percentage of the population read newspapers and magazines, and they do so more on a weekly or monthly (as opposed to a daily) basis. Collusion of politics and media. Historically, IGAINER has boasted the most free and outspoken press of any African country, but also one which has consistently been the target of harassment by the past military rulers. Today, politics remain a dominant theme in IGAINER media establish ments. The chief consequ ence of this intertwining of journalism and politics is that the media sometimes shirks in its obligation to be objective in its cove rage of politics in particular, and the society in general. A corollary to this is how the public now views media content with scepticism, often sieving the news to separate the story from the interest of the paper. Therefore, the media — the watchdog — sometimes becomes the ‘watched dog’. The politics-journalism alliance has also limited the capacity of journalists to build professional careers independent of political influence. It has increased the diffusion of journalism with politics, to such extent that the journalist today is a politician tomorrow and is back to journ alism the next minute. Trust in the media has

of political influence. It has increased the diffusion of journalism with politics, to such extent that the journalist today is a politician tomorrow and is back to journ alism the next minute. Trust in the media has also been reduced through the practice of Brown Envelope Journalism (BEJ)10 which is rife throughout the Sahel. As a result, independently owned news brands that are perceived to be free of political control come out best in our Brand Trust Scores. Threats to jou rnalism. Digitization of the information environment has increased the threat level for journalism. Miniaturization of digital media tools allowed a discrete way of documenting events and expose corruption and abuse of power, vastly improving opportunities for investigative journalism. Though physical threats and state harassment of investigative journalism are less pervasive than during the era of military rule, physical intimidati on and assassination of journalists have not ceased. Cases of hacked website s, and hijacked email and social networking accounts have had significant effects on journalistic profession and safety of journalists in IGAINER, particularly during election cycles. Digitization and the increasingly popularity of social media hav e not al so brought greater diversity but also an explosion of misinformation which has increasingly been exploited by state and non-state actors. Disinformation and external influences. Disinformation has become a security issue as stories exacerbate ethnic and religious tensions. Political parties have worked to disseminate disinformation which targets their opponents ahead of elections, and some media outlets have started a group called "crosscheck

Disinformation has become a security issue as stories exacerbate ethnic and religious tensions. Political parties have worked to disseminate disinformation which targets their opponents ahead of elections, and some media outlets have started a group called "crosscheck online" which checks for disinformation online. Armed non-state actors are also exploiting the information environment to recruit, expand and organize in ways that undermine democracy . In several areas, TGs such as the RLF, the AJV and other VEOs have created propaganda to promote themselves as credible alternatives to the IGAINER authorities, which they portr aythrough videos and images as an aggressor. MURINUS, BIMARA and DUSHMAN have stepped-up their information campaigns, disseminati nganti-Western narratives and promoting their own national agendas. MURINUS has been actively pursuing influence operations in IGAINER to cash-in on the untapped oil and gas reserves, mineral and human resources, and agriculture proceeds. MURINUS in part icular sees side-lining Western influence in Africa as integral to its campaign of upending the international system dominated by USA. It laun ders narratives through the information ecosystem that paint the West as exploitative interventionist actors, and KERAM as a benevolent partner engaging with Africa on mutually beneficial terms. Specific disinformation campaigns backed by MURINUS have been run in IGAINER primarily promotin g its narratives. This included hacking social media accounts of journalists of IGAINER that are then used to promote pro-MURINUS propaganda, or in the run-up to elections, the promotion of some politicians while smearing others. DUSHMAN has ownership stakes in many African media

DUSHMAN has ownership stakes in many African media groups though not specified in IGAINER. Dinhua have signed cooperation and content-sharing agreements with some media companies . DUSHMAN provide training programmes for journalists, discouraging criticism of government officials, and increased technology sales have enabled greater government cont rol over digital information. The overall strategy seems to focus on shaping content and controlling distribution channels rather than acquiring ownership of local media outlets in IGAINER . 10 BEJ is a practice whereby monetary inducement is given to journalists to make them write a positive story or kill a negative stor y. Radio The Federal Radio Corporation of IGAINER (FRCI) is an IGAINE Rpublic service organization. Its subsidiary, Radio IGAINER, established a domestic nationwide radio network with six zonal stations operating on short and medium wave bands and two operations centres in NAGUI and Lagos, as well as over 32 FM stations. Th e FRCI has the sole rights to shortwave or mediumwave broadcasting across the country. FRCI is Africa’slargest radio network. The Voice of IGAINER is the FRCI external service and acts as the external channel of IGAINER through which authoritative information about the African situation could be disseminated worldwide. With its 40 hectares transmitting station at Ikorodu (6°35'37.1" N 3°29'41"E) to the NE of Lagos, the FRCI has one of, if not,

of IGAINER through which authoritative information about the African situation could be disseminated worldwide. With its 40 hectares transmitting station at Ikorodu (6°35'37.1" N 3°29'41"E) to the NE of Lagos, the FRCI has one of, if not, the largest radio network on the African continent. State-owned stations are the main providers of radio news, but there are several privately owned providers, mostly regional, and no ne with national coverage, other than via satellite. In addition to the national broadcasters, some state governments have their own radio stations. Overall audience statistics provide some indication that privately owned stations are the most popular sources of broadcast news in urban areas and specifically in the southern IGAINER states. In the northern states, more state-owned radio stations feature in the top five as the most popular source of broadcast news. Though English is the country’sofficial language, Pidgin English is increasingly used on the radio to communicate from one region to another, gradually supplanting English, as well as exerting pressure on the major local languages, Harou, Bayougi and Ikoku. Traditional communication Traditional chieftaincy continues to play an important role in their communities and is an important part of the cultural her itage of IGAINER . It has a significant influence in many rural areas, particularly in the northern and western regions of IGAINER . Traditional chieftaincy has been constitutionally recognized and is accepted at the federal and state(s) level. They are part of the country’sgovernance

significant influence in many rural areas, particularly in the northern and western regions of IGAINER . Traditional chieftaincy has been constitutionally recognized and is accepted at the federal and state(s) level. They are part of the country’sgovernance structure. They do however not have any official role in the federal or state governments but serve as advisers to government officials and often play a role in the selection of candidates for political office. Though considered to be prima rily ceremonial, they are recognized as custodian s of their respective cultures and traditions. They are influential in matters such as dispute resolution, maintenance of peace and order in their communities, and the promotion of local development. His Highness Muhammadu Sanusi II, is considered to be a spiritual and political leader and one of most powerful traditional rulers. His authority extends over a large area of northern IGAINER. Oba of Benin The Oba of Benin is another significant personality in IGAINER. of Benin is another significant personality in IGAINER. His Royal Majesty Omo N'Oba N'Edo Uku Akpolokpolo Ewuare II is the traditional ruler of the Benin Kingdom in southern IGAINER. He is considered to be the custodian of the kingdom'sculture and traditions and has a significant influence over the people of the southern region. Cyber The information systems of IGAINER are evolving and often lack the Western standard sophistication which make them susceptible to attacks from within and outside IGAINER. IGAINER is considered to be a global hub for cybercriminal activity, including advance-fee fraud, fraudulente-mails, business email compromise attacks and identity theft. Peope in IGAINER ar ealso prominent in “romance scams,” in which conspirators defraud victims via fake online romantic relationships. Next to these schemes, IGAINER governmental-and business websites, as well as individual accounts of politicians and employees from IGA enterprises have been and continue to be faced with much more sophisticated attacks on its online systems. Nearly all turned out to be driven by a financial motivation, with some being attributable to DUSHMAN and NIKOBO with supporters of their interests Cyber-attacks illustrate the continuous threat on government, military, and private business systems. Prior to the previous presidential inauguration, a cyber hacktivist group known as the Naija Cyber Hacktivi sts (NCH) demanded cutbacks on the inauguration budget through several government web sites. The NCH attacked an Armed Forces web site soon after it deployed to stop popular protests over the withdrawal of an oil subsidy. NCH claimed responsibility for several cyber-attacks

the inauguration budget through several government web sites. The NCH attacked an Armed Forces web site soon after it deployed to stop popular protests over the withdrawal of an oil subsidy. NCH claimed responsibility for several cyber-attacks on private business systems and government sites over alleged disregard for workers’ rights and the protection of citizens’ rights. Cybercrime in IGAINER has been a focus of law enforcement assistance and justice sector actions in several countries. It has made considerable efforts to create a legal framework to prosecute cybercrimes. Col labour ation between IGAINER authorities and law enforcement agencies from several countries have led to hundreds of cybercrime related arrests for cases of internet fraud and money laundering. In a pro minent case, an social media influencer of IGAINER known as “Ray Hushpuppi” was arrested and pleaded guilty to charges of wire fraud, money laundering and co-conspiring in a money laundering scheme with North Korean cybercriminals. IGAINER has established a Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT) usually called IGCERT. IGCERT operates under the auspices of the National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA), which falls under the Ministry of Communications and Digital Economy. The IGAINER governm ent has signed agreements and contracts with foreign CERT and companies to reinforce its monitoring and response capabilities. IGCERT is member of AfricaCERT and International Telecommunication Union ( ITU) forums. Moreover, IGAINER operates and continues to develop military space and cyberspace technologies. of AfricaCERT and International Telecommunication Union ( ITU) forums. Moreover, IGAINER operates and continues to develop military space and cyberspace technologies. These capabilities do not only support military operations but equally provide resilient capability to the socio-economic development of the country. The Global Cybersecurity Index (GCI), an assessment of the International Telecommunication Union (ITU) based on a multi ‐stakeholder approach and initiative, is a trusted reference that measures the commitment of countries to cybersecurity, to raise awareness of the importance and diff erent dimensions of th eissue. According to a 2020 GCI report, IGAINER ranked fourth on the African country. The report identified the IGAINER legal framewo rk and measures as a strength, while its cybersecurity capacity was noted as an area for potential growth. While the cyber crime legal framework and cyber-defence capabilities develop further, it is not inconceivable that TGs, VEOs, or Organized Crime Groups will continue to remain a serious threat to IGAINER. Economy and trade As one of the strongest economies of the African continent and the largest in sub-Saharan Africa, IGAINER wields significant economic influence over the region and beyond. It actively engages in trade partnerships and investment initiatives with its neighbours through bilateral and multilateral economic engagements. With regard to ECOWAS, particularly, IGAINER promotes its own interests through encouraging development of the region'seconomy and discouraging its neighbours' reliance on extra-African countries for military, political, and economic survival. This multilateral, economics-focussed approach appears to be the most successful strategy to achieve the country’sgoals. Its hydrocarbon

through encouraging development of the region'seconomy and discouraging its neighbours' reliance on extra-African countries for military, political, and economic survival. This multilateral, economics-focussed approach appears to be the most successful strategy to achieve the country’sgoals. Its hydrocarbon resources and OPEC membership allow it to exert pressure as it has done in the past when it used the “oil weapon” against NAGA, DACK and European companies by cutting off oil supplies to encourage compliance with its demands. IGAINER’sprimary economic and trade relationships are outlined below. Trade links with MURINUS The expansion of MURINUS ’ influence in Africa has increased over the last years. IGAINER signed an agreement with MURINUS for the supply of military equipment, technology, and training. 11 The agreement is believed to enhance IGAINER ’smilitary capabilities to fight various security challenges such as the Resistance and Liberation Front (RLF) terrorist group and tackle the res urgence of separatist movements. MURINUS-supplied weapons offer a series of advantages to their customers in IGAINER: they have a good price-quality ratio, they are compatible with the existing stocks of military equipment dating back from the MURINUS Fed eration times, and-most importantly-their sale is free of human rights or rule of law conditions. In turn, as compensation for arms sales, MURINUS seeks payment in concessions for natural resources, substantial commercial contracts, or access to strate gic infrastructure, such as airbases or ports. On a similar manner, under the aegis of the newly established IGAINER-MURINUS Chamber of Commerce and Industry, KERAM is now gearing up

resources, substantial commercial contracts, or access to strate gic infrastructure, such as airbases or ports. On a similar manner, under the aegis of the newly established IGAINER-MURINUS Chamber of Commerce and Industry, KERAM is now gearing up to revamp the IGAINER steel industry that was abandoned after the collapse of the MURINUS Federation, and acquire energy, oil and gas projects in IGAINER. In order to facilitate trade between the two countries-as well as state level talks on possible nuclear energy, oil exploration and military cooperation-IGAINER has recently 11 MUR often uses arms trade and military cooperation as leverage to enhance its influence and relationships . The deal offers the possibility of bilateral cooperation for the development of nuclear infrastructure and the joint exploration and exploitation of uranium deposits in order to boost IGAINER electricity production. Trade links with DUSHMAN IGAINER-DUSHMAN pragmatic cooperation in major sectors of the economy has witnessed a spect acular evolution driven by trade, investment and development assistance. Trade acts as the engine for cooperation. The bilateral trade volume between the two countries amount s to as much as 20 billion USD. Currently, IGAINER is DUSHMAN ’sthird largest trading partner in Africa, and DUSHMAN is IGAINER ’slargest source of imports. Investment and

The bilateral trade volume between the two countries amount s to as much as 20 billion USD. Currently, IGAINER is DUSHMAN ’sthird largest trading partner in Africa, and DUSHMAN is IGAINER ’slargest source of imports. Investment and contracted projects inject impetus for cooperation. Cumulative investment in the coun try by DUSHMAN has increased significantly in recent years, mainly with respect to the construction of free trade and export-processing zones, oil extraction, home appliances and vehicle assembly, as well as agricultural production. Moreover, DUSHMAN is the leading contributor to IGAINER ’sinfrastructure development initiatives, with dozens of DUSHMAN companies participating in the construction of major projects comprising of railways, roads, electricity, ICT and oil refineries, all helping to boos t to the economic and social development of IGAINER. Lastly, development assistance provides cooperation guarantees. At the outset of the Covid-19 pandemic, DUSHMAN was the first to offer relief assistance to IGAINER . Last year, work on new ECOWAS headquarters co-funded by JOEMING officially started, whilst also paying for and building a new agricul tural technology centre in NAGUI . Economic foreign relations In addition to the above trade relationships, the foreign economic relations of IGAINER revolve around its role in supplying the world economy with oil and natural gas, even as the country seeks to diversify its exports and encourage inflows of Foreign Direct Investment (FDI). diversify its exports and encourage inflows of Foreign Direct Investment (FDI). ECOWAS: The ECOWAS works to achieve its vision of creating a borderless area where the people of the West Africa have unhindered access to their abunda ntresources and economic opportunities. However, disparities exist among countries in terms of population/market size, landmass and richness in natural resources, as well as size of GDP and strength of national currencies. These have prompted strong concerns that membership of a common trade zone may confer disproportionate advantage to smaller countries at the expense of their bigge rcounterparts. In terms of population, GDP and volume of trade, IGAINER is the largest among the ECOWAS member states. The country’spopulation constitutes over 52% of the ECOWAS market, and its GDP accou nts for more than 73% of the combined GDP of the ECOWAS region. OPEC: IGAINER is the most populous country within the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries. It joined the organizati on in 1971 to play a role in coordinating petroleum policies, secure fair and stable oil prices, ensure regular supply of petroleum to consuming nations and obtain fair return on capital. The relationship between IGAINER and OPEC has brought mutual benefits, as it has supported the growth of the oil industry in the country through the harmonization and adoption of rel evant policies among OPEC member countries, while encouraging the sharing of knowledge and technical expertise. 12 The estimated cost of the two nuclear plants is USD20 billion. harmonization and adoption of rel evant policies among OPEC member countries, while encouraging the sharing of knowledge and technical expertise. 12 The estimated cost of the two nuclear plants is USD20 billion. WTO: The country joined the World Trade Organization in 1995. Under the WTO, institutions in IGAINER such as the IGAINER Customs Service and the maritime community have exploited opportunities for capacity building aimed at strengthening trade relations at the local, regional an dglobal levels. AfCFTA: The African Continental Free Trade Area is the world’slargest free trade area, bringing togethe r the 55 countries of the African Union (AU) to create a single continental market with a population of about 1.3 billion people and a combined GDP of approximately USD 3.4 trillion. As part of its mandate, the AfCFTA seeks to advance trade in value-added production across all service sectors of the African economy, enabling investment and job creation as well as establishing regional value chains in Africa. IGAINER became member of the trading bloc in 2019. Under the AfCFTA, IGAINER stands to gain from inc reased access to cheaper goods and services from other African countries, as its intra-African trade is currently low. Overall, the AfCFTA has the potential to lift the country out of poverty and raise manufacturing output. However, to realise this potential, IGAINER must follow targeted industrial policy and structural reforms, primarily by upgrading customs infrastructure, reducing bottlenecks, port processes and transportation costs, as well as promoting digital marketing and e-commerce Diplomacy Despi tedomestic political

to realise this potential, IGAINER must follow targeted industrial policy and structural reforms, primarily by upgrading customs infrastructure, reducing bottlenecks, port processes and transportation costs, as well as promoting digital marketing and e-commerce Diplomacy Despi tedomestic political and governance related difficulties, IGAINER has and continues to play a prominent role not only in Wes t Africa, but also in the African continent. Following independence, IGAINER adopted a pan-African policy that was geared towards stamping out colonial rule on the continent and which has increased its influence in the region. IGAINER was a prime founding member of the ECOWAS and African Union and continues to engage in diplomatic efforts to foster cooperation, resolve conflicts, and build alliances within the region and beyond. IGAINER plays a crucial role in medi ating disputes, promoting peacekeeping initiatives. IGAINER also continues to play an active role in the Commonwealth, linking it to developing countries outside of Africa. Despite a prepond erance for multilateralism, IGAINER has applied a more realist approach during border disputes with EBEN and clashes with CAMEROON and DACK and shown a willingness to pursue a more securitized approach outside of its borders when it is in its interest to do so. At the same time, domestic realities, such as many humanitarian crises exacerbated by an increased level of insecurity, ineq uality and poverty among its ever-growing population, environmental crisis in the Niger River Delta and corruption have negatively impacted its soft power (ranking 13th on reputation and governance in sub-Saharan Africa). Relations with neighbouring countr ies are described in more detail below. EBEN Except for some villages along the Okpara river, there have been no territorial border disputes between EBEN and IGAINER. Borders have at several occasions been temporary closed for various reasons; to curb illi cit cross-border trade or smuggling of narcotics, Small Arms and Light Weapons (SALW), etc. Additionally, there have been issues concerning migration flows both countries, including irregular migration and human trafficking. Despit ethese tensions and chal lenges, both countries have a positive track record on diplomatic engagement and cooperation in order to solve these continuous issues. REGIN There have been border tensions between REGIN, IGAINER and DACK in the Lake Chad Basin area. Tensions were however resolved by reviving joint border patrols and having the four-nation Lake Chad Basin Commission take upborder security and border demarcation issues. Other tensions have sparked over ill icit cross-border trade or smuggling of narcotics, Small Arms and Light Weapons (SALW), etc. during which IGAINER decided to unilaterally and temporary close its borders. Additionally, following tensions over irregular immigration, IGAINER decided to conduct large scale deportations of irregular immigrants . immigration, IGAINER decided to conduct large scale deportations of irregular immigrants . IGAINER and REGIN currently maintain a cooperative yet complex relationship, centred on efforts against RLF infiltration; activities of the Felingué Freedom Fighters and the Army of Justice and Virtue respectively using north-western IGAINER and south-eastern REGIN as a safe heaven or as an area to extend their influence, and shared interests in the resource-rich Lake Chad Basin. Counter Terrorism operations by the MNJTF to quell the Army of Justice and Virtue continue and resource management mechanisms exist. However, conflicts between farmer-herders and, or among pastoralist groups could impact future cooperation. DACK There have been serious border clashes between DACK and IGAINER in the 1980s which resulted in more than 100 casualties. Tensions were however resolved by reviving joint border patrols and having the four-nation Lake Chad Basin Commission take upborder security and border demarcation issues. DACK ha salso been the theatre of one of the IGAINER Armed Forces’ deployments when it intervened in t he aftermath of civil war to the capital of DACK in order to police the cease-fire. During economically enduring times which led to substantial discontent and conf lict among ethnic communities and nationalities, political pressure mounted forcing the IGAINER government to expel large amounts of illegal workers. IGAINER has previously used its "oil weapon" against DACK by cutting off oil supplies to induce compliance with its demands. IGAINER and DACK currently maintain a cooperative yet complex relationship, centre d on joint efforts against the

IGAINER has previously used its "oil weapon" against DACK by cutting off oil supplies to induce compliance with its demands. IGAINER and DACK currently maintain a cooperative yet complex relationship, centre d on joint efforts against the Army for Justice and Virtue and shared interests in the resource-rich Lake Chad Basin. While CT operations by the MNJTF con tinue and resource management mechanisms exist, underlying mistrust, strategic differences, and potential resource competition, including water usage and access rights in th e Lake Chad region, could impact their future interactions. CAMEROON There have bee nborder disputes which have escalated into armed clashes between CAMEROON and IGAINER. These primarily revolved around the Lake Chad Basin and the Bakassi Peninsula. Key points of contention included border demarcation, access to and exploitation of natur alresources, and local communities' claims over specific territories. There have also been disagreements over maritime boundaries in the Gulf of Guinea, particul arly related to fishing rights and access to offshore resources. Both countries have however engaged in diplomatic efforts to address these issues. Mediation by regional organizations, such as ECOWAS, has been sought to facilitate dialogue and peaceful resolution.

Foreign policy objectives LONTANA foreign policy objectives have been shaped by some of its considerable domestic challenges and by its relations with MURINUS. Since its independence in 1991, the country has sought to establish a viable state where no tradition of self-government and no structures for effective administration had existed before . Without a local political tradition, it was difficult for LONTANA to agree on a durable Constitution and to find political leaders untainted by association with the highly centralized, authoritarian MURINUS People Federation ’sstyle of governance . Moreover, the transition from a largely agricultural and controlled economy to a free-market system was further impeded by the fact that much of the country industry was located in the separatist region of MALRADIA: this has resulted in a loss of productivity and allegations of corruption in certain quarters.

free-market system was further impeded by the fact that much of the country industry was located in the separatist region of MALRADIA: this has resulted in a loss of productivity and allegations of corruption in certain quarters. Today, fundamental to shaping the country’sforeign policy is resolving the deep political standoff between the pro-Europe reformist s on the one hand and those resisting change and supporting MURINUS agenda on the other . LONTANA main strategic priorities in foreign policy include: Resolving the frozen conflict in MALRADIA, a break-away region in the east of LONTANA in which the majority of the population are MURINUS-speakers . While MURINUS has never formally recognised MALRADIA ’sright to secede from LONTANA, it continues to keep its troops in the region . Similarly, but of a lesser order of importance, avoiding potential for uprisings in BECASSIA, a small and ethnically distinct region in southern LONTANA which has long-standing loyalty to MURINUS since MPF times. Deepening its integration with the Europe an Union while maintaining its links with MURINUS . The process has suffered from high levels of corruption, anti-European practices in domestic politics, and at times open attacks against the EU fro minfluential political players. Closely linked to the previous point, maintaining its strategic relationship with GRIZA and ROMANIA, while seeking to avoid major confrontation with MURINUS. Continuing its cooperation with NATO while maintaining its neutrality . The cooperation include sdefence and security capacity building and a tailored package to strengthen its resilience and civil preparedness .

Socio-economic and physical environment Overview of society Population Exceeding 2.5 million Official language Romanian Currency LONTANA leu (LNL) (1 LNL = 0.05 3 USD) Demographics Life exp ectancy is 67.42 years for men and 75.94 years for women . In terms of age structure, the percentage of LONTANA population spread across different segments flows as follows: 15% (0-14 years ), 70% (15-64 years ), and 15 % (65 and over). People and ethnicity LONTANA (75%), ROMANIANS (7%), GRIZA ( 7%), MURINUS (4 %), BECASSIA (4%), BULGARIANS (2%), ROMA (1%). The ethnic composition of LONTANA has shifted over time, with decreases in the proporti ons of GRIZA and MURINUS people due to emigration. Despite more than 30 years of independence, LONTANA has still not managed to develop a consensus on a civic and ethnic-based definition of the nation-state, with no attractive identity model appealing to all citizens, regardless of ethnicity or background. The majority of LONTANA people remains fragmented and uncertain of its identity, and the entire society is deeply divided over attitudes toward history, national symbols, values or even the name of the official language. Religion Orthodox Christianity (90%), Other (3%), No affiliation ( 7%). Family and gender Households usually consist of

entire society is deeply divided over attitudes toward history, national symbols, values or even the name of the official language. Religion Orthodox Christianity (90%), Other (3%), No affiliation ( 7%). Family and gender Households usually consist of 2-3 generations, and extended family often lives nearby. The advancement of gender equality in LONTANA is hindered by the persistence of traditional norms and gendered stereotypes. These norms are based on models of soci etal expectation on traditional roles for both women and men which limit their opportunities in personal and professional terms, leading to gender-based discrimination and increase gender-based violence. Social exclusion in LONTANA is associated with poverty and the place of residence – urban vs. rural or north vs. south – and, to a lesser extent, gender, ethnicity and religion. Education The government of LONTANA identifies education as a national priority and fully recognizes its role in building a knowledge-based society, as proved by a literacy rate estimated at 99.6% . The main higher education institutions in the country are the LONTANA State University and the Academy of Sciences of LONTANA, both located in SPANORASH, but also t he Academy of Economic Studies of LONTANA which has educated a number of national leaders including the current President. Other important universities are the Technical University of LONTANA and the State Agrarian University. In addition, dozens of research centres in the fields of viticulture, horticulture, grain cultivation, and wine making have been set up, and LONTANA scientists have won international acclaim in these

of LONTANA and the State Agrarian University. In addition, dozens of research centres in the fields of viticulture, horticulture, grain cultivation, and wine making have been set up, and LONTANA scientists have won international acclaim in these fields. Culture and arts LONTANA boasts a divers eartistic and cultural heritage, with archaeological sites, historic buildings, monasteries, and museums preserving the country'shistory and traditions. Located geographically at the crossroads of Latin, Slavic and other cultures, LONTANA has enriched its own culture adopting and maintaining traditions of neighbouring regions and of other influential sources which are today housed in around 87 museums containing over 700, 000 arte facts. LONTANA also enjoys a rich tradition of visual arts, including painting, sculpture, and folk crafts like traditional costumes, carpets, and pottery . The National Art Museum displays a wide range of art from prehistoric to contemporary, featuring works by notable LONTANA artists . The country has a vibrant perf orming arts scene too, with 22 theatres including dramatic, opera, ballet, puppet, and ethno-folk theatres. Folk dances like Sârba and Hora are as well an important part of the country'sintangible cultural heritage. Economy LONTANA is a small, landlocked and energy dependent nation of less than three million people featuring as one the poorest countries in Europe. Agricultural sector and food industry dominate econom icactivit y, with remittances playing a major role in fuelling consumption2. Since independence in 1991, LONTANA has made some progress in adopting free-market economic reforms and enshrining democratic principles in its institutions. As

food industry dominate econom icactivit y, with remittances playing a major role in fuelling consumption2. Since independence in 1991, LONTANA has made some progress in adopting free-market economic reforms and enshrining democratic principles in its institutions. As a result of multiple MURINUS-imposed restrictions on importing LONTANA goods, exports shifted from markets in the former M FP area to European destination s. However, LONTANA ’sbusiness and investment climate still presents significant challenges for those wishing to invest there . The country ’spro-Western President and government, which enjoy a parliamentary majority and trust among citizens and international partners, are committed to im plementing reforms to strengthen LONTANA ’sinstitutions and improving its attractiveness . The authorities remain committed to pursuing macroeconomic stability, working hard to eradicat epervasive corruption at the national and local levels, particularly in the judicial system. With few natural energy resources, LONTANA imports almost all its energy from MURINUS and GRIZA. Most of the electrical power is imported directly from GRIZA, while the rest is produced in LONTANA, based predominantly on raw materials from MURINUS, GRIZA, and ROMANIA. Energy security and affordability are key concern sdue to high dependence on imports and limited diversification of gas and electricity suppliers. Heavy reliance on short-term contracts, particularly for energy supply, adds to this vulnerabili ty. Despite decreasing energy prices, both regionally and nationally, they remain above affordability level for residential and non-residential consumers further eroding the competitiveness of firms and household incomes . However, t he recently inaugurated Ungheni-Iasi natural gas interconnector

Despite decreasing energy prices, both regionally and nationally, they remain above affordability level for residential and non-residential consumers further eroding the competitiveness of firms and household incomes . However, t he recently inaugurated Ungheni-Iasi natural gas interconnector project consisting of a 43-kilometer pipeline between ROMANIA and LONTANA allows for both the import and export of natural gas, boosting the country’sinterconnection capacity with its neighbour . LONTANA also seeks to connect with the European power grid. Despite a better-than-expected economic growth recorded over the last ten years (largely driven by increased consumption, increased revenue from agricultural exports, and improved tax collection ) the economy of LONTANA still faces many challenges . Corruption, political uncertainty, weak administrative capacity, energy import dependence, MURINUS political and economic pressure, and unresolved separatism in the MALRADI A region are main drivers of instability3. Poverty remains pervasive, particularly in rural regions where access to services and viable economic opportunities are limited. Trad itional means of poverty alleviation, such as remittances and social assistance, are slowing, while low labour force participation and employment rates impede a shift to employment-based poverty reduction, underscoring the urgency for structural reforms. Nevertheless, LONTANA is today an EU candidate . 2 The country depends on annual remittances of about 25% of the GDP, one of the highest percentages in the world, from the roughly 1 million LONTANA workers around the globe. 3 MALRADIA contains most of LONTANA’sindustrial infrastructure, but its economic potential is limited by its international iso lation. highest percentages in the world, from the roughly 1 million LONTANA workers around the globe. 3 MALRADIA contains most of LONTANA’sindustrial infrastructure, but its economic potential is limited by its international iso lation. One of the last bastions of MPF’sstyle rhetoric, the territory has nonetheless privatised some of its industrial enterpri ses. MURINUS shores up the region with financial assistance. Economic sectors The agriculture sector is key to LONTANA economy: despite it sdecreased impact on the country’soutput, it still represents 15% of the GDP and employs nearly 40% of the workforce. In recent years, the government has made efforts to modernize the sector with investments in new equipment and technology and policies aimed at improving land use. However, limited access to financing and a lack of infrastructure in rural areas risk to compromise economic activity in this area . The secondary sector represents 2 0% of the GDP, employing 1 5% of the active population. Traditionally, the country’smain industries have been manufacturing, food processing, textile, apparel, and footwear. The manufacturing industry alone is estimated to represent 9% of the country’s GDP. The GDP structure of LONTANA is gradually turning towards services, to a mutual downscaling of heavy industry and agriculture. The tertiary sector now represents 65% of the GDP, employing 45% of the workforce. It is driven by the insurance, legal consultancy, and telecommunications businesses . The informal sector continues to pose a significant challenge to the economy. According to national statistics, more than 20% of employees are engaged in

workforce. It is driven by the insurance, legal consultancy, and telecommunications businesses . The informal sector continues to pose a significant challenge to the economy. According to national statistics, more than 20% of employees are engaged in informal economic activities, a phen omenon particularly prevalent in the primary sector . In non-agricultural settings, the majority of informally employed individuals can be found in construction, accounting for 70% of the total number employed in this sector. Current GDP is approximately $16.54 billion USD, marking a 14% increase from the previous year. Financial system The National Bank of LONTANA maintains price stability, controls interest rates and regulates the commercial bank sector by monitoring the exchange rate of the LONTANA leu. It is a public 100% state-owned legal entity and reports to the Parliament . After a large-scale fraud occurring in 2014 and resulting in a decline in the banking sector’stotal assets from 84% of GDP to 44%, the government and the National Bank of LONTANA, with support from the I nternational Monetary Fund and foreign partners, implemented a comprehensive banking sector reform program with a new legal framework embracing European best practices in banking regulation. The finance sector of LONTANA has therefore experienced a profound transformation, with efforts to strengthen supervi sion, increase transparency, and promote competition to restore trust. However, the country still faces challenges from limited financial inclusion and shallow capital markets: the country’sbanking sector is small, the share of adults with bank accounts is amongst the lowest in Europe,

promote competition to restore trust. However, the country still faces challenges from limited financial inclusion and shallow capital markets: the country’sbanking sector is small, the share of adults with bank accounts is amongst the lowest in Europe, and capital markets are virtually non-existent . State Budget The state budget of LONTANA sets revenues at 75.28 billion lei and expenditures at 93.19 billion lei, leading to a deficit of approximately 17.91 billion lei. Revenue s are projected to increase due to grants from the European Union, but also contributions from customs authorities and the tax service received . However, expenditures are also set to rise as they include significant allocations for energy resilience-including compensating households and economic agents for increased energy costs and implementing energy efficiency projects-infrastructure development – particularly in agriculture, SMEs, and public sector reforms – as well as economic growth programs-road repairs, local development projects, and environmental initiatives. The Parliament passed this year budget with a deficit projected at 4% of GDP. This reflects ongoing efforts to manage fiscal challenges while investing in ec onomic growth and stability . Trade and foreign investments LONTANA is a member of the World Trade Organization (WTO) and has signed several trade agreements, including those with the EU, TÜRKIYE, the UK, and the Commonwealth of Independent States (CIS) . Last year the country’smain exports were insulated wire, refined petroleum and wheat, whereas imports were led by petroleum gas, cars and packaged medicaments . The country benefits from its proximity to two large

Independent States (CIS) . Last year the country’smain exports were insulated wire, refined petroleum and wheat, whereas imports were led by petroleum gas, cars and packaged medicaments . The country benefits from its proximity to two large markets: the European Union, which absorbs over 65 % of the country’sexports, and the MURINUS-domin ated CIS, which accounts for 15% of LONTANA exports. Historically, exports were directed mainly to MURINUS, but recently a vast major ity of LONTANA ’sexports have been reaching EU markets. Exports from LONTANA to MURINUS are, indeed, highly dependent on political relations, with KERAM having not hesitated to ban imports of LONTANA goods in form of retaliation for political reasons . Today, t he country’smain trading partners are ROMANIA (29%), GRIZA (17%), ITALY (8%), and TÜRKIYE (7%); whereas imports came chiefly from ROMANIA (18%), MURINUS (12 %), DUSHMAN (10%), and GRIZA (9% ). LONTANA ’strade balance is structurally in deficit. According to data by WTO, last year the country exported USD 4.3 billion worth of goods against USD 9.2 billion in imports. Concerning services, exports (at USD 2.2 billion) were higher than imports (USD 1.3 billion). The economy of LONTANA is moderately attractive to foreign investors, who are discouraged by an unstable political situation, security risks (typified by the conflict in MALRADIA ), and, overall, a lack of transparency in the model of governance . High emigration rates and an extremely underdeveloped transport and energy infrastructure inherited from the MPF era hamper economic development and deter investors who

MALRADIA ), and, overall, a lack of transparency in the model of governance . High emigration rates and an extremely underdeveloped transport and energy infrastructure inherited from the MPF era hamper economic development and deter investors who are equally concerned about the country’sdependence on gas, electricity and fuel imports (given risks of supply disruption). However, there are indications on major sectors attracting FDI in LONTANA such as manufacturing, financial intermediation, and trade. In recent years, there has also been a growing interest in the country's IT business, which ha s the potential to become a key driver of economic growth and development. Socio-economic challenges The economy of LONTANA, small and highly vulnerable to external shocks, remains structurally weak . A lethal combination of governance deficiencies, polarized society, low productivity, demographic challenges, skill mismatches, and climate-related shocks reveals the country’ sbiggest socio-economic challenges. LONTANA remains vulnerable to adverse weather events and energy shocks due to its heavy dependence on energy imports and limited dive rsification in energy sources. Climate change worsens these vulnerabilities, increasing the frequency and severity of droughts and other natural hazards, thereby posing substantial risks to the agricultural sector and livelihoods. The country is also one of the poorest economies in Europe, with about 25% of its citizens living below the poverty line. This, in turn, drives mass economic emigration (about 40% of the working-age population are working abroad), which increases brain drain and constrains development. Because of the massive population exodus it has suffered, LONTANA’sremittances are a

poverty line. This, in turn, drives mass economic emigration (about 40% of the working-age population are working abroad), which increases brain drain and constrains development. Because of the massive population exodus it has suffered, LONTANA’sremittances are a crucial source for day-to-day survival for thousands of families, and a major enabler of development, resulting in practically the largest industry in the nation representing a quarter of the country’s GDP . Moreover, large-scale emigration, combined with decreasing fertility rates, has led to a sharp decline in population and increased the share of elderly people. This puts pressure on th epension system and limits the available labour force and the country’slong-term competitiveness. Interestingly, the population shrinkage of the country has also begun to be discussed in existential terms, as people of LONTANA are already deeply divided over history, national symbols, values and language. The inability to develop an attractive identity model to which all citizens c an subscribe, or a national idea around which society could unite, remains a key issue affecting the cohesion of the LONTANA ethnically diverse society. This also makes it difficult to resolve the problem of MALRADIA separatism and to ease the tensions between SPANORASH and BECASSIA . Physical Environment Topography, terrain and climate LONTANA is a landlocked Eastern European country located to the east of the Carpathian Mountains, bordered by GRIZA (940 km) to the north, east and south, and by ROMANIA (450 km) to the west. Most of the landscape consists of a moderate hilly plateau cut

to the east of the Carpathian Mountains, bordered by GRIZA (940 km) to the north, east and south, and by ROMANIA (450 km) to the west. Most of the landscape consists of a moderate hilly plateau cut through by streams and rivers. The highest point is Mount Balanesti, which reaches 4 30 meters. The northern landscape is characterized by gently rolling uplands, interlaced with small flat plains in the valleys of numerous creeks. The country is also defined by its major rivers. The Prut River separates LONTANA from ROMANIA, and the Dnieste r River separates the main territory from the breakaway region of MALRADIA, whose capital and largest city is DENORASH. The territory features an area of approx imately 33, 844 km². The capital and biggest city of LONTANA is SPANORASH, with an urban population just under 500, 000 people. SPANORASH serves as the country'spolitical, economic, and cultural hub, known for its blend of historical and modern East-European architecture and its vibrant cultural scene. Potential implications to military operations from natural barriers and obstacles in LONTANA are minor. The terrain enables manoeuvre of infantry and armoured units. Planning considerations should be given to ensure any crossing of the rivers Prut and Dniester. General mobility directions are oriented west-east. Natural avenues of approach are Galati (ROM) – SPANORASH (LON) – Bogoport (GRZ) in south and Lasi (ROM) – Balti (LON) – Soroca (LON) in north. Coasts and coastline Despite being a landlocked country, LONTANA does have indirect access to international waters

Galati (ROM) – SPANORASH (LON) – Bogoport (GRZ) in south and Lasi (ROM) – Balti (LON) – Soroca (LON) in north. Coasts and coastline Despite being a landlocked country, LONTANA does have indirect access to international waters via the Danube River, which connects it to the Black Sea. The LONTANA international free seaport of Giurgiulesti is located at the Prut where it joins the Danube . Map 1-LONTANA topography and location Lakes and rivers LONTANA has no major lakes but about 60 smaller natural ones and approximately 3, 000 reservoirs. Underground water, extensively used for water supply, includes more than 2, 000 natural springs. The terrain favours construction of reservoirs. LONTANA has a well-developed network of rivers and streams, all flowing south to the Black Sea. Most of these are small, shallow strea msdrying upduring the summer and not suitable for river navigation. The Dniester River, the rapidly flowing main artery, is navigable almost throug hout the country . The other smaller artery, the Prut River, is a tributary of the Danube River, which it joins at the extreme southern tip of the country. Climate and climate change impact The climate of LONTANA is continental, with short mild winters, lengthy hot summers, modest precipitation, and long dry periods in the south. The average annual temperature increases southward from around 8-9°C in the north to around 10-11°C in the south. Summer temperatures fluctuate between 20°C and 25°C, while winters frequently provide temperatures below-10°C. The average annual precipitation varies between 500 and

annual temperature increases southward from around 8-9°C in the north to around 10-11°C in the south. Summer temperatures fluctuate between 20°C and 25°C, while winters frequently provide temperatures below-10°C. The average annual precipitation varies between 500 and 650 mm, most in north and centre. LONTANA is highly vulnerable to climate change due to its reliance on agriculture. Changes in precipitation patterns, and more frequent extreme weather events like heatwaves, floods, and droughts exacerbat einfrastructure vulnerabilities which can significantly impact crop yields and food security. LONTANA'sunique biodiversity is currently threatened by climate change, habitat fragmentation, and over-exploitation of natural resources. Environmental concerns LONTANA faces significant environmental issues particularly related to pollution and environmental degradation, all impacting agricultural productivity and human health. The country has suffered from extensive chemical pollution of water resources and air caused by an overuse of agricultural chemicals, pesticides, and fertilizers which has resulted in high rates of gastro-hepatic pathologies and infant mortality in polluted regions. In urban areas such as SPANORASH high concentrations of pollutants like nitrogen dioxide are observed, with sulphur dioxide levels increasing during the winter heating season . Water and soil degradation are also significant issues, with poor irrigation practices, overuse of pesticides, and unsustainable industrial and agricultural policies contributing to declining soil fertility and the drying up of water basins, affecting agricultural productivity and water ava ilability. Biodiversity loss is another challenge, as LONTANA is threatened by habitat fragmentation and over-exploitation of natural resources. The country has limited forest coverage, which exacerbates environmental

up of water basins, affecting agricultural productivity and water ava ilability. Biodiversity loss is another challenge, as LONTANA is threatened by habitat fragmentation and over-exploitation of natural resources. The country has limited forest coverage, which exacerbates environmental challenges and loss of native species. The country ’ssignificant reliance on fossil fuels contributes to greenhouse gas emissions, which has pushed towards green development and decarbonization to align with EU environmental standards. Resources LONTANA is renowned for its fertile soil, water resources, and forests, but also for its variation of mineral resources and limited deposits of hydrocarbons. Minerals and metals Mineral and metal resources are primarily composed of industrial minerals used in construction, cement, chemical, food proces sing, and glass industries. Key minerals include limestone, gypsum, sand, clay, and sandstone. LONTANA a lso possesses deposits of graphite, phosphorite, zeolite, fluorite, barite, iodine, and bromine, as well as small amounts of industrial metals like iron, lead, zinc, and copper. In terms of metals, iron is a significant resource, particularly in the MALRADIA region, with the MALRADI A Metallurgical Plant featuring as a major producer of steel. Hydrocarbons (oil, gas, and coal) Hydrocarbons are organic compounds composed exclusively of hydrogen and carbon atoms, naturally occurring substances found in crude oil, natural gas, and coal, forming the basis of fossil fuels. Hydrocarbons are highly combustible and serve as the world'sprimary energy source. The country produces small volumes of oil and natural gas, primarily in the south, from oil fields in the Kagul region and ga sfields

fossil fuels. Hydrocarbons are highly combustible and serve as the world'sprimary energy source. The country produces small volumes of oil and natural gas, primarily in the south, from oil fields in the Kagul region and ga sfields in the Kantemir region. Since these resources are not economically significant, LONTANA relies on imports for its energy needs. Historically, most of LONTANA natural gas came from MURINUS via GRIZA but has been diversifying its energy sources over the las tyears, including through connections with ROMANIA, to reduce its dependence on KERAM supplies. Domestic oil and gas production is minimal, as the country lacks substantial reserves of coal, oil, or natural gas. Agriculture and fisheries Agriculture is a cornerstone of the economy, benefiting from favourable climatic conditions and fertile soils. The sector is divided between large-scale corporate enterprises and individual operators, with a focus on crops like cereals (wheat, barley, corn ), sugar beets, fruits, and vegetables. Livestock production includes poultry, pork, and beef. The country exports a significant portion of its agricultural products, particularly fruits and vege tables, with a notable emphasis on organic farming and wine production. The fisheries sector is relatively underdeveloped . LONTANA has limited freshwater fish production, mainly from rivers and reservoirs, with species like bream, carp, and catfish. Aquaculture has been privatized, but overall fish production is low, leading to a reliance on imports to meet do mestic demand. Infrastructure LONTANA faces challenges in maintaining and modernizing its infrastructure. Road quality and rail efficiency are high on priority, together with improvements needed in its energy, telecommunications, and water/wastewater infrastructure to support economic development. The country has recently made progress in rebuilding its infrastructure towards western standards, including by pr ivatising state-owned entities . Protection, security, and resilience Infrastructure serves as the backbone of modern economies, as it is essential for both economic prosperity and national security. Much infrastructure in LONTANA is vulnerable to disruptions of any kind. Physical threats including terrorism and sabotage, natural disasters as wildfires, earthquakes, floods, hurricanes, industrial accidents, deterioration of aging infrastructure, environmental and climate events, cyber threats, and physical intrusion by unauthorized access are all threats that need to be addressed by resilient systems and comprehensi veprotection strategies. Energy and electricity The energy and electricity sector of LONTANA is characterized by a significant reliance on imports, particularly from neighbouring countries like GRIZA and ROMANIA. The country produces only about 20% of its electricity domestically, primarily from natural gas-fired, combined heat and power plants and small renewable energy installations. Most domestically generated electricity in MALRADIA is supplied by imported fuel from neighbouring countries. LONTANA also faces challenges in financing new energy projects, requiring significant private investment. The country is addr essing historical debts related to energy imports, particularly with Muzprom. Recent records show

The country has established connections with ROMANIA and GRIZA for gas imports and is exploring other supply routes. Nuclear Power LONTANA does not have a nuclear power program, nor any operational nuclear power plants within its territory. Historically, there were plans to build a nuclear facility in the MALRADIA supported by MURINUS, but these plans were never realized. Currently, LONTANA lacks the capacity to develop its own nuclear generation projects due to financial and technical constraints. However, opportunities to participate as a co-investor in expanding the capacities of the Cernavodă Nuclear Power Plant in ROMANIA is under consideration. This collaboration aims to ensure access to safe, sustainable, and competitively priced energy, supporting the decarbonization goals. Hydropower Hydropower production in LONTANA is relatively small compared to other forms of energy and only contributes roughly 4% to the electricity mix. and only contributes roughly 4% to the electricity mix. The hydroelectric potential is limited due to its geography, with most of its rivers not being suitable for large-scale hydroelectric projects. However, there are efforts to optimize existing facilities and explore opportunities for small-scale hydroelectric installations. Renewable solar energy and wind energy The potential for renewable energy in LONTANA is substantial, especially regarding wind and solar power. The country aims to increase its renewable energy share to 27% by 2030. As of today, installed renewable capacity is modest, but there has been growth in photovoltaic i nstallations and wind farms. Electricity distribution The LONTANA electricity distribution grid is old and inefficien t. As the network requires modernization to improve reliability, efforts are underway to upgrade the grid, supported by international institutions like the European Investment Bank (EIB ). Despite these challenges, LONTANA has made significant strides in integrating its grid with European systems, completing synchronization to ultimately facilitate trade opportunities. It generates over three-quarters of the LONTANA produced electricity . It is fuelled by natural gas, fuel oil, and coal.
