CET-Nord: (24 MW) Another gas-fired power plant . 2. Transport The transport sector of LONTANA is undergoing significant development and integration efforts, particularly with the European Union. LONTANA is aligning its transport infrastructure with European standards, focusing on multimodal hubs and digital technologies to enhance connect ivity and efficiency. The transport sector plays a crucial role in facilitating trade, which has increased exports and strengthened economic ties with the EU. Railway network and transportation LONTANA has an aging, single-track, broad-gauge and non-electrified railway system inherited from the MPF era. It is in poor condition, leaving average commercial speed for passenger trains at 35-40 km/h. The rail network is underdeveloped and primarily us ed for freight transport, with passenger services being limited in speed and frequency. The rail network spans around 1, 232 km of 1, 520 mmgauge (broad gauge) and requires gau ge-changing equipment at border crossings with ROMANIA and GRIZA. The major national railway junctions are SPANORASH, Bender, Ungheni, Ocnita, Balti and Basarabeasca. Connections to GRIZA at Kuchurhan, Mohilyv-Podilsky, and Ocnita, and to ROMANIA at Ungheni, Cantemir, and Giurgiulesti (all with break-of-gauge). The system, including all associated

The major national railway junctions are SPANORASH, Bender, Ungheni, Ocnita, Balti and Basarabeasca. Connections to GRIZA at Kuchurhan, Mohilyv-Podilsky, and Ocnita, and to ROMANIA at Ungheni, Cantemir, and Giurgiulesti (all with break-of-gauge). The system, including all associated infrastructure, is state owned. Investments and maintenance have been limited, resulting in delays to upgrades to infrastructure and rolling stock, and leaving the efficiency of the network below expected standards. How ever, significant investments are being planned and made to modernize the railway infrastructure, rehabilitating key sections of the network to enhanc econnectivity with neighbouring countries and align with EU standards. Road network and transportation Road transportation is a primary mode of transport in LONTANA, with highways connecting major cities. The total length of the road network is around 10, 600 km, including 3, 600 km of national roads and 7, 000 km of local roads, of which approximately 85% is paved. The network has suffered from lack of investment and maintenance, leaving especially rural roads in poor condition. Approximately 20% of roads are in critical cond ition. The government in SPANORASH is trying to increase the national budget to improve road quality . External investments, especially from EU, have focused on upgrading main highways transiting LONTANA from east to west and linking to GRIZA and ROMANIA, to renovate and integrate the transport networks with European corridors. LONTANA is also working to align its road transport regulations with EU standards through technical assistance projects, which will enhance road safety, streamline operations, and foster

ROMANIA, to renovate and integrate the transport networks with European corridors. Despite these efforts, challenges remain, including limited and unpredictable financing and poor maintenance. Airports and air transportation SPANORASH International Airport has experienced significant growth in recent years and handled a record 4.1 million passenger s in 2024. With an annual capacity expanded to serve up to 5.4 million passengers, with ongoing projects aiming to accommodate increasing passenger num bers and air freight, the SPANORASH International Airport provides connectivity to 45 destinations in 23 countries. Other airports like Balti Internati onal Airport and Cahul International Airport are certified but have limited or no sch eduled flights. The LONTANA air transportation sector is considered an emerging market with significant potential for further growth in air traffic and connectivity. Sea transportation LONTANA does not have direct access to the sea but relies on the Giurgiulesti International Free Port for its maritime trade. This port is located on the Danube River at its confluence with the Prut River, near the border with ROMANIA and GRIZA. It serves as LONTANA'ssole access point to international river and sea transport and trade-routes, facilitating both inland and sea-going vessels to dock. The port is strategically positioned at the crossroads of several international trade routes between EU countries and Eastern European states. The port

international river and sea transport and trade-routes, facilitating both inland and sea-going vessels to dock. The port is strategically positioned at the crossroads of several international trade routes between EU countries and Eastern European states. The port includes specialized terminals for handling grain, oil, liquid cargo, and containers. It also has a bulk goods terminal, vegetable oil terminal, cereal products terminal, and a railway terminal with combined gauge. The port operates as a free economic zone until 2030, offering favourab lecustoms and tax regulations that attract businesses and enhance trade. The port operations are managed by Danube Logistics, and it has become a vital logistics centre for the region. Inland waterways Some 400 kms of waterways are used by the inhabitants and businesses of LONTANA, mostly on the Dniester River, partly controlled by MALRADIA. Most important is the small 400-600 mlong access to the Danube at Giurgiulesti port, that facilities both access to sea trade routes, both als o to Danube and cross European riverine transport networks. 3. adequate in terms of quantity and quality. Frequent pipe bursts and leakages lead to significant water losses, often exceeding 50%, and pose risks of contamination. Mobile penetration is exceeding 100% and 5G technology has been rolled out in SPAN ORASH and other cities. LONTANA boasts one of the best wired internet connections globally, with high speeds and low costs. LONTANA boasts one of the best wired internet connections globally, with high speeds and low costs. The country ranks among t he top for gigabit coverage, with a median fixed internet download speed around 140 Mbps and a mobile broadband penetration at 89%. The country has an extensive fibre optic backbone network, and the infrastructure supports high-speed internet services contributing to its position as a leader in digital inclusion. 5. Space infrastructure LONTANA does not have a developed space infrastructure. However, the country has made notable advancements in space explorati on through international collaborations. The most prominent achievement is the deployment of TUM nanoSAT, LONTANA'sfirst satellite, which was launched from the International Space Station (ISS) in 2022. This CubeSat was developed by the Technical University of LONTANA (TUL) with suppo rt from the United Nations Office for Outer Space Affairs (UNOOSA) and the Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency (JAXA) under the KiboCUBE program. TUM nanoSAT aimed to demonstrate technology in various modules and subsystems, including satellite communications, solar power management, and sensor behaviour in space. Although LONTANA does not have a domestic space industry capable of producing satellites or launching them independently, this achievement marks a significant step in its space exploration efforts. Currently it relies on international partnerships to engage in space activities, and there are no domestic facilities for sate llite manufacturing or launch. 6. LONTANA relies heavily on imported energy, making its energy infrastructure critical for national security and economic stability. Political and key actor landscape Political system LONTANA is a multi-party parliamen tary representative democratic republic. As per its 1994 constitution, the government of LONTANA consists of executive, judicial and legislative branches. Separation of powers is formally established in the country; however, the role various democratic institutions play depends on the position and influence of key political actors. The executive branch consists of the President, the Prime Minister, and the Cabinet of Ministers. The President acts as the Head of State with ceremonial duties promoting unity within the country, while t he Prime Minister is the Head of Government. He/she is directly a ppointed by the President.

Cabinet of Ministers. The President acts as the Head of State with ceremonial duties promoting unity within the country, while t he Prime Minister is the Head of Government. He/she is directly a ppointed by the President. The legislative assembly is the uni-cameral Parliament which is the supreme representative organ and the single legislative authority of the state. The right of legislative initiative belongs to the members of Parliament . The Parliament has 101 members and is responsible for approving the appointment of both the Prime Minister and the Cabinet of Ministers . The judicial branch consists of a hierarchy of courts, including (in order): courts of first instance, the Court of Appeal, the Supreme Court and the Constitutional Court. Although LONTANA has documented commercial laws covering key areas, effective implementation and adhere nce to best practice remains a challenge, and improvement measures need to be implemented. As an example, the Constitution provides for an independent judiciary, but the executive branch has historically tended to exercise undue influence over the judiciar y. Governance As the national capital with the institutions of govern ment, SPANORASH is also where the political opposition is most prominent . The city has become a central location for demonstrations and protests against the authorities, with huge differences between a few billionaire oligarchs and the rest of the population spreading discontent. LONTANA'sgeostrategic dilemma is also a source of socio-political friction: the dilemma between deepening integration with the West, the EU and NATO, and strengthening ties with MURINUS,

few billionaire oligarchs and the rest of the population spreading discontent. LONTANA'sgeostrategic dilemma is also a source of socio-political friction: the dilemma between deepening integration with the West, the EU and NATO, and strengthening ties with MURINUS, manifests itself in LONTANA through internal unrest, often driven by external support. These can result in confrontations with the police and security forces. Levers of power Military The President of LONTANA exercises command of the Armed Forces through the Ministry of Defence (MoD) in SPANORASH. Military Strategic planning and execution is conducted by the General Staff. The Armed Forces of LONTANA comprise land and air forces augmented by border guards and gendarmerie. The country has structured its military to meet the requirements for peace-keeping operations, with no nuclear, biological, or chemical weapons programmes. The land forces strength is estimated to be around approximately 5, 000 and includes a Special Forces battalion. In addition, LONTANA has nearly 2, 000 paramilitary personnel and some 60, 000 strong reserve force. Its capabilities include light infantry with fighting vehicles and armoured personnel carriers supported by artillery. LONTANA faces a shortage of spare parts for military equipment, fuel, ammunition, bulletproof vests, food supplies and anti-tank light weapons. Emphasis has been placed on overhauling and repairing military vehicles that had not been operated for years, accumulating rust in their MPF depots but this manoeuvre is not expected to yield a very favourable result in the number of operational equipment. The country’sannual defence budget has ranged mainly between 0.3 and 0.6 % of GDP over the last few years (remaining below $30 million in real terms ) with most of the funds being allocated to salaries of contract personnel

defence budget has ranged mainly between 0.3 and 0.6 % of GDP over the last few years (remaining below $30 million in real terms ) with most of the funds being allocated to salaries of contract personnel and subsistence of conscripted soldiers . LONTANA does not have a military-industrial complex, therefore relying on foreign procurement . The country has been recently receiving material ail from the UNITED STATES, ITALY and GERMANY. MURINUS is believed to have an estimated 4500 forces making up the MALRADIA army – to be added to the existing 1500 personnel already present in the region-expanding the military resources at its disposal to further its goals in both LONTANA and GRIZA . Additionally, the largest armament storage facility in Eastern Europe is located in the hamlet of Cobasna, a municipality in the separatist territory, containing up to 20, 000 tons of MPF era weapons . Although legally and internationally recognized as part of LONTANA, MALRADIA controls both the village and the ammunition depot and has denied access to international observers-an exception being the MURINUS military forces located in the region . This make s the site a potential military target for parts to conflict . Mission and strategy The National Security Strategy reflects LONTANA'sgeopolitical stance and its commitment to strengthening its security and defence capabilities in the face of evolving regional threats . The country 'scurrent National Security Strategy aims to: Resolve the MALRADIA conflict. Fulfil all prerequisites for LONTANA to join the European Union as a

its security and defence capabilities in the face of evolving regional threats . The country 'scurrent National Security Strategy aims to: Resolve the MALRADIA conflict. Fulfil all prerequisites for LONTANA to join the European Union as a full member by 2030, with EU membership identified as a key national security interest. Strengthen LONTANA'ssecurity partnerships with the EU, its member states like ROMANIA, FRANCE, GERMANY, as well as the UNITED STATES, UNITED KINGDOM and other international partners. Modernize and build resilient armed forces and security sector through investments, training, equipment and enhanced interope rability with partner countries. Counter hybrid threats, corruption, and build societal resilience against interference and disinformation. Uphold values of democracy, human rights, rule of law and ensuring protection of all citizens. Address climate change as a major security threat to LONTANA'spredominantly agricultural economy. Enhance LONTANA'srole as a reliable regional and international security partner. LONTANA's Armed Forces The mission of LONTANA’s Armed Forces is: Defend LONTANA'sindependence, sovereignty, unity and territorial integrity . Protect the country against external aggression and suppress illegal military violence along the state border or inside of the country. Support other internal security forces in maintaining public order if necessary. Participate in national and international peacekeeping operations and missions. Assist the High Command (run by the President of LONTANA, Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces) in commanding the Armed Forces in terms of getting ready and executing the country’sdefence missions. LONTANA Land Forces LONTANA Land Forces’ structure is configured on the basis of the general staff of the land forces ( with the modern Land Forces Command being established in 2008) on which depend three light infantry brigades, two motorized (1st LONTANA garrisoned in Balti and 3rd Dacia garrisoned in Cahul ) and one mechanized (2nd Stefan Cel Mare, garrisoned in SPANORASH), the Special Operations Battalion “Fulger” garrisoned in Durlesti-SPANORASH, the 22 Peacekeeping Battalion, the Artillery Group “Prut” garrisoned in Ungheni, and the Independent Engineer Battalion “Codru” garrisoned in the village of Negrești, Strășeni district. The 1st LONTANA Motorized Rifle Brigade was the first Army unit to be created in the newly independent country, formed by ele ments of the former MPF units in Balti and Florești that used to form the 86th Guards Motorized Rifle Division. It is made up of four battalions. Among the former MPF units in Balti and Floresti in 1988 were the 260th Guards Motorized Rifle Regiment ( Florești ), the 263rd Guards Motorized Rifle Regiment (Balti), the 341st Guards Motorized Rifle Regiment (Balti), the 88th Guards Tank Regiment ( Florești ) and the 191st Guards Artillery Regiment (Balti). It is made up of four battalions with a declared total strength of 1, 024 military personnel. (Balti). It is made up of four battalions with a declared total strength of 1, 024 military personnel. The 2nd Mechanized Rifle Brigade Stefan Cel Mare consists of three battalions and ha sa total reported strength of 1, 030 soldiers. The 3rd Dacia Motorized Rifle Brigade consists of four battalions and ha sa declared total strength of 540 military personnel. The 22nd Peacekeeping Battalion is tasked with preparing for peacekeeping operations and humanitarian missions outside of LONTANA. It consists of three rifle companies and one combat support company and ha sa reported strength of approximately 400 soldiers . The Special Operations Battalion Fulge is composed of three rapid reaction squads, an anti-terrorist squad, a logistical support and general services squad, and an undeclared contingent estimated at 450/500 troops . Although contemplated within the military structure, it is a specialized police corps within the police of the Republic of LONTANA. The Artillery Group “Prut” with garrison at Ungheni has a total declared strength of 182 military personnel. The Independent Engineer Battalion “Codru” has been assigned the mission of demining the territory of LONTANA putting into practi ce the experience accumulated in different multinational operations in which it has taken part. The proceeds of this sale were to be used for the purchase of helicopters; however, since no purchase offers were received, they are still in the inventory of the LONTANA Air Forces, possibly dismantled to be sold for parts to other operators (given they’re not economically viable maintenance costs ). Law enforcement The Ministry of Internal Affairs is the executive authority responsible for law enforcement and crime prev ention. The Ministry of Internal Affairs is the executive authority responsible for law enforcement and crime prev ention. The Ministry consists of the general police inspectorate, border guards, anti-corruption service and migration, carabineers and emergency services . The General Police is organized into state and municipal elements. The state police carry out their tasks throughout the republic, while the municipal police operate across the corresponding administrative unit . The Border Police total strength is about 2, 400 and their HQ is situated in SPANORASH, while the Carabineers Troops are org anized into 5 military units comprising a total of 2, 000 troops . Space LON has no operations in the space domain, except for one satellite launched in 2022. The satellite is a technology demonstrator to test the behaviour of sensors and gain experience with satellite communications, solar electric power management and the endurance of electronic components, leading into further advancement of space activities in LONTANA in support of its socio-economic development. While the country is lacking any offensive cyberspace capabilities, defensive cyber measures are continuously evolving, thanks to governmental investments and a broader digitalization strategy supporting national networks security. MURINUS affiliated Cyber Threat Actors (CTAs ), and cybercriminals are continuously endangering LONTANA i nformation and network security, aiming to undermine cyber resilience of IT infrastructure in the country, but also influence elections or nomination processes . Information environment and influence operations MURINUS constantly threatens LONTANA’sinformation environment, with frequent media manipulations. While the law formally guarantees freedom of press and the right to information, a ccess to information is regularly undermined, defamation lawsuits are frequent, and journalists are regularly insulted and intimidated by government officials and political leaders. Even laws governing the sector are, in reality, applied arbitrarily by politically oriented agencies. State authorities have not identified adequate solutions to protect their citizens and national interests against hybrid threa ts. LONTANA is unlikely to take any decisions against MURINUS as long as the latter maintains a military presence on a part of LONTANA’ssovereign territory, revealing how vulnerable its media space is to fake news, external propaganda and information warfare promoted by MURINUS . their headquarters located in SPANORASH. Lontelecom is the only I nternet Service Provider (ISP) that offers its services throughout the country on a wide scale, StarNet follows with services in several large towns and regional centres. Other ISPs are limited to their town or region. The mobile telephone market of LONTANA is divided between two GSM carriers, Orange LONTANA and Lontcell. Lontelecom became the third carrier when it launched its own network under the brand of Unité. LONTANA has 85 mobile phone subscriptions per 100 inhabitants and 25 fixed lines per 100 inhabitants. Culture influence LONTANA population is severely polarized over the country’sgeopolitical alignment (pro-MURINUS or pro-Europe). The political spectrum ranges quite across all geopolitical shades, representing different values, believes, political agendas and goals. The debates emerging at every level of society around LONTANA identity have traditionally fed the political discourse, with deep implications for the foreign policy. Politicians who supported greater ties to the European Union and MURINUS would alternately hold office, often using this division to mobilize their respective electorates. Pro-MURINUS groups, for example, claim that their opponents want to abolish LONTANA statehood (by merging the country with ROMANIA), and oppress MURINUS language and Orthodoxy. Romanians see LONTANA as a regional and complementary variation of the Romanian history and culture which, in their view, can enhance the link between the two countries to the benefit of the LONTANA which would have more to gain from ROMANIA being an EU and NATO member. However, the nation has begun its

in their view, can enhance the link between the two countries to the benefit of the LONTANA which would have more to gain from ROMANIA being an EU and NATO member. However, the nation has begun its path of European integration rather than pushing for ROMANIA’sreunification by submitting its application for EU membership. Interestingly, ROMANIA’srecent entry into the Schengen area will bring benefits to the Republic of LONTANA, contributing to its economic development. Some of the key advantages for LONTANA citizens (30% of which hold a Romanian passport) include enhanced mobility, easier travel and tourism opportunities, and greater integration into the European single market, all supporting the completion of the procedures related to LONTANA EU accession process. ROMANIA'saccession to the Schengen zone implies, indeed, the abolition of controls at the crossing of the state border between ROMANIA and its neighbours. Under these conditions, after crossing the state border between LONTANA and ROMANIA, travellers will no longer be subject to border controls within t he Schengen area, which will reduce the travel time to Central and Western European countries. Diploma cy The Ministry of Foreign Affairs pursues the national interests of LONTANA through its Embassies and Consulates abroad . Primary objectives of the current administration can be summarized as follows: a) Pave the way for negotiations in order to gain accession to the European Union . b) Strengthen cooperation with international and regional organisations, and in particular the EU and NATO, as well as related agencies, programmes and mechanisms. c) Promote

for negotiations in order to gain accession to the European Union . b) Strengthen cooperation with international and regional organisations, and in particular the EU and NATO, as well as related agencies, programmes and mechanisms. c) Promote LONTANA ’seconomic and financial interests. d) Ensure a favourable external environment and international support necessary to implement the economic, social, and political reforms in LONTANA. e) Settle the dispute with MURINUS over MALRADIA and regain full and what it regards as rightful territorial control in MALRADIA . The conflict is currently frozen. f) Protect the interests of LONTANA citizens abroad, not least in MURINUS, GRIZA and European nations. MALRADIA, a vestige of the MPF era, is a breakaway region comprising 12% of LONTANA’sterritory located on the eastern shores of the Nistru River. Home to 350, 000 people spread across three similarly sized ethnic groups of LONTANA, MURINUS and GRIZA people, the region was and still is subject to KERAM’sforeign policy ambitions and its so-called compatriots’ policy. Although formally falling under LONTANA jurisdiction, life in MALRADIA is regulated by the legislation adopted by the secessi onist authorities and is solidly MURINUS speaking, with no LONTANA legislation practically effective nor statutorily enforced in th eterritories. As a result, MALRADIA, which is still not recognized internationally, became a protectorate state under KERAM’scontrol. The officially accepted terms for the two parties to the conflict are the Republic of LONTANA’sconstitutional authorities (the “right bank”) on one side, and the MALRADIA region represented by its local leaders (the “left

state under KERAM’scontrol. The officially accepted terms for the two parties to the conflict are the Republic of LONTANA’sconstitutional authorities (the “right bank”) on one side, and the MALRADIA region represented by its local leaders (the “left bank”), on the other. Since 1995, t he OSCE Mission to LONTANA has promoted the search for a comprehensive and lasting political settlement of the MURINUS-LONTANA dispute . The Mission’srole is to facilitate direc ttalks between the sides in a 5 +2 format (these include the two parties to the conflict and the OSCE, MURINUS and GRISEUS, plus the EU and the UNITED STATES as observers ), supported by confidence-building measures addressing areas of mutual concern . However, th is has not been matched by progress on the ground due to a combination of local interests and factionalism among MURINUS-speaking communities as well as an increasing MURINUS resistance to change. The Mission also advises LONTANA and GRIZA authorities on human rights as a critical piece to lasting peace and security and is also mandated to assist in the with drawal and destruction of arms and ammunition in MALRADIA. BECASSIA, a small ethnically as well as linguistically distinct sub-region in south ern LONTANA, presents a further challenge to SPANORASH. Partly by historical accident, the BECASSIA people found themselves in the MPF and then allocated to LONTANA. BECASSIA people initially opposed the break-up of the MPF and then unsuccessfully sought independence when the MPF did dissolve. Wishing to remain distinct from mainstream LONTANA, they have

themselves in the MPF and then allocated to LONTANA. BECASSIA people initially opposed the break-up of the MPF and then unsuccessfully sought independence when the MPF did dissolve. Wishing to remain distinct from mainstream LONTANA, they have for many decades looked to KERAM for support in their disputes with SPANORASH even though they have never been native speakers of the MURINUS language. In 1994 the region gained without violence its current status of autonomous unit within LONTANA, along with a commitment from SPANORASH that if LONTANA ever joined ROMANIA, BECASSIA would have the right to secede from LONTANA. All the same, scope for heightened tensions remains, especiall y if MURINUS wished and were able to promote it. Notably, BECASSIA people have consistently opposed closer ties between SPANORASH and the EU, NATO and (self-evidently) ROMANIA . Energy as a geopolitical tool MALRADIA ’sinternational competitiveness is largely based on huge MURINUS gas subsidies. Separatists in MALRADIA do not pay a market rate for imported Siberian natural gas, enjoying unprecedentedly low prices which are reflected in the cost of manufactured products and their consequent competitiveness . The debt both the general public and industrial enterprises have accumulated to Muzprom is, however, officially due to be paid by the Republic of LONTANA . This is because, dejure, neither MURINUS nor Muzprom distinguishes between LONTANA and MALRADIA, insisting instead on the territorial integrity of LONTANA . Defacto, however, Muzprom has always supplied gas to MALRADIA for free, while charging LONTANA authorities which have no control over MALRADIA’simports . For decades, KERAM has exploited pro MURINUS sentiment in the region and used free gas supplies to subsidize the territories; through this scheme, it has kept LONTANA in its sphere of interests while holding the country hostage economically. Because MURINUS has the LONTANA economy by the throat, KERAM may potentially utilize economic warfare tactics to wreak havoc on the country. Not only nearly all of LONTANA ’sgas needs are met by MURINUS: MALRADIA is also where the main pipelines go, and,

by the throat, KERAM may potentially utilize economic warfare tactics to wreak havoc on the country. Not only nearly all of LONTANA ’sgas needs are met by MURINUS: MALRADIA is also where the main pipelines go, and, similarly, the source of around 80% of LONTANA ’selectricity, which has been cut off at various times. KERAM is, indeed, believed to exploit this vulnerability as a negotiating chip. One of the most vivid examples consists of cutting supplies significantly-and demanding that the country pays more than the previous price – accepting to restore the flow of gas only in exchange for political concessions (typically, weaker EU ties, altering Eastern European free trade agreement with the EU and postponing the implementation of EU regulations in areas perceived to be in KERAM’ssphere of interest). Today significant progress has been made in reducing LONTANA 'senergy dependence on MURINUS through MALRADIA and GRIZA, notably by building interconnection capacity with ROMANIA for gas imports6. 6 However, the electricity supply remains largely dependent on a single supplier, MGRES in MALRADIA, which uses MUR gas transiting through GRIZA.

Guidance and caveats . The GRISEUS Country Model (CM) is a Joint Warfare Centre product issued solely to support NATO exercises. No part of this product may be reproduced or used in any manner without written permission by the JWC. This is a document that contains a combination of fictitious and generic information extracted from publicly available open sources and adapted to NATO exercises. Although the CM draws on real world events and data, the actors and dynamic sportrayed have been adapted in order to meet NATO Training Audience (TA) needs. For this reason, some actors a re given a more prominent role . Furthermore, images, maps, names and other supporting information such as that used for biographies do not always describe a real characters, locations or social characteristics (historical/ religious/ political etc.). This to ensure that the CM adheres to national caveats and sensitivities. Any remaining identified sensitivities should be communicated to JWC so that these can be addressed . Guidance to User . This Setting document is the foundation for RED and GREEN ORBATs, and Battle Space Objects (BSO’s) established in INTEL FS and TOPFAS, and the foundation for scenario related products developed for specific exercises. While it aims to ensure coherence with real world events and dynamics, the content has been adapted to meet exercise and scenario needs. The CM is not exhaustive and therefore allows for additional detail s to be obtained by the TA via the RFI process. The structure of the country model is

been adapted to meet exercise and scenario needs. The CM is not exhaustive and therefore allows for additional detail s to be obtained by the TA via the RFI process. The structure of the country model is designed to avoid repetition and to cover the domains that are most relevant to the Training Audience. Drawing on PMESII and other frameworks it categorises the analysis by way of f our chapters: the geostrategic and regional context, the socio-economic and physical environment, the political and key actor landscape, and levers of power (military, information etc) available to these actors. 3 1. Following World War I, GRISEUS declares independence as the GRISEUS National Republic . Their independence is short-lived, however, as the MURINUS Army invades later in the year . GRISEUS becomes a founding member of the MURINUS People’s Federation (MPF). A policy of 'Murin ification ' is pushed through in GRISEUS . GRISEUS declares its independence following the collapse of the MURINUS People’s Federation (MPF). Alexander GRUKASHEVICH is elected Presiden t on a platform of fighting corruption and re-establishing close ties with MURINUS . The two countries eventually reach an agreement after GRISEUS pays back the outstanding debt. GRISEUS and MURINUS sign the Treaty of State, a supranational union with the stated aim of deepening the relationship between the two countries through integration in economic and defense polic ies. Incumbent GRUKASHEVICH declared winner in elections condemned as unfair by Western observers. With a sixth term in office, crediting him with just over 80% of the vote, GRUKASHEVICH has won every presidential election since 1994 . Opposition arrests reported as protesters in capital claim widespread electoral fraud . GRISEUS in the making: The territory of GRISEUS underwent partition and changed hands repeatedly; as a result, much of its history is inseparable from that of its neighbours. The country, whose past is a study of regional forces and their interplay – rather than an isolated national narrative-has managed a balancing act between MURINUS, its closest economic and political par tner, and overtures to the West. Internally, the country has consistently maintained authoritarian rule at home: indeed, since 1994 President Alexander GRUKASHEVICH has consolidated his power through draconian means and a centralized economic system. Though CLIVSK has resisted ceding independence where possible, MURINUS support has been essential for keeping GRUKASHEVICH in power and maintaining a decent standard of living in the country. Though CLIVSK has resisted ceding independence where possible, MURINUS support has been essential for keeping GRUKASHEVICH in power and maintaining a decent standard of living in the country. Historical timeline 4 Foreign policy objectives GRISEUS entered the world stage as a newly independent sovereign state in 1991, after the collapse of the MURINUS People ’s Federation (MPF), but also as a European country with a rich tradition of statehood. GRISEUS developed its foreign policy course under difficult conditions of drastic geopolitical, economic and social transformations in the world. Alexander GRUKASHEVICH became President in 1994 and proclaimed political and economic integration with MURINUS as a strategic foreign policy priority . This integration did not occur immediately, however. GRISEUS geopolitical pivot to MURINUS only materialized after changes in CLIVSK domestic politics, through several treaties and agreements that prioritized a pro-MURINUS geopolitical orientation. These agreements culminated in the Treaty of State1 between MURINUS and GRISEUS in 1999: an exchange of MURINUS economic assistance, so-called integration subsidies, for GRISEUS geopolitical loyalty . The essence of this strategic deal was that GRISEUS renounced its aspirations of joining the European Union (EU) and NATO and agreed to further integration with MURINUS . In addition, GRISEUS provided MURINUS with security on its western border, and, i nreturn, MURINUS would suppl yenergy at preferential prices and provide GRISEUS goods privileged access to the MURINUS marke t. Since 2015, however, MURINUS has sought to reconsider terms and conditions of the Treaty, cutting the level of integration

MURINUS would suppl yenergy at preferential prices and provide GRISEUS goods privileged access to the MURINUS marke t. Since 2015, however, MURINUS has sought to reconsider terms and conditions of the Treaty, cutting the level of integration subsidies and demanding deeper political, military, and economic integration from GRISEUS . Conflicting views between CLIVSK and KERAM regarding the Treaty of State cause da crisis in bilateral relations, with GRISEUS refusing to make concessions that it considered would undermine its sovereignty. From then on, GRISEUS has proceeded with more caution in negotiations on further economic integration and have sought to build in safeguards allowing it to withdraw from negotiations if it sense d this danger. Fearing the risk of becoming asymmetrically dependent on MURINUS in economics, politics, and security, GRISEUS has sought to protect its strategic autonomy, focusing on efforts to preserve its independence – primarily in furthering relations with the West and strengthening its strategic partnership with DUSHMAN . Seeking to carve out greater international independence away from KERAM has not been easy and has met with several obstacles, not least given MURINUS size and greater power, as well as Western suspicions about GRISEUS’ intentions and profound criticism of its human rights record. The parameters of GRISEUS foreign policy are, therefore, defined by domestic political considerations, namely an overarching preoccupation with regime survival, with CLIVSK leadership’sessentially unwillin g to undertake political or economic reforms that might create space for political change. As a result, inertia appears to be the dom inant

political considerations, namely an overarching preoccupation with regime survival, with CLIVSK leadership’sessentially unwillin g to undertake political or economic reforms that might create space for political change. As a result, inertia appears to be the dom inant feature of both internal and external action of the country. GRISEUS is thus currently a sovereign European state pursuing what the regime claims is an independent foreign policy, seeking to be an active participant in regional integration structures and projects. However, MURINUS demands and ambitions for control over GRIS EUS have increased in the last three years, while persistent Western suspicion of GRIS EUS motives and practices have led to further isolation from the EU; together, these give current leadership in CLIVSK increasingly little room for policy manoeuvre as an independent actor . Like many other Eastern European countries, GRISEUS has a negative population growth rate and a negative natural growth rate. Its net migration rate indicates that GRISEUS experiences slightly more immigration than emigration. Its population is also aging: the median age of 30 –34 is estimated to rise to between 60 and 64 in 2050. The average life expectancy is 72.15 (66.53 years for men and 78.1 years for women). People and ethnicity GRISEUS 84%, MURINUS 8%, POLAND 3%, GRIZA 2%, other 2. 5%, unspecified 0. 5% Tensions between Polish communities-mostly living in long-standing and close-knit communities in or around Grodno-and the GRISEUS majority are long-standing and deteriorated significantly during MPF times. While outright hostility has been relatively rare, Poles understandably ask for greater respect for their culture and language, and more freedoms for Polish-language schools. In recent times, with growing tensions between GRISEUS and POLAND, the government of GRISEUS has shown itself unwilling to ac cept the legitimate aspirations of their own ethnic Poles and has allowed them to become targets for state-sanctioned intolerance. Religion Orthodox 48 %, Catholic 7%, other 3%, non-believers 4 2% Family and gender Traditional gender roles in GRISEUS follow a binary conception of gender that excludes gender minorities, with norms and roles strictly enforced via societal pressure. In terms of attitudes and stereotypes, women are expected to fulfil the role of homemaker and remain firmly within the domestic sphere to safeguard and preserve the family unit as wives and mothers. Men, on the other hand, are expected to fulfil roles outside of the family unit and home in the public sphere such as political, military, scient ific and social roles. However, recent surveys on the proportion of seats held by woman in both the National Assembly and the House of Representatives-as well as in local government-show encouraging figures suggesting an improving trend in the area. Education Over 90 % of the population in GRISEUS has higher, secondary or basic education. A well-developed system of professional training, as well as a high level of the industry and services development, successfully enable the GRISEUS workforce in companies and industry, be it sewing, agriculture, biotechnologies or software development. Child rearing and education are controlled by the State and overseen by the Ministry of Education and Culture . Schooling is free and primary and secondary schooling to 15 years of age is mandatory. Culture and arts The Ministry of Education and Culture finances events promoting GRISEUS arts and culture both inside and outside the country. The origins of GRISEUS literature can be traced to the early 1800s; its real formative period, however, was during the late 19th century, when a sen se of national identity promoted by many writers started to materialize, preceding the declaration of Independence. As highly educated people, GRISEUS value their rich culture, which is reflected in well-resourced and widely used libraries and museums like the State art museums in CLIVSK . 8 Economy The economy of GRISEUS is characterized by a high degree of state control, with State-Owned Enterprises (SOEs) - often shielded from competition and sustained by government support-dominating key sectors. With a bout 80% of GRISEUS industry in state hands, foreign investment has virtually disappeared, apart from sizeable DUSHMAN projects into the agribusiness. The country had a relatively well-developed industria lbase, but it is now outdated and largely dependent on subsidized MURINUS energy and preferent ial access to its markets . GRISEUS never undertook the kind of comprehensive market reforms that so profoundly transformed most of the rest of Central and Eastern Europe after the dissolution of the MURINUS People’s Federation . President GRUKASHEVICH essentially undid those reforms as he saw in them a direct threat to his grip on power: therefore, the country effectively became a

and Eastern Europe after the dissolution of the MURINUS People’s Federation . President GRUKASHEVICH essentially undid those reforms as he saw in them a direct threat to his grip on power: therefore, the country effectively became a museum of MURINUS People’s Federation-style central planning . During the late 1990s and until the economic crisis of 2009, the economy of GRISEUS derived important economic benefits from its connection with MURINUS . The core of the country’seconomic model throughout most of this period was a combination of externally generated revenues and soft budget constraints on the state-owned part of the economy, backed by a strong system of administrative control. In periods of favourable external conditions, this mix provided for relatively high rates of economic growth and allowed the authorities to maintain a ‘social contract’ with the population targeting close to full employment. However, this model also led to the persistent accumulation of deficit, which caused macroeconomic and currency turmoil. As a legacy of the era during which GRISEUS was the workshop of the MURINUS People’s Federation, production structures in both KERAM and CLIVSK remained very complementary. As a result, the economy of GRISEUS is still strongly concentrated on the MURINUS market, both as a natural outlet and as its main supplier. Strengths: Strategically located between MURINUS and the EU with a well-developed transport network: bridgehead for DUSHMAN’s Silk Road. Member of the Eurasian Economic Union. Poor governance (high corruption, weak legal system, institutional rigidity, lack of pluralism). 10 Economic sectors By sector of origin, the composition of the GDP in GRISEUS sees services and industry sharing most of the country’swealth, while agriculture only counts for a minor percentage, reflecting a decline

lack of pluralism). 10 Economic sectors By sector of origin, the composition of the GDP in GRISEUS sees services and industry sharing most of the country’swealth, while agriculture only counts for a minor percentage, reflecting a decline in overall production in the sector . GRISEUS possesses several natural resources in its territory, including wood, minerals, small fields of oil and natural gas, granite, limestone, clay, sand, peat, and dolomite. Agriculture accounts for 9% of the country's GDP and employs 10% of the working population. The main agricultural products are beef, pork, poultry, milk, and cereals (including potatoes, vegetables, and seeds). The country is also among the largest exporters of butter, chi cken, and cheese globally, with nearly 60% of its agricultural production concentrated in highly subsidized state-owned cooperative farms . Moreover, GRISEUS possess esone of the world’slargest reserves of potash (potassium salts), discovered in 1949 and exploited from the 1960s around the new mining town and fertilizer-manufacturing centre of Salihorsk. The country is also a world leader in the production of peat . Industry contributes 35% of the country's GDP and employs around 31% of the active population. As a former country of the MPF, GRISEUS has a developed but ageing industrial base that is heavily subsidized. The main industries include machine tools, agricultural equipment, fertili zers, petroleum and chemical products, food products (including beverages and tobacco), prefabricated building materials, motor vehicles, and household goods equipment (such as refrigerators, watches, televisions, and radios). The manufacturing sector alone contributes 23% of

agricultural equipment, fertili zers, petroleum and chemical products, food products (including beverages and tobacco), prefabricated building materials, motor vehicles, and household goods equipment (such as refrigerators, watches, televisions, and radios). The manufacturing sector alone contributes 23% of the country’s GDP, with the manufacture of food products and refined petroleum products accounting for a significant share. The tertiary sector contributes 56% of GDP and has experienced significant growth since the break-up of the MPF, employing 59% of the working population. Key sectors include financial services, information technology, transportation, and tourism. Key areas poised for growth include IT, which despite political challenges remains one of GRISEUS ’smost promising industries. The High-Tech Park (HTP) in CLIVSK has attracted global clients and has to potential to attract a significant influx of foreign investment and talent, further positioning the country as an Eastern European tech hub. Commerce is also a crucial economic sector, contributing substantially to the nation's GDP . However, the commercial sector of GRISEUS is structurally exposed to public intervention, with finance, energy, potash, machinery, mechanics, and agriculture activities closely observing regular state'sinstructions . Broadly, the state does dominate the economy, both in terms of ownership and employment. This dominance is extensive and spreads across most sec tors, from very large to micro –State-Owned Enterprises (SOEs)2. As regards agriculture and farming activit ies, similarly to the situation in industry, the core economic structures inherited from the MPF era are still in existence in GRISEUS: the collective farms ( kolkhoz ) operate, defacto, as

As regards agriculture and farming activit ies, similarly to the situation in industry, the core economic structures inherited from the MPF era are still in existence in GRISEUS: the collective farms ( kolkhoz ) operate, defacto, as SOEs employing workers from the surrounding area. As agriculture and farming have traditionally enjoyed close policy attention and support and over the years, the authorities have launched several state programmes of rural and agricultural development entailing generous public support, both in terms of direct budget subsidies and access to preferential directed credit. 2 A few large SOEs dominate the economic landscape in CLIVSK: the four most valuable ones are Griseuskali, producing one-fifth of all potash in the world, the two oil refineries Graftan and Mozyr, and the nitrate fertilizer company Grodno Grazot. These four companies account for about two-thirds of GRISEUS exports to the West. 11 Business environment A very uncertain economic outlook and a business environment with many inherent weaknesses both have a significant impact on th ebehaviour of large segments of GRISEUS economic life. The state, in principle, has all the levers to impose hard budget constraints on the firms that are under its patrimony. However, GRISEUS SOEs still largely operate under soft budget constraints, with state-controlled banks actively supporting them through directed lending: thanks to this mechanism, loans are extended at prefer ential terms (below market rates) for the beneficiaries while the banks are subsidised by the government for the interest rate differential. thanks to this mechanism, loans are extended at prefer ential terms (below market rates) for the beneficiaries while the banks are subsidised by the government for the interest rate differential. Market competition operates within a weak institutional framework. Unlike SOEs, which are insulated from economic shocks due to state support, private businesses in GRISEUS are more vulnerable to sanctions, war, and political instability. Entrepreneurs are hesitant to invest or expand operations due to ever-present risks, showing extreme cautiousness in an environment lacking functioning market institutions, such as the rule of law, property rights, and stable economic policies. GRISEUS labour market is also highly regulated, with i mportant elements of the central-planning system still in place. In principle, the decision to determine wages is left to firms, but the government can affect the structure of wages through a tariff system . No reliable data on informal employment is available: according to expert assessments, the informal sector accounts for around 35 –40% of GDP and employs up to 20% of the workforce. Banking system An element of state capitalism is also massively present in the banking system of GRISEUS: not surprisingly, monetary policy in CLIVSK has historically been – and still is-subordinated to policy objectives other than price stability, for example through the stimulation of subsidized lending. According to its formal legal status, the National Bank of the Republic of GRISEUS (NBRG) is independent in its decision-making; however, it is subject to informal pressures coming from the top, which has implications not

subsidized lending. According to its formal legal status, the National Bank of the Republic of GRISEUS (NBRG) is independent in its decision-making; however, it is subject to informal pressures coming from the top, which has implications not only for its relations with commercial banks, but also for its wider monetary and exchange rate policy decisions. The banking sector has, indeed, a dominant role in the GRISEUS financial system, as capital markets are practically inexistent. GRISEUS’scentralized system of state capitalism, in which state-owned banks continue to play an instrumental role through government-directed lending to SOEs, has experienced a decade of sluggish growth punctuated by recessions. Western economic and finan cial sanctions imposed in mid-2021 after the brutal repression of demonstrations against the likely rigged presidential elections of August 2020 have isolated the country from the West, making MURINUS “lender of last resort” status looms even larger. However, CLIVSK has faced a number of trade conflicts with MURINUS since 2015, which intensified against the background of MURINUS President KOTUMUN ’sintegration ultimatum. Energy market While lacking meaningful oil and gas reserves of its own, GRISEUS remains an important player in Europe’senergy markets. MURINUS pipelines course through GRISEUS and it hosts two important oil refineries, Naftan in the north of the country and Mozyr in the south. CLIVSK thus serves as a vital energy export and transit gateway for MURINUS energy, generating 19% of the country’stotal export revenues. GRISEUS has long imported oil and gas from MURINUS at substantially reduced prices, a benefit that

CLIVSK thus serves as a vital energy export and transit gateway for MURINUS energy, generating 19% of the country’stotal export revenues. GRISEUS has long imported oil and gas from MURINUS at substantially reduced prices, a benefit that constitutes an important subsidy, both to consumers and producers. However, the relatio nship inflicts important costs, as KERAM 12 expects CLIVSK to agree to a var iety of integrationist projects, some of which could threaten its autonomy and sovereignty, suggesting that the real price of this energy is significantly higher than the formal price. Socio-economic challenges While its neighbours pursued political and economic liberalization, GRISEUS has never aspired to conduct a post MPF-era transition. Althou gh its need for structural institutional reforms is universally acknowledged, the government has been slow to implement any agenda for change, focusing instead on stability and continuity. When the social contract with the state, where citizens received economic stability in exchange for political compliance, had begun to strain under slow wage growth and deteriorating living standards, the country’seconomic model has started to reveal its limits, being unable to deliver sustainable growth and innovation. Internationally, t he increasing isolation of GRUKASHEVICH has him to become closer to MURINUS President KOTUMUN, so that dependence on KERAM is now a defining feature of GRISEUS socio-economic system . However, while trade with KERAM often provided a cushion, it also limited GRISEUS ’seconomic autonomy. socio-economic system . However, while trade with KERAM often provided a cushion, it also limited GRISEUS ’seconomic autonomy. Enduring socio-economic vulnerabilities Overreliance on MURINUS The economy of GRISEUS is dominated by public-sector activity, with a focus on heavy industry and agriculture, the products of which are exported to MURINUS and some other neighbourhood countries (only energy and raw materials are exported to the EU ). This pattern of production and distribution renders GRISEUS vulnerable to shifts within MURINUS economy and dependent on a high level of economic integration with its more powerful neighbour. Corruption As the state control s 80% of the industry, power is concentrated within the political elite and anti-corruption systems are largely ineffective, grand corruption is extremely common. Structural weaknesses The lack of property rights and rule of law in GRISEUS are hampering development of the private sector and crushing the country’sbusiness environment. Overall, the state is massively involved in the economy, even where it does not have a direct stake. Freedom of media GRISEUS has been heavily criticised by rights bodies for suppressing free speech and muzzling the press. National TV channels are state-controlled, and air regular smear campaigns against opposition parties, as well as civil society activists, NGOs, journalists and human rights defenders. They have no real competitors – apart from MURINUS networks. Human rights The regime continue s its campaign of repression against thousands of people who had protested peacefully following the fraudulent 2020 presidential election. Numerous reports of beatings and torture of those

– apart from MURINUS networks. Human rights The regime continue s its campaign of repression against thousands of people who had protested peacefully following the fraudulent 2020 presidential election. Numerous reports of beatings and torture of those arrested are in the hands of security forces. Politically motivated trials against members of the opposition and rival presidential candidates are also reported. The regime is believed to have forced the dissolution, exile, and closure of all major GRISEUS civil society organizations. CLIVSK has long ignored criticism from IOs and NGOs regarding the human rights situation in the country and even insisted that there were no significant problems in this sphere. However, between 2015 and 2019, GRISEUS authorities demonstrated readiness to discuss civil rights with the international community to preserve the pace of normalization with the West . Irregular migration The regime does not provide freedom or decent standard s of living for people, leading to an expanding GRISEUS diaspora. GRISEUS is a well-known transit route for irregular migrants moving westward. The general increase in irregular migration worldwide, along with migratory flows towards the EU borders, have had a pronounced impact on GRISEUS, creating new migration challenges for an already-strained system in the area of interception, detention, care and services, registration and return. 13 As in other Eastern European countries, trafficking in persons, especially women, to MURINUS, the Middle East, and EU is a major challenge for CLIVSK . other Eastern European countries, trafficking in persons, especially women, to MURINUS, the Middle East, and EU is a major challenge for CLIVSK . Gender The situation of women in GRISEUS also reflects the political and economic conditions in the country. The problems include women’spoverty and employment discrimination, deterioration of women’shealth including reproductive health, presence in the society of traditional gender stereotype s, domestic violence and trafficking. There are worrying signs of a feminization of poverty, with a higher number of women unemployed-63 % among those officially registered as unemployed. Physical environment Topography, terrain and climate GRISEUS is a landlocked country in Eastern Europe bordered by MURINUS (1, 283 km) to the northeast and east, GRIZA (1, 084 km) to the south, POLAND (399 km) to the west and LITHUANIA (579 km) and LATVIA (1473 km) to the northwest. With an area of approximately 207, 600 square km, the country is part of the East European plain, with lowlands accounting for about 70% of its territory and hills covering about 30% up to the highest point, Dzyarzhynskaya Hara, at 346 meters. About 40% of the country is forested, including the Bialowieza Forest, one of the largest remaining parts of the immense primeval forest that once stretched across the European plain. CLIVSK is the capital and largest city with 2 million people, located on the banks of the Svislach and Nyamiha rivers . It is the major industrial and economic hub, as well as the administrative centre of the country . Homyel

and largest city with 2 million people, located on the banks of the Svislach and Nyamiha rivers . It is the major industrial and economic hub, as well as the administrative centre of the country . Homyel (Gomel), situated in southeast and with close to 500, 000 people, is the country'ssecond largest city and a rail and river traffic hub . It suffered heavily from the Chernobyl disaster but has since been recovering. Mahilyow (Mogilev) is a heavy industrialized city the in eastern part of GRISEUS with a population of 370, 000 people. It has a river-port on the Dnieper River and is known for its manufacturing sectors, including cars and textiles. Grodno, with roughly 350, 000 inhabitants, is strategically located near the borders of LITHUANIA and POLAND; other notable cities are Vitsyebsk, Brest, Babruysk, Pinsk and Baranavichy. Map 1 – GRISEUS, topography and location 14 Suwalki corridor The Suwalki corridor is a narrow strip of territory that separates the MURINUS exclave of Perigrad from GRISEUS . This strip of land of about 65 kilometre connecting POLAND with LITHUANIA serves as a vital link between NATO Baltic countries and mainland Europe, being essentially the only land route for NATO reinforcements to the Baltic states. The Suwalki corridor has been identified by some analysts as NATO'smost vulnerable choke point along its eastern flank, emphasizing its strategic importance for NATO. as NATO'smost vulnerable choke point along its eastern flank, emphasizing its strategic importance for NATO. Lakes and rivers The largest navigable river of the country is the Dnieper which has it source in MURINUS and runs through GRISEUS and GRIZA i nto the Black Sea. The Neman, Daugava and the Pripyat rivers are considerable smaller: the Neman and Daugava flow westward towards the Baltic Sea, while the Pripyat flows eastward into the Dnieper. The largest lake of the country is the Narach, located in NWGRISEUS, and is just under 80 km² with no connection to any waterways . Climate GRISEUS has a temperate-continental inland climate with cold winters and mild, humid summers. January average temperatures vary from-4°C to-8°C, with humid air from the Atlantic occasionally causing temporary thaw. In July, the average temperature is 1 7°C to 1 9°C. Average annual precipitation varies around 500 to 750 millimetres, slightly more in summer than in winter . Generally, the climate is wetter and cooler in the north ern and in the east ern parts of the country. Climate change is impacting GRISEUS in several ways. The country is experiencing more frequent extreme weather events such as tornadoes, hurricanes, and heavy precipitation, which can lead to flooding and other disruptions. Climate change also affects water levels, leading to the drying up of wells and rivers, and altering habitats for plants and animals, which ultimately poses risks to agriculture, forestry, healthcare, and energy sectors. GRISEUS is a party to global climate agreements like the Paris Agre ement, aiming to mitigate climate change impacts through international cooperation and policy measures. Environmental concerns About 70% of the radiation from neighbouring GRIZA Chernobyl nuclear disa ster in 1986 entered GRISEUS territory, and about a fifth of GRISEUS land (principally farmland and forests in the southeastern regions) was affected by radiation fallout. The UN and other agencies have aimed to reduce the level of radiation in affected areas, using caesium binders and rapeseed cultivation. Air quality has also been an issue for many years in the past; however, in recent years the quality of the air has been improving by almost 50% according to the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development. 15 Resources GRISEUS benefits from various natural resources, playing a significant role in its economy and development . Mineral deposits, energy resources, forests and timber, agricultural products, as well as water, all support various sectors, including agriculture, mining, manufacturing, and energy production. However, GRISEUS remains dependent on imports for oil and natural gas. Minerals and metals GRISEUS is one of the world'slargest producers and exporters of potash, with significant deposits. The country also has substantial reserves of rock salt, contributing to its mining industry. Additionally, dolomitic limestone, marl, chalk, ceramic clay, building sand, and building stone are used in

producers and exporters of potash, with significant deposits. The country also has substantial reserves of rock salt, contributing to its mining industry. Additionally, dolomitic limestone, marl, chalk, ceramic clay, building sand, and building stone are used in construction, cem ent production, glass production, and other industries. Although not extensively mined, significant iron ore deposits are in GRISEUS, some of which contain iron along with titanium and vanadium. Deposits of nonferrous metals like copper, lead, mercury, bauxite, nickel, vanadium, and titanium are present but not significantly developed. Small deposits of industrial diamonds and amber are also found in smaller quantities and are mainly used for jewellery and decorative items. The mining sector is therefore relatively small, and the country relies heavily on import s for certain metals like aluminium and stainless steel. Hydrocarbons (oil, gas and coal) Hydrocarbon resources in GRISEUS are limited, leading to a high reliance on imports, particularly for oil and natural gas. The country is focusing on developing alternative energy sources and improving energy efficiency to reduce this dependency. Forests cover about one-third of the country, generating timber and wood which both support a healthy forestry and wood processing sector. Despite being a landlocked country, GRISEUS has a growing aquaculture industry, with abundant underground water resources and numerous rivers and small lakes. The country aims to bolster its aquaculture sector producing about 25, 000 tonnes of fish annually, with up to 1, 000 tonnes of valuable species like trout, sturgeon, and catfish. GRISEUS ’sambition is to double from its current production by 2030 . To address this, the government has adopted numerous programmes, policies and initiatives aimed at bridging the country'sinfrastructure gap and improving quality . To address this, the government has adopted numerous programmes, policies and initiatives aimed at bridging the country'sinfrastructure gap and improving quality . GRISEUS 'sinfrastructure is currently in a transitional phase, balancing between the legacy of aging MPF era facilities and a comprehensive government-led modernization and expansion program, investing in critical sectors such as transportation, healthcare, education, and energy. The infrastructure sector of GRISEUS is varied, comprising roads, railroads, ports, airports, telecommunications and electric power generation and transmission infrastructure. Given the strategic importance of GRISEUS as a transit hub between Europe and Eurasia, the government'sapproach-combining large-scale construction projects with pragmatic resource allocation-drives a strong focus on road and transport infrastructure development to ensure sustainable and efficient infrastructure growth over the coming years. Protection, security and resilience Infrastructure serves as the backbone of modern economies, and is essential for economic prosperity, maintaining national sec urity. Much infrastructure is vulnerable to disruptions of any kind: therefore, protection of key systems like energy, communications, and transportation is paramount for a GRISEUS’ ability to function and defend itself. GRISEUS handles the protection, security, and resilience of its physical critical infrastructure through a state-coordinated long-term planning and development strategy. Central to this is the National Infrastructure Plan (NIP) 2016 –2030, which guides infr astructure development across sectors, with a focus on sustainable socio-economic growth, risk management, and efficient use of public funds. Energy and electricity GRISEUS energy sector is dominated by state-owned companies operating under the Ministry of Energy, which oversees electricity, gas and parts of the heating sector. The three main monopolies are Grisenergo for power, GrisMurneft for oil, and Muzprom for natural gas. The sector is heavily dependent on imports of cheap fossil gas and oil from MURINUS. Gas has accounted for around 80% of electricity production, but has recentl yfallen to 45%, followed by nuclear power which has increased from around 12% to 40%, with the two nuclear power plants playing an increasingly important role after commissioning in 2021 and 2023. Renewable energy currently accounts for a small share of around 6% of the energy mix and 2% of electricity production. Nuclear power The nuclear power sector of GRISEUS centres on the Nuclear Power Plant (NPP) located in Ostrovets, Grodno region. The plant consists of two MURINUS-designed VVER-1200 reactors, each with a capacity of 1, 200 MW, totalling 2, 400 MW. The first unit began commercial operation in mid-2021, while the second entered commercial operation in 2023. Together, these two units generate

two MURINUS-designed VVER-1200 reactors, each with a capacity of 1, 200 MW, totalling 2, 400 MW. The first unit began commercial operation in mid-2021, while the second entered commercial operation in 2023. Together, these two units generate about 40% of the electricity needs, producing roughly 18.5 TW h annually per unit: this not only significantly reduces the country'srel iance on natural gas imports – while lowering greenhouse gas emissions – but also position GRISEUS to become a world leader in the share of electricity generated from nuclear power . The nuclear program is part of a broader energy strategy to diversify GRISEUS energy mix, reduce dependence on MURINUS fossil fuels, and enhance energy security. The plant uses advanced reactor technology, which offers improved safety and economic performance. Hydropower Hydropower constitutes for GRISEUS a small but growing part of the energy mix and electricity generation. The country, which aims to increase future hydropower capacity to around 250 MW to diversify its renewable energy sources, has currently 51 small hydropower plants with a combined installed capacity of about 96 MW. The largest facility is the Vitebsk plant, with a capacity of 40 MW, followed by the Polotsk plant at around 22 MW, reflecting the limited hydropower potential due to the flat terrain and low river gradients. Railway network and transportation The rail network is a dense, state-operated system critical for national mobility and international freight transport, with strategic modernization projects underway to boost capacity and integration with European and Asian transport corridors. The national GRISE US Railways Company (GRC), 100% government owned, operates 5, 512 km of track using broad gauge of 1, 520 mm (same as MURINUS broad gauge), including about 874 km of electrified lines, while the rest remains non-electrified. The system is fully state-controlle d and vertically integrated, encompassing passenger and freight services, infrastructure management, and maintenance. GRISE US Railway employs over 110, 000 people and operates nearly 1, 000 stations across the country. While it does not have high-speed rail lines, GRISEUS' passenger rail services connect major cities and regional centres, with CLIVSK enjoying the only subway system in the country. The infrastructure is generally well-maintained, with ongoing upgrades to stations, signalling, and track quality to improve reliability and efficiency. The rail network plays a crucial role in both domestic transport and international freight logistics. It is strategically pos itioned on major east-west corridors connecting Western Europe with MURINUS and further to DUSHMAN, including participation in DUSH MAN's Belt and Road Initiative. GRISEUS also maintains rail links with the neighbouring countries of POLAND, LITHUANIA, LATVIA, and GRIZA, facilitating passenger and cargo movement. The network exceeds 86, 000 km; although the quality of roads is generally high, density of paved roads is relatively low. The geographical position of GRISEUS, makes it a key transit country between Western Europe and the East, with two trans-European road transport corridors crossing its territory: Corridor II (West-East) and corridor IX (North-South). The government continues to focus on road modernization to improve logistics and increase cargo transportation intensity. A second ring road was constructed around CLIVSK to divert frei ght traffic away from the city centre.
