Water supply, sewage and dams GRISEUS has developed a comprehensive water supply, sewerage, and dam infrastructure system focused on providing high-quality drinking water, efficient wastewater treatment, and sustainable water resources management . The country ranks high in access to centralized water supply and sanitation systems, with about 93% of the population

dam infrastructure system focused on providing high-quality drinking water, efficient wastewater treatment, and sustainable water resources management . The country ranks high in access to centralized water supply and sanitation systems, with about 93% of the population connected to centralized water supply. On the wastewater side, treatment facilities have been upgraded to enhance environmental protecti on and operational efficiency. A notable example is the Baranovichi wastewater treatment plant, which uses anaerobic digestion of sludge to produce biogas, generating renewable energy that covers about 70% of the plant’senergy needs. This in novation reduces CO2 emissions and sludge volume signi ficantly. The CLIVSK facilities are undergoing upgrades aimed at achieving near zero-waste production by 2028. The dam and reservoir infrastructure plays a key role in water supply and management. The largest artificial reservoir is the Vileyka reservoir created to increase freshwater availability for CLIVSK ensuring a stable water supply . It covers about 65 square kms and holds approximately 238 million cubic meters of water transferred via canals to the capital and surrounding areas . The country operates 51 hydropower plants, many associated with dams on rivers. While these dams support energy production, their construction has raised environmental concerns, such as habitat fragmentation and impacts on fish migration. 4. Communication infrastructure The communications infrastructure of GRISEUS combines a strong state-controlled backbone with competitive mobile operators, extensive fibre-optic networks, near-universal LTE coverage, and planned 5G deployment. These elements support broad internet access, high-quality mobile services, and ongoing digital modernization across the country. The

GRISEUS combines a strong state-controlled backbone with competitive mobile operators, extensive fibre-optic networks, near-universal LTE coverage, and planned 5G deployment. These elements support broad internet access, high-quality mobile services, and ongoing digital modernization across the country. The largest state-owned operator, GRIS telecom, holds a monopoly on fixed-line and backbone data transmission services and controls all international internet connections. It operates an extensive fibre-optic network connecting major cities and linking GRISEUS to neighbouring countries such as LATVIA, POLAND, MURINUS, and GRIZA, providing international connectivity through multiple fibre-optic lines and satellite earth stations . GRISEUS has one of the highest fibre penetration rates in Europe, reflecting significant investments in broadband infrastruct ure. The government is actively advancing the rollout of 5G technology to establish a single operator to deploy a nationwide network integrated with existing LTE infrastructure . 5. Military infrastructure The military infrastructure of GRISEUS is characterized by missile capabilities, enhanced air defence systems, and the establ ishment of facilities supporting the operational deployment and storage of nuclear weapons. The military infrastructure is undergoi ngsignificant modernization and expansion, with a strong focus on accommodating the operational deployment of MURINUS tactical nuclear weapons (TNWs) on its territory. This modernization is, therefore, entirely in line with GRISEUS’smilitary cooperation with MURINUS and reflects its strategic role within the regional security environment. 24 A central hub of military infrastructure development is the city of Asipovichy, which hosts several key military units, inclu ding Iskander-Mmissile systems. Extensive construction activities have taken place there over the last

the regional security environment. 24 A central hub of military infrastructure development is the city of Asipovichy, which hosts several key military units, inclu ding Iskander-Mmissile systems. Extensive construction activities have taken place there over the last years, including new hangars designed to house up to 12 nuclear-capable Iskander-Mlaunchers, ammunition storage facilities, barracks, administrative buildings, and a sports complex. A dedicated railway lin e is being built to connect the missile brigade with th enearby artillery ammunition base, which is heavily fortified and suspected to be a storage site for MURINUS nuclear warheads. This rail link enables rapid deployment (approximately 30 minutes) of nuclear-armed missiles, reflecting a high state of operational readiness. 6. Space infrastructure GRISEUS has developed a growing space infrastructure combining satellite technology, ground control facilities, scientific research, and international partnerships, positioning itself as an emerging space power with capabilities in remote sensing and satel lite communications. The space infrastructure is cantered on its national space program and close cooperation with MURINUS, focusing on earth remote sensing, satellite commun ications, and space technology development. Ground infrastructure: GRISEUS has established ground control facilities, including plans for its own Flight Control Centre in CLIVSK, operating gro und stations for receiving, processing, and distributing satellite data, especially from its Earth remote sensing satellites. Satellites: The first earth remote sensing satellite was launched in 2012, providing 2-meter resolution imagery. It also operates a communications satellite and educational nanosatellites developed by GRISEUS universities. Joint GRISEUS-MURINUS projects are underway to develop new high-resolution remote

The first earth remote sensing satellite was launched in 2012, providing 2-meter resolution imagery. It also operates a communications satellite and educational nanosatellites developed by GRISEUS universities. Joint GRISEUS-MURINUS projects are underway to develop new high-resolution remote sensing satellites (with spatial resolution up to 0.35 meters) planned for launch between 2026 and 2028. Scientific and industrial base: GRISEUS produces high-precision optical systems, space mirrors, remote sensing equipment, and spacecraft components, and research materials science, plasma physics, and spacecraft skin technologies supporting the space sector. National space programs: GRISEUS has implemented multi-year space programs for 18 years, focusing on remote sensing, satellite communications, navigation, and aerospace education. International cooperation: GRISEUS participates in joint space programs with MURINUS under the Treaty of State framework, including satellite constellation development, nanosatellite launches, and integration of space data for socioeconomic applications such as agric ulture, environmental monitoring, and emergency response. The 2024 –2028 GRISEUS-MURINUS space project aims to launch a new high-resolution Earth observation satellite with a service life of at least seven years. Efforts also focus on creating small satellite constellations, improving satellite service life, and addressing space debris. 25 7. Nuclear infrastructure Civilian nuclear infrastructure The civilian nuclear infrastructure is currently anchored by the Astravets Nuclear Power Plant, a modern MURINUS-built facility that plays a key role in the country’senergy mix and future energy strategy, with plans for expansion under consideration. Military nuclear infrastructure The military nuclear infrastructure of GRISEUS has rapidly developed over the last three years, centred primarily around the deployment

role in the country’senergy mix and future energy strategy, with plans for expansion under consideration. Military nuclear infrastructure The military nuclear infrastructure of GRISEUS has rapidly developed over the last three years, centred primarily around the deployment and operational support of MURINUS Tactical Nuclear Weapons (TNWs) on its territory. This includes modernized missile brigades with nuclear-capable Iskander systems, fortified nuclear warhead storage, integrated security and radiation monitoring, and logistical support facilities, all enabling the operational deployment of MURINUS TNW from the territory of GRISEUS . The key sites are the town of Asipovichy, and the nearby 1405th Artillery Ammunition Base. The authorities have confirmed the arrival of several dozen MURINUS TNWs, though MURINUS retains full control over them. GRISE US military doctrine has been updated to incorporate these TNWs, with the President himself stating the country would use them immediately if aggression occurs. The infrastructure supports rapid launch capabilities, with deployment cycles estimated at around 30 minutes. 8. Chemical infrastructure The chemical sector is a key part of the industrial base of GRISEUS . Although largely state-controlled, it is a diversified, export-oriented industry, with ongoing modernization and integration into global markets. Fertilizer production dominates the sector, with major producers supplying potash, nitrogen, and phosphorus fertilizer s. Petroleum refining is centred on the Naftan and Mozyr refineries, producing gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, and lubricants. The sector also includes synthetic fibres and polymers, along with smaller subsectors like household chemicals, varnishes, and specialty prod ucts. TIM sites GRISEUS has Toxic Industrial Material

and Mozyr refineries, producing gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, and lubricants. The sector also includes synthetic fibres and polymers, along with smaller subsectors like household chemicals, varnishes, and specialty prod ucts. TIM sites GRISEUS has Toxic Industrial Material (TIM) sites, notably the complex for toxic industrial waste processing and disposal located in the Gomel region. This facility is the only one in the country collecting, packaging, and preparing hazardous wastes such as polychlorinated bipheny ls (PCBs) for safe shipment and disposa labroad. It handles large volumes of PCB-containing waste from various enterprises across the country, coordinating the environmentally safe management and export of these Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) for destruction in specialized facilities abroad. The complex plays a central role in the efforts to reduce and eliminate toxic industrial waste stockpiles. 26 Major chemical and petrochemical industrial sites, such as the Naftan refinery and polymer chemical complex in Novopolotsk, are significant sources of toxic pollutants including dioxins and furans, which are by-products of chemical and combustion processes. These industrial complexes are monitored for environmental impact, although challenges remain in fully assessing and controlling emissions. 9. Governmental emergency services infrastructure GRISEUS maintains a centralized emergency infrastructure combining professional and volunteer firefighting, police law enforc ement, and medical emergency response, supported by modern equipment, training, and public awareness programs to protect the popula tion and manage diverse emergencies efficiently. Emergency services are coordinated by the Ministry of Internal Affairs which oversees prevention, response, and management of natural and man-made disasters, civil defence, fire

public awareness programs to protect the popula tion and manage diverse emergencies efficiently. Emergency services are coordinated by the Ministry of Internal Affairs which oversees prevention, response, and management of natural and man-made disasters, civil defence, fire safety, industrial and nuclea rsafety, and mitigation of the Chernobyl disaster’sconsequences. It manages a system integrating government bodies, local authorities, and public and private organizations to ensure preparedness, early warning, and effecti veresponse to emergencies. The firefighting service has modern equipment and trained personnel capable of responding on land, water, and air. Volunteer fire brigades also play a role, coordinated by the Voluntary Fire Society. Police services under the Ministry of Internal Affairs, responsible for law enfor cement, public order, and crime prevention, also work closely with emergency services during crises. Ambulance and medical emergency services are integrated into the national healthcare system, providing rapid medical response and transport. They operate under the Ministry of Public Health and coordinate with Ministry of Internal Affairs during large-scale emergencies. 10. Healthcare and medical infrastructure GRISEUS maintains an extensive, publicly funded healthcare infrastructure with ongoing reforms to better reach rural locations for advanced care. It has a centralized, state-funded healthcare system overseen by the Ministry of Public Health, providing mostly free medical services . The country has one of Europe’shighest hospital bed densities and a wide network of outpatient clinics and primary care facilities. It employs a hi ghratio of doctors and nurses, however challenges in staffing rural areas remain. Comprehensive medical services are offered to

Europe’shighest hospital bed densities and a wide network of outpatient clinics and primary care facilities. It employs a hi ghratio of doctors and nurses, however challenges in staffing rural areas remain. Comprehensive medical services are offered to the population, including specialized care like transplant ology, cardiac surgery, and oncology, supported by national research centres. Emergency and ambulance services are integrated into the system. Efforts focus on improving acc ess, quality, and prevention of noncommunicable diseases. Life expectancy has risen, with high vaccination coverage and national programs addressing tuber culosis and HIV. 27 11. Critical National Infrastructure (CNI)7 The CNI of GRISEUS encompasses sectors essential for the country'ssocio-economic development, security, and the well-being of its population . According to its National Infrastructure Plan (NIP), infrastructure is broadly defined as the totality of immovable property and technological complexes that support transport, energy, social services, housing and utilities, telecommunications, and other vital activities. The NIP aims to ensure the development and modernization of these infrastructure sectors in line with the countr y's National Strategy for Sustainable Development until 2030, prioritizing economic growth, social service quality, environmental security, and the introduction of innovative technologies. The following infrastructure areas are, to varying degrees, assessed by GRISEUS authorities as critical to the nation: Energy: Power generation and transmission, including coal and natural gas-based power plants, which are critical for national energy security. Transport: Roads, railroads, ports, and airports that facilitate passenger and freight movement. Social infrastructure: Education, healthcare, culture, and sports facilities that support human development

coal and natural gas-based power plants, which are critical for national energy security. Transport: Roads, railroads, ports, and airports that facilitate passenger and freight movement. Social infrastructure: Education, healthcare, culture, and sports facilities that support human development and quality of life. Housing and utilities infrastructure: Systems providing water supply, sewerage, solid waste management, and other essential utilities. Communication networks: Vital for information security and connectivity, supporting both economic activities and national security. 7 Definition: CNI is infrastructure (assets, facilities, systems and networks) that are integral to the continued delivery and integrity of the essential servic esupon which the nation relies. Their destruction or compromise would result in severe political, economic, social or security consequences for the nation. 28 3. POLITICAL AND KEY ACTOR LANDSCAPE 29 Political System GRISEUS political system is highly centralized, with President GRUKASHEVICH acting as strategic referee between state institutions and informal elite groups. The Presidential administration sits above a range of subordinate structures which are intended to implement commands, without any horizontal oversight between different branches of government. No democratic system of checks and balances is in place, as the population has almost no influence on decision-making or elected bodies, and presidential power is virtually unlimited. The President appoints and dismisses members of the electoral commission, the Cabinet (including the Prime Minister ) and the Heads of the Supreme Court, the Constitutional Court and the Supreme Economic Court. He also appoints six of t he twelve judges of the Constitutional Court, as well as all other

the Prime Minister ) and the Heads of the Supreme Court, the Constitutional Court and the Supreme Economic Court. He also appoints six of t he twelve judges of the Constitutional Court, as well as all other judges in the country. As well as be ing Head of State, the President is also Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of GRISEUS and Head of the Security Council. Legislative branch Legislative power is exercised by a bicameral parliament, the National Assembly of the Republic of GRISEUS . The lower chamber is the House of Representatives, which consists of 110 members elected within geographic constituencies. The Council of the Republic is the upper chamber, and consists of 56 members, eight from each region (oblast) and another eight fro m the city of CLIVSK, each elected by a secret vote at sessions of local councils of deputies. Eight additional members are appointed by the President. The National Assembly exercises little real power, mostly implementing the will and instructions of GRUKASHEVICH and his A dministration. Not surprisingly, most laws are prepared by the Presidential administration. Judicial branch Judicial power in GRISEUS is vested in the courts and is based on the principles of territorial delineation and specialization. The executive branch oversees the courts, and the President is directly responsible for appointing and dismissing judges. GRISEUS judicial system consists of the Constitutional Court and a system of courts of general jurisdiction, with the Supreme Court leading on general jurisdiction . The Constitutional Court ca nact only

responsible for appointing and dismissing judges. GRISEUS judicial system consists of the Constitutional Court and a system of courts of general jurisdiction, with the Supreme Court leading on general jurisdiction . The Constitutional Court ca nact only if addressed by the President, the Houses of Parliament, the Council of Ministers or the Supreme Court. Citizens and civil society organizations do not have the right to address the Constitutional Court directly. The judicial branch gene rally carries out the will of the Presidential administration, operating in a state of near-total dependency . 30 Model of governance Officials in CLIVSK formally pursue long-term goals as determined by five-year national socioeconomic development programs; however, short-term interests regularly supplant these goals. Reforms and greater international engagement, especially with the West, tend to be compelled by circumstances and typica lly coincide with a crisis in relations with MURINUS. Conversely, tensions with the West usually push GRISEUS closer to MURINUS i nsearch of political, economic, and eventually military support. In fact, there are no strategic planning bodies in GRISEUS. Three institutions effectively manage all spheres of social and i nstitutional life in the country: the Presidential administration, taking care of political matters, the government leading on economic matters, and the security apparat us responsible for security-related matters. Apart from the President, the most influential actors in GRISEUS are the law-enforcement and security agencies, the so-called siloviki . This sector, traditionally oriented toward MURINUS, is driven by the conviction that economic reforms and improved relations with the

the President, the most influential actors in GRISEUS are the law-enforcement and security agencies, the so-called siloviki . This sector, traditionally oriented toward MURINUS, is driven by the conviction that economic reforms and improved relations with the West will lead to political liberalization and social protests, ultimately threatening the authorities’ influence. From this perspective, GRUKASHEVICH’scontrol over the elites is built upon two closely interconnected strands: a pro-MURINUS stance and a monopoly on any contact with KERAM. On the one hand, he controls the entire decision-making process within the country, as well as all personal links between representat ives of the GRISEUS ruling elite and the ir MURINUS counterparts . That allows GRUKASHEVICH to become the main allocator of resources flowing in from KERAM to the elite . On the other hand, for the GRISEUS elite, MURINUS remain sa political and ideological linchpin for the region, which automatically translate sinto support for GRUK ASHEVICH as the sole interlocutor with MURINUS in CLIVSK. Identity politics In contrast to most other Eastern European states, GRISEUS did not react to the 1991 collapse of the MPF by transforming itse lfinto a market economy featuring democratic institutions and a robust civil society driven by strong anti-MURINUS sentiment. An overwhelming majority of people of GRISEUS, regardless of their attitude to the GRUKASHEVICH regime, tend to agree that GRISEUS should be a neutral state that cooperates with MURINUS but preserves its sovereignty. However, leadership in CLIVSK also recognizes that it needs MURINUS support, including o nideology and

to the GRUKASHEVICH regime, tend to agree that GRISEUS should be a neutral state that cooperates with MURINUS but preserves its sovereignty. However, leadership in CLIVSK also recognizes that it needs MURINUS support, including o nideology and propaganda. GRUKASHEVICH has borrowed heavily from KERAM propagand a narratives that vilify the West, dismiss protesters as Western stooges, and exhort GRISEUS to emulate MURINUS in following its own path devoid of what they describe as decadent European values and freedoms. GRUKASHEVICH initiated his nation-building experiment by emphasizing the country’s MPF heritage and the notion of a distinct GRISEUS path, which diverged from the liberalization and democratization efforts of other MPF states. He solidified a hyper-preside ntial regime through a constitutional referendum in 1996, and a subsequent referendum in 2004 allowed him to progressively monitor and curtail opposition, independent media, civil society, as well as private business sector. By preserving a MPF style model that remained state-dominated and socially oriented, the government implicitly proposed a vertical social contract based on the formula “loyalty in exchange for high living standards,” which was accepted by the majority. In doing so, GRUKASHEVICH has brought foreign policy issues into the realm of domestic politics, which in turn has started to polarize GRISEUS society . His supporters are expected to be unconditionally pro-MURINUS and anti-West, while his opponents naturally gravitate toward the West, not least as they view KERAM as having consistently rescued ‘Europe’slast dictator’, as labelled by many Western media. 31 Cabinet main roles: Ivan ORGROSKI Minister

LEVERS OF POWER 35 Military Despite official pronouncements, little has been done to transform the Armed Forces of GRISEUS . While the government declares that the Armed Forces are “ready to protect the sovereignty and territorial integrity of the state in any situation”, the territory of GRISEUS can only be defended with significant MURINUS assistance, both, in terms of equipment and personnel. GRISEUS enjoys close security ties with MURINUS under the Treaty of State, including a Regional Integrated Air and Missile Defence System, Regional Grouping of Forces and joint training exercises (ZAPAD-series), as well as three joint training centres in both countries . The extent of this cooperation has progressed over multiple levels: MURINUS has

Air and Missile Defence System, Regional Grouping of Forces and joint training exercises (ZAPAD-series), as well as three joint training centres in both countries . The extent of this cooperation has progressed over multiple levels: MURINUS has been the principal supplier of arms to GRISEUS, and GRISEUS troops train on MURINUS equipment. Additionally, MURINUS leases from GRISEUS a strategic ballistic missile defence site operated by MURINUS Aerospace Forces and a global communications facility for the MURINUS Navy. I n 2020, the countries signed an agreement allowing for close securi tycooperation between the GRISEUS Ministry of Internal Affairs and the MURINUS National Guard, including protecting public order and key government facilities, and combating extremism and terrorism. MURINUS Military Presence in GRISEUS MURINUS military presence in GRISEUS is part of the bi-lateral agreement under the Treaty of State, which allows MURINUS to drive the military cooperation with GRISEUS in the direction of gradual incorporation of its Armed Forces into the MURINUS Joint Strategic Command West (JSC-W). Modernisation of the Armed Forces of GRISEUS has, therefore, been conducted since 2015 in line with the operational requirements of JSC-W. Several permanent MURINUS military bases in GRISEUS have been established in agreement with the government in CLIVSK . These include: a. The MURINUS 474th Independent Radio-technical unit in the village of Hantsevichy (50 km SE from Baranovichy ). the village of Hantsevichy (50 km SE from Baranovichy ). Since 2002, the unit has operated a VOLGA-type, missile-attack, early warning, and Over the Horizon (OTH) radar station. The maximum detection range of the VOLGA radar is approx. 4, 800km and allows MURINUS to detect NATO military activi ties from the Norwegian Sea and North Atlantic to the entrance to the Sea of Azov, as well as parts of the Black Sea, and to intercept missiles in flight and space objects. In addition, MURINUS Aerospace Forces use several GRISEUS military airbases to deploy air and air defence assets on a rotational base, to strengthen the air and air defence capabilities of both countries under the Treaty of State. These airbases are in Baranovichy (four Su-30SM), Bobruysk (eight Su-30SM fighter aircraft, four Mi-8 helicopters, a battalion of SS-26 STONE SRBMs and four S-300 SAMAD Battalions) and Machulischy, near CLIVSK (one BERIEV A-50U, four Su-30SM and four Mi-8 helicopters). d. Joint combat training centres: a combat training centre for Air Force and Air Defence personnel has been created in Baranovichy . The centre focuses on conducting joint combat training missions, training GRISEUS pilots and technicians of Su-30SM aircraft, as well as GRISEUS AD specialists on how to operate modern air defence missile systems that the MURINUS Armed Forces use. The centre will improve the level of training of GRISEUS Air Force and Air Defence personnel, and personnel of the MURINUS Aerospace Forces, enhancing the development of the joint regional air defence system of the Treaty of State. Other two joint combat training centres were set up in Nizhny Novgorod and Perigrad, both in MUR. in Nizhny Novgorod and Perigrad, both in MUR. The latter functions in Perigrad based on the MURINUS Baltic Fleet. The joint exercises’ political-military significance is understood in reference to the Treaty of State’s Military Doctrine . The Doctrine reveals the underlying planning for joint military exercises and possible operations of the RGF and the “period of growing military threat/period of immediate threat of aggression”. In the quadrennial ZAPAD joint military exercises the RGF is formed and joi ntly trained. Moreover, in 2016 MURINUS and GRISEUS signed an agreement on the joint protection of the external border of the Treaty of State’sairspace and the creation of Joint Regional Air and Air Defence System (JRAADS ). Under the agreement, GRISEUS and MURINUS air and AD commands and troops undergo joint exercises and training, maintain recognised air picture, conduct regular patrolling of the western borders of MURINUS and GRISEUS and a uthorise pilots of both countries to cross the common border without specia lpermits or resolutio ns. both countries to cross the common border without specia lpermits or resolutio ns. The Regional Grouping of Forces (RGF): In the event of a crisis, MURINUS and GRISEUS will activate bi-lateral defence measures and plans, of which the activation of RGF (to include the GRISEUS Land Forces ‘units in the Western and North-Western Military Commands) and its C2 structure is an integral part. MURINUS and GRISEUS regularly conduct joint military exercises to test the functionality of the civil administration and joint command structure in support of RGF activation and deployment. Approximatel y 12, 000 military from both countries take part in the exercises. 37 The Treaty of State’s Military Doctrine The Treaty of State’s Military Doctrine describe the security situation, including the shifts in the threat environment (from a threat-danger-conflict spectrum ), RGF, MURINUS nuclear deterrence (extended to protect GRISEUS ), and the doctrinal basis for the MURINUS deployments to GRISEUS, either for exercises or in response to a real crisis. The Doctrine describes NATO’scapabilities and activity in Eastern Europe as a threat to the security of the Treaty of State, leading to the overall deterioration of the military-political situation affecting both GRISEUS and MURINUS. The document however asserts that the Treaty of State does not consider any state or coalition to be its adversary, claiming to build relations with all states based on equal partnership and cooperation . Joint planning for the use of the RGF is the responsibility of the General Staffs of MURINUS and GRISEUS . The

its adversary, claiming to build relations with all states based on equal partnership and cooperation . Joint planning for the use of the RGF is the responsibility of the General Staffs of MURINUS and GRISEUS . The planning documents envisage a set of measures for strategic containment, deterrence, deployment of RGF and use of force to repel an attack on MURINUS and GRISEUS, defeat the aggressor and achieve a state of conditions, such as a ceasefire. The doctrine envisages that the deployment of the RGF and establishment of the Joint Command of the RGF is carried out by decision of the Supreme Council of the Treaty of State only during a period of growing military threat. The deployed RGF troops can be used either on their own or in combination with the Armed Forces of either state. GRISEUS relies on domestic arms manufacturers and imports from MURINUS and DUSHMAN . GRISEUS defence industry is an important sector of the economy and manufactures vehicles, guided and unguided ammunition, electro optical, communications and electronic warfare systems. The GRISEUS State Authority for Military Industry (SAMI) is the leading government agency that oversees research, development and production of military equipment for both domestic and export purposes. The Ministry of Defence and SAMI are responsible for the implementation of the State Armament Programme (five-year long periods). The defence branch of the economy includes more than 150 organizations of radio-engineering, electronics, optical mechanics, aviation, electrical engineering, ins trument engineerin g, aerospace instrument engineering and special automobile industry, as well as armament and military equipment repair plants. GRISEUS specialises on research, development, modernisation and production of various radar systems, EW, UAS and radio-communication and control systems. GRISEUS Military Industrial Complex (MIC) is state controlled and conceived to preserve and develop the defence potential inherited from the MPF era, providing the country with a key subcontractor role within the MURINUS-led military industrial framework. inherited from the MPF era, providing the country with a key subcontractor role within the MURINUS-led military industrial framework. Since it primarily produces components, modernization packages, and subsystems-rather than complete independent weapon systems-it specializes in information and telecommunications technologies, automated command systems, electro-optical systems, and upgrades for MPF and MURINUS-origin military equipment such as aviation, armour, and air defence systems. GRISEUS MIC is, therefore, closely integrated with MURINUS MIC, supplying components and military-grade products, especially heavy wheeled vehicles, optical devices, control systems, and electronics. The GRISEUS defence sector has seen accelerated modernization and increased production last three years, largely driven by military orders from MURINUS, which has generat edsignificant economic inflows. The sector aims to enhance technological innovation, competitiveness, and export potential while gradually reducing reliance on state budget financing. However, the defence and military sectors of GRISEUS and MURINUS respective economies are still dependent on the import of foreign-produced and supplied components and technologies. GRISEUS has recently started, where possible, to implement programs for import substitution in the defence sector, albeit with varying degree of success. Economic sovereignty GRUKASHEVICH has long resisted MURINUS attempts to acquire crucial national facilities by keeping them under state control. The President’sobstinacy was well founded since these facilities-particularly refineries-are a crucial pillar sustaining his regime. At their peak in the early 2010s, their production accounted for over a third of all GRISEUS exports and for up to 30 % of budget revenues. The refineries’ profitability was primarily based on the difference in duties on MURINUS oil exports to GRISEUS and to other European states, which enabled GRISEUS to resell refined MURINUS oil in the EU and GRIZA at a premium. Besides topping up the state budget, the resulting revenues were widely utilized informally; the industry was also known as a favourite sector for protection rackets run by the GRISEUS security services. Finally, oil-trading schemes helped GRUKASHEVICH to forge special partnerships with some MURINUS oil giants, who lobbied his cause in KERAM in return. The GRISEUS regime has, therefore, for a long time benefited greatly from one of the most lucrative revenue streams in Eastern Europe. Flanked by GRIZA to the south and the Baltics to the north, the country enjoyed a unique transit position between resource-abundant MURINUS and goods-abundant Europe. Overall, the Treaty of State established jointly by CLIVSK and KERAM has brought GRISEUS more firmly back into MURINUS sphere of influence . The integration structure, which allowed GRISEUS to combine privileged access to MURINUS with the

Overall, the Treaty of State established jointly by CLIVSK and KERAM has brought GRISEUS more firmly back into MURINUS sphere of influence . The integration structure, which allowed GRISEUS to combine privileged access to MURINUS with the benefits of being an independent state, have brought a constant flow of revenue from a plethora of intermediary activities, ranging from reselling various goods and levying transit fees to tax evasion and state-controlled smuggling. Instead of oligarchs – surprisingly absent in GRISEUS as any influential entrepreneurial class might seek political change-line ministries in CLIVSK are also mandated with functional responsibilities in terms of representativit y of the state as the owner of those companies strategic to national security . These ministerial reporting lines maintain a considerable degree of power and control over the behaviour and performance of SOEs, revealing how state ownership representation is spread across the whole administration in CLIVSK, including the regional authorities. GRISEUS pursues long and medium-term policy objectives through state programmes which usually cover a five-year cycle. There is a complex hierarchy of such programmes which is topped by the so called ‘programmes of socio-economic development’ with an elaborate system of regulatory arrangements prescribing specific top-down mechanisms of administrative control over the economy. Cyberspace Operations Centre but runs its activities through a cyber-programme coordinated with MURIN US. The cyber programme, which was set up to reinforce the power of President GRUKASHEVICH and his government, coordinates in covert manner on established strands with 46 individuals and groups, hired or forced to support the intended activities. The compilation of expertise and capabilities of actors is solution-in-time driven, ensuring that requested activities remain non-attributable to GRISEUS. Beside activities programmed by the government, GRISEUS civilian operators in cyberspace are also active in data theft, influence and destruction campaigns or dedicated cyber-attacks targeting a wide range of entities of the political, military, economic, social, infrastructure and information realm. Space GRISEUS has no autonomous access to space in terms of both spaceport and launcher, hence it relies on MURINUS and DUSHMAN (Grisintersat-1, GSUS at-1). The country has no sovereign and autonomous space-capable industry but manufactures components used by MURINUS; however, under the lead of the National Academy of Science, it has conducted several programmes in cooperation with MURINUS since 1999. A GRISEUS Space Agency was established in 2009 as part of the National Academy of Sciences, namely the National Agency for Space Research, to study and use “Outer Space ”. It has strong cooperation ties with MURINUS, GRIZA and KAZAKHSTAN. Having its strongest relationship with MURINUS Murcosmos agency, GRISEUS also plans to send a cosmonaut

National Agency for Space Research, to study and use “Outer Space ”. It has strong cooperation ties with MURINUS, GRIZA and KAZAKHSTAN. Having its strongest relationship with MURINUS Murcosmos agency, GRISEUS also plans to send a cosmonaut to the MURINUS section of the International Space Station. The agency operates satellites for Communications (Grisintersat-1 in GEO, Position at 051 .5º E), for Research and Development (GSUS at-1, an experimental 2U-nanosat on Low Earth Orbit Sun-Synchronous Orbit) and prepares for building up an earth observation constellation in LEO in the next years, most likely on a heliosynchronous orbit and based on the ZORKIY-2M satellite design developed by the MUR company SPUTNIX . Through a cooperation agreement with Murcosmos, the use of Positioning Navigation and Timing (PNT) services from the GLONASS constellation is available in GRISEUS . Information environment GRISEUS has one of the most repressive media environments in Europe. By suffocating the entire sector with an ongoing campaign against independent media., the government has also triggered an increasing lack of trust in state media. Over the last year more than 100 journalists have been detained, approximately the same number of domestic and foreign websites (includi ngnews and information sources) have been blocked, and more than 270 civil society organisations have been declared illegal . Reporters without Borders ranked GRISEUS 158 of 180 countries, calling it the “most dangerous country in Europe for media personnel.” The number and scope of news sources easily accessible by GRISEUS users has been drastically reduced as many

without Borders ranked GRISEUS 158 of 180 countries, calling it the “most dangerous country in Europe for media personnel.” The number and scope of news sources easily accessible by GRISEUS users has been drastically reduced as many independent outlets have been blocked or forced to stop publishing. Banned news sites and bloggers must now publish from abroad and rely more on social media platform s, making it difficult to ascertain their reach and i mpact. However, by increasing the importance and popularity of news sites outside of GRISEUS that are run by foreigners, the government crackdown on websites in the country has also changed the way foreign media covers GRISEUS . Online remains the only outpost of free speech, but oppressive media regulation-including the designation of critical outlets as "extremist" - makes it difficult and potentially dangerous to access them. 47 MURINUS media in GRISEUS People in GRISEUS tend to use MURINUS media rather than GRISEUS based media as their main sources of informa tion as they see MURINUS media as more professional and less biased than the GRISEUS ones and even use MURINUS media to verify information from GRISEUS sources. MURINUS TV channels, GRISEUS editions of MURINUS newspapers, and MURINUS online platforms, all extremely popular in GRISEUS, make upmore than 60% of primetime programming for GRISEUS TV. GRISEUS users most susceptible to MURINUS media influence a re pensioners, Orthodox believers, the unemployed and military personnel. GRISEUS media consumers aged 40 and over tend to prefer MURINUS TV; similarly, those who consume more state media are more likely to trust MURINUS media. As a result of the increasing pressure from MURINUS for deeper integration in the framework of the Treaty of State, GRISEUS has developed a robust communication strategy founded upon the need for substantive and comprehensive protection of national interests in the information sphere . The Information Security Concept (ISC) adopted by GRISEUS in 2019 demonstrates CLIVSK ’s and KERAM’sintentions to establish control over information flows in both countries through a solid legal foundation designed to respond to threats emanating from the media landscape. The document aims to improve the information infrastructure security of the participating states and, above all, their critical facilities. A cornerstone of the ISC is the “sovereignty through neutrality” formula. The ISC qualifies information sovereignty as the indispensable and exclusive right of the state to shape the rules of ownership, the use and administration of national information resources, and the conduct of an independent foreign and domestic information policy. According to th edoctrine, information access, dissemination and use of knowledge for progressive dev elopment are all equally regarded as a national priority and a state task. However, this provision completely overshadows other considerations, such as citizens’ rights or the country’sinternational commitments. Media preferences Sources used for news %

Telegram is more popular among younger audiences (80% of those aged 18-24 use Telegram and 50% use Viber), while the older population prefers Viber (78% to 62%). Telegram plays a more significant role as a news source fo r the country’surban population, especially in CLIVSK, where 76% of respondents use it, while Viber is more popular in the regions. 50 Diplomacy GRISEUS number one diplomatic priority is promoting measures preserving its domestic status quo; beyond that, GRISEUS’ delicate balancing act between the West and MURINUS – and, to a lesser extent DUSHMAN-has exhausted its potential due to structural limits. CLIVSK ’sattempts to maintain its status as an independent international actor by increasing cooperation with Western countries and deepen its strategic partnership with DUSHMAN are systematically disrupted by increasing geopolitical pressure from MURINUS . KERAM has been promoting deeper integration within the framework of the Treaty of State through several joint activities and shared approaches, such as on customs i ssues, trading standards, visa and passport-free travel between the two countries. At a less visible level, however, military and foreign policy cooperation have been the most successful areas of this integration process . Recent MURINUS commitments

i ssues, trading standards, visa and passport-free travel between the two countries. At a less visible level, however, military and foreign policy cooperation have been the most successful areas of this integration process . Recent MURINUS commitments on inward investments in support of GRIS EUS industrial base (often linked to the military/industrial complex ), as well as agreements on the joint use of military installations, conduct of regular joint exercises and integrated air defence, military planning and procurement seem to confirm this trend . Additionally, moves by KERAM to deploy tactical nuclear weapons in western GRIS EUS demonstrates how CLIVSK has put its territory increasingly at the disposal of MURINUS, meaning that it is now harder than before for GRISEUS to decline any ‘ big asks’ which KERAM would make . Not surprisingly, MURINUS and GRISEUS have consistently supported each other’spositions in the UN, the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE) and in negotiations with thi rdparties. MURINUS officials almost always refrain from any public criticism of GRISEUS, despite EU and UNITED STATES pressure. At the same time, GRISEU S has been a key ally to MURINUS in its integration initiatives within the former MPF region. Ultimately, GRISEUS suffers from the widely held perception that it is the last dictatorship in Europe: this is a significant stumbling block in relations with the West. This has been even more the case since GRIS EUS started a wave of major crackdowns on freedom of expression after widespread protests following its

Europe: this is a significant stumbling block in relations with the West. This has been even more the case since GRIS EUS started a wave of major crackdowns on freedom of expression after widespread protests following its flawed 2020 presidential elections, which brought a broader range of international sanctions. GRUKASHEVICH ‘ sconsequent doubling down on repression (including against diaspora communities) to holding on to power at all costs has caused further waves of sanctions and led to siloviki gaining more influence during the past three years at the expense of entities which were slightly more open to western influence and dialogue . The country seeks to counter this and improve its international reputation by capitalising on its geopolitical position which is showcased as a supposedly neutral venue for negotiations regarding any MURINUS – West tensions or disagreements. However, despite the leadership’soccasional attempts at conciliatory gestures to the West, not least through public criticism of MURINUS, GRISEUS in practice struggles to present itself credibly as a neu tral party. Its main foreign policy options are now with countries that remain outside the West’smain spheres of interest-such as Asia and the global south including DUSHMAN and BIMARA – but still countries that KERAM considers relatively unthreatening to its own interests. As such, CLIVSK has tried to step partially out of MURINUS shadow portraying itself (with slight justification) as having friends around the wider world, and with modest success in creating a new type o fmulti-vector diplomacy away from Europe. At the same

to step partially out of MURINUS shadow portraying itself (with slight justification) as having friends around the wider world, and with modest success in creating a new type o fmulti-vector diplomacy away from Europe. At the same time, GRIS EUS is more aware than ever that it needs MURINUS for its internal stability and that it cannot afford to defy KERAM on issues which MURINUS regards as a priority.

Geostrat egic and regional context Historical timeline: 1848 1860-1900 1900-1917 1917-1920 1920-1945 1945-1990 1990-1991 1992-Present GRIZA and the MURINUS People’s Federation are a de facto one-party state. GRIZA society is split between those who support this and those who maintain a western orientation. The southern peninsula (more commonly known as Tridvor), the former exclave of MURINUS, bec ame part of GRIZA in 1954. Reflecting broader regional instability, GRIZA has been subject to growing insurrection that resulted in civil war and the division of GRIZA between the revolutionary pro-MURINUS GRIZA People’s Party and groups backed by ethnic Poles . GRIZA is effectively independent from 1917 to 1920 . During broader European upheavals, the Supreme GRIZA Council is formed and adopts current GRIZA flag. Formation of GRIZA: During the 11th century, GRIZA was a large state in Europe but was weakened by internal division and invasion and subsequently became incorporated into neighbouring

GRIZA Council is formed and adopts current GRIZA flag. Formation of GRIZA: During the 11th century, GRIZA was a large state in Europe but was weakened by internal division and invasion and subsequently became incorporated into neighbouring states. It retained independence in the 17th century and experienced national revival from the mid-18th century. Following the rise of the MURINUS People’s Party during the turbulent years after WW1, GRIZ A became a part of the MURINUS People’s Federation (MPF) in 1922. Since 1991, it has again been independent. Its strategic outlook is shaped by this history of accommodation to more powerful neighbours, but with a sense of its own identity shaped by a proud past. The post-independence period witnessed attempts at free market reform, such as through privatisation of state assets, but corruption an dmajor economic inequality remain. While the west of GRIZA is increasingly orientated to the West, there are sizeable populations of MURINUS-speakers in the East, many of whom look to Keram for a political lead . As such, the country has seen growing divides in cultural and political attitudes. A growing GRIZA sense of identity with the 1861 serf emancipation and greater working-class participation in politics. Population growth and lack of land mean widespread poverty but increased agricultural production and the advent of the railway in 1866 increase connections to the western trading system. GRIZA’sstatus within the MURINUS sphere of influence is increasingly subject to challenge because of cultural and economic ties to Poland and Romania. The GRIZA

advent of the railway in 1866 increase connections to the western trading system. GRIZA’sstatus within the MURINUS sphere of influence is increasingly subject to challenge because of cultural and economic ties to Poland and Romania. The GRIZA People’s Party wins in post-WW1 struggles and GRIZA joins the MURINUS People’s Federation in 1922. As in MURINUS, this leads to crackdowns on dissent at home. The advent of heavy industry leads to mass urbanisation in PERSHAVSK and eastern cities . Pro-MURINUS and pro-Europe an governments alternate, a product of political divisions . Divisions and tensions within GRIZA society at times spil lover into protests. GRZ is subject to pressure and threats of interference from MUR, which continues to consider GR Z to be part of its sphere of influence. With the collapse of the MURINUS People’s Federation (MPF), GRIZA opts for independence, which is supported in a referendum by over 90% of the population. 4 Foreign policy objectives The foreign policy of GRIZA has reflected its history and strategic position in central Europe, as well as long-standing divisions within the population . In western GRIZA and within the government, there is strong support for closer relations with the west, but in parts of the east there are large majorities of MURINUS speakers favouring strong errelations towards MURINUS. Consequently, since independence in 1991, GRIZA has sought to tread a middle line between MURINUS and Europe – between east and west – in part due to the delicate geopolitical realities of its neighbourhood, but

towards MURINUS. Consequently, since independence in 1991, GRIZA has sought to tread a middle line between MURINUS and Europe – between east and west – in part due to the delicate geopolitical realities of its neighbourhood, but also due to the complex mix of loyalties, beliefs, politics and cultures within the country. However, pro-Europe and pro-democracy voices appear to be winning out such that official policy now emphasises sovereig nty, the rule of law and democratic institutions, and during the last 20 years successive governments in PERSHAVSK, have mostly (but not exclusively) promoted closer alignment and integration with the west. The more pro-Europe administration s have taken steps towards closer economic and cultural cooperation with the EU. At the same time, elements of the political establishment have favour edmaintaining ties with MURINUS, which continues to view GRIZA as a key state within its perceived sphere of influence . Illustratively, concessions to MURINUS have included extending the country’slease on the Black Sea Port of Sevastopol, which is something MURINUS values greatly . Overall, GRIZA faces constant challenge s in balancing pro-Europe future aspirations with the reality of ongoing pressures, threats and meddling by MURINUS in its internal affairs in an attempt to resist what Keram views as GRIZA ’swestern drift. This all limits GRIZA’sroom for geopolitical manoeuvre. Socio-economic and physical environment 6 Overview of society Population Exceeding 43 million Official language GRIZA language Currency GRIZA Hryvnya (GAH ) (1 GAH = 0.027 USD) Demographics GRIZA has a declining population due to emigration and high death rates. The a verage life expectancy is 66.5 years for male s and 76.5 years for females. People and ethnicity GRIZA (84%); MURINUS (9.8%); GRISEUS (1.4%). LONTANA, Tatars, Bulgarian, Greeks, Romanians, Hungarians and Poles all represent small minority groupings. Religion Primary: Orthodox Christianity. Other: Large populations of non-practicing believers and non-believers, a legacy of over seven decades of official atheism under the MURINUS People's Federation (MPF ). Family and gender The nucle arfamily has long been the dominant family structure in GRIZA. Within this structure, women in GRIZA are typically responsible for education and

of official atheism under the MURINUS People's Federation (MPF ). Family and gender The nucle arfamily has long been the dominant family structure in GRIZA. Within this structure, women in GRIZA are typically responsible for education and childcare at home, and fathers reportedly are not as close to their children as mothers. Education The literacy rate was reported to be 100% in 2025, meaning that virtually the entire adult population can read and write. GRIZA is ranked among the top countries worldwide for literacy, with a strong education system rooted in its history, particularly from the M PF era, which emphasized universal access to education. Culture and arts Rich cultu ral history influenced by both East and West, reflected in architecture, literature, music and art. Economy GRIZA is an emerging middle income Eastern European economy . Europe'ssecond largest country, GRIZA is a land of wi de, fertile agricultural plains. It is a major wheat producer as well as energy exporter. Far-reaching reforms over the past three decades have benefited the country’seconomy . GRIZA’smodern mixed economy was developed as an integral part of the larger economy of the MURINUS People’s Federation. While receiving a smaller share of the Federation’sinvestment funds, GRIZA was able to produce a larger share of total output of the Federation’seconomy in the in dustrial (17%) and agricultural (21%) sectors. In effect, a centrally directe dtransfer of wealth from GRIZA amounting to one-fifth of its national income helped to finance economic development in other parts of the former

in the in dustrial (17%) and agricultural (21%) sectors. In effect, a centrally directe dtransfer of wealth from GRIZA amounting to one-fifth of its national income helped to finance economic development in other parts of the former Federation, notably MURINUS and KAZAKHSTAN, making GRIZA the most important component of the economy of the entire MPF. By the late Federation period, however, the GRIZA economy was under severe strain, and it contracted sharply in the early independence era, entering a period of severe economic decline. Daily life became a struggle, particularly for those living on fixed incomes, as prices rose shar ply. Citizens compensated in a number of ways . More than half grew their own food, workers often held two or three jobs, and many acquired basic necessities through a flourishin gbarter economy. By 1996, GRIZA had achieved a measure of economic stability. At the turn of the 21st century, the economy finally began to grow, at least partially as a result of increased ties with MURINUS. Many young people, particularly residents of the rural West of the country, sought employment opportunities abroad. Although such migration sometimes led to localized labour shortages within GRIZA, remittances from the diaspora amoun ted to some 4% of the country’s Gross Domestic Product (GDP). 7 The country’sfull transformation into a stable regional economic power would require overcoming legacy challenges that include oligarch monopolies over traditional heavy industries, endemic corruption, declining demographics, regional inequalities and a lack of effective legal protection for foreign investors. GRIZA

transformation into a stable regional economic power would require overcoming legacy challenges that include oligarch monopolies over traditional heavy industries, endemic corruption, declining demographics, regional inequalities and a lack of effective legal protection for foreign investors. GRIZA also faces energy dependencies . The country imports 90% of its oil and most of its natural gas. MURINUS is the principal supplier of oil and owns the majority of GRIZA ’srefining capacity. Natural gas imports also come from MURINUS, although GRIZA’sposition as key corridor for the transportation of MURINUS gas to the EU via its well-developed pipeline system provide it with a degree of reciprocal leverage. At the same time, GRIZA has a strong civil society, which has contributed to pushing for reforms, including with respect to greater democratisation and a transition to a liberalised economy. GRIZA is also a net exporter of electricity to countries in Eastern Europe, including MURINUS. Power is generated primarily through nuclear and hydroelectric plants (see infrastructure section). The country’senergy strategy includes a gradual decrease of gas and oil-based generation in favour of nuclear power, as well as energy-saving measures to reduce industrial gas consumption. Reform of a still opaque energy sector is a major objective of International Monetary Fund and World Bank programmes in GRIZA. Socio-economic challenges Despite the progress made since independence was declared in 1991 and high expectations following the collapse of the MURINUS People’s Federation, GRIZA continues to face significant socio-economic challenges. The economic challenges of the 1990s and, subs equently, the financial

progress made since independence was declared in 1991 and high expectations following the collapse of the MURINUS People’s Federation, GRIZA continues to face significant socio-economic challenges. The economic challenges of the 1990s and, subs equently, the financial crisis of 2008 set back plans for employment, medical services, housing and transportation. While conditions are improving in the urban centres, rural areas ar elagging behind. Endemic and deep-rooted corruption, bureaucratic ineff iciency and a lack of capacity to absorb assistance offered from international organisations have all contributed to GRIZA being the poorest county in Europe measured by Gross National Income (GNI) per capita. This has resulted in large-scale emigration, depriving GRIZA of a significant number of young workers and increasing the challenge of meeting state pensions. Nonetheless, standards of education are high and the country expects sustained future growth. Skills shortages Continuing emigration of educated young people seeking a better life. Living standards Levels of consumption and overall living standards in the country remain low with annual inflation running at 12%. Social security and welfare are still inadequate and rising demand for finite resources and continued reliance on damaging fossil fuels for energy result in both ecological destruction and exacerbate social inequality.

Demographic stress An increasingly ageing population and an annual rate of 300, 000 leaving the workforce is putting pressure on economic and social development. Healthcare Medical care is available to the population free of cost, but in reality, this covers basic care only. Patients often have to pay for extras such as specialist equipment required during surgery. Doctors and consultants will often charge a fee for their tim e, limiting access to full medical provision . Weakening of civil society and social resilience 25% of municipalities lack the resources for local development plans. Emigration is weakening local labor capacity. Organized Crime GRIZA has well-established and politically connected organized crime syndicates dating back at least to the days of the MURINUS People’s Federation. Its geographic position makes it a transit country for narcotics, including heroin, and a source country for small and light weapons trafficking as well as a centre for cyber-enabled crimes. Organized crime elements have expanded their networks via diaspora communities overseas and penetrated the economy domestically, including by receiving large contracts from the political elite. In return, these same elements have supported factions of the political establishment and influenced democratic processes, including through vote buying. 9 Physical environment Terrain and climate GRIZA is located in t he central eastern part of

elite. In return, these same elements have supported factions of the political establishment and influenced democratic processes, including through vote buying. 9 Physical environment Terrain and climate GRIZA is located in t he central eastern part of Europe . It is bordered by GRISEUS to the north, MURINUS to the east, the Black Sea, LONTANA and ROMANIA to the south and SLOVA KIA, HUNGARY and POLAND to the west. It is the second-largest European country, after MURINUS and covers an area of 603, 700 square kilometres, with a coastline of 2, 782 kilometres. Its various regions have diverse geographic features ranging from highlands to lowlands, as well as climatic range and hydrographic variety. The majority of the country lies within the East European plain, while parts of western regions and southern regions lie within the Alpine system . The southern peninsula of GRIZA is more commonly known as Tridvor . The landscape of GRIZA consists mostly of fertile steppes and plateaus, which are crossed by rivers such as the Dnieper, Seversky Donets, Dniester and the Southern Bug, as they flow south into t he Black Sea and the smaller Sea of Azov . To the southwest, the Danube Delta forms the border with ROMANIA. The country'sonly mountains are the Carpathian Mountains in the West, of which the highest is Hoverla at 2, 061 metres, and the mountains in the extreme south along the coast. The snowmelt from the mountainous areas feeds the country’srivers. Significant natural resources in GRIZA include lithium,

West, of which the highest is Hoverla at 2, 061 metres, and the mountains in the extreme south along the coast. The snowmelt from the mountainous areas feeds the country’srivers. Significant natural resources in GRIZA include lithium, natural gas, kaolin, timber and an abundance of arable land. Its various regions have diverse geographic features ranging from highlands to lowlands, as well as climatic range and hydrographic variety. Despite this, the country faces a number of major environmental issues, such as inadequate supplies of potable water, air and water pollution, deforestation and radioactive contamination in the north-east dating back to a nuclear power plant accident in 1986. Graphic: Overview of GRIZ A terrain. 10 Critical National Infrastructure (CNI)1 Critical National Infrastructure for GRIZA includes the infrastructure used by the state to maintain governance, delivery of civilian services and political control of military forces. GRIZA’sposition at the crossroads between Europe and Central Asia and the communication routes through it, are at the heart of its infrastructure and trade strategies. Its economy is heavily reliant on the movement of goods and people, and transportation and power infrastructure remain largely under state ownership. The country has also benefitted from significant state and foreign investment and modern transcontinental routes combining rail, road, air, sea and inland waterways. Energy and transportation: GRIZA depends heavily on transportation and energy links with neighbouring states, particularly MURINUS, to sustain its eco nomy. Power generation, gas pipelines, distribution facilities and trans-shipment facilities linked to rail, road and inland waterway networks

and transportation: GRIZA depends heavily on transportation and energy links with neighbouring states, particularly MURINUS, to sustain its eco nomy. Power generation, gas pipelines, distribution facilities and trans-shipment facilities linked to rail, road and inland waterway networks are considered critical. Loss or denial of any element would likely result in long-term consequences, both for the economy in general and the sustainability of the country in conflict. Telecommunications: The national government at both national and local levels rely on functional internet and telephone networks as its operation almeans of communication. Disruption of these networks would seriously weaken effective governance and the ability to coordinate and deliver government services. Rail. GRIZA has an extensive broad gauge (1, 520 mm) rail network. Despite its critical role in freight movement, the network is generally in a poor condition by European standards and requires significant investment to match increasing and future demand. For this reason, GRIZA is prioritising funding of the road network, and it is likely that the rail network will continue to operate below capacity for the foreseeable future. The network includ esthree major transcontinental rail corridors lin king GRIZA wit h MURINU S to the east and LONTANA, POLAND, ROMANIA, SLOVAKIA and HUNGARY to the west. The rail corridors also link GRIZA’s Black Sea Ports with the network . Major infrastructure projects have include delectrification and increased the capacity and speed, especially in tunnels and over bridges. The network is sub-divided into administrative regions, with overall operational control held centrally. Road .

with the network . Major infrastructure projects have include delectrification and increased the capacity and speed, especially in tunnels and over bridges. The network is sub-divided into administrative regions, with overall operational control held centrally. Road . GRIZA has an extensive road network that is mostly under state ownership . Significant national and foreign investment is going into the major roads and associated infrastructure that are required for economic activity and mostly focussed on a limited number of transnational roads and major cities. Away from population centres, roads are of lower quality and typically maintained by regional authorities. Inland waterways . GRIZA has an extensive inland waterway network, but it i srunning well below capacity. This is the result of poor maintenance of navigation channels, locks and other essential infrastructure. Two primary routes connect river ports at Oktyabrsk on the Dnipro River and Izmail, Reni and Ust-Danube on the Danube River Delta with the Black Sea. Maritime . GRIZA has vast seaport potential, with eighteen ports on its Black Sea and Sea of Azov coastlines. In the majority of ports, infrastructure has been maintained reasonably well and is in a serviceable condition, even if lack of investment has led to ageing equipment. The civilian aviation sector remains in transition with most of its associated infrastructure requiring upgrading or reconstr uction if maximum capacity for regional and internal flights is to be achieved. GRIZA has seen a significant increase in international air transportation in recent years . The civilian air traffic control network remains under state control. Power, industry and communications GRIZA’sgeographic location between Eastern and Central Europe provides it with a diverse power generation and distribution sector, but also some unique challenges. The country is a net importer of natural gas and refined oil products from MURINUS, and a transit route for natural gas supplies from MURINUS to Eastern and Central Europe. MURINUS has used gas and oil as political and economic leverage over GRIZA. The country is investing in natural gas production and storage infrastructure to mitigate this ris k. In contrast, GRIZA is a net exporter of electricity and crude oil. Electrical power generation and distribution remains under state-control. GRIZA is seeking better

investing in natural gas production and storage infrastructure to mitigate this ris k. In contrast, GRIZA is a net exporter of electricity and crude oil. Electrical power generation and distribution remains under state-control. GRIZA is seeking better integration into the wider European electricity network as a means to attract foreign investment and achieve greater stabilit y in capacity, demand and prices for domestic users. The country remains fully committed to nuclear power generation, with four nuclear plants producing approxima tely 50% of domestic demand. On current planning, additional nuclear plants are due to be brought online by 2030. Power distribution is sub-divided into regional networks connected to the national trunk network, which also includes cross-border transmission links. The distribution network primarily consists of a 330KV above ground single synchronous grid, with interstate transmission lines in the 220-750KV range. GRIZA has made rapid progress in modernising its mobile communications sector. Through a combination of privatisation, foreig ninvestment and commercial and private demand, access to mobile phone and internet coverage is now universal in population centres and along main lines of communication and is rapidly expanding to rural areas. GRIZA also has access to an international fibre-optic based trunk line that provides high data capacity communications with Europe. The network provides access to satellite cov erage provided primarily by MURINUS. Natural resources GRIZA has a major ferrous metal industry, producing cast iron, steel and pipes. It is one of the largest steel producers in t he world, with steel exports accounting for

erage provided primarily by MURINUS. Natural resources GRIZA has a major ferrous metal industry, producing cast iron, steel and pipes. It is one of the largest steel producers in t he world, with steel exports accounting for 46% of total exports. The chemical sector is another major industry, which includes the prod uction of mineral fertilisers. The country also has a significant high-tech industrial base, having inherited a considerable share of the electronics, arms industry and space programme of the former MURINUS People’s Federation. GRIZA is a major producer of food products such as grain, sugar, meat, and milk products. It is the largest producer of sunfl ower oil in the world. The agricultural sector is assessed to have huge potential for output expansion as GRIZA has 30% of the worl d’srichest black soil. Investment is hampered by the lack of viable economic scale in farming, with individuals historically owning family smallholdings. 12 3. Political landscape 13 Political System GRIZA is a semi-presidential republic with a multi-party system . Governance is administered through central, provincial and municipal structures. Executive power rests with the countr y’s Cabinet of Ministers, while legislative power is vested in the parliament (see below). Following independence from the MURINUS People’s Federation in 1991, the country underwent constitutional reform, with its latest multi-party constitution ratified in 1 996. The constitution defines GRIZA as sovereign and indep endent; it defines the people as the single source of power; and establishes that no single ideology should be dictated by

its latest multi-party constitution ratified in 1 996. The constitution defines GRIZA as sovereign and indep endent; it defines the people as the single source of power; and establishes that no single ideology should be dictated by the state . Freedom of religion is guaranteed by law, while churches and other religious organis ations in GRIZA are separate from the state. Minority rights are protected in accordance with a 1991 law guaranteeing the rights of ethnic minorities and the right to education . While the GRIZA language is specified as the official state language, the MURINUS language is widespread in eastern GRIZA, including in the conduct of business . Freedom of speech and press are guaranteed by law, but on occasions the authorities have been known to interfere with the news media through different forms of pressure. Several labour unions are affiliated to the Federation of Labour Unions, although a number of independent unions exist. The right to strike is legally guaranteed but strikes based solely on political demands are prohibited . Executive Branch The President is elected by pop ular vote for a five-year term and is the Head of State. The President acts as the commander in chief of the Armed Forces, oversees the government’sministries and has the power to propose and veto legislation . Furthermore, the President nominates the country’s Prime Minister, who nominally serves as the Head of Governme nt. The President also has the power to declare martial law in cases of national emergencies (initially

and veto legislation . Furthermore, the President nominates the country’s Prime Minister, who nominally serves as the Head of Governme nt. The President also has the power to declare martial law in cases of national emergencies (initially for up to 30 days) subject to parliamentary approval. Ministers in GRIZA’s Cabinet are nominated by the President with the consent of the Prime Minister and approval of the Parliament . The Cabinet brings together ministers from the country’smain ministries and oversees and coordinates government business. As a unitary republic – rather than a federal state – the country is divided into, and administered through, provincial-level structure sultimately accountable to the central government. The early post-independence years featured a weak presidency and strong parliament, but this proved unstable. Subsequent amendments to the cons titution have strengthened the Presidency at the expense of other branches of government. Additional government alstructures include a National Security Council, which is responsible for developing national security policy relating to domestic and international matters and providing advice to the President. Regular National Security Council meetings are chai red by the President and are attended by Cabinet minister s as well as senior military officers and representatives of the country’ssecurity agencies. Diagram: GRIZA Political System. 14 Legislative Branch The Parliament of GRIZA is unicameral and consists of 450 members elected for five-year term s. It is a hybrid system, with half of the members elected by proportional representation and the other half via single-seat constituencies elected by a simple majority

unicameral and consists of 450 members elected for five-year term s. It is a hybrid system, with half of the members elected by proportional representation and the other half via single-seat constituencies elected by a simple majority vote. The Parliament has the power to propose legislation, ratify international agreements and app rove the budget. The highest legislative structure in GRIZA is the Supreme Council, which can introduce constitutional amendments as long as these are supported by at least one third of its members. Political parties represented in GRIZA ’sparliament are divided along ideological and policy lines. The primary division is between parties who favour continued alignment with MURINUS and those advocating for a closer relationship with the Euro-Atlantic region, including taking steps towards European Union accession . Additional differences exist with respect to economic policy as well as approaches to address wealth inequality. Beyond this, most political parties are cantered on personalities and their specific business interests . In order to be recogn ised as a parliamentary faction a party must hold at least 15 seats in th e 450 seat Parliament. The largest faction in Parliament is the pro-Europe Fatherland Party which currently governs in coalition with other pro-Europe parties . Nearly 6 0 deputies are believed to ha ve Keram-leaning sympathies, of which nearly a half belong to the pro-MURINUS Life and Peace Party. Judiciary The Supreme Court is the main, higher-level judicial body in GRIZA. Its main responsibilities are to supervise judiciary activities and the country’swider system

of which nearly a half belong to the pro-MURINUS Life and Peace Party. Judiciary The Supreme Court is the main, higher-level judicial body in GRIZA. Its main responsibilities are to supervise judiciary activities and the country’swider system of courts. It is generally considered to be impartial and has ruled against the government on a number of occasions. Constitutional matters, meanwhile, are dealt with by a separate Constitutional Court. Laws and acts of Parliament, as well as decrees by the President or the Cabinet, may be nullified by the Constitutional Court if they are found to violate the Constitution . The independence and freedom of courts in GRIZA is protected in law, and judges are similarly protected from arbitrary dismissal. Court justices are appointed by Presidential decree, subject to confirmation by the Supreme Court. The Constitution provides for trial by jury, but this has not yet been implemented in practice. Prosecutors in GRIZA come under the Office of the Prosecutor General and have significant powers in comparison to most European countries (accordingly, the country also has very high prosecution rates).
