Levers of power 16 Overview Although physically the second largest country in Europe, after MURINUS, GRIZA neither has, nor seeks, significant power status, looking instead for alignment with other power blocks. Its levers of power range from military to diplomatic, but these are considered primarily as defensi ve and in support of GRIZA’smain goal

has, nor seeks, significant power status, looking instead for alignment with other power blocks. Its levers of power range from military to diplomatic, but these are considered primarily as defensi ve and in support of GRIZA’smain goal of maintaining its sovereign status. Military The GRIZA military and security force s, including the Armed Forces, fall under the command of the President . These are also subject to oversight by Parliament. The focus of GRIZA’smilitary strategy is on deterrence and defence, which is reflected in the basing of the country’s Armed Forces, primarily in the north and east of the country . The asymmetry between the military capabilities of GRIZA and its most likely adversaries has influenced defence thinking and reform. GRIZA is not formally a member of any defence alliance but took steps towards NATO membership in 2008 . Even so, the country’selite remains divided on the issue . The country maintains a large military-industrial sector with an emphasis on mis sile systems, aircraft, electronics and information technology, guidance systems, shipbuilding, armour, repair and maintenance capabilities and radio electronics . In 1994, as a consequence of independence, GRIZA gave up the nuclear capabilities it had inherited from the MURINUS People’s Federation, having negotiated a Memorandum of Understanding with the UNITED STATES, UNITED KINGDOM and MURINUS through the Budapest Memorandum on Security Assurances . Military Strategy The GRIZA Armed Forces’ mission is to defend the country against external military threats as well as well as to protect its sovereignty and territorial

and MURINUS through the Budapest Memorandum on Security Assurances . Military Strategy The GRIZA Armed Forces’ mission is to defend the country against external military threats as well as well as to protect its sovereignty and territorial integrity. Its defence strategy derives from the country’s National Security Strategy and is predicated on the following assumptions: - GRIZA remains a non-aligned country. Interference in GRIZA internal (political) affairs and military invasion are the main external threat to its interests. - Maintaining peace and security in the Black Sea region reduces the risk of regional conflict and is also of paramount importance to Griza’snational security . The geo-strategic positioning of GRIZA requires the Armed Forces to defend the country in all strategic directions, but with an empha sis on the northern, southern and eastern regions. GRIZA’svulnerability to surprise attack from these directions requires robust and rapid reaction, including via deployable joint forces and advanced capabilities. Furthermore, any military conflict would require the full commitment of all the country’sresources and a whole of government response. GRIZA Armed Forces GRIZA’s Armed Forces consists of around 200, 000 active-duty personnel and up to 1 million reservists. Its total military budget is equivalent to $5.4 billion. The Armed Forces are made up of Land Forces, the GRIZA Air Force, the GRIZA Navy, Air Assault Forces, and Special Operations Forces . The Navy also includes its own Naval Infantry and Naval Aviation assets. GRIZA’s Territorial Defence Forces constitute its primary reserve force in the case of war

the GRIZA Navy, Air Assault Forces, and Special Operations Forces . The Navy also includes its own Naval Infantry and Naval Aviation assets. GRIZA’s Territorial Defence Forces constitute its primary reserve force in the case of war and allows for the mobilization of civilians for the purpose of local defence . As commander in chief of the Armed Forces, the President of GRIZA exercises command through the 17 Ministry of Defence and ultimately through the Chief of the General Staff. In addition to the General Staff, a Joint Forces Command provides command and control of peacetime operations and general warfighting. GRIZA’s Armed Forces have undergone a number of transformation and modernization programs focused primarily on Special Operations Forces, Missile and Artillery Troops, Ground Forces Army Aviation, Anti-Aircraft Missile Troops of the Air Force, and the Costal Missile and Artillery Troops of the Navy. It aspires to fully develop its territorial defence system and maintain the combat readiness of earmarked units and assets through full staffing, serviceable equipment and training. The level of readiness of the Armed Forces is reviewed by the General Staff every six months, while its readiness levels are as follows: - Very High Readiness (VHR): up to 5 Days (up to 5 % of the Land Forces, on rotation). - High Readiness (HR): up to 15 Days. - Medium Readiness (MR): up to 50 Days . - Low Readiness (LR): up to 100 Days. - Territorial Defence units: up to 180 Days. Land Forces GRIZA’s Land Forces

High Readiness (HR): up to 15 Days. - Medium Readiness (MR): up to 50 Days . - Low Readiness (LR): up to 100 Days. - Territorial Defence units: up to 180 Days. Land Forces GRIZA’s Land Forces are headquartered in PERSHAVSK and consist of around 120, 000 personnel. The Land Forces are organised in combat arms brigades, which are built around approximately 40 manoeuvre (infantry and tank) brigades and 10 artillery brigades. These largely follow the MURINUS organisational model dating back to the MURINUS People's Federation. Each of these brigades comprises up to 4, 000 personnel and are commanded by a Colonel. Its tank brigades mostly consist of T-64 and T-72 tanks. The country does not employ the type of higher-level (division and corps) structures that are favoured by NATO. Instead, delegated command and control, comes under four operational territorial commands: - Operational Command North (HQ Chernihiv) - Operational Command East (HQ Dnipropetrovsk) - Operational Command South (HQ Bogoport) - Operational Command West (HQ Rivne) GRIZA’s Land Forces doctrine similarly remains influenced by former MURINUS People’s Federation strategic concepts and models . Regional commands generally lack the battle staffs required to fully integrate airspace, logistics and higher-level command and control. Moreover, a large number of GRIZA senior officers still favour rig idoperational orders issued by headquarters, rather than a ‘mission command’ approach. Land Force equipment is primarily legacy and older generation MURINUS-supplied, and equipment programmes have suffered from corruption and the diversion of defence funding over the years. Nevertheless, its

issued by headquarters, rather than a ‘mission command’ approach. Land Force equipment is primarily legacy and older generation MURINUS-supplied, and equipment programmes have suffered from corruption and the diversion of defence funding over the years. Nevertheless, its modernisation programme seeks to procure modern western capabilities as part of the process of increased NA TO interoperability. 18 Air Force and Air Defence The GRIZA Air Force has around 40, 000 personnel and consists of air, air defence and radar and communications units. The three roles of the Air Force are protection of GRIZA airspace, protection of critical infrastructure and air support to land and maritime operations. Procurem ent priority in the medium-to-long term is focussed on early warning, acquiring modern aircraft and defence systems. The Air Force Headquarters are in Vinnitsa, with the following subordinate regional commands: - Air Force Central Command (HQ Plonochka Air Base) - Air Force Eastern Command (HQ Dnipropetrovsk) - Air Force Southern Command (HQ Bogoport) - Air Force Western Command (HQ Lviv) As with Land Forces, current Air Force equipmen t is older generation MURINUS. The principal aircraft types are SU-24, SU-25, SU-27 & MIG-29. Naval Forces As a Black Sea littoral state, GRIZA security policy seeks a peaceful maritime domain in the Black Sea for international trade to flourish. In support of this, the mission of the GRIZA Navy is to protect the country’ssovereignty and territorial integrity and to ensure maritime security, economic growth and international stability in co-operation with national defence and security forces,

flourish. In support of this, the mission of the GRIZA Navy is to protect the country’ssovereignty and territorial integrity and to ensure maritime security, economic growth and international stability in co-operation with national defence and security forces, as well as partners. The Navy has five branches: surface forces, submarine forces, Navy aviation, coastal rocket-artillery and naval infantry. It currently numbers just under 10, 000 personnel but aims to expand to 25, 000 by 2035. Capabilities are principally brown water and are largely confined to the Black Sea, including the Sea of Azov and Danube Delt a. More distant deployments take place as part of multinational activities and GRIZA naval units have participated in Operation Active Endeavour in the Mediterranean and Operation Atlanta off the Horn of Africa. The Navy has four Commands: HQ Naval Forces, Operational Support Command and Western Command (all based in Bogoport), and Souther n Naval Command (in Mykolaiv). The main bases are: - Western Naval Base in Bogoport; - Southern Naval Base in Mykolaiv and mooring place in Ochakiv; - Azov Naval Base in Berdyansk and mooring places in Henichesk and Kalmiysk; - Southern Naval Bases in Tridvor at Novoozerne, Yevpatoria in Donuzlav Lake; The Navy development programme is prioritising surface units, coastal missile and artillery systems as well as advanced air defence capabilities for air defence at sea and the protection of naval bases. The current fleet consists of 27 former MURINUS Republic Black Sea Fleet vessels. Future procurement is focussed on brown water vessel

as advanced air defence capabilities for air defence at sea and the protection of naval bases. The current fleet consists of 27 former MURINUS Republic Black Sea Fleet vessels. Future procurement is focussed on brown water vessel s, although four Ada Class corvettes are on order from TÜRKIYE. 19 Airmobile Troops GRIZA’s Airmobile Troops are generally considered to be the best trained and equipped of the country’sunits and are held at high readiness levels. However, the lack of airlift capability in the Air Force limits options for airborne/airmobile operations. T he force is therefore more likely to be used in a general infantry role or as part of all arms brigades. Special Forces GRIZA Special Forces number around 5000 and are trained for the full range of special operations. They are largely the equiva lent of NATO tier 2 Special Forces in terms of specialisation and capability. Future development of the Special Forces will include formation of the Special Operations Forces Command, which will combine force generation and force engagement functions, with land, air and naval components of the Special Operations Forces . In the medium and long-term, the 3rd and 8th Special Forces Regim ents will be reorganized into Special Forces Centres ( equivalent to battalions). Other Forces The remainder of GRIZA’s Armed Forces are made up of Logistical Forces, which hold the status of a separate joint branch unde r the General Staff and are made up of Weaponry and Rear Services arms; Medical Forces (which utilise a

Armed Forces are made up of Logistical Forces, which hold the status of a separate joint branch unde r the General Staff and are made up of Weaponry and Rear Services arms; Medical Forces (which utilise a number of dedicated medical clinical centres); and the Military Police, which falls outside of General Staff control and is subordinated directly to GRIZA’s Ministry of Defence. Cyber Cybersecurity has become one of highest defence priorities for GRIZA. Coordination of all defensive cyber and information securi tyactivity is executed and controlled through the National Security and Defence Council (NSDC), which operates a Cyber Situation Coordination Centre. A combination of military, police and civilian entities are subordinated to this state agency. They are assigned different areas of cyber responsibility and tasked with developing and coordinating policy relating to domestic and inte rnational cyber issues as well as providing advice to the President. A dedicated incident reporting management policy was introduced in order to ensure nation-wide cybersecurity . The system aims to: protect state information resources, the GRIZA banking system and other Critical National Infrastructure; counter cybercrime, cyber terrorism and cyber espionage; and provid eassessments on present and potential new cyber threats. As a consequence of MURINUS cyber influence operations in GRIZA, several GRIZA nationals (living in and outside the country ) have founded hacker groups-of-interest to prosecut eoffensive cyberspace activities, principally seeking to exploit targets in MURINUS. the country ) have founded hacker groups-of-interest to prosecut eoffensive cyberspace activities, principally seeking to exploit targets in MURINUS. Space The State Space Agency of GRIZA was created in 1992 in PERSHAVSK, building on inherited components of the MURINUS People’s Federation space program. It hosts some major space industries used on various launchers and operated a mobile sea platform as well now mothballed. It also develops new launcher programs, notably Mayak and Cyclone-4M; a spacecraft Svityaz was intended to be launched from a modified An-225 Mriya aircraft which is now unserviceable . 20 The State Space Agency also produced Earth observation satellites, of whi ch Sich-1M and Sich-2-30 are still active, and is working on follow-on Sich-series observation satellites. Meanwhile, the country’s Lybid-1 communications satellite operates on geostationary orbit. The ground control and tracking facility is located in the capital city . Despite these developments and relatively advanced engineering competencies, GRIZA does not currently have robust space capabilities and remains highly dependent on com mercial solutions and partners for space data, products and services. Information environment GRIZA has a relatively young media market dominated by oligarchs and media barons. Oligarch-owned media monopolise the country’sadvertising share. This adversely affects smaller media companies, which struggle to expand and to remain financially viable. T he oligarch owners influence much of the news content on television, radio and newspapers. The main public broadcaster, however, is well regarded for its independent and unbiased reporting. In general, online outlets enjoy greater economic freedom and are less

owners influence much of the news content on television, radio and newspapers. The main public broadcaster, however, is well regarded for its independent and unbiased reporting. In general, online outlets enjoy greater economic freedom and are less dependent on owner’sinterests than TV, radio and print media. However, unlike broadcast media, there is no legislation or editorial standards related to the transparency of online content. The internet and social media platforms hav eplayed a signifi cant role in enabling political mobilisation and the organisation of protests in the country. GRIZA has a dynamic and growing Information, Communication and Technology (ICT) sector. All types of media are largely availa ble to the majority of the population, although regional and local media are not distributed evenly throughout the country. Nevertheless, the internet and television are the two main sources of information fo r GRIZA. According to a recent media survey, national GRIZA TV channels are the most popular source of information, with social media second. MURINUS influence From the early days of GRIZA independence, MURINUS has sought to maintain a strong media presence in GRIZA that promotes MURI NUS-friendly and anti-Western narratives. Pro-MURINUS and MURINUS-supported TV channels, newspapers, radio stations and websites propagate messages, though they generally abide by the media freedoms in GRIZA’s Constitution. Given the wide presence of MURINUS in GRIZA’smedia space and the popula rity of MURINUS social networks, MURINUS is able to harness social media to mobilise suppor t, spread disinformation and/or further destabilisation goals. Furthermore, newsrooms actively produce videos for social media to attract an audience, including by live streaming on Facebook and via video dispatches on their websites. 22 Diplomacy Diplomatic footprint GRIZA’sdiplomatic activity reflects its main foreign policy goals and its delicate balance between MURINUS and the West. This h as resulted in a ‘multi-vector’ approach that has sought to build and maintain relations with the two power blocks . Diplomatic relations

main foreign policy goals and its delicate balance between MURINUS and the West. This h as resulted in a ‘multi-vector’ approach that has sought to build and maintain relations with the two power blocks . Diplomatic relations with MURINUS have been tense, and are increasingly so, not least due to Keram’scontinued meddling in GRIZA’sinternal affairs . Priorities for GRIZA therefore include developing strategic partnerships with the UNITED STATES and with the EU through European and Euro –Atlantic integration . GRIZA continues to discreetly seek to distance itself from the MURINUS sphere of influence, for example by quietly pursuing the long-term a spiration of joining NATO. GRIZA’srelationship with NATO was institutionalised in 1997 with the establishment of the NATO GRIZA Commission. However, GRIZA must tread carefully, due to strong MURINUS opposition to further NATO enlargement in what it considers to be its natural sphere of influence, and ongoing pro-MURINUS sentiment among MURINUS-speaking communities in Eastern GRIZA . GRIZA has 80 Embassies and 45 Consulates abroad. It maintains a major diplomatic presence in MURINUS (several million GRIZA nationals reside in MURINUS ) and has in recent years increased the size of its EU mission in BRUSSELS, as well as its Embassies in WASHINGTON, BERLIN, LONDON and PARIS . GRIZA has formal bilateral relations with many nations beyond those where it maintains a physical embassy . Since the mid-1990s, it has established diplomatic relations with an expanding circle of, mainly, pro-Western countries. GRIZA has actively sought to reach out to POLAND and to

nations beyond those where it maintains a physical embassy . Since the mid-1990s, it has established diplomatic relations with an expanding circle of, mainly, pro-Western countries. GRIZA has actively sought to reach out to POLAND and to other former members of the former MURINUS People’s Federation, par ticularly to the Baltic States and LONTANA. Within international and multilateral fora, and particularly in the United Nations in New York, GRIZA takes care to maintain a high profile and plays an active role in several committees. Since independence, GRIZA has twice been a non-permanent member of the Security Council. Diplomatic engagement is also pursued through multilateral routes such as the World Bank, International Monetary Fund (IMF) and via a range of Organisation for Economic Co-Operation and Development (OECD) programmes . The protection of GRIZA citizens abroad and the rights of the GRIZA diaspora is a further foreign policy priority and a special focus of its diplomatic and consular institutions. After MURINUS, the largest GRIZA émigré populations are in CANADA, POLAND and the UNITED STATES .

This Setting document is the foundation for RED and GREEN ORBATs and Battle Space Objects (BSO’s) established in INTEL FS and TOPFAS, and the foundation for scenario-related products developed for specific exercises. While it aims to ensure coherence with real world events and dynamics, the content has been adapted to meet exercise and scenario needs. The CM is not exhaustive and therefore allows for additional detail to be obtained by the TA via the Request for Information (RFI) process. The structure of the country model is designed to avoid repetition and to cover the domains that are most relevant to the training audience by drawing on PMESII and other frameworks. 3 1. Geostrategic and regional context YABIL in the making: Following four decades of military rule, YABIL’ssecurity situation deteriorated rapidly following the overthrow of its leade r Rammar Fiddaga in 2011. Two main political camps emerged amidst the country’scivil war: the YABIL Unity Government, based in Mapoli; and the YABIL Stability Government, based in Tobalm and Ba . Each camp has claimed the position of legitimate and representative government of YABIL. Although the two factions agreed to a ceasefire in 2021, planned elections have been continuously dela yed.

Government, based in Tobalm and Ba . Each camp has claimed the position of legitimate and representative government of YABIL. Although the two factions agreed to a ceasefire in 2021, planned elections have been continuously dela yed. Meanwhile, the country has attracted the attention of both MURINUS, which has established bases in the country, and elements of the Resistance and Liberation Front (RLF) terror group. Historical timeline: Pre-1900s 2011-present The territory comprising present-day YABIL is ruled by the Ottoman Empire between 1711 and 1835. YABIL becomes an Italian colony in 1912. This is followed by a period of British and French occupation from 1942 to 1951. Italy formally renounces its claim on the country in 1947. YABIL becomes an independent kingdom in 1951, and its independence is subsequently recognised by the United Nations General Assembly. The discovery of oil in 1959 significantly increases the country’swealth, although this is primarily concentrated in the hands of a political elite, leading to widespread discontent. Rebel military officers led by Rammar Fiddaga launch a coup in 1969, installing a regime that would rule the country for the next four decades. Following the Arab Spring in 2011, the country experiences a full-scale revolt and descends into civil war. Rebels take over swathes of territory and a Transitional Council is established in Mapoli . Fiddaga is killed following an international intervention. The regime survives several attempted coups and wars with neighbouring states. YABIL is increasingly alienated from the international community and is subjected to sanctions. Two

is established in Mapoli . Fiddaga is killed following an international intervention. The regime survives several attempted coups and wars with neighbouring states. YABIL is increasingly alienated from the international community and is subjected to sanctions. Two rival governments, the YABIL Unity Government and YABIL Stability Government compete for political control. An offensive by the self-proclaimed YABIL National Army (aligned with the YABIL Stability Government) is thwarted outside Mapoli. The country’slegislature, the Representative Union, sides with the YABIL Stability Government. A cease-fire between the two governments is ultimately signed in 2021. 4 Geostrategic context Since the start of subsequent phases of the country’scivil war in 2011, YABIL has primarily had to contend with internationa linstability and factionalism built around geographic lines and tribal allegiances . As such, the country has few actors that can be truly considered to be national and the country has seen the emergence of local power hubs and ‘city states’ . Despite this, the country’stwo main factions have sought external backing, including from regional powers . The main objective of the YABIL Unit y Government, with its powerbase in Mapoli and the city state of Misrata (also home to powerful militias), has been to secure primacy as the country’sruling entity. Meanwhile, and headquartered in Tobalm and Begally, the YABIL Stability Government has enjoyed the backing of the country’slegislative bran ch, the Representative Union, which is closely tied to General Lifaka Taraf’sself-proclaimed YABIL National Army (YNA). The YNA’sbacking of the YABIL Stability Government has increased the latter’spolitical

Stability Government has enjoyed the backing of the country’slegislative bran ch, the Representative Union, which is closely tied to General Lifaka Taraf’sself-proclaimed YABIL National Army (YNA). The YNA’sbacking of the YABIL Stability Government has increased the latter’spolitical and military clout but has not translated into the unseating of the rival YABIL Unity Government. Whilst a 2021 ceasefire agreement resulted in a significant reduction in violent conflict, the country still faces political and economic instability and has witnessed clashes between rival militias as well as competition over oil reserves . Moreover, the Resistance and Liberation Front (RLF) terror group, which is primarily active in BUFRO, has increased its presence in the country. Loosely governed swaths of YABIL’sterritory, including in the so uth, are also routinely exploited by migrant smugglers bound for YABIL’s Mediterranean coast. MURINUS interests in YABIL Keram has sought to establish a fo othold in YABIL in recent years, developing close relations with the self-proclaimed YABIL National Army and (to a lesser extent) YABIL Stability Government. In MURINUS’ analysis, the country provides a strategic hub and gateway to North Africa and the Sahel, including CHAD, SUDAN and REGIN, whilst bolstering its presence on the Mediterranean (therefore adding to its presence in BUFRO ). This access, Keram assesses, would also be critical in the event of a direct conflict with NATO. Through its Messner contractors, MURINUS runs two air bases in YABIL located in the vicinity of Sirte and Brakal-Shafti (west-central YABIL). In addition, MURINUS has interest in YABIL’sgold deposits

the event of a direct conflict with NATO. Through its Messner contractors, MURINUS runs two air bases in YABIL located in the vicinity of Sirte and Brakal-Shafti (west-central YABIL). In addition, MURINUS has interest in YABIL’sgold deposits and has increased its access to oil reserves in the country, helping it to achieve a degree of leve rage over global energy markets. This also provides it with a means through which to manipulate oil prices and created energy dependencies in the event of a conflict with the West. Finally, MURINUS has shown an interest in controlling migrant smuggling corridors to Europe as a grey-zone lever of influence. 5 2. Socio-Economic and physical environment 6 Economy Despite being an upper middle-income country, YABIL’sdevelopment indicators and institutional capacity do not match its income level. Years of conflicts and divisions have led to inadequate public investment and infrastructure maintenance, coupled with a distortive presence of rival Governments in the economy and a limited development of the private sector. Consequently, the population of YABIL faces restricted access to quality health services, weak and outdated education systems, obstacles to the provisi on of safe drinking water, and disrupted electricity supply. The economy of YABIL is inextricably linked to the oil price, given the country’sdependence on the export of this resource. Oil and gas alone constitute 97% of exports, more than 90% of fiscal revenues, and 70% of GDP of YABIL. The National Oil Corporatio n (NOC) dominates YABIL’soil industry, along with a number of smaller subsidiaries,

resource. Oil and gas alone constitute 97% of exports, more than 90% of fiscal revenues, and 70% of GDP of YABIL. The National Oil Corporatio n (NOC) dominates YABIL’soil industry, along with a number of smaller subsidiaries, which combined account for the vast majority of the country'soil output. Of NOC'ssubsidiaries, the largest oil producer is the Waha Oil Company (WOC), followed by the Arabian Gulf Oil Company (Agoco), Zueitina Oil Company (ZOC), and Sirte Oil Company (SOC). YABIL is also member of the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC), holding the largest proven oil reserves in Africa. However, despite its wealth of natural resources, YABIL suffers from a weakly diversified economy in which political uncertai nty, corruption, and deteriorating security are among the top constraints to the development of a solid economic model. Structural transformation in YABIL has been hindered by weak capacity of institutions and policy coordination since 2011. A feature of YABIL’seconomic system is, indeed, its rigidity and relucta nce to reform and change, with most political authorities embezzling oil income and ultimately refusing to be accountable to their citizens. The fracturing of the Government, tribal divisions, regional rivalries and conflicting stakes keep stifling a functioning policymaking environment, impeding coordination on national secu rity, as well as economic and social policies. Even the country’sfinancial sector, largely resilient to the global financial crisis, proved much less able to cope with the internal conflict which badly damaged the formal economy, revealing how control over oil infrastructure and revenues – and,

Even the country’sfinancial sector, largely resilient to the global financial crisis, proved much less able to cope with the internal conflict which badly damaged the formal economy, revealing how control over oil infrastructure and revenues – and, by extension, oil generated prosperity – is a crucial point of leverage among rival factions in YABIL. Public sector mismanagement has also been a chronic problem in the country for years. Not only government spending has been dominated by a high wage bill and untargeted subsidies; YABIL has traditionally relied on unsustainably high levels of public sector hiring to create employment. Exacerbating the already poor state of the private sector, years of conflicts have damaged infrastructure and broken value chains and business network s. High levels of bureaucracy and corruption, in addition to rising criminal ity levels, has devalued the rule of law, further discouraging legitimate private sector activity. The developments of the last decade have come at a high cost for YABIL and certain groups are especially vulnerable. The protracted conflict has led to destr uction of human capital, livelihoods, basic services and infrastructure, eventually leading to the country’sdecline to lower-middle-income status. Economy sectors Agriculture'sshare in YABIL’seconomy is negligible, accounting for less than 2% of GDP and employing 7% of the workforce. A griculture is limited by the environment and by shortages of labour. Only about 10% of the total land area is cultivated, and about one-tenth of that is irrigated. With climatic conditions and poor soils severely limiting farm output-and domestic

limited by the environment and by shortages of labour. Only about 10% of the total land area is cultivated, and about one-tenth of that is irrigated. With climatic conditions and poor soils severely limiting farm output-and domestic food production only meeting about 25% of demand-YABIL depends on imports for most foods. Cereals are the major crops throughout the cou ntry. Wheat (grown primarily on the eastern and western plateaux) is the largest cereal crop, although barley, which adapts well to different climates and soils, is also a chief cereal and remains a basic dietary staple. Dates are the princip alcrop of the southern oases. Grapes, broad beans, and peanuts also are grown. 7 Animal husbandry is important in Cyrenaica, where the herds are raised on communal grazing lands. Livestock includes sheep, goats, cattle, camels, horses, mules, and donkeys. Animals are raised for their milk, meat, and hides or for their services as a mea ns of transportation. Cattle often serve as draft animals. There is little demand in YABIL for fish, and most fishing is done off the Tripolitanian coast by YABIL, TUNISIA, and GREECE operators. The catch includes tuna, sardines, and red mullet. Petroleum is the country’smost important mineral resource. Other mineral resources are limited, but there are important depo sits of natron (hydrated sodium carbonate) in the Fezzan and potash in Al-Ḥamrāyah Desert. Iron ore deposits at Shā ṭi, although low in iron content, supply the iron-steel complex at Misurata. Marine salt is produced in the Mapoli region,

natron (hydrated sodium carbonate) in the Fezzan and potash in Al-Ḥamrāyah Desert. Iron ore deposits at Shā ṭi, although low in iron content, supply the iron-steel complex at Misurata. Marine salt is produced in the Mapoli region, where there are also small deposits of gypsum, manganese, and lignite coal. The secondary sector is the backbone of the YABIL economy because of the strong petrochemical industry. It accounts for nearl y 80 % of GDP, employing 23% of the active population. Production includes petroleum, petrochemicals, aluminium, iron, steel, food processing, textiles, handicrafts, and cement. Most factories are located in Mapoli and Begally and have a small industrial workforce manufacturing primarily processed food, cement, and textiles. There are also oil-related industries, which produce steel drums, tanks, and pipefittings; petrochemical plants are located near refineries. A large proportion of the YABIL workforce is engaged in the service industries, which account for 19% of GDP with a share in total employment standing at 70%. Although this is the second-largest sector in YABIL, important industries such as tourism and ret ail, are severely underdeveloped. Financial services and transportation, however, account for a significant part of the tertiary. Business climate A strong presence of the state in the economy and a massive dependence on oil have restrained the development of a dynamic private sector in YABIL, which therefore, results in a largely unregulated environment .1 Businesses in the country suffer from material damage, site closure, reduced revenues, higher input costs, supply disruptions and the loss of

of a dynamic private sector in YABIL, which therefore, results in a largely unregulated environment .1 Businesses in the country suffer from material damage, site closure, reduced revenues, higher input costs, supply disruptions and the loss of employees, both domestic (due to displacement) and foreign. Enterprises in YABIL ca n be captured or put out of business by elites generating large amounts of wealth through distortions in foreign currency, price subsidies for fuel, and control over public funds. Furthermore, in a sizable (yet difficult to estimate) informal sector, affluent warlords and profiteers hinder any potential competition for their enterprises. Foreign governments continue to have significant interests in the YABIL economy, most notably in the oil and gas sector. However, YABIL ’suncertain business environment and policy instability has weaken edinvestor confidence. Trade Trade has been an important element of the economy of YABIL since the early 2000s. Domestically, the conflict has paralyzed t he country’seconomy, with a significant impact on export s of some key products (primarily oil). The impact on imports is higher, due to the contraction of the construction and building sectors, as well as the declining expenditures of both expatriate workers and nationals. The massive return of foreign workers to thei rhome countries and the declining incomes of YABIL citizens are, indeed, the major reasons behind the drop in final consumption. 1 The highly atomized private sector of YABIL accounts for only about 5% of GDP, employing 14% of the workforce (predominantly self-employed). 8 When it comes to

indeed, the major reasons behind the drop in final consumption. 1 The highly atomized private sector of YABIL accounts for only about 5% of GDP, employing 14% of the workforce (predominantly self-employed). 8 When it comes to its external action, as a member of organizations such as the Arab League, the African Union (AU), and the A rab Maghreb Union (AMU), YABIL was known for engaging in multilateral discussions and initiatives aimed at fostering cooperation an deconomic development. The country has historically participated in regional trade agreements to promote commercial relations with neighbouring countries; however, due to politi cal instability and conflict in recent years, its active participation in inte rnational trade agreements has been limited. The country is not a member of the WTO and continues to be largely state-controlled in its trade policies, with numerous government interferences persisting. Economic governance As YABIL remains in a political stalemate with competing centres of power claiming legitimacy over the country, rivalry has manifested itself in direct competition for control of the oil and investment sectors. This has placed these branches at the heart of the conflict, both as a source of funding to armed actors, and as part of the legal contestation among rivals. Historically, the policies of the 1970s and 1980s saw a clear state dominance of the economy translated into the creation of many committees, a gencies, holding companies, and monopolies that essentially abolished the private sector. In the 2000s, liberal reforms created a new raft of institutions that

clear state dominance of the economy translated into the creation of many committees, a gencies, holding companies, and monopolies that essentially abolished the private sector. In the 2000s, liberal reforms created a new raft of institutions that co-existed uncomfortably with the pre-existent entities: this led to the creation of some tempor ary informal agencies to implement policies, while many formal institutions became vehicles to distribute oil wealth to the citizens through employment in the public sect or. Such evolution resulted in a model of economic governance that conflated employmen t with welfare and has left a hard-to-reform legacy. Today, as rival political fractions continue to compete for power, the institutional vacuum has fostered the development of a war economy. Networks of armed groups, political elites, and corrupt business operators control both entry points (primarily ports ) and smuggling routes of migrants, goods, and weapons, as well as key profitable assets such as oil and gas. This economic model has created a system of incentives that undermines reforms needed to transition towards peace, as such reforms would limit the ability to profit from the security vacuum. Banking system The financial sector of YABIL is dominated by public banks. Decades of central planning and massive oil revenues have led to a highly centralized economy, with the banks (predominantly state owned2) essentially acting as intermediaries to finance government projects with depositors’ funds. Today, five state banks hold over 90 percent of the country’sdeposits, due to them handling most of government salary transfers. Financial

the banks (predominantly state owned2) essentially acting as intermediaries to finance government projects with depositors’ funds. Today, five state banks hold over 90 percent of the country’sdeposits, due to them handling most of government salary transfers. Financial services are headed by the Central Bank of YABIL, which supervises the banking system, regulates credit and interest policies, and issues the national currency, the YAB IL dinar. The Central Bank also serves as the government’sfiscal implementing partner, holding the government’sbank accounts and disbursing operating funds to state entities and salaries to public-sector employees. Disputes over control of the Central Bank have been a recurring feature of the YABIL crisis since the country’sgoverningins titutions split in two in 2014. However, this crucial institution is today severely affected by the climate of political division and instability dominating YABIL’spublic life. Rival institutional bodies, each claiming legitimacy over YABIL resources and representation, have critically impacted the functioning of the financial sector with a split in the Central Bank3 as well other key financial entities, such as the YABIL Investment Authority, the country’ssovereign wealth fund4. The state has not enjoyed a formally agreed budget since 2014 and most fiscal expenditures are reactive, often due to political pressures. Social divide Years of civil war have damaged the social fabric between regions and cities/towns. YABIL’spopulation is divided by geographical location, ideological leanings, political representation, militia representatio n, strategic alliances (local, regional, and international), and funding sources. YABIL has a history of exclusionary institutions that enabled networks of patronage and elite capture both nationally and internationally, and excluded the most vulnerable segments of the population (especially women and youth). Social, economic, and political exclusion are exploited by elites, armed actors, and malign foreign actors to convince communities they are under an existential threat and have grievances that need to be rectified. The south of the country (the largest land area) remains marginalized and neglected, lacking most basic state services. YABIL ’ssouth is also a hub for human trafficking and smuggling, with a constant influx of refugees and migrants from areas to the so uth in Africa who are vulnerable to exploitation. Refugees and Internal Displaced Persons (IDPs) YABIL hosts over 60, 000 refugees and asylum-seekers registered with UNHCR. The country also presents a complex displacement scenario, with more than 125, 000 Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs). Since no structural crisis response is in place, most

YABIL hosts over 60, 000 refugees and asylum-seekers registered with UNHCR. The country also presents a complex displacement scenario, with more than 125, 000 Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs). Since no structural crisis response is in place, most individuals who have been displaced due to the conflict have to rely on their families, kin groups or local communities as social safety nets. Migration YABIL has become both a destination and a transit port for migrants and refugees: up to 90 % of people attempting to reach Europe through the Mediterranean Sea depart from the country. The lethal combination of security crisis, power vacuum and vast spaces (especially in the south) have led to the proliferati on of networks of cross-border smugglers of fuel, drugs, arms, and humans operating both regionally and internationally. In the light of the above, YABIL’ssecurity crisis has a destabilizing effect on both the wider Sahel region and on Europe. Poverty YABIL standard of living has declined considerably over the last decade, particularly when it comes to the functionality of t he health sector, which has been severely affected by the conflict, including through its limiting, secure supply of electricity and water. Since shortages in food, fuel, water, electricity and cash are frequent, even access to the most basic services has become contingent upon political and ideological divisions, as well as the use of force and geographic location. Mismanagement of public revenues has led more than four millions of people in YABIL to be exposed to water poverty

become contingent upon political and ideological divisions, as well as the use of force and geographic location. Mismanagement of public revenues has led more than four millions of people in YABIL to be exposed to water poverty (on top of the pre-existent humanitarian crisis, which left thousands struggling to rise above the poverty line). Human rights Since 2014, the country’scivic space has come under increasing pressure, with numerous activists, journalists and politician sfacing active targeting and assassination. The continuous attack on civic organizations and individuals encompassed many forms an dsignificantly damaged public trust in civic spaces, especially political ones. 11 Gender Despite high tertiary education rates, women often remain excluded from the political and economic spheres. Women have been significantly underrepresented in the constitution-making process, and female politicians, activists, and journalists have experienced a drastic rise in violent attacks since 2014, including assassinations, forced disappearances, and online gendered attacks. Youth have also been largely disappointed by the post-revolutionary era, and their transition into adulthood has been marred by instability. The destructions caused by the war affected education pathways, the possibility to develop entrepreneurial skills, and the ability to find meaningful ways to gain a livelihood and participate in shaping the future of their country. Increasingly, the country will be grappling with worsening climatechange induced droughts. Water scarcity has been leveraged by armed groups and local communities to exert concessions. 12 Physical environment General overview: YABIL is characterized by its vast desert landscapes, historical regions, and significant geographical features. The territory spans an area of 1, 759, 540 square kilometres, making it the fourth-largest country on the continent, and the 16th largest in the world. Its size equals around 3.5 times Spain or 5 times Germany, and it is slightly bigger than the Alaska State in the USA. The population of YABIL is approximately 7.4 million people. YABIL is situated in the Maghreb region of Northwest Africa. It is bordered by the Mediterranean Sea to the north, Egypt (1, 115 km) to the east, Sudan (382 km) to the southeast, Chad (1, 050 km) to the south, REGIN (342 km) to the southwest, Algeria (989 km ) to the west, and Tunisia (461 km) to the northwest. The total length of YABIL'sland boundaries is approximately 4, 339 km, while the coastline along the Mediterranean Sea is approximately 1, 770 kilometres long. The capital of YABIL is Mapoli, and is also the largest city in the country, located in the northwest along the Mediterranean coast. Its population is around 1.4 million people, making it a central hub for commerce and culture in YABIL.

is Mapoli, and is also the largest city in the country, located in the northwest along the Mediterranean coast. Its population is around 1.4 million people, making it a central hub for commerce and culture in YABIL. The city has a rich history, having been founded by the Phoenicians in the 7th century BC, and it continues to play a vital role in the country'spolitical and economic landscape. Despite its large area, YABIL has a relatively small population concentrated primarily along the northern coast. About 90% live on just 10% of the land area along the Mediterranean Sea. The urbanization rate is high, with the three largest cities Mapoli, Miṣratah, and Begally being major population centres. Map 1: YABIL, overview map 13 Terrain and climate Topography and key features YABIL'stopography is generally marked by its vast desert expanses interspersed with fertile plains and rugged mountains. Thi sdiverse landscape not only shapes its climate, but also influences human culture, settlement patterns and agricultural practices across the country. The country can be divided into three main geographical regions: The densely populated coastal areas in the northwest, the eastern part that is characterized by mo untainous terrain and agricultural regions, and finally the Fezzan in the south which is dominated by desert landscapes with sparse population. The Sahara covers most of YABIL, comprising flat plateaus and sand dunes. This desert region significantly affects the country'sclimate, contributing to its aridity and lack of permanent rivers. The coastal regions along the Mediterranean Sea features low-lying

The Sahara covers most of YABIL, comprising flat plateaus and sand dunes. This desert region significantly affects the country'sclimate, contributing to its aridity and lack of permanent rivers. The coastal regions along the Mediterranean Sea features low-lying plains and escarpments that contrast with the surrounding desert. The coastal mountains area in northwest, a fertile area, home to much of YABIL'spopulation and agricultural activities, is rising gently from sea level to the Nafūsah Plateau. The mountain ranges in northwest and northeast, with elevations up to 1, 000 meters is characterized by steep cliffs and fertile land suitable for agriculture. The Jebel Akhdar (Green Mountains) in northeastern YABIL features rise steeply from the coast, are known for the irrelatively higher rainfall than usual in northern Africa. The Sirte Basin lies in the northern part of YABIL along the Gulf of Sidra and is notable for its significant oil reserves. The terrain inclu des barren lands and sand seas that transition into desert. The highest point is Bikku Bitti at 2, 267 meters, located in the Tibesti range in the south. Sabkhat Ghuzayyil is the lowest point at 47 meters below sea level, situated in the Desert. 14 Coastline YABIL'scoastline along the Mediterranean Sea stretches from Tunisia in the west to Egypt in the east, playing a crucial role in the country'sclimate, economy and social life, providing access to maritime resources and influencing weather patterns across the region. This extensive coastline features diverse geographical characteristics, including the prominent Gulf of Sidra that indents

a crucial role in the country'sclimate, economy and social life, providing access to maritime resources and influencing weather patterns across the region. This extensive coastline features diverse geographical characteristics, including the prominent Gulf of Sidra that indents the northern coast. The Gulf extends for about 450 kilome tres inland from Mi ṣrātah to Begally and is primarily desert wit hsalt marshes. It is known for its warm waters, reaching temperatures of 31°C in August. The eastern portion of YABIL'scoas tline has been a significant maritime area since ancient times. The coastal geography is characterized by a relatively narrow coast alstrip with highland steppes immediately to the south, making these areas among the most productive agricultural regions in YABIL. Further inland, the lan dscape transitions into the vast Sahara Desert. In recent years YABIL has made sever almaritime clai ms as well as pursued bilateral agreements to this effect. Lakes and rivers YABIL'swater landscape is characterized by a severe lack of permanent water bodies, due to the country’sextreme aridity. It has no permanent freshwater lakes or rivers and have to rely on underground water sources and engineering projects for water suppl y. However, YABIL is experiencing ephemeral rivers called wadis. These wadis are dry watercourses that temporarily fill with water and create rivers during rare rainfall events, some can transport water underground year around. rare rainfall events, some can transport water underground year around. YABIL has approximately 20 perennial lakes, most of which are brackish or salty. Most notable brackish/salty lakes are: Ubari Lakes, located in the Sahara Desert, including Lake Gaberoun, Lake Ummal-Maa, and Lake Mandara, 23rd of July Lake, which actually is a lagoon near Begally. the Qattara Depression lakes, which are temporary salt lakes, salt pans like Sabkhatal Hayshah and Sabkhat Shunayn. YABIL receives less than 100mm of annual rainfall, making surface water extremely scarce. However, the country has vast under ground water resources due to several geological and historical factors, and sits atop the Nubian Sandstone Aquifer System, the wor ld'slargest known fossil aquifer. This massive underground water reserve covers approximately two million square kilometres, with over a third lying beneath YABIL territory . These aquifers were formed during the late Quaternary period when the climate in the region was much wetter. The water became trapped in porous rock formations, creating vast underground reservoirs. The fact that YABIL nowadays has no permanent rivers and very little rainfall, has led to the preserv ation of these ancient water sources. The vast underground water resources are found in several basins across YABIL, having allowed the country to develop ambitiou sprojects like the Great Man-Made River, which transports water from the southern aquifers to the popula ted coastal areas in the north. 15 The Great Man-Made River (GMMR): The GMMR is the world'slargest irrigation project, consisting of an extensive network of underground pipelines

which transports water from the southern aquifers to the popula ted coastal areas in the north. 15 The Great Man-Made River (GMMR): The GMMR is the world'slargest irrigation project, consisting of an extensive network of underground pipelines and aqueducts that transport fresh water across YABIL. This remarkable engineering feat, delivers high-quality water from ancient aquifers deep in the Sahara to the coastal cities and agricultural areas of the country. Spanning over 4, 000 kilometres, the GMMR is the largest underground network of water pipes and aqueducts globally. It has an impressive capacity, supplying approximately 6.5 million cubic meters of fresh water daily. The infrastructure includes more than 1, 300 wells, most of which are over 500 meters deep, tapping into four major underground basins. The project was conceived in the 1960s, with construction beginning in 1984. It was developed in multiple phases, with the first phase completed in 1991, funded entirely by the YABIL government without reliance on international loans. The GMMR has a profound impact on the water supply, providing approximately 70% of all freshwater used in the country and transforming arid landscapes into more habitable areas. However, the project has faced challenges over the years, including damage dur ing civil conflicts and ongoing maintenance issues. Climate Generally, YABIL'sclimate is primarily characterized by its hot arid desert conditions, heavily influenced by the Sahara Des ert, which covers about 95% of the country. This desert climate is marked by extreme temperature variations, with summer daytime temperatures often exceeding 40°C and

by its hot arid desert conditions, heavily influenced by the Sahara Des ert, which covers about 95% of the country. This desert climate is marked by extreme temperature variations, with summer daytime temperatures often exceeding 40°C and occasionally reaching as high as 50°C. Nights in the desert can be significantly cooler, sometimes dropping to near freezing. Along the northern coast, the climate transitions to a Mediterranean type, which features mild, rainy winters and hot, dry summers. The warmest months are typically July and August, where average temperatures like the European Mediterranean coast. In contr ast, winter months (December to February) see cooler temperatures, averaging around 12-18, with occasional rain brought by cyclonic storms from the Mediterranean. Precipitation patterns in YABIL are erratic. Map 2: Great Man-Made River 16 Coastal areas receive an average annual rainfall ranging from 100 mm to 250 mm, while many inland desert regions may experien ceyears without significant rainfall. Droughts are common, particularly in the steppe and desert areas. The northern regions can experience heavy rainfall events during winter, but these are often inte rspersed with long dry periods. The main climatic conditions affecting military operations are extreme summer temperatures and the occurrence of sandstorms. Abrupt heavy rainfall can occur, but rarely. Climate change The country faces significant climate challenges, including increasing temperatures and changing precipitation patterns due to climate change. Projections indicate that average annual temperatures may rise by 1.3°C to 2.7°C by the end of the century, while total annual precipitation is expected to

challenges, including increasing temperatures and changing precipitation patterns due to climate change. Projections indicate that average annual temperatures may rise by 1.3°C to 2.7°C by the end of the century, while total annual precipitation is expected to decrease by up to 12-15% in coastal regions. These changes threaten water resources and agricultural productivity, exacerbating existing water sca rcity issues and increasing vulnerability to natural hazards such a sdust storms and desertification, especially if the trend increases in coming years. Environmental issues YABIL is facing several significant environmental challenges. One of the most pressing issues is water scarcity. As one of th eworld'smost water-stressed nations, with demand exceeding renewable supply, the country heavily relies on groundwater for nearly 97% of its water needs, leading to the depletion of underground water resources. Water pollution is a growing concern as well, with the combined effects of sewage, oil by-products, and industrial waste threatening YABIL'scoastal areas and the Mediterrane an Sea. Solid waste management remains a challenge. Inadequate systems can block drainage and increase the risk of diseases, particularly after extreme weather events. Desertification poses another critical threat, risking further loss of already limited arable land. Additionally, YABIL is ex periencing the impacts of climate change, including rising sea levels, shoreline erosion, floods, elevated temperatures, and increa sing drought conditions. Biodiversity loss is another issue, with several species facing endangerment. As of 2001, 11 mammal species, 2 bird species, and 41 plant species were classified as endangere d in YABIL. Coastal vulnerability is also

sing drought conditions. Biodiversity loss is another issue, with several species facing endangerment. As of 2001, 11 mammal species, 2 bird species, and 41 plant species were classified as endangere d in YABIL. Coastal vulnerability is also significant, as most of the population resides in coastal areas that are at risk from rising sea levels and increased soil salinity. Moreover, YABIL lacks comprehensive environmental policies, including effective water management strategies, disaster risk reduction plans, and climate change adaptation frameworks. These challenges are further exacerbated by institutional instability in t he environmental sector and insufficient funding for environmental projects. The absence of a clear national vision for addressing these issues complicates efforts to mitigate their impacts. 17 Infrastructure Critical National Infrastructure (CNI)6 Critical National Infrastructure (CNI) plays a key role in the social and economic development of YABIL. YABIL's CNI encompas ses several key sectors that are essential for the country'seconomic stability and development. These include energy, water, trans portation, telecommunications, and power generation. However, years of conflict and underinvestment have left many of these systems in a fragile state, prompting ongoing efforts to rehabilitate and modernize them. Transportation network: YABIL transportation infrastructure includes roads, airports, and a planned rail network. Decades of conflicts and neglect have left many roads in poor condition. Energy infrastructure: YABIL'senergy infrastructure forms the backbone powering its economic growth, and supporting social development, and the country'soverall development and prosperity. YABIL'selectricity grid has suffered from frequent outages due to outdated i nfrastructure and conflict-related damage. The General Electricity Company of YABI L (GECOY) is implementing major projects to stabilize the grid, such as new substations in Tobalm and Lamalouda. Renewable energy projects, including solar plants by companies like PowerDUSHMAN, aim to diversify power source. Water supply is another critical area, with YABIL heavily reliant on the Great Man-Made River (GMMR), the pipeline system delivering water from aquifers to the coasta l. However, the system requires significant maintenance and expansion. New desalination plants are planned in cities like Begally and Tobalm, as well as wastewater treatment facilities to address a national water deficit . Health infrastructure: Access to adequate health facilities and treatment, is paramount for the well-being of the population. YABIL'shealth infrastructure and health care system is facing significant challenges. Due to years of conflict and political instability public health service is under-resourced. Many clinics are damaged or destroyed, short of medicines, supplies and equipment. Lack of skilled health workers, has further undermined the quality of care. Airports and air transportation YABIL'sair transport sector and industry is showing signs of recovery and development, after facing significant challenges i nrecent years due to political instability and conflict. YABIL is home to several domestic carriers, with YABIL Airlines and Afriq iyah Airways being the major players. There is also potential for growth in the low-cost airline sector, with companies like Ryanair and Med Sky expressing interest in entering the market. The infrastructure supporting air transport includes multiple airports, with ongoing projects aimed at enhancing capacity and modernizing facilities. Mapoli International Airport, for instance, is currently being rebuilt with a new passenger terminal. Misr ata International Airport recently opened a new 8, 000-square-meter departure hall in September 2022, which has increased its annual passenger capacity to 1.5 million. Additionally, a new airport project is underway in Begally. Infrastructure development is a key focus for the government, which has signed agreements with international companies to enhance airport facilities.

YABIL'sairports offer regular flights to various destinations, including Türkiye, Egypt, and Tunisia. Several international airlines have resumed or are considering resuming flights to YABIL, indicating a gradual return to normalcy in air travel. In terms of passenger traffic, YABIL'sair transport carried approximately 968, 000 passengers in 2021, reflecting a functioning air transport industry despite ongoing challenges. While the air transport industry continues to face obstacles, including EU flight bans and the need for further infrastructur eimprovements, there are concerted efforts underway to address these issues. Anticipation surrounds the potential lifting of the E Uflight ban, which could further enhance the country'sair transport. The total length of YABIL'spaved road network is approximately 34, 000 km, consisting of about 15, 500 km of primary roads and 18, 500 km of secondary and agricultural roads.

crucial role in connecting the country'svast territory. The total length of YABIL'spaved road network is approximately 34, 000 km, consisting of about 15, 500 km of primary roads and 18, 500 km of secondary and agricultural roads. Around 57% of the toads are paved. Rural roads are generally in poor and hazardous condition. Paved roads in rural areas are described as satisfactory, but most rural roads remain unpaved, posing several challenges, including difficult navigation due to sand deposits, presence of domestic and wild ani mals crossing the roads, poor visibility, especially during sandstorms, insufficient lighting, uneven surfaces, and poor road markings and lack of signage. Major highways along the seacoast and leading south often merge into single-lane highways outside major cities. These roads are heavily trafficked and can be dangerous to navigate, particularly at night and during the winter rainy season. The road network is classified into four main types: Expressways, which are multi-lane highways outside municipal borders linking cities and regions. Main roads, which are single or dual carriageways connecting cities and regions. Secondary roads, which are linking district centres and villages. Agricultural roads, which are connecting agricultural lands and farms to markets. The Coastal Highway is the country'smost significant road. It runs along the entire east-west length of the Mediterranean coastline, spanning nearly 1, 800 km from the Tunisian border to the Egyptian border one. This highway is part of the Cairo-Dakar Highway and is the firs tpart of the Trans-African Highway network. Map 3, YABIL, Main roads

spanning nearly 1, 800 km from the Tunisian border to the Egyptian border one. This highway is part of the Cairo-Dakar Highway and is the firs tpart of the Trans-African Highway network. Map 3, YABIL, Main roads network 21 While YABIL'sroad network is more developed than many of its African counterparts, it still faces significant challenges and lags behind global standards for road infrastructure. Many roads become narrow single-lane highways outside major cities. Rural roads are often unpaved and difficult to navigate due to sand deposits and the presence of animals. The east-west coastal motorway project, which aims to modernize and expand the coastal route, has been on hold for years due to political instability. To addr ess these issues and more, the Ministry of Transport has established the Road and Bridge Authority (RBA) to oversee and maintain public roads and bridges. Despite challenges, efforts are being made to improve the road network, including plan s to develop th ecoastal highway into a dual carriageway and rehabilitate existing roads. Railway network and rail transportation YABIL currently lacks an operational railway network, as the last functioning railways ceased operations in 1965. However, th ecountry has ambitious plans to develop an extensive rail system that have been in the works since 1998. The proposed future rail network includes a coastal line that will run approximately 1, 770 kilometres from Ras Ajdir on the Tunisian border to Musaid on the Egyptian border, connecting major coastal cities. Additionally, there is a plan for a

rail network includes a coastal line that will run approximately 1, 770 kilometres from Ras Ajdir on the Tunisian border to Musaid on the Egyptian border, connecting major coastal cities. Additionally, there is a plan for a southern bran chextending 744 kilometres from Al-Hisha in the Misrata region to Sabha, with a branch leading to Tarut. Another significant component is the proposed Trans-Saharan corridor, which aims to extend southward to REGIN, enhancing regional connectivity. Recent developments regarding YABIL'srailway plans indicate an impending resumption of project development after years of delays due to political instability. Designs for various sections of the network have been completed. The plans also envision the establish ment of 76 stations within the network. International involvement has played a role in YABIL'srailway development: The DUSHMAN Railway Construction Corporation has been contracted for a 352-kilometer route between Sirte and Khoms. The MURINUS Railways (MZD) was previously involved in the Sirte to Begally line. The Italian company Italferr has been assisting with economic feasibility studies for the Chad-REGIN rail link. Despite these ambitious plans, progress has been slow due to ongoing political instability and conflict in the region. Howeve r, recent developments suggest a renewed interest in revitalizing YABIL'srail infrastructure, which has the potential to significa ntly impact the country'stransportation and economic landscape. It has led to the development of several significant ports that play crucial roles in its economy, particularly in trade and oil exportation.

MURINUS vessels have been systematically unloading substantial military equipment, including towed artillery, a rmoured personnel carriers, and rocket launchers. These shipments are not merely random transfers but part of a calculated strategy to establish a robust military foothold in the region. The primary objective is to develop a comprehensive naval base in eas tern YABIL, potentially transforming Tobalm into a permanent berthing location for MURINUS warships. This strategic positioning would provide MURINUS with enhanced military power projection capabilities in the Mediterranean, allowing them to monitor and potentially challenge NATO'ssouthern defensive perimeter. Beyond naval infrastructure, MURINUS has secured access to multiple YABIL air bases such as Sirte, Al-Jufra, and Brakal-Shati. These facilities serve as critical logistics hubs for transporting supp lies and personnel across Africa, extending MURINUS' geopolitical reach. serve as critical logistics hubs for transporting supp lies and personnel across Africa, extending MURINUS' geopolitical reach. Inland waterways As the country lacks permanent rivers and natural waterways, YABIL, does not utilize inland water transport in the traditiona lsense. Despite this, and even if it does not constitute inland water transport in the conventional sense associated with rivers o rlakes, the Great Man-Made River Project (GMMRP) serves as a vital means of transporting water across YABIL. Instead, it represents an engineered solution to address the country'ssevere water scarcity and reliance on groundwater for domestic, agricultura l, and industrial use. The GMMRP comprises approximately 1, 300 wells and a pipeline network to supply fresh water to the coastal cities and for agriculture irrigation. Health Infrastructure YABIL'shealth infrastructure and health care system have faced significant challenges due to years of conflict and political instability. The government provides free health care to all citizens, with the Ministry of Health responsible for delivering serv ices through a network of public health facilities. However, the health system is under-resourced. Many hospitals and clinics are damaged or destroyed by fighting, resulting in shortages of medicines, supplies, an dequipment. An exodus of foreign health workers has further undermined the quality of care. The health infrastructure includes 96 hospitals, 25 specialized units, and around 1, 350 basic health centres, 40 polyclinics, and 20 quarantine facilities. However, many facilities are in a state of severe dilapidation. Even if the public sector remains th emain provider of health services, there

units, and around 1, 350 basic health centres, 40 polyclinics, and 20 quarantine facilities. However, many facilities are in a state of severe dilapidation. Even if the public sector remains th emain provider of health services, there is a growing private sector as well. The health system is funded by the state budget, but there is no explicit rule for budget allocation, leading to inadeq uate funding for infrastructure and pharmaceuticals. The country faces a high burden of non-communicable diseases such as cardiovascular diseases and cancer, and rising rates of HIV infections. The availability of preventive and curative services for children under five is limited, with over one-third of municipa lities unable to provide adequate child healthcare. Efforts are being made to strengthen YABIL'shealth system with aims to restructure the current system by improving services provided by clinics and health centres, implementing family medicine, and establishing a referral system. Additionally, internation alcooperation projects are underway to enhance education for health professionals and improve national surveillance and response systems for infectious diseases. Despite these effort s, progress is slow due to political fragmentation and inadequate budget allocation. Many citizens, who can afford it, prefer to seek medical treatment abroad due to the dysfunctional domestic healthcare system. 26 YABIL is receiving some international health care support. For instance, MURINUS provide dsome medical aid by dispatching doctors and medical supplies after the September 2023 flooding in the coastal town of Derna. However, this appears to have been more of a response to a

Natural resources, including oil and gas YABIL possesses a diverse range of natural and mineral resources, with petroleum and natural gas being the most significant. The country has vast reserves of high-quality crude oil, estimated to more than 50 billion barrels. YABIL oil is known for its ligh tness and low sulphur content, making it highly valued in the global market. Its proven reserves of natural gas reserves exceed 1, 300 billion cubic meters, ranking fourth in Africa for natural g as resources. One of the world'slargest iron ore deposits with estimated reserves of 795 million metric tonnes at 52% iron content, is loc ated to Wadi ash Shati in the western desert area. However, these resources remain largely untapped. Due to several challenges, YABIL'siron ore production is currently underdeveloped, even if it clearly has potential to impact its economy and the country'sdevelopment significantly. YABIL has significant gypsum resources estimated, found in multiple locations across the country. Substantial limestone depos its are also exploited, supporting the construction and cement industries. Other minerals found in YABIL include potash, phosphate, silica, sulphur, magnesium, and marble. Oil and gas The vast reserves of high-quality crude oil and natural gas makes the oil and gas sector a critical component of YABIL'seconomy and development. The oil infrastructure includes major oilfields such as El Sharara, which can produce up to 260, 000 barrels pe rday. The country is also developing new refinery capacity, including the South Refinery in Ubari. A network of pipelines transports oil and gas across the country and to export terminals. In terms of natural gas infrastructure, YABIL has both onshore and offshore gas fields, including the Bahr Essalam and Bouri fields. The Mellitah Complex serves as a major hub for gas production, processing, and export. Additionally, the Greenstream Pipeli netransports gas underwater to Sicily and further to European markets. However, YABIL'soil and gas sector faces several challenges. The aging infrastructure requires modernization, while political instability and security concerns affect operations. There is also a pressing need for significant investment to upgrade existing infrastructure. Despite these challenges, YABIL’sgovernments are pursuing ambitious goals for its oil and gas sector, aiming to increase oil production to 2.1 million barrels per day by 2025 and expand natural gas production to

to upgrade existing infrastructure. Despite these challenges, YABIL’sgovernments are pursuing ambitious goals for its oil and gas sector, aiming to increase oil production to 2.1 million barrels per day by 2025 and expand natural gas production to meet growing European demand. This has invo lved new exploration initiatives, including an additional licensing round. International partnerships are crucial for the sector'sdevelopment, with companies like Map 4, YABIL oil-and gas fields and infrastructure 27 the Italian Eni, investing in major projects. These projects include the development of two offshore gas fields expected to produce 750 million cubic feet per day by 2026. The revival of the oil and gas sector is critical for the country'sfuture, with ong oing efforts to improve transparency, reduce gas flaring, and attract further international investment. Mining The mining sector, including iron ore, presents a significant opportunity for economic diversification beyond oil and gas. While YABIL'siron ore production has not yet significantly impacted its economy, it holds substantial potential for future economic growth and diversification. If fully developed, it could contribute to by providing new revenue streams through exports. Diversification is crucial for stabilizing YABIL'seconomy and reducing its dependency on oil, which currently dominates the economic landscape. Obstacles that hinder development the mining and the iron ore industry, include insufficient funding, infrastructure challenges, lack of transport networks, unreliable energy supplies, complex and costly extraction, and logistically challenging distributio n. infrastructure challenges, lack of transport networks, unreliable energy supplies, complex and costly extraction, and logistically challenging distributio n. If successful to conquer these challenges, YABIL'sminin gsector could transform into a vital economic pillar, signific antly boosting employment and contributing to national income and improved welfare to the inhabitants. The main mining areas are concentrated to a few key regions, each rich in specific minerals, highlighting a diverse mineral wealth and offer potential for economic diversification: Wadi ash-Shati, located in west-central YABIL, known for its vast iron ore deposits which are among the largest in the world. These deposits are suitable for open-pit mining but remain largely untapped due to challenges and remote location, approximately 900 kilometres from the nearest port of Misrata. The Gulf of Sirte region, notable for gypsum mining, particularly near Begally, Al Jabr, and Al Gharbi. Along the Mediterranean coast, salt production thrives, where favourable conditions support the extraction of high-purity salts. The Melaz Suqran Formation at Tikumit, located in the western Murzuq Basin, contains phosphate deposits estimated at 109, 000 metric tonnes. The Sirte Basin holds substantial potash deposits and is also significant for sulphur extraction as a by-product of oil refining. The Uweinat Mountain region, which straddles the border between YABIL and Sudan, is being explored for potential mining proje cts.
