Energy situation in general YABIL'senergy system is predominantly based on fossil fuels, with a heavy reliance on its abundant oil and natural gas resou rces. As the seventh-largest crude oil producer in OPEC and the third-largest petroleum liquids producer in Africa, YABIL holds 3% of the world’sproven oil reserves and 39% of Africa's. It is facing significant challenges, including political instability that has led to frequent disruptions in oil production and exports, and an electricity grid that suffers from frequent blackouts, forcing many businesses to rely on diesel generators. The country'selectricity demand is growing rapidly at 6-8% annually rate, straining the existing infrastructure. YABIL'sgenerated in 202 4 approximately 34 terawatt-hours (TW h) (34, 000 gigawatt-hours (GW h)) of electricity, with natural gas accounting for two thirds and oil for one third of the production. Despite its vast potential for solar and wind energy, YABIL'srenewable sector remains underdeveloped, leaving almost 100% from non-renewable sources (gas and oil). 28 Power system and electrification YABIL has a high electrification rate, with 99.8% of the population having access to electricity, the highest among African countries. The national electric grid consists of approximately 12, 000 km of high voltage network, 12, 500 km of medium voltage netwo rk, and 7, 000 km of low voltage network. However, some remote areas rely on diesel generators due constraints in grid connection. The country has faced chronic power shortages

network, 12, 500 km of medium voltage netwo rk, and 7, 000 km of low voltage network. However, some remote areas rely on diesel generators due constraints in grid connection. The country has faced chronic power shortages and blackouts in recent years due to damaged infrastructure resulting from confl icts since 2011. However, there have been recent improvements, with especially Mapoli experiencing more stable electricity supply since 2023. The energy mix is heavily dominated by fossil fuels, with oil and natural gas account for nearly all of the country'selectri city generation. Despite extreme potential, renewable energy sources contribute a minimal amount to the energy mix, with less than 1% coming from solar energy. Even if YABIL so far has neglected its significant potential for clean energy development, the government has recently launched a Renewable Energy Strategic Plan, a iming to achieve 10% renewable energy contribution to the elect ric energy mix before 2030. Several solar projects are in the pipeline, including a 14 Megawatt (MW) power station in Houn, a 40 MW project in Sabha, and a 15 MW power station in Ghat. Nuclear power YABIL'snuclear ambitions have undergone significant changes over the past decades, and does not currently have an active nuc lear power program. It signed the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT) in 1968 and ratified it in 1975. Their only nuclear facili ty was built in 1981 by MURINUS. This 10 MW research reactor at Tajoura east of Mapoli, is shut down and currently inactive awaiting decommissioning. The

in 1968 and ratified it in 1975. Their only nuclear facili ty was built in 1981 by MURINUS. This 10 MW research reactor at Tajoura east of Mapoli, is shut down and currently inactive awaiting decommissioning. The reactor has not been operational since 2013. An outbuilding caught fire in April 2024, but was soon after reported to be under control. The facility is now under the supervision of the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) as part of YABIL'scommitment to dismantle its nuclear weapons program. YABIL pursued a clandestine nuclear weapons program from 1969 until 2003, when the Fiddaga regime announced the voluntary eli mination of all materials, equipment, and programs that could lead to internationally proscribed weapons. In 2006 and 2007, YABIL signed agreements with France for peaceful uses of atomic energy and the potential construction of a nuclear plant for seawater desalination. However, these plans did not mater ialize. In 2003, YABIL agreed to dismantle its weapons of mass destruction progra m, and IAEA reported that YABIL'snuclear program was "in the very initial stages of development". The nuclear weapons program was soon after officially dismantled. While YABIL expressed interest in developing a civilian nuclear energy sector in 2010, there is no signs of an active nuclear power program in the country today. Water supply, wastewater and sewerage This sector faces significant challenges due to the country'sarid climate and infrastructure issues. The water supply system relies on three main sources: First, the Great Man-Made River Project (GMMRP) supply fresh water

Water supply, wastewater and sewerage This sector faces significant challenges due to the country'sarid climate and infrastructure issues. The water supply system relies on three main sources: First, the Great Man-Made River Project (GMMRP) supply fresh water to major cities and irrigation, providing 70% of all freshwater used in YABIL. Second, groundwater is managed by the General Company for Water and Wastewater (GCWW) and serves as a supplementary source. Third, desalination is becoming increasingly important, but currently only covers about 2% of the country'swater needs. This process is operated by the General Desalination Company (GDC). 29 The wastewater and sewerage sector is severely underdeveloped. Out of 74 wastewater treatment plants in the country, only 7 a re operational, treating only 6% of the target. Most plants are inoperative due to poor maintenance and mismanagement, and much unt reated sewage is discharged directly into the sea, violating international environmental agreements. Despite efforts and initiatives to address these issues, YABIL continues to face significant challenges in water management. The country urgently needs to expand its desalination capacity, improve wastewater reuse, and implement sustainable water managemen tpractices to reduce reliance on depleting groundwater resources. The country'scommunication infrastructure has seen improvements in recent years, but it still faces challenges, particularly in southern and rural a reas. Overall, YABIL'scommunication infrastructure has improved, particularly in urban areas, but is faces several issues. Poor connectivity in southern regions and rural areas, occasional internet shutdowns due to political instability, relatively slow interne tspeeds compared to global averages, and low internet resilience, are significant concerns, proving considerable room for development, especially in remote areas. Internet access infrastructure YABIL is connected to the global internet system primarily through submarine cables and satellite connections. Its main inter net infrastructure is managed by YABIL Telecom & Technology (YTT), a government-owned company that provides much of its internet access through Digital Subscriber Line (DSL) and 4G services. Connections to Europe and the world is provided via 5 different submarine cables systems, landing in both Mapoli, Begallyand Derna. The last one was notably affected during the September 2023 floods, which cut off the submarine cable linking YABIL to Europe, disrupting internet and telecommunications services. In addition to submarine cables, satellite internet access is available, with YTT offering Satellite (DVB-RCS) Access as one of its solutions. The company also provides VSAT (Very Small Aperture Terminal) services, which enable satellite-based internet connectivity. Space YABIL has a nascent space program cantered around the YABIL Centre for Remote Sensing and Space Science (YCRSS), which is pri marily government-funded (by the YABIL Unity Government), accounting for about 85% of its budget. The YCRSS specializes in remote sensing, space research, seismology, and astronomy, with its headquarters located in Mapoli, and a seismology centre near the capital, employing approximately 200 sta ffacross its three locations. The 30 YCRSS collaborates with universities on research-based projects and works with international organizations to provide satellite data and research services to local institutions. However, the space program faces significant challenges due to political insta bility, which has hindered substantial development in space technology. In addition, insufficient funding and minimal private sector involvement in space activities have resulted in lim ited progress. There is a growing interest in space education within YABIL. The first space camp was held in Shahat in May 2023, aimed at training YABIL youth in space science and promoting astronomical knowledge. Political and key actor landscape 33 The two main political factions, each effectively representing rival governments and accompanying administrations, consist of the Mapoli-headquartered YABIL Unity Government (western faction) and the Sirte-headquartered YABIL Stability Government (eastern faction) . The two factions, each of which is led by a Prime Minister, have competed for both political primacy and control of the country’soil resources, meaning that YABIL is not governed by one monolithic political entity . This duality of power and lack of internal cohesion has also opened upopportunities for interference, including from MURINUS, which has established close ties with the leadership of the YABIL Stability Government . Both factions have at various times been accused of corruption and are closely connected to militias operating in their respective areas of influence. The respective characteristics and structures of the two factions as outlined below. YABIL Unity Government (YUG) The YABIL Unity Government is the primary political faction within YABIL and arguably the closest resembling a national government. It controls the country’scapital, Mapoli as well as the cities of Khoms and Misarata, also located along the Mediterranean coast . According to successive UN-brokered agreements, the YABIL Unity Government should also have been endorsed by the rival Representative Union (i.e. the formal legislature, currently loyal to the YABIL Stability Government), but this has not yet occurred, resulting in a frozen, two-faction status quo. YABIL Unity Government Structure Executive

have been endorsed by the rival Representative Union (i.e. the formal legislature, currently loyal to the YABIL Stability Government), but this has not yet occurred, resulting in a frozen, two-faction status quo. YABIL Unity Government Structure Executive branch: A Prime Minister who, on paper, is appointed by the Representative Union (the country’sformal legislature) leads the YABIL Unity Government. However, given that the Representative Union has sided with the YABIL Stability Government, the Prime Minister is instead appointed by a Presidential Council, which is itself led by a Chairman. The Prime Minister of the YABIL Unity Government is support by a Deputy Prime Minister and leads the Cabinet of Ministers, which brings together ministers res ponsible for key departments. The most important positions in the Cabinet are the Minister of Foreign Affairs, Minister of Defence, Minister of Interior, Minister of Finance, and Minister of Oil and Gas. As well as leading the government and chairing the Council of Minister, the Prime Minister of the YABIL Unity Government is responsible for directing and guiding policy, coordinating national security, representing the government internationally, managing financial decisions and overseeing the implementation of national projects. A number of senior roles within the government, including the positions of Minister of Defence and Minister of Interior, are held by former militia commanders. This shows the exten t to which militias are intertwined with government structures. militia commanders. This shows the exten t to which militias are intertwined with government structures. However, in practice, the split with the country’sformal legislature – the Representative Union – has meant that the YABIL Unity Government does not technically have a functioning legislative branch. Instead, bills introduced by the government are therefore introduced and passed without legal assent. Judiciary: YABIL’sjudicial system consists of four tiers of courts. A Supreme Court guided by a Supreme Judicial Council is the body responsible for ultimate adjudication of legal matters. It sits abo vecourts of Appeal, First Instance and localised Summary Courts. The judicial system draws on a mix of civil law and Sharia law. Once again, however, the fractured character of the country’sinstitutions has resulted in a complex relationship between the executive and judicial branc h, with the latter struggli ng to act a formal check on the former. YABIL Stability Government Structure Executive branch: Like the YABIL Unity Government, the YABIL Stability Governm ent is led by a Prime Minister, who is both ‘head of state’ and commander in chief of the armed forces. The Prime Minister responsible for driving government policy; overseeing governance and the admini stration of areas under YABIL Stability Government con trol; representing the YSG overseas; and introducing bills to the legislature, the Representative Union. The Prime Minister also leads a Cabinet comprising of 27 minister, including the positions of Minister of Foreign Affairs; Interior; Defence; Health; Finance and Trade; and Oil, Gas and Energy. Legislative branch: The YGS enjoys a close relationship with the country’sofficial legislature, the Representative Union. The Representative Uni on’sofficial seat is Begally . It is comprised of 200 elected members and is led by a Speaker (supported by Deputy Speaker), who also leads the legislatu re’sengagement with the office of the Prime Minister. It also comprises a number of Committees nominally responsible for overseein gkey areas of policy competence such as tax and finance policies. Beyond this, it score roles include ratifying legislation, approving the general budget, granting confidence in the Cabinet, monitoring the executive branch and state institutions and approving general

areas of policy competence such as tax and finance policies. Beyond this, it score roles include ratifying legislation, approving the general budget, granting confidence in the Cabinet, monitoring the executive branch and state institutions and approving general policy proposals submitted by the government. Judicial branch: YABIL’sjudicial system, as we have seen, technically operates independently from the two competing executive branches. However, continuous attempts at political interference have reduced its ability to operate as a cohesive entity. Furthermore, the Repr esentative Union established its own YGS-aligned constitutional court in Begally – a move that was ruled as unconstitutional by YABIL’s Supreme Court. Jurisdictional and procedural challenges, combined with the a lack of a nationally-unified legal framework, have added to the difficulties experienced by the courts operating within YGS-controlled territory, undermining the provision of justice and the rule of law. Yamal Binocha Minister of Foreign Affairs Foreign Minister Yamal Binocha is a relative newcomer on YABIL’spolitical scene, having been educated and then lived in exile in the West during the Fiddaga years . Despite this, he has proven himself capable within the context of securing support from international partner s, including MURINUS. Binocha, in line with Prime Ministeral-Rassa’sthinking, is bought into the view that increasing international recognition is key to the survival and, ultimately dominance, of the YABIL Unity Government. Bilal Tanrifi Minister of Finance and Trade Bilal Tanrifi studied Business Administration and worked for a leading oil company before joining the YABIL Stability Government, having previously served briefly within YABIL Transitional Council. Lekad Hamish Minister of Interior As Minister of the Interior, Hamish is holds responsibility for managing security within areas of YABIL Stability Government Control. Lati Ahmadi Minister of Oil, Gas and Energy An economist by trade, Ahmadi’s Ministerial portfolio includes managing the country’ssignificant oil and gas reserves, most of which are located in YABIL Stability Government-controlled areas. This has involved working closely with the Minister of Foreign Affairs and Minister of Finance and Trade on YABIL’sexport partnerships. MURINUS MURINUS has increased its presence in YABIL in recent years, including by brokering relations with and supporting the self-proclaimed YABIL National Army. Its main objectives in the region, enabled by airbases and Messner Group personnel are to establish an additional foothold in the Mediterranean, access natural resources and project power regionally. MURINUS’ presence in YABIL is driven primarily

Army. Its main objectives in the region, enabled by airbases and Messner Group personnel are to establish an additional foothold in the Mediterranean, access natural resources and project power regionally. MURINUS’ presence in YABIL is driven primarily by national interests, many of which are linked to the African continent. Stra tegically positioned at the crossroads of Africa and Europe, YABIL provides MURINUS with a gateway to its operations in SUDAN, CHAD, REGIN, and other Sahel and Central Africa countries, projecting power and influence across these regions. Not least, the country’songoing political chaos and fragmented governan ce has guaranteed MURINUS much-needed access to air and naval military bases, allowing it to coordinate its military expeditions. The strategic location of Tobalm in particular, near the Suez Canal and the Mediterranean, would effectively turn YABIL into a critical operational base for MURINUS African and Medit erranean military an dstrategic actions, offering KERAM a triangulation between it, its ports in BUFRO and Port Sudan. 37 North African ports are essential to MURINUS international trade logistics too. By maintaining a strong presence in YABIL, MURINUS is able to pursue its broader geopolitical goals, including securing critical resources that are essential to sustain its eco nomy and long-term strategic aspirations. Of particular note, by getting access to YABIL’sextensive oil reserves, KERAM not only ensures a steady flow of energy resources, but also gains leverage over global energy markets.9 This control can be used to manipulate oil prices, exert political pressure on oil-dependent nations, and create economic

reserves, KERAM not only ensures a steady flow of energy resources, but also gains leverage over global energy markets.9 This control can be used to manipulate oil prices, exert political pressure on oil-dependent nations, and create economic dependencies. Furthermore, by establishing a presence in YABIL, MURINUS can potentially disrupt European energy supplies, particularl y as Europe seeks to diversify its energy sources away from MURINUS gas. Another declination of KERAM’splaybook in YABIL focuses on how flows from Africa to Europe could be manipulated in order to exert pressure on both the European Union and NATO. This could include transporting illegal drugs like captagon from BUFRO, covertl ymoving gold, and trafficking migrants. Resistance and Liberation Front (RLF) The Resistance and Liberation Front (RLF) has sought to expand its regional influence and presence beyond BUFRO, including via YABIL. The Terror Group has conducted attacks across YABIL, supporting its strategic objective of fomenting instability and catapulting the country i nto another civil war. Indeed, and building on its experience in BUFRO, the RLF sees widespread instability and conflict as providing the enabling conditions most conducive towards controlling territory and establishing shadow governanc estructures. Accordingly, the group has targeted both YABIL Unity Government and YABIL Stability Government securing forces and government buildings. It has established a presence, via its cells, in Begally, Tobali and Mapoli as well as southern YABIL. It has previous lycontrolled areas of Sirte but was subsequently displaced from the city following counter-terrorism operation by the self-proclaimed YABIL National Army. The

via its cells, in Begally, Tobali and Mapoli as well as southern YABIL. It has previous lycontrolled areas of Sirte but was subsequently displaced from the city following counter-terrorism operation by the self-proclaimed YABIL National Army. The RLF has regularly clashed with YABIL’smilitias. Its manpower and recruits consist primarily of foreign fighters recruited in the Middle East as well as North and Sub-Saharan Africa, many of whom have been attracted by RLF’sattractive salaries. 9 The Messner Group controls some key oil production facilities in the country, thus positioning KERAM to oversee output from YABIL. 38 4. Levers of power 39 The fragmented character of YABIL’spolitical landscape means that instruments of power, including defence and security lever s, are not consolidated under the control of one dominant and/or sovereign entity. Instead, different factions, including the competing YABIL Unity Government and YABIL Stability Government Council have harness their own networks combining remnants of the country’sarmed forces and militias . Some of these non-state armed groups have become increasingly integrated within de-facto government and security sector structures, with militia leaders and commanders entering positions of responsibility wit hin political institutions. At the local level, this has sometimes translated into semi-autonomous centres of power, with some militia leaders using their positions and influence for financial gain. Armed forces and security levers YABIL Unity Government YABIL Unity Government armed and security forces span across conventional military units built around remnants of the Rammar Fiddaga regime and militias. These constitute of a hybrid between a defence

forces and security levers YABIL Unity Government YABIL Unity Government armed and security forces span across conventional military units built around remnants of the Rammar Fiddaga regime and militias. These constitute of a hybrid between a defence capability – including within the context of deterring possible attacks by the self-proclaimed YABIL National Army – and policing. As such, their command and control is split across the Ministry of Defence and Ministry of the Interior. Critically, the effective administration and coordination of the armed forces and security units is inhibited by a lack of robust institutional frameworks. This is particularly complicated when trying to exe rtformal control over militias, many of which effectively operate as semi-private armies. Land Forces: The element of YABIL Unity Government land forces mostly closely resembling a ‘regular’ force consists of the YABIL defence force, which comprises unit s that were formally part of Fiddaga’sarmy. This is located in the Map oli Protective Zone. 85th Mechanised Battalion: around 900 personnel supported by a small fleet of Infantry Fighting Vehicles – primarily the MURINUS-built BMP-1 and BMP-2 – as well as Mine Resistant Ambush Protected vehicles. Its headquarters are located in the Misrata Protective Zone. 91st Infantry Battalion: this is a depleted unit counting fewer than 600 personnel, located within the Zintan

and BMP-2 – as well as Mine Resistant Ambush Protected vehicles. These are primarily concentrated around Mapoli and consist of: The so-called ‘1911 Brigade’, which is primarily active in eastern and southern Mapoli, including the Az-Zawiyah and the city of Garyan, and the as well as desert areas under the nominal control of the YUG. The militia has recently engaged in operations within southern YABIL aimed at tackling oil smuggling and criminality. The so-called ‘Deterrence Collective’, operating in northeaster Map oli. The militia is nominally loyal to the YUG and is able to draw on around 2, 000 fighters. It takes an active role in local security and (unofficial) policing. The militia has participated in counter-terrorism operations, including activities 40 aimed at degrading local incursions by the Resistance and Liberation Front (RLF). The militia has clashed on a number of occasions with the ‘1911 Brigade’ over territorial control. Front (RLF). The militia has clashed on a number of occasions with the ‘1911 Brigade’ over territorial control. The so-called ‘Stabilisation and Protection Force ’, which is primarily active around Misrata, Zliten and Khoms and nominally answers to the YUG Ministry of Interior. The militia’snarrative and stated role focuses on protecting local communities, preserving public orde r and law enforcement. The militia has also a cquired a small fleet of patrol vessels, which it operates on YABIL north-western coast. Despite this, it has been regularly accused of abuses, including extra-judicial arrests and incarceration. Air Force: the YABIL Unity Government nominally took over the country’sair force following the toppling of the Fiddaga regime. In real ity, this is much depleted and consists only of around 20 aircraft, including a combination of MiG-21 fighters and SU-22 fighter-bombers, as well as MI-17 helicopters. These aircraft are at a low-level of servicing with only a handful being operational. Naval Forces: The YUG has a small maritime force consisting primarily of coast guard vessels and patrol boats, ranging between 9 and 35 met res in length. These are primarily employed for the purpose of patrolling within territorial waters as well as counter-trafficking and counter migrant-smuggling operations. Intelligence apparatus: The YUG’ sintelligence apparatus remain somewhat fractured and lacking in effective oversight. It has also been accused of failing to adhere to international human rights standards. It consists of two main services, namely: The Agency for Internal Security (AIS): this overse esintelligence collection within YABIL, including with

effective oversight. It has also been accused of failing to adhere to international human rights standards. It consists of two main services, namely: The Agency for Internal Security (AIS): this overse esintelligence collection within YABIL, including with respect to counter-terrorism, organised crime and counter-intelligence risks. The AIS has also increasingly reported on YABIL Stability Government operations in the country. Although formally under the Ministry of Interior, the service is staffed by a combination of professional intelligence officers and militia members, incl uding from the ‘1911 Brigade’. The General Service of Intelligence (GSI): this service is primarily focussed on external and foreign intelligence collection and analysis. As such, it comes under the responsibility of the YUG Ministry of Foreign Affairs, although its head enjoys a close and direct relationship with the Prime Minister. Although it can be described as a semi-professional service, the GIS often competes with the AIS for influence and primacy. YABIL Stability Government YABIL Stability Government armed forces are primarily built around the self-proclaimed YABIL National Army (YNA), loyal to General Lifaka Taraf. The YNA comprises a combination of regular forces drawn from remnants of the Fiddaga regime army and militias. Although larger in num ber than the armed forces of the YABIL Unity Government, an YNA’sincursion into Map oli was thwarted by the YUG in 2019, revealing shortcomings in doctrine, cohesion and command and control. Land Forces: YABIL Stability Government forces are concentrated in the YNA, which has close links to the Representative Union, the YSG’s legislature.

Although the YNA nominally comes under the YSG Ministry of Defence, its day-to-day command and control rests firmly in the hands of General Lifaka Taraf. General has further cemented this control by placing key units under the control of family members (including his sons) and loyal com manders. The YNA’shybrid structure (regular forces and militias) consists of around 25, 000 personnel and comprises approximately four (mechanized) infantry brigade, some of which are also bolstered by armoured battalions. The YNA has benefitted from equipment donated by MURINUS, who has sought to build close relations with General Taraf. A number of these unit have been involved in criminal profit-making activities. Intelligence apparatus: The main intelligence service aligned with the YSG con sist of the Military Intelligence Service (MIS). This service is subservient to the YNA and conducts a combination internal and external intelligence collection and analysis, whilst also having a counter-intelligence remit. It also supports YNA operations, including through support to targeting activity. The service is divided into branches supporting government institutions (including the YSG Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Minis try of the Interior (such as with respect to counter-terrorism intelligence), YNA land forces, and the air force. MURINUS 42 MURINUS’ influence in YABIL manifests itself primarily in two ways. Firstly, MURINUS has sought to establish a physical foothold along the Mediterranean coast, adding to its presence in BUFRO. This presence also allows MURINUS to project power in north and Sub-Saharan Africa, whilst also providing it with access to YABIL’senergy resources (thus potentially giving it leverage over European countries) . MURINUS is also likely considering establishing a naval base in Begally and has held discussions with General Taraf to that effect . The port’sstrategic location and existing facilities in the city could support a naval presence and add to MURINUS ’ use of the port of Tartus in BUFRO. MURINUS vessels have reportedly already secured docking rights and are likely using

location and existing facilities in the city could support a naval presence and add to MURINUS ’ use of the port of Tartus in BUFRO. MURINUS vessels have reportedly already secured docking rights and are likely using the port for the purposes of re fuelling and resupply. Vessels operated by MURINUS that have used the facility include: 1 x Kilo class Project 06363 submarine equipped with Kalibr (Club-S) cruise missiles. 1 x Buyan-M Guided missile corvette Project 21631 equipped with cruise and anti-ship missiles. 1 x Maritime Tanker Project 03182 multi-purpose auxiliary support vessel. This access and accompanying assets have been protected by a surface missile battery and military police battalion. Secondly, and in order to enable this presence, MURINUS has built relations with the self-proclaimed YABIL National Army and, to a lesser degree, the YABIL Stability Government. Its relationship with the YNA has included the gifting of military supplies, equipment and ammunition aimed at bolstering the YNA’sstrength and control of territory (thus serving the interests of MURINUS). Politically, MURINUS also supports the YSG in multilateral fora, including by using its position on the United Nations Security Council. Information Information environment YABIL has had a fluid and fractured media landscape since the overthrow of the Rammar Fiddaga regime in 2011 . This has contributed to a polarized information environment, with various factions, including the rival governments, TGs, RLF, VEOs and external actors engaged in narrative battles, using propaganda to influence public opinion, legitimize their actions, and recruit supporters. The existence

contributed to a polarized information environment, with various factions, including the rival governments, TGs, RLF, VEOs and external actors engaged in narrative battles, using propaganda to influence public opinion, legitimize their actions, and recruit supporters. The existence of rival authorities in the west and east created political and media polarisation and enduring rivalry betwee nkey players. Foreign players such as MURINUS, whose interventions in YABIL have increased, are also involved in their own media messaging. The RLF with a sophisticated campaign have radicalized the information environment with a strong anti-Western narrative undermining support for moderate or opposing factions. Social media: Approximately 85% of the population (5.9M) use social media . Facebook remains by far the preferred social media platform and is used for official statements, political discussion and popular mobilisation. Approximately 40% of social media users are female and 60 % male. Digital divide: The digital divide in YABIL is an issue that affects access to information and communication technologies across the country. YABIL has made progress in internet connectivity and approximately 90% of YABIL (+/ - 6M) has access to the internet. 43 There is nonetheless some disparity in internet access and quality between urban and rural areas in YABIL, though specific data on this divide is limited. Urban centres typically have better infrastructure and connectivity compared to remote regions. The ongoing political instability and security issues in YABIL have impacted the development of digital infrastructure and during periods of conflict, internet access has been disrupted or cut off

better infrastructure and connectivity compared to remote regions. The ongoing political instability and security issues in YABIL have impacted the development of digital infrastructure and during periods of conflict, internet access has been disrupted or cut off entirely. While censorship has decreased since the Fiddaga era, some restrictions still exist and the current political fragmentation has led to varying degrees of control and censorship in different parts of the country. The digital divide in YABIL is not just about access, but also about the ability to use digital technologies effectively. There are significant gaps in digital literacy between different age groups and socioeconomic classes, though specific data i slimited. T he education system'scapacity to teach digital skills is affected by the country'sinstability. The cost of devices and internet services is prohibitive for many in YABIL, especially given the country'seconomic difficult ies. Limited investment in digital infrastructure due to political instability has slowed progress in bridging the divide. Addressi ng the digital divide in YABIL will require significant improvements in infrastructure, education, and economic stability to ensure more equitable access to digital technologies across the popul ation. Governm ent influence and media freedom: The media environment has undergone significant changes since the 2011 revolution, but continues to face major challenges regarding government influence and press freedom. YABIL ranks low on press freedom indices, currently at 143rd ou t of 180 countries in the World Press Freedom Index. The media landscape is characterized by a lack of independence and plurality, political polarization

press freedom. YABIL ranks low on press freedom indices, currently at 143rd ou t of 180 countries in the World Press Freedom Index. The media landscape is characterized by a lack of independence and plurality, political polarization and affiliation of outlets with armed groups, the spread of hate speech and disinf ormation and the safety concerns for journalists, including threats of violence and kidnapping. There is a lack of a clear, coherent legal framework for media regulation. While direct state control has decreased since Fiddaga’sfall, the rival governments still exerts influence. For example, the state broadcaster YABIL Radio and Television Corporation (YRTC) remains under YABIL Unity Government control. Moreover, high licensing fees for broadcasters limit media diversity and sel f-censorship remains common due to fear of reprisals. Journalists have for years been the target of intimidation, physical violence, and moral harassment, though the situation seems to have improved since 2021. While the post-2011 period saw a proliferation of new media, the environment remains highly challenging for press freedom and independent journalism in YABIL. Political instability, armed conflict, and lack of strong institutions continue to hamper efforts to establish sustainable protections for media freedom. Disinformation and misinformation: Disinformation and misinformation are significant issues in YABIL'scurrent media and information environment. Fake news and disinformation campaigns are widespread, particularly on social media platforms like Facebook. These campaigns have intensified exi sting political polarization and led to conflicts between rival factions. The spread of false information has contributed to confusion about political reforms and

are widespread, particularly on social media platforms like Facebook. These campaigns have intensified exi sting political polarization and led to conflicts between rival factions. The spread of false information has contributed to confusion about political reforms and democratic processes. Various political actors, RLF, VEO and armed groups exploit misinformation to influence public opinion and undermine opponents . The lack of media literacy among many YABIL citizens, makes it difficult to identify false information. Increasingly sophisticate dmethods are used by disinformation producers, including AI and deep fake videos. Disinformation has contributed to societal unrest and violence. It has affected public perception of political processes and reforms and has hindered efforts towards politic alstability and democratic integrity. Information security and cyber threats: YABIL faces significant challenges in cybersecurity and information security, with the country being particularly vulnerable to cyber threats. YABIL is considered one of the most exposed countries to substantial cybersecurity threats in Africa. This high risk is attributed to inadequate safeguards against cybercrimes, weak or non-existent legislation against cybersecurity threats and the lack of a comprehensive national cybersecurity strategy. 44 The YABIL Telecom & Technology (YTT) has reported persis tent cyber-attacks on their data centre, causing service disruptions. Targeted DD oS attacks, affecting the country'spublic network IP addresses are becoming increasingly common. A controversial Anti-Cybercrime Law passed in 2022 has been criticized by UN expe rts for potentially infringing on rights of free expression, privacy, and association Due to an increasing reliance on digital platforms for information dissemination and communication,

controversial Anti-Cybercrime Law passed in 2022 has been criticized by UN expe rts for potentially infringing on rights of free expression, privacy, and association Due to an increasing reliance on digital platforms for information dissemination and communication, the information infrastru cture became increasingly vulnerable to cyber attacks, ranging from website hacking to more sophisticated cyber espionage campaigns employed by internal and external actors. Efforts to develop and modernize the ICT infrastructure face setbacks due to the conflict and stalled investment slowing progress towards digita ltransformation. Media landscape: YABIL has a diverse media environment, with a multitude of outlets and a variety of platforms. Satellite TV is a leading news medium. Stations' social media accounts are key news sources. Some prominent outlets are based outside YABIL. Traditional media, having taken sides in the conflict, can no longer play their role of ensuring free, independent, and balanced reporting that reflects the real issues facing YABIL society, especially the aspirations of young people. Independent outlets face significant obstacles including a lack of sustainable funding, political pressure and interference and considerable safety concerns and threats of violence . Radio and television broadcasting facilities face enduring challenges, including damage from conflict and control by various factions. The satellite dishes proliferation allows for a degree of access to international broadcasts, but local media outlets oft enreflect the biases of their controlling factions. Journalists are usually forced to conform to the biases of the media they work for, leading to chronic disinformation, while corruption is also widespread.

Diplomacy The fractured, dual-government character of YABIL’spolitical landscape has complicated the establishment of diplomatic relations. While the country is technically member of the African Union, Arab League and Arab Maghreb Union (AMU), its ability to meaningfully participate in regional bodies and trade agreements is limited. Still, each political faction – the YABIL Unity Government and YABIL Stability Government – has sought via their respective Ministries of Foreign Affairs to secure relationship with int ernational backers, not least in order to increase their legitimacy and standing. The YABIL Unity Government arguably benefits from the highest level of recognition at the international level, including that of the United Nations Gene ral Assembly. It has also developed relations with a number of European countries with whom it cooperate s on issues such as migration policy and energy security. The YUG also nominally controls the country’snetwork of around 35 embassies as well as additional diplomatic missio nsabroad. Meanwhile, the YABIL Stability Government has established relations with MURINUS (which has translated into material support for the YNA). Following the catastrophic Derna flooding in September 2023, MURINUS strategically utilized its port access to deliver humanitarian aid, simultaneously demonstrating soft power and reinforcing diplomatic relationships with local authorities . Meanwhile, DUSHMAN, while officially backing the UN-recognised YUG, has maintained back-channel relations with both governments as part of a

port access to deliver humanitarian aid, simultaneously demonstrating soft power and reinforcing diplomatic relationships with local authorities . Meanwhile, DUSHMAN, while officially backing the UN-recognised YUG, has maintained back-channel relations with both governments as part of a likely hedgi ngstrat egy within YABIL – not least given its oil imports from the country. DUSHMAN has also supported a number of infrastructure projects in the country, primarily as a means of securing economic and political influence (see also infrastructure section).

No part of this product may be reproduced or used in any manner without written permission by the JWC. This document is a document that contains a combination of fictitious and generic information extracted from publicly available open sources. The information in this document should not be considered as an official position of NATO, or any member nation of the Alliance. Although the CM draws on real world events and data, the actors and dynamics portrayed have been adapted in order to meet NATO Training Audience (TA) needs. For this reason, some of its content – such as with respect to geo graphical boundaries and political objectives – does not always reflect reality. Furthermore, images, maps, names and other supporting information such as that used for biographies do not necessarily describe real characters or geographical locations . This is to ensure, inter alia, that the CM adheres to national caveats and sensitivities. Any remaining identified sensitivities should be communicated to JWC so that these may be appropriately addressed . Guidance to User. This Setting document is the foundation for RED and GREEN ORBATs and Battle Space Objects (BSO’s) established in INTEL FS and TOPFAS, and the foundation for scenario-related products developed for specific exercises. (BSO’s) established in INTEL FS and TOPFAS, and the foundation for scenario-related products developed for specific exercises. While it aims to ensure coherence with real world events and dynamics, the content has been adapted to meet exercise and scenario needs. The CM is not exhaustive and therefore allows for additional detail to be obtained by the TA via the Request for Information (RFI) process. The structure of the country model is designed to avoid repetition and to cover the domains that are most relevant to the training audience by drawing on PMESII and other frameworks. 4 1. GEOSTRATEGIC AND REGIONAL CONTEXT 5 Historical timeline MURINUS in the making: MURINUS' modern history and experience have shaped its current strategic outlook and foreign policy interests. Its historical rise as a significant regional political player, followed by the perceived erosion of its traditional sphere of influence, are all reflected in its political narrative and world view. MURINUS People Party tightens its grip and centralises government, whilst cracking down on dissent. Engages in limited foreign policy ventures, including support to Spanish Republicans during the country’scivil war . Rising discontent and internal unrest leads to civil war fought between the government and factions organised under the banner of the MURINUS Solidarity Movement. Accordingly, the MURINUS Empire remains neutral in WW1. Unification of various states under imperial rule of the MURINUS Empire . Emperor abdicates following months of escalating violence and in the face of MURINUS Solidarity Movement gains. The latter subsequently splinters following factional infighting

in WW1. Unification of various states under imperial rule of the MURINUS Empire . Emperor abdicates following months of escalating violence and in the face of MURINUS Solidarity Movement gains. The latter subsequently splinters following factional infighting and the strongest faction, the MURINUS People’s Party emerges as the dominant political force in MURINUS, declaring the creation of the MURINUS People’s Federation. Following World War 2, MURINUS People’s Federation significantly increases its defence spending . Fuelled by ambitions to become a global power, it develops nuclear weapons, initiates a space programme, and aggressively pursues interests in its ‘near abroad’, whilst supporting aligned (armed) political movements around the world. Decades of defense overspending, overstretch and costly foreign policy pursuits culminate in economic-followed by political – collapse and disintegration of MURINUS People’s Federation . The (new) Federal Republic of MURINUS, emerges as the dominant force alongside a smaller GRISEUS and GRIZA . Defacto single party state despite notional elections. Opposition increasingly suppressed . Political rhetoric focusing on restoring the traditional MPF sphere of influence . MURINUS, becomes significantly more assertive on the international stage, seeking close political connections to former MURINUS People’s Federation states, including GRISEUS . MURINUS Empire rises as a global political power, although modernisation and industrialisation is slower than that of Western powers. Expansionist foreign policy takes its toll on the population, which is heavily taxed to fund campaigns. Restructuring of Federal Republic of MURINUS political system and economic reform, including the privatization of state assets. Politically-connected businessmen control major

Expansionist foreign policy takes its toll on the population, which is heavily taxed to fund campaigns. Restructuring of Federal Republic of MURINUS political system and economic reform, including the privatization of state assets. Politically-connected businessmen control major infrastructure and resources, including oil, gas and steel. Great United MURINUS Party formed by former MURINUS People’s Party members consolidates political power despite a multi-party constitution. 6 Foreign policy objectives MURINUS ’ strategic outlook is shaped by a combination of its history, long-standing interests, and the current hardline rregime. At the heart of its foreign policy are the preservation of its sovereignty, expansion of its perceived sphere of influence and restoration of its position as a global power . Its political narrative, which is reflected both in its foreign policy and associated overseas activities, places a heavy emphasis on grievances relating to the treatment and marginalisation of MURINUS over the past three decades by the West . This same narrative highlights what it sees as the aggressive and expansionist policy and activities of NATO-including a greater eastward focus-and a nalleged concerted drive by the West to isolate MURINUS on the global stage. Its princip alstrategic objectives include: Expanding its geostrategic sphere of influence in its ‘near abroad’ (including Eastern Europe) . Central objectives here include defacto control over buffer states, most notably GRIZA; political influence over aligned, proxy regimes such as that of GRISEUS (the latter being tied to MURINUS via a Treaty of State ); and influence in the Baltic, Black and

control over buffer states, most notably GRIZA; political influence over aligned, proxy regimes such as that of GRISEUS (the latter being tied to MURINUS via a Treaty of State ); and influence in the Baltic, Black and Mediterranean Sea regions . Thwarting perceived Western attempts at redrawing the international order in its image, including through what it considers to be a selective interpretation of sovereignty and self-determination. Containing Western/NATO expansion and influence eastwards. Securing economic and political footholds in contested spaces, exploiting pockets of conflict and instability in the Middle East, North Africa and Sub-Saharan Africa and shaping local balances of power in its favour – including via maintaining a sizeable presence in BUFRO. Seeking to reduce its economic and trade reliance on the West, whilst seeking to use its vast natural resources for the purpose of leverage and influence. Securing influence over the Arctic region in advance on the anticipated (further) melting of the ice caps, both with respect to accessing untapped energy resources and controlling the North-East Passage shipping route . The country has also reoccupied military bases in the Arctic . Using foreign policy interventions as the basis for building domestic support and cementing the political legitimacy of the existing regime. Translation of interests into political activity and warfare: MURINUS has pursued its interests through the combined application of multiple levers of power (described in more detail in subsequent sections), including by harnessing its permanent seat on the United Nations Security Council (UNSC) and membership of the

has pursued its interests through the combined application of multiple levers of power (described in more detail in subsequent sections), including by harnessing its permanent seat on the United Nations Security Council (UNSC) and membership of the Artic Council, re-enforcing diplomatic relations around the world, including with the dominant DUSHMAN, establishing a presence in North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and through reform of its defence and military establishment. A nuclear power with the world’slargest nuclear weapons arsenal, MURINUS has also developed sizeable conventional and irregular warfare capabilities and is a major exporter of defence equipment around the world . It has demonstrated the intent and ability to embark in clandestine, non-linear and subversive activities including, at times, in the West, using its advanced intelligence and special operations apparatus, disinformation campaigns, private security companies and proxy groups . 7 2. Working women often carry the double burden of a job and family-raising responsibilities . Most women also consider their role in the family to be more difficult than that of their husband. Such dissatisfaction is a factor in MURINUS’ accelerating divorce rate and declining marriage rate. MURINUS literature occupies an important place in the culture, politics and linguistic evolution of the country. 9 Economy Overview MURINUS has an upper-middle income economy with state ownership in strategic areas. The country'seconomy is based on oil, gas, precious metals, agriculture and arms production . Energy makes uptwo thirds of MURINUS' exports and around half of its federal budget. MURINUS has undergone significant changes since the 1991 collapse of the MURINUS People’s Federation. Despite moving from a centrally planned economy towards a market-based system, a high concentration of wealth is held by politically aligned oligarchs. Economic reforms in the 1990s privatized most industries, with notable exceptions in the energy, transportation, banking, and defence sectors. The protection of property rights is sti llweak, and the state continues to interfere in private sect or operations. MURINUS holds a large share of the world'snatural resources. It is one of the world'sleading producers of oil and natural gas, with exten sive pipeline systems linking production sites across the country to former MURINUS People’s Federation republics and numerous European countries. Most, if not all, of the raw materials and minerals required by modern industries are found within its borders. It has extensive coal reserves and is a top exporter of metals such as steel and aluminium. Agricultu re, meanwhile, constitutes only around 4% of the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) . Segments of the

within its borders. It has extensive coal reserves and is a top exporter of metals such as steel and aluminium. Agricultu re, meanwhile, constitutes only around 4% of the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) . Segments of the economy that are considered to have national security value are shielded from competitive pressures and have been integrated within corporations under government control . A number of these state corporations enjoy advantages including debt guarantees and access to cheap credit from state-controlled financial institutions . Importantly, they function as a mechanism of political control on in a system of vertical power by enriching allies such as government-aligned oligarchs. Accordingly, MURINUS’ economic strategy is centred on consolidating, maintaining, and expanding the political and economic power of the ruling elites through the control of select edindustries and enterprises. Moreover, MURINUS economy is also designed to withstand the impact of conflict, including by incorporating key sectors within a war economy. The country’sfinances can be supported by a massive stimulus stemming from soaring public spending, including with respect to defence procurement. This would include drawing on multiple sectors and associated output – infrastructure projects, electronic and information technologies and industrial-scale manufacturing as well as weapons production – and, in so doing, shift the economy from private consumption to a public wartime economy. MURINUS major imports: cars ($7.75B), motor vehicle parts and accessories ($7.28B), broadcasting equipment ($7.15B), packaged medicaments ($7.06B) and computers ($4.1B), with the most significant imports originating from DUSHMAN ($50.7B). 11 Fiscal policy and the financial sector The budget system of MURINUS revolves around the federal budget, which allocates the distribution of national income and gross domestic product between different sectors of the economy. The MURINUS National Wealth Fund is MURINUS’ sovereign wealth fund and is split into two separate investment funds: the Reserve Fund, which is invested abroad in low-yield securities and used when oil and gas incomes fall; and the National Wealth Fund, which invests in riskier, higher return vehicles, as well as federal budget expenditures. The liquid assets in the National Wealth Fund are currently invested in foreign currency instruments. MURINUS buys foreign currency when oil prices are high and sells when prices gobelow USD 44 per barrel, shielding its currency from oil price swings. MURINUS has historically allocated a large share of national output to military expenditure – between 4 and 6% of GDP – that has been, on average, significantly higher than in most other countries for the last three decades. Even

historically allocated a large share of national output to military expenditure – between 4 and 6% of GDP – that has been, on average, significantly higher than in most other countries for the last three decades. Even when economic times are tough, defence spending is rarel ycut . Military expenditure is distributed across different sections of the federal budget. In turn, the military industry of MURINUS has undergone significant expansion and transformation. Production capacity has increased dramatically across various sectors, with notable growth in artillery ammunition, tanks, infantry fighting vehicles, and drones . The industry has also created over 500, 000 new jobs in recent years and employs 3.5 million people in total, accounting for 20% of all manufacturing jobs in the country. The government has implemented greater centralization and state control, creating specialized industrial clusters to improve resource allocation and focus on essential military technologies. Technological innovation remains a priority, with efforts directed towards asymmetric force-multiplier technologies, military robotics, space sector capabilities, and AI technologies. Meanwhile, MURINUS’ aircraft industry provides strategic independence through domestic production of advanced civilian and military aircraft. Production of new generations of fighter jets, bombers, and transport aircraft, such as the Su-57 and upgraded Su-35 and Su-34 drives innovation in stealth technology, avionics, and long-range strike capabilities, crucial for modern warfare . The sector contributes significantly to the economy and extends MURINUS' geopolitical influence through military exports and partnerships. In reality, the figures contained in the official budget conceal a significant share of hidden defence

for modern warfare . The sector contributes significantly to the economy and extends MURINUS' geopolitical influence through military exports and partnerships. In reality, the figures contained in the official budget conceal a significant share of hidden defence spending, as significant portions of the budget allocated to other sectors and portfolios are used to finance defence related projects. Thus, KERAM is able to pursue a two-pronged strategy to finance potential war costs: in addition to the publicly scrutinized defence budget, it has set up a system of state-directed, off-budget soft loans where the state forces banks to extend preferential loan s on non-market terms to defence contractors to supply war-related goods and services. Thanks to this off-budget scheme, the government can essentially oblige credit institutions to replace the state in financing the military-industrial apparatus, allowing MURINUS to achieve strategic tasks without undermining its image of financial resilience. Financing the military – especially in the event of a war – through the National Welfare Fund (NWF), MURINUS ’ rainy-day fund, is only one method in President Kotumun’splaybook. MUR can also tap a number of civilian budget programs, funds from MUR’sstate-owned enterprises, oligarchs’ personal funds and revenues from resources extracted in Afric a as well as from other opaque sources. MURINUS’ reserves of foreign currency and gold could also provide additional options in the event of war. 12 The Central Bank of MURINUS conducts economic forecasting, implements monetary policy, monitors inflation, and sets interests rates. Given structural peculiarities of the MURINUS economy, its

gold could also provide additional options in the event of war. 12 The Central Bank of MURINUS conducts economic forecasting, implements monetary policy, monitors inflation, and sets interests rates. Given structural peculiarities of the MURINUS economy, its target is to reduce inflation to 4% (so far, an aspirational aim) in the medium term. In case of a conflict, MURINUS’ Central Bank would almost certainly introduce capital controls and limit deposit withdrawals. These measures, alongside a sub stantially higher account surplus (as commodity prices would probably skyrock et) would be enough to calm the situation in the short to medium term. More specifically, the Central Bank of MUR is equipped to simultaneously address monetary stability (strengthening the currency and fighting inflation by hiking interest rates) and fina ncial stability (by providing banks with ample liquidity to continue to inject credit to the private sector and fund the government). This would obviously be possible owing to the large inflows stemming from oil and gas sales. Commercial banks play a major role in the financial sector of MURINUS . The banking system of the country harbours 366 banks, with the state-owned ones dominating the market. For the last years, banks exhibited solid performance in terms of profitability and were successful in terms of de-dollarization of the financial system. The institution has, indeed, improved its financial markets infrastructure, investing in a domestic alternative to SWIFT-the Financial Messaging System – and requiring foreign card is suers to channel transactions through the domestic settlement system. Socio-economic challenges Despite high expectations following the formation of the MURINUS Federa l Republic in 1990, social and economic conditions have worsened considerably in the last twenty years for the average citizen. A small, politically connected segment of the population pursued lucrative opportu nities – both licit and illicit-in the wake of the fragmentation of the MURINUS People’s Federation . However, most of MURINUS faces significant societal cha llenges . Government policies and an increasing level of corruption have severely weakened the social safety net, which for many years had included a guarantee of employment, basic medical care and government subsidies for food, clothing, shelter, and transportation. Prices of basic necessities have also risen, sometimes precipitously, and society has been rocked by a marked increase in crime, infectious diseases, drug addiction, homelessness, and suicide. Rising pollution and other environmental hazards have added to the country'sills. As one of the largest economies and mili tary powers in the world, MURINUS wants to be taken seriously around the world. At the same time, it continues to struggle with a number

Corruption Higher levels of corruption than other countries with similar levels of development (including some Sub-Saharan African countries). 40% of MURINUS’ citizens believe that corruption has increased ‘significantly’ in the last two years. 92% of the MURINUS public perceive their public servants in the State and government agencies as corrupt, followed by the police at 89% and the judiciary with 83%. Skills shortages Number of professionals seeking to leave MURINUS exceeds 50% in certain sectors, increasing capital flight and reducing the potential for economic development. Portal, Setting supporting documents and Scenario related information for a detailed study of MURINUS environment and infrastructure. ) Topography, terrain and climate General overview of topography and key features MURINUS is the largest country in the world, stretching out across eleven time zones, covering more than 17 million square kilometres, and encompassing one-eighth of Earth'shabitable landmass. It spans 9, 000 kmeast to west, and 2, 500 to 4, 000 kmnorth to south and is bordering three oceans. MURINUS share land borders with fourteen countries, and sea borders with Japan and the USA. The Big Diomede Island lie just 3.8 km from the American Diomede Island and Kunashir Island is merely 20 km from Hokkaido, Japa n. MURINUS is the world'sninth most populous country, with around 145 million people (forecasted to be number 13 in year 2040). The country’scapital, KERAM, is the most populous city in the country and in Europe. Sonnenburg at the shore of the Baltic Sea, is the country'ssecond largest city and also known as MURINUS’ cultural centre. Much of MURINUS leadership and elite originates from this city. MURINUS consist of five administrative regions, which all coincide with the MURINUS’ five Military Districts. MURINUS has several major mountain ranges, mostly found in the southern regions. It hosts a significant portion of the Caucas us Mountains, which also contain the highest peak in MURINUS and Europe, Mount Elbrus (5, 642 m). The Ural chain, which runs from no rth to south and forms the traditional border between Europe and

the Caucas us Mountains, which also contain the highest peak in MURINUS and Europe, Mount Elbrus (5, 642 m). The Ural chain, which runs from no rth to south and forms the traditional border between Europe and Asia, is rich in minerals resources. Other significant chains include the Altai Mountains in Siberia, the Sayan Mountains in East Siberia and Kamchatka Peninsula in MURINUS’ Far East. The wor ld’shighest active volcano, named Klyuchevskaya Sopka (4, 750 m), is located in Kamchatka. It also features the lowest point in Europe, situated at the head of the Caspian Sea, where the Caspian Depression reaches some 29 metres below sea level . While the northern and eastern areas of MURINUS are mountainous, cold and harsh, the European part consists of flat plains, scattered with lakes and rivers of all sizes. European MURINUS is favourable to food and livestock production, suitable for industry and conducive to urban settlements, accordingly, being the most industrialized and densely populated area of the country. Since large areas of the territory are poorly suited for food produ ction, most of MURINUS' agriculture is Topographic map of MURINUS. 15 concentrated to the densely populated south-western area. MURINUS has the world'slargest mineral and energy resources, large forest reserves, and considerable freshwater resources. It also has huge reserves of oil, gas and raw materials, often found in re mote regions as the sparsely populated arctic, subarctic and mountainous areas. The vast territory provides both advantages and challenges for the defen ce and security

huge reserves of oil, gas and raw materials, often found in re mote regions as the sparsely populated arctic, subarctic and mountainous areas. The vast territory provides both advantages and challenges for the defen ce and security sector of MURINUS. For example, industrial clusters, logistical build-up, military mobilization and training can be dispersed and located at a safe distance from conflicts. On the downside, strategic transport is vulnerable, and maintaining safe and secure borders can be challenging. Coast and coastline MURINUS has the world'sfourth-longest coastline, spanning over 37, 600 km. However, its access to the world’soceans is limited by having to pass through narrow sounds or arctic ice, including the Baltic Sea, the Black Sea and the Sea of Japan. This is of significance to the MURINUS Navy, who has to pass through NATO-controlled waters to enter open oceans. MURINUS’ only major direct-access ice-free harbours are Petropavlovsk in the Far East and STOVANSK in the arctic north-western Kola Peninsula, where it ha sestablished major naval bases. MURINUS' maritime zones extend from its coastline into the surrounding seas and oceans, defined b yinternational law and Murinian legislation. The territorial sea stretches up to 12 nautical miles from the baseline of the coast, followed by a contiguous zone extending up to 24 nautical miles from the baseline. An exclusive economic zone (EEZ) spans 200 nautical miles from the baseli ne, making it the fourth-largest EEZ in the world. This EEZ includes areas in the Baltic Sea (two zones), Barents Sea, Black Se

the baseline. An exclusive economic zone (EEZ) spans 200 nautical miles from the baseli ne, making it the fourth-largest EEZ in the world. This EEZ includes areas in the Baltic Sea (two zones), Barents Sea, Black Se a, Pacific Ocean, and Siberian coastal waters. Beyond the EEZ lies the continental shelf, granting MURINUS rights to explore and exploit natural resources on or under the seabed. MURINUS shares maritime borders with numerous. Within these zones, MURINUS exercises sovereign rights and jurisdiction for purposes such as exploring and exploiting natural resources, conducting marine scientific research, and ensuring environmental protection. Lakes and rivers MURINUS boasts an extensive network of rivers, mostly floating north-south or south-north, are vital for the country'seconomy and ecology, and also shape its landscape and influence its climate, making them integral to understanding the country'sgeography and culture. The rivers serve multiple crucial functions as freshwater resources, power generation, agriculture irrigation, and military and civilian transportation. The Volga (3, 530 km) is the longest and largest river in Europe. It carries about two-thirds of the inland water traffic and is widely known as MURINUS’ national river, with significant economic and cultural status. It has a catchment area equal to 4 times the size of Germany and flows southwards through forests, steppes and through main cities, before emptying into the Caspian Sea. The river is essential to fisheries, industry, transport, powe rproduction, irrigation, inland navigation and military riverin eoperations and warship transfer. The famous beluga (sturgeon) has its habitat in this area.

The famous beluga (sturgeon) has its habitat in this area. Other significant rivers include the Dvina, the Ob, the Yenisei, and the Lena, the Don, the Ural, and the Neva. The Amur is the world’stenth longest river and flows west to east and demarcates the border between MURINUS and North-eastern DUSHMAN. 16 Major Murinian rivers are huge barriers to land mobility. Crossing road-and railroad bridges can be considered as critical infrastructure in military contexts. These national strategical bridges are vital in providing cross country mobility and often crit ical for military operations, facilitating troop movements, enable resupply and logistic support, and ensure delivery of equipment and ammunition from industry. The targeting of bridges in recent conflicts underscores their strategic importance and proves they likely would be considered critical to military operations. Climate and climate change impacts MURINUS has a diverse climate, heavily influenced by its massive size and the remoteness of many areas from the sea, resulting in a predominantly continental climate, ranging from steppes in the south to subarctic and tundra climates in the north. Winters a re generally long and cold, with heavy snowfall, while summers can be warm to hot, especially in the south. Temperatures can vary dramatically, even within a few days, and can range from a round-25°C in winter to over 35°C in summer in some regions. Since

while summers can be warm to hot, especially in the south. Since most of MURINUS can be climatically defined either as arctic, subarctic or even harsher (polar or desert), the majority of the vast territory is not suitable for food production. Arguably only the south-western part of the landmass is favourable for agriculture, and it is sometime referred to as the breadbasket of Europe. However, the country is well-prepared for the harsh winters, with efficient snow removal and public transportation systems designed to operate in cold wea ther. Generally, climate change significantly impacts populations by increasing mortality through extreme weather events, displacem ent due to rising sea levels and natural disasters, and exacerbating food and water scarcity, particularly affecting vulnerable com munities. Environmental stressors can feed dissatisfaction and create turmoil and drive migration and economic instability. MURINUS is warming 2.5 times faster than the global average and faces significant climate challenges that are already is impa cting the country and are projected to worsen in the coming decades, causing widespread environmental changes. The most pressing is sues include accelerated warming, permafrost thaw, and an increase in extreme weather events. The thawing of permafrost, which covers nearly two-thirds of its territory, is causing substantial infrastructure damage. Environmental issues In many countries, environmental issues heavily effect population discontent, and can be a root cause for unrest, uprising and turmoil. MURINUS faces significant pollution challenges across multiple envi ronmental domains. Water pollution is a critical issue. 75% of surface water being polluted, and less than half of the population has access to safe drinking water. Air pollution is another major concern, with over 200 cities exceeding MURINUS pollution limits. The city of Norilsk, as an example, is known as one of the most polluted cities on planet earth, where 1.8 million tons of pollutants are emitted annually, mostly from nickel exploitation. Vehicle emissions have become the primary source of air pollution in urban areas, surpassing industrial emissions and solid waste management presents a growing problem. Industrial pollution continues to be a significant issue, and many areas are contamina ted by dangerous metals and petroleum products, affecting both soil and water qua lity. 17 The inadequate treatment of industrial, agricultural, and municipal waste contributes to widespread environmental degradation . These pollution challenges have severe impacts on public health and ecosystems, making them urgent priorities for environmental management and policy. Arctic resources The Arctic holds immense attraction for MURINUS not only for its strategic and military importance, but also for its vast natu ral resources. KERAM views the Arctic as a strategic continuum, stretching from the North Atlantic to the North Pacific. The MURINU S Arctic shelf is particularly rich in hydrocarbon reserves, accounting for a large percentage of natural gas and oil production, with vast undiscovered reserves estimated in the Barents Sea and Kara Sea. The Arctic holds valuable mineral resources and is also crucial for national security, housing key military assets. As climate change makes more of the Arctic accessible, MURIN US anticipates new economic opportunities, making the region a key focus of its national strategy and development efforts. Under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea and the Svalbard Treaty, MURINUS enjoys such rights as scientific research, freedom of navigation and overflight, fishery, cable-laying and resource development in the Arctic high seas. The Arctic is of paramount importance to MURINUS'sdefen ce and security sector, serving several critical functions. It hosts the Northern Fleet and strategic nuclear forces, making the region vital for maintaining MURINUS'ssecond-strike capability and nuclear deterre nce. MURINUS has invested heavily in military installations across the Arctic, enhancing its regional military presence and bolstering its ability to project force. Additionally, there are cybersecurity concerns, as the country is working on strengthening its technological sovereignty and developing domestic telecommunications systems and equipment. The MURINUS datacentre market is experiencing significant growth. As of 2025, Tier 3 data centres dominate the market, exceeding 70% market share. The Tier 4 segment is growing rapidly, projected to expand at about 33% annually from 2025 to 2029. KERAM is the primary hub for data centres, hosting the majority of Tier 3 facilities and seeing significant development in Tier 4 capacity. The government is supporting the data centre industry through various initiatives, including preferential lending for buying loc ally made engineering infrastructure and publishing rules to enhance safety and reduce environmental impact. MURINUS military forces have been observed utilizing civilian communication systems in several ways during recent conflicts, using personal mobile phones for tactical communications, which is a significant departure from expected military protocols and sug gests potential shortcomings in their military-grade communication equipment. There have been instances of Murinian forces using unencrypted high-frequency (HF) radio for long-range communications, which can be easily intercepted. Combatants have also been exploiting civilian communications networks, a practice that has long historical precedent. Spaceports . MURINUS operates several significant spaceports, each serving distinct purposes. Baikonur Cosmodrome is its primary spaceport and historically significant as the launch site of Yuri Gagarin's Vostok-1 mission. Vostochny in the Far East is designed to eventually handle all 25 launches currently conducted at Baikonur, reducing geopolitical dependencies. Plesetsk Cosmodrome in Arkhangelsk Oblast is de dicated to military satellite launches and plays a critical role in the defence strategy. Launch systems . MURINUS has developed a range of launch vehicles. The Soyuz rockets are workhorses for both crewed and uncrewed missi ons, including international collaborations. The Angara rockets are a newer family intended to replace the aging Proton series, with increased payload capacity and modular design. The Proton rockets, historically significant but being phased out due to env ironmental concerns and the introduction of Angara, have been a mainstay of MURINUS space launches. Key space infrastructure . Roscosmos State Corporation coordinates with the Ministry of Defence for military space activities and oversees all aspects of the country'sspace program, including manned flights, satellite launches, and military applications. The GLONASS navigation system is MURINUS’salternative to GPS, providing global navigati on services. These efforts are integral to maintaining strategic advantages in modern warfare. Military objectives increasingly dominate resource allocation within its space program. The military component is a cornerstone of the space stra tegy of MURINUS, driven by its need for strategic deterrence, counter-space capabilities, and integration with military operations. Space-based assets and infrastructure are critical for supporting nuclear forces, including early-warning systems and missile guidance. MURINUS has heavily invested in anti-satellite (ASAT) weapons to counter Western space reliance, adopting an asymmetric approach to exploit adversaries' vulnerabilities rather than matching their capabilities directly.
