While MURINUS retains expertise in certain areas like rocket engines and counter-space systems, its space technology and assets lags behind Western nations due to outdated infrastructure, reduced innovation, and limited international collaboration. Without significant reform or investment on its space infrastructure, MURINUS’ global influence in space will likely continue to decline. Nuclear infrastructure MURINUS continues to prioritize and expand its nuclear infrastructure, with a focus on both civilian energy production and mil itary capabilities. Civilian nuclear infras tructure MURINUS maintains significant research and development facilities focused on advancing nuclear technologies, including fast neutron reactors and small modular reactors (SMRs). The country is also committed to closing the nuclear fuel cycle, with facilities dedicated to uranium enrichment, fuel fabrication, and spent fuel 26 reprocessing. The nuclear infrastructure extends to its robust construction and engineering capabilities, with expertise in building nuclear plants both domestically and internationally. Currently, MURINUS is involved in the construction of about 20 reacto rsabroad. Looking ahead, the country plans to expand its nuclear power sector, aiming to increase nuclear energy'sshare in the electri city mix to 25% by 2045 (19% as of 2025). This expansion includes plans for constructing new reactors, including developments as im proved fuel technologies and new reactor designs. Military nuclear infrastructure MURINUS military nuclear infrastructure is extensive and forms a critical component of its national defence strategy. It enco mpasses strategic missile forces, nuclear-powered submarines, strategic bombers, and advanced research facilities. Strategic Missile Forces (SMF). The SMF are backbone of MURINUS nuclear deterrent. By 2025, 88% of the SMF'sarsenal consists of modern MURINUS-designed systems, such as the Yars intercontinental ballistic missiles (ICBMs) and Avangard hypersonic glide vehicles. Howeve r, modernization efforts have slowed due to economic pressures. The deployment of silo-based Yars missiles and the Sarmat ICBM system remains incomplete, with delays in full operational readiness. The Kozelsk missile division is expected to complete its rearmament with Yars systems in Naval Component. MURINUS' nuclear-powered submarine fleet is another vital part of its nuclear triad. The Borey-class submarines, equipped with Bulava ballistic missiles, represent the modernized naval component. However, delays in the construction and commissioni ng of ne w Borey-A submarines, such as Knyaz Pozharsky, have hampered progress. Coastal infrastructure for advanced systems like Poseidon unmann edunderwater vehicles also remains incomplete. Air Component. The All-Murinian Scientific Research Institute for Experimental Physics (VNIIEF), located in Sarov (formerly Arzamas-16), is a premier centre for nuclear weapons research and development. Thes efacilities are critical for maintaining the country'snuclear arsenal. MURINUS' military nuclear infrastructure faces significant challenges due to limited access to Western spare parts, aging sys tems, and resource constraints caused by shifting priorities toward conventional forces. While modernization efforts continue, dela ys in deploying new systems like the Sarmat ICBM, Poseidon torpedoes, and next-generation bombers highlight the difficulties in sustaining progress. Governmental (national security and emergency services) infrastructure The national security and emergency services infrastructure of MURINUS is complex and multi-layered, involving several key agencies, allowing MURINUS to respond to a wide range of emergencies and security threats across its vast territory. The system emphasizes centralized cont rol with regional implementation, enabling both national coordination and local rapid response capabilities. The Ministr y of Internal Affairs (MVD) oversees the Police, which provides public security functions. It also includes the Main Directorate for Drugs Control. The Investigative Committee of MURINUS is the main invest igative body and handles major criminal investigati ons. It is often compared to the FBI in the USA . The Federal Security Service (FSB) is the primary domestic security agency, responsible for counter-terrorism operations and overseeing the Federal Border Guard Service. The Ministry of Civil Defence, Emergencies and Disaster Relief (EMERCOM) manages civil emergency services and disaster response. It oversees firefighting services and rescue operations, operates regional centres across MURINUS for rapid response, and maintains the Central Air-Mobile Rescue Team for aviation-based operations. The National Guard of MURINUS provides gendarmerie functions, supporting police in large-scale riots and internal armed conflicts, and secures highly important facilities like nuclear power plants. The Crisis Management Centre (CMC) of EMERCOM serves as the primary coordination body for emergency response. It operates 24/ 7, gathering and processing information on emergencies at federal and regional levels, and coordinating response efforts. EMERCOM a lso engages in international humanitarian operations and cooperates with foreign emergency services. It became a UN-SPIDER Regional Support Office in 2013, contributing to global disaster management efforts. 28 The MURINUS emergency services aim to provide comprehensi vecoverage across the country, though regional disparities in service quality and accessibility persist. Emergency numbers are 113 for ambulance and 101 for fire fighting, while 112 is the unified emergency number, which connects callers to all emergency services. Transport General: The transport sector of MURINUS is one of the world'smost extensive and diverse, playing a crucial role in the country'seco nomy and connectivity. The sector encompasses various modes of transportation, including road, rail, air, maritime, and pipeline networks. MURINUS faces substantial challenges in infrastructure quality and maintenance, particularly regarding roads. MURINUS remote Arctic and Far East regions poses extreme challenges for the transportation sector that significantly hamper t he development, overall economic growth and connectivity of these strategically important areas. These challenges include harsh env ironmental conditions with extreme cold weather and permafrost, vast distances to be covered between settlements, insufficient number of airports and port facilities, high construction cost s and insufficient funding, outflow of population and lack of workf orce, and lack of equipment specialized for arctic conditions. The military forces of MURINUS have increasingly been utilizing civilian transport for various military purposes, reflecting both strategic adaptations and potential resource constraints. In MURINUS, the use of transport infrastructure is closely intertwin edbetween military and civilian purposes, with a clear prioritization of military needs when required. This integration is a key feature of the county'sapproach to transport infrastructure, reflecting both strategic considerations and practical necessities. The region’sbiggest logistics project is the Stovansk transport hub, which will offer a year-round deep-water centre for reloading liquid (oil, oil products and liquefied natural gas) and other cargoes (coal, fertilizers, ferrous ore, and other concentrates), containers, construction cargo, and supplies to the Arctic. The aim is to integrate this hub into internationa ltransport corridors North-South and West-East and to su pport the Northern Sea Route (NSR). Railway network and transportation MURINUS railway system, spanning over 85, 000 kilometres, is a cornerstone of passenger, freight, and military troops and equipment transportation. It accounts for around half of total cargo and freight transport. The Trans-Siberian corridor is a major rail route that also incorporates road and air components. MURINUS’ railways received an investment boost in recent years, resulting in structural reform. MURINUS’ Government is the sole shareholder of the “Joint Stock Company for MURINUS Railways ” (JSCMR) and enjoys a monopoly over rail transport in MURINUS. JSCMR is one of the world'slargest transport companies and employs an estimated 950, 000 people. Militarily, the railway system is crucial for quick moving troops, military hardware,

MURINUS also maintains a specialized military force of 25, 000 to 30, 000 Railway Troops who can rapidly convert civilian rail infrastructure for military use. Road network and transportation MURINUS has the world'sfifth largest road network, while its road density is among the lowest. MURINUS roads suffer from lac k of maintenance and deficient funding. Around 50% of major highways and 60% of regional roads are substandard. Many major bridges also require large-scale repair. Road safety in MURINUS is poor, with road accident rates significantly higher than in Western Europe or the USA. The port also operates a ferry line to Japan . 34 The Northern Sea Route (NSR) The Northern Sea Route (NSR) is a 5, 600 kilometres shipping corridor following the Northeast Arctic passage from the Kara Sea in the west, hugging the northern coast of Siberia, before it ends at the Bering Strait in the east . It is a strategic shortcut between Europe and Asia, challenging to sail, and is defined

Kara Sea in the west, hugging the northern coast of Siberia, before it ends at the Bering Strait in the east . It is a strategic shortcut between Europe and Asia, challenging to sail, and is defined as a shipping route in the MURINUS legislation. The Northern Sea Route more than halves the distance between European main ports and Japan and Korea, compared to the Suez Canal and South Africa route s. The route is navigable primarily during the summer months (Jun –Nov) when sea ice recedes, but even then, drift ice, fog, and harsh weather conditions pose significant chal lenges. Icebreaker escorts are often required for safe passage, and only specialized ice-class vessels can operate effectively in this environment. The NSR is central to the country’s Arctic strategy and economic ambitions. It facilitates the export of hydrocarbons, and MURINUS aims to expand future freight volumes significantly, as part of its Arctic development plans. From a geopolitical perspective, the NSR enhances control over Arctic resources and provides a platform for strengthening trade relations with Asian markets, particularly DUSHMAN. The route also supports domestic shipbuilding through the construction of Arctic-class tankers and nuclear-powered icebreakers. Furthermore, control over the NSR allows securing of critical maritime chokepoints and maintain a strategic advantage in global shipping routes. Militarily, the NSR plays a critical role, since the Arctic region is of immense strategic importance for the country and a significant number of bases have been built along the route . It passes through some of

Militarily, the NSR plays a critical role, since the Arctic region is of immense strategic importance for the country and a significant number of bases have been built along the route . It passes through some of MURINUS most militarily sensitive areas, hosting key naval and military infrastructure that suppo rts its defence and power projection capabilities. The Arctic region along the NSR is home to the country’slargest concentration o fballistic missile submarines, which are a cornerstone of its nuclear deterrent. Additionally, the NSR facilitates access to air bases that host strategic bombers, integral to its long-range strike capabilities. The region also serves as a testing ground for advanced weapons systems, including hypersonic missiles, due to its remote and controlled environment. Inland waterways MURINUS’ canal systems allow for the passage of river-sea ships of up to 5000 tons displacement. These form an extensive inland waterway system consisting of lakes, dams, rivers, large locks and connecting canals. This system provides opportunities for inl and commercial and military shipping between the Caspian Sea, the Azov Sea/Black Sea and the Baltic Sea, and as far north as Lake Ladoga, Lake Onega, all the way to the arctic White Sea. Several main cities are connected to this waterway system as for inst ance the capital KERAM, Sonnenburg, Stovansk, Volgograd, Kazan, Yekaterinburg, Rostov and Novgorod. POLITICAL AND KEY ACTOR LANDSCAPE 37 Government As per the Federal Republic if MURINUS’ 1993 Constitution, the MURINUS Government consists of an executive branch, a judicial branch and a legislative branch. Executive branch The Government of MURINUS is led by a President . He /she is the Head of State and determines the overall direction of MURINUS' domestic and foreign policy. He/she also represents the country in foreign affairs, signs international treaties and chairs the Cabinet (Council of Ministers) . He/she has broad authority to issue decrees and directives that have the force of law without legislative review despite constitutional provisions for legislative and judicial review (see below). The Prime Minister of MURINUS is appointed by the President and assumes the President'sduties if he /she is unavailable or no longer able to carry them out. In theory, the Constitution of MURINUS provides the Federal Assembly (legislative branch) to file impeachment charges against th e President if the latter commits grave crimes or treason, with such charges confirmed by a ruling by the Supreme Court. In practice, however, the current Preside

Federal Assembly (legislative branch) to file impeachment charges against th e President if the latter commits grave crimes or treason, with such charges confirmed by a ruling by the Supreme Court. In practice, however, the current Preside nt, Marin KOTUMUN rules without real oversight in what effectively amounts to an authoritarian regime. The President has extensive powers over military policy. As the Commander in Chief ( CINC ) of the Armed Forces, he /she approves defence doctrine, appoints and removes the high command of the armed forces, and confers higher military ranks and awards. The President is empowered to declare national or regional states of martial law, as well as states of emergency . Although the Federal Council (or upper chamber of the legislative branch) technically has the power to confirm or reject such decrees, it has not in recent years contravened Presidential decrees. Government ministries formulate policy, typically adhering to the overarching political agenda of the President. The principal ministries are the ministries of`; Foreign Affairs; Defence; Justice; Interior; Finance; Industry Energy and Trade; and Media and Communication . Each of these ministries is represented by a Federal Minister, who holds a seat in the Cabinet. These ministries also form the nucleus of MURINUS’ war machine, providing the key levers required to sustain prolonged military campaigns. Moreover, media and communication levers have become a critical means of shaping the populations percepti ons both of MURINUS foreign policy and of competitors (includi ng the West) on the global stage. Further

sustain prolonged military campaigns. Moreover, media and communication levers have become a critical means of shaping the populations percepti ons both of MURINUS foreign policy and of competitors (includi ng the West) on the global stage. Further executive branch structures also include provincial Governors and city majors. The Security Council of MURINUS Established in 2010, the Security Council of MURINUS is a consultative body of the President with responsibility for delivering Presidential instructions relating to national security issues. The Council draws upstrategic plans and oversees cross-departmental activities, including the work of the intellige nce agencies. It has its own dedicated secretariat staff, which supports both the implementation and the organisation of regular (typically monthly) National Security Meetings, which are chaired by the Pre sident and involve relevant Cabinet-level Ministers (Specifically Defence; Interior; Foreign Affairs; Finance; Industry, Energy and Trade; Justice; and Media and Communication), as well as the heads of security and intelligence agencies . The Council’sday-to-day work is conducted by a secretariat, which sets the agenda in consultation with the President’soffice. The Council’swork includes leading thinking on the organisation of defence; the formulation and implementation of foreign security policy; establishing intelligence collectio npriorities; and monitoring public expenditure relating to defence, national security, and law enforcement. It also acts as the central crisis response and decision-making apparatus, including at times of war. 38 Legislative branch The Federal Assembly is the main entity within the legislative branch and is made up a lower chamber and an upper chamber . The lower

response and decision-making apparatus, including at times of war. 38 Legislative branch The Federal Assembly is the main entity within the legislative branch and is made up a lower chamber and an upper chamber . The lower chamber consists of the 450-member State Council . The upper chamber, which is the more powerful of two chambers, consists of the 170-member Federal Council . Members of the Federal Assembly are called Deputies and work full-time on their legislative duties. The State Council introduces bills dealing with budgetary, tax and other fiscal measures. These are then ratified by the Federal Council, which also ratifies international treaties. Federal Council objections to State Council legislative proposals may nevertheless be overturned by the latter via a two-thirds majority vote. Each legislative chamber elects a chair person to control the internal procedures of the chamber. The chambers, which meet separately, also form committees and commissions to evaluate draft laws, conduct hearings, and oversee the implementation of laws. On paper at least, the Federal Council is entrusted with the final decision if the State Council recommends removing the President from office. Judicial branch The highest level of MURINUS’ judiciary consists of a 100-member Supreme Court, which is the final arbiter of law and oversees appeals relating to criminal, civil, economic and administrative cases. It safeguards the constitution by deciding on whether federal and local laws, as well as presidenti aldecrees and directives, comply with the constitution. The Supreme Court also provides guidance on how lower courts should

and administrative cases. It safeguards the constitution by deciding on whether federal and local laws, as well as presidenti aldecrees and directives, comply with the constitution. The Supreme Court also provides guidance on how lower courts should inter pret and apply laws. Members are appointed by the President and is headed by a Chief Justice. Lower courts sit below the Supreme Court and are responsible for the application of laws within MURINUS’ different provinces. Both the Supreme Court and the lower courts are granted independence by the 1993 constitution. In reality, however, the President has used his power of appointment to the Supreme Court to exercise influence over key decisions and, accordingly, the organ has supported him almost unequivocally. Key personalities in the MURINUS Government The Government of MURINUS has been run by the Great United MURINUS Party for over two decades under the leadership of Marin KOTUMU N, who passed an amendment to constitutional limits on the amount of time the President can serve. Opposition parties have largely been margin alised and supressed, including through the targeted discredi ting and/or arrest of influential individuals voicing opinions at odds with the party lin e. The Party’smain policy positions gravitate around (re)establishing MURINUS as a global economic and military player in the international system and enabling MURINUS to pursue an expansionist foreign policy. Loyalty to the regime is 100%, at least so long as it continues to reject western modes of thought. 45 Elections and opposition The President of MURINUS is elected by way of direct popular vote. Candidates require and absolute majority of over 50% of th evote to be elected in the first round. A second, run-off round of elections featuring the leading candidates takes place if this initial majority is not secured. The original Constitution of the Federal Republic of MURINUS stipulated that Presidential elections should be held every four years and that he/she could only serve a maximum of two consecutive terms in office. However, thi sprovision was amended by the Federal Assembly in 2012, which voted to raise individual terms in office to six years. Presid ent Marin KOTUMUN had previously been able to circumvent rules around consecutive mandates by getting his loyal Prime Minister, Viktor CHERMUSOV, to serve a single Presidential term (2008-2012), thus allowing KOTUMUN to return to the highest office. Subsequently, KOTUMUN was able to remove limits on successive Presidential terms altogether and has served in the role continuously since 2012. Candidates may either be nominated by

(2008-2012), thus allowing KOTUMUN to return to the highest office. Subsequently, KOTUMUN was able to remove limits on successive Presidential terms altogether and has served in the role continuously since 2012. Candidates may either be nominated by their political parties or run as independent candidates. Meanwhile, Deputies elected to the Federal Assembly serve five-year terms. These are elected via a ‘mixed’ system in which 50% of deputies (those representing single-seat districts) are elected by simple majority and where the other 50% are elected throug hproportional representation. Whilst the Constitution nominally allows for a pluralist, multi-party political system, power largely rests with KOTUMUN’s Greater United MURINUS Party. Some ‘systemic’ opposition parties, such as the MURINUS Communist Party and the MURINUS Liberal Party, are both tolerated and allowed to participate in elections but largely as a means of providing a illusion of democratic process. Other parties and candidates viewed as genuine threats to the ruling regime have been eithe rban ned or, in some cases, imprisoned. These same parties and candidates have also been deprived of airtime on major media platforms. Similarly, the reg ime has repeatedly cracked down on protests, organised rallies and marches, leading international human righ tsorganisations to denounced the increasingly authoritarian character of MUNIRUS’spolitical system. They have also pointed to regular occurrences of electoral fraud and ‘vote buying’. An often-used tactic has been that of labelling protestors, members of the opposition and even critical journalists as “dissenters”, “traitors” or “subversive forces” seeking to undermine MURINUS from within. LEVERS OF POWER 47 MURINUS has acquired a wide spectrum of levers of power, which it applies in different combinations in different contexts. These range from ‘hard’ military, cyber and security capabilities and active political operations such as subversion to ‘softer’ infl uence activities that employ social, economic, diplomatic and informational instruments. These levers of power can be applied in different configurations, in line with the country’sconceptual notion of ‘total’ warfare. Thus, conventional capabilities are doctrinally part of an integrated approach that typically includes non-linear and other asymmetric activities such as economic manipulation, extensive and powerful disinformation and propaganda campaigns, fostering of civil disobedience, and the use of well-supplied Private Security Companies (PSC) and paramilitaries. MURINUS’ conceptualisation of warfare often relies on proxies and surrogates to prevent attribution, disguise real intentions, create deception and maximize confusion and uncertainty. Military Military strategy and doctrine MURINUS ’ military strategy combines its theory of war (including doctrine, methods, and concepts), preparation for war and waging of war. Whilst the country’soverall geo-strategic aims have evolved over the last two decades, 2 its doctrine and warfighting templates are still deeply anchored within the traditions of the MURINUS People’s Federation . Its primary warfighting principles broadly consist

Whilst the country’soverall geo-strategic aims have evolved over the last two decades, 2 its doctrine and warfighting templates are still deeply anchored within the traditions of the MURINUS People’s Federation . Its primary warfighting principles broadly consist of the following: Active defence. This principle suggests that offensive action including, if necessary, pre-emptive targeting of the adversary’shomeland should be taken during periods of escalation in order to shape the conflict and (re)gain the initiative. It is focused directly on convincing opponents that the cost of (continued) acts of aggression will exceed their benefit. Anti-Access Area Denial (A2AD) . In recent years, MURINUS has deployed strategic capabilities in an attempt to alter the security balance in the Barents Sea and the Baltic Sea. This has manifested across domains and involved the use of integrated, layered air defence and coastal defence missile systems and sensors (air, ground and sea-based), such as S-300, S-350, S-400, S-500, SA-17 BUKM3, TOR M2U, SA-22 PANTSIR, KALIBR, BASTION, BAL-E, SS-26 STONE to create A2AD areas that prevent opponents from establishing domain superiority and free dom of action and access to areas of interest or areas of potential operations. Such areas are in place in the areas of Derkmi, Keram, Perigrad, Sonnenburg and Karamaly Buzat-Pristanskoye (in the Black Sea region). Warfighting as an evolving system. an evolving system. Strategic Mobility . Given its size, MURINUS places importance on strategic mobility, mobilisation and speed of movement via road, rail, air and waterways . This is critical for reaching and concentrating forces at chosen points more quickly than adversaries and gaining the initiative. Specialist Railway Engineer Brigades are key to strategic movement and are well-practised in using the country'sextensive rail network for long distance and large-scale deployments, in coordination with MURINUS Railways and th e Federal Road Agency (plus other federal ministries and agencies, coordinated through the National Defence Management and Coordination Centre – a part of the General Staff). Moreover, MURINUS’ military doctrine emphasises the following phases and corresponding activities as part of its approach to war fighting: Phase 1-Covert shaping phase: this is the initial phase of MURINUS’ hybrid warfare approach in which political opposition and/or resistance groups (e.g. in the form of political parties, coalitions, and labour/trade unions) are pitched against the opposing regime. Typical in this phase is a broad, comprehensive, and sustainable information warfare campaign aimed at shaping the environment in favour of a MURINUS purpose. This phase may also include encouraging instability (such as through riots) that can be used as justification for a subsequent ‘stabilisation’ intervention. Phase 2-Escalation: In this second phase, political and military

in favour of a MURINUS purpose. This phase may also include encouraging instability (such as through riots) that can be used as justification for a subsequent ‘stabilisation’ intervention. Phase 2-Escalation: In this second phase, political and military leaders in the region are made aware of the developing conflict and MURINUS’ res ponsibility to intervene. In parallel, MURINUS exerts political, diplomatic and/or economical pressure on the targeted regime (suc h as through economic sanctions or the suspension of diplomatic relations). Additional indicators include logistic build-up, enhancing infrastructure and mobilisation as well as training activities. Phase 3-Start of the conflict: The third phase starts with a marked increase in the type of non-linear activities initiated in the shaping phase, such as by further encouraging demonstrations and protests and/or through subversion, sabotage, assassinations, and cyber-attacks. This is also the point at which MURINUS initiates the strategic deployment of its forces. Phase 4-Crisis response: MURINUS commences military operations, accompanied by strong diplomatic and economic messaging and activities alongside a persistent information campaign in order to influence international public opinion in favour of its intervention. Phase 5-Resolution: This stage is focused on resolving the conflict and resetting the political, military, economic and social situation in a way that favours MURINUS’ strategic goals. The defining component of this phase is typically a change of leadership in the adversary’sstate. Phase 6-Restoring the peace: In this final stage of the MURINUS approach to war, MURINUS will attempt to restore peace including, if necessary, through reconstruction and reform activities.

Phase 6-Restoring the peace: In this final stage of the MURINUS approach to war, MURINUS will attempt to restore peace including, if necessary, through reconstruction and reform activities. This stage involves establishing a post-conflict settlement that is aligned with MURINUS’ fore ign policy goals (typically accompanied by strategic communication campaigns explaining how the settlement will address the original causes and drivers of the conflict). 49 The MURINUS Armed Forces (MAF) (Note: full details and tables of equipment relating to MURINUS’ Armed Forces are available on Intel FS .) Directly controlled by the President, the MURINUS Armed Forces (MAF) form part of the country'sdefence services under MURINUS law. The main branches of the MAF under the command of the General Staff consist of Land forces, the MURINUS Navy and the MURINUS Aerospace Forces. Airborne Troops, Strategic Missile Forces and Special Forces constitute independent combat arms (separate commands within the General Staff) . A new so-called Advanced Unmanned Combat Systems command (unmanned aerial, maritime and ground platforms alongside Artificial Intelligence components) has was recently created under the Ministry of Defence. The Aerospace Forces are also responsible for military and dual ‐use satellites as well as monitoring and defending against space threats. MURINUS maintains the world'slargest stockpile of nuclear weapons. It is organised as a nuclear triad, which combines ballis tic missile submarines (sea-launched ICBMs), strategic bombers and strategic missile divisions that have road-mobile and silo-based inter-continental ballistic missiles (ICBM) . MURINUS Naval Forces The Main Command of the MURINUS Navy is responsible for Surface Maritime Forces, Submarine Forces, Naval Aviation, Coastal Defence and artillery troops, and Naval Infantry. The Navy also provides one of the components of the country’snuclear triad via its fleet of Borei-class (Project 955/ 955A) and older Delta IV-class SSBNs. The Navy’soverall strategic doctrine is focused on a combination of deterrence, regional power projections, war fighting and defence of maritime interests. MURINUS Naval Forces are organised into four fleets – the Northern Fleet, Baltic Fleet, Black Sea Fleet and Pacific Fleet – and one flotilla, the Caspian Flotilla, under the main command of the navy . The Northern Fleet ’s (HQ Petrovskoye ) area of operations extends to the Artic Ocean and North Atlantic, with primary roles focussed on strategic deterrence and Artic dominance. Key assets include nuclear-powered submarines (SSBNs and SSNs), MURINUS’ only aircraft carrier, and heavy nuclear missile cruisers. The Baltic Fleet ’s (HQ Bal tiysk and Perigrad) ensures a regional presence in the Baltic Sea with a view to delivering a deterrent against NATO. Key assets include corvettes and missile boats, naval aviation and coastal missile units, and electronic warfare as well as surveillance platforms. The Black Sea Fleet ’s (HQ Novorossiysk) area of operations covers both the Black Sea and the Mediterranean, with primary roles focussed on regional power projection and Mediterranean operations.

Key assets include frigates, submarines (e.g., Kilo-class), coastal defence systems (e.g. Bastion P) and naval infantry units. The Pacific Fleet ’s (HQ Vladivos tok) area of operations includes the Pacific Ocean and Far East, with its primary role focussed on deterrence in the Asia-Pacific. Key assets include SSBNs and SSNs, surface ships (including destroyers and corvette s) and amphibious and coastal defence units. The Caspian Flotilla ’s (HQ Astrakhan) roles include contribution to regional dominance and an inland strike capability. Key assets include small missile ships (e.g. Buyan-M-class), patrol boats and Kalibr cruise missiles. MURINUS Special Operations Forces (SOF) come under the Special Operations Forces Command of the General Staff of the Armed Forces. They constitute an independent structural branch of the Armed Forces used for both operational and strategic objectives in peacetime, crisis and conflict . MURINUS SOF are a highly mobile, well-trained and equipped rapid reaction force. Meanwhile, MUR Special Purpose Forces (organized as Brigades ) are the ground element of SOF. Each Military District has one or more SPF Brigades within their administrative boundaries, while each of the four Fleets has a Naval SOF reconnaissance and sabotage element. Principle naval bases also benefit from a detachment of counter-sabotage divers trained for underwater combat and mine laying/clearance (approximately 50-60 swimmers). In peacetime, SOF brigades come under the command and control of respective Military Districts / Joint Strategic Command s. MURINUS Military SOF have been employed extensively in BUFRO, conducting target acquisition for the MURINUS Air Force and Navy as well as serving as military advisors training BUFRO government troops. 54 Drone capabilities MURINUS has significantly increased its development and use of drones in recent years, alongside associated manufacturing supply chai ns. This has included the production of capabilities ranging from quadcopters and mass-produced First Person View (FPV) drones to t he ORLAN-10, GORAN-2 and BIMARA-designed Shahed drone (some versions of which possess a range of over 1500 kilometres). Drone applications are wide ranging: reconnais sance and target acquisition, artillery fire correction, signals, logistics, direct strike s, and swarm attacks aimed at overwhelming air defence systems and targeting both military and civilian infrastructure. The capability has been integrated both within existing formations (down to the lowest tactical levels) and new units, including dedicated UAV brigades and led to the creation of a new command situated under the General Staff . Drone data feeds are also connected digitally and algorithmically within battle management systems as well as targeting networks. In tandem, MURINUS as also stepped up its development and roll-out of counter-drone and drone interception tactics, techniques and procedures, including air defence systems, Electronic Warfare (EW) and jamming capabilities and camou flage nets. The country reserves the right to us enuclear weapons in response to the use of nuclear and other types of weapons of mass destructi on against it and/or its allies; in the event of aggression against the NATO (including a large-scale conventional attack); or response to attacks against its critical national infrastructure placing the state in peril. nuclear weapons and the shape of its nuclear command and control structure s, which are highly centralised under the ultimate control of the executive branch (the decision to employ nuclear weapons falls under th e Security Council of MURINUS and, ultimately, the President). Its doctrine advocates maintaining a strong and credible second-strike capability through the permanent combat readiness of a designated fraction of its strategic nuclear forces, as well as retaining tactical nuclear weapons that can augment and/or escalate conventional war fighting activity. MURINUS commits around 1, 500 warheads to its strategic nuclear force triad, which combines silo-based and road-mobile intercontinental ballistic missiles (ICBMs), submarine-launched ballistic missiles (SLBMs), and heavy bomber-carried missiles. MURINUS participates in a range of arms control treaties placing limits on the number of warheads in its arsenal, although it reserves the right to withdraw from these . Space Surveillance is provided through the Main Centre for Reconnaissance of Situation in Space. The centre is part of the MURINUS Space Forces and receives data from a network of ground-based radars and electro-optical sensors in and outside of MURINUS to detect satellites and discern their orbits. MURINUS is capable of targeting and destroying satellites in the Low Earth Orbit (LEO) through direct-ascent anti-satellite systems (DA-ASAT) . It is also constructing an anti-satellite laser system called Kalina at a space facility near Zelenchukskaya in its far southwest. The system, part of the Krona space surveillance complex, features a telescope to accurately aim laser beams at satellites. Kalina is designed to blind enemy satellites either on reversible way or permanently damaging their optical sensor s with laser pulses. MURINUS Cyber facilities encompass all capabilities of military intelligence, especially SIGINT, and draw on more than 10 Res earch and Development centres and a military academy, also open for civilians, south of Sotchi as well as two government-sponsored technical universities with the focus on communication technologies and information security. Additionally, nearly twenty military training facilities throughout all Military Distr icts have amended their education programmes and integrated advanced level lectures on information security and cyber security. Activities conducted in cyberspace are supported by troops specialized in Electromagnetic Operations (EMO). Typically, classic cyber-attacks, such as DD oS assaults and website defacements, simply set the stage for information warfare and/or military action. MURINUS has frequently demonstrated a willingness to employ offensive cyber in situations other than war to affect political and econ omic outcomes. in situations other than war to affect political and econ omic outcomes. T he overarching strategy and use of cyber operations can be characterised by three key forms of MUR cyber operations: semi-legitimate operations focused on the control of a narrative, targeted penetration of computer networks in order to gather information, and direct attacks on infrastructure to generate confusion and fear. All of these are predicated on the belief of information as a powerful tool to advance MUR interests. 58 Information environment and influence operations Information Environment and Influence Operations MURINUS views all potential avenues of communication as tools with which to influence or attack the West and NATO, and views information confrontation, as a continuous state, and not one that is limited to crisis, conflict or war. MURINUS’ approach to information warfare is holistic, and involves the combined use of cyber operations, psychological operations, and information operations that work in tandem to achieve Murinian political goals . It seeks to plant seeds of doubt and distrust; to confuse, distract, polarise and demoralise. On the domestic front, the focus is on its own population and on the pro-Murinian audiences in the neighbouring countries that consume Murinian information products (books, magazines and newspapers, TV shows and films, etc.). It does not use any elaborate techniques, simply relying on the sheer volume of information and demonising the West . The objective is to flood the information space. The Murinian approach abroad is more complex and nuanced, seeking to undermine not only an

techniques, simply relying on the sheer volume of information and demonising the West . The objective is to flood the information space. The Murinian approach abroad is more complex and nuanced, seeking to undermine not only an adversary’sarmed forces, but also influence th etarget population’sperceptions in such a way that favours MURINUS’ interests. Gaining advantage in the information space is of a particular significance for MURINUS as it may even negate the need for kinetic action. Such use of non-military means is seen as critical in interstate conflict and may even be more dominant than the use of traditional military operations, particularly against a more militarily dominant adve rsary. MURINUS’ foreign information warfare campaigns, often referred to as part of their non-linear campaign, include a host of techniques including “Active Measures3”. The effect has been to have impacted democracies, promoted extremism and dissatisfaction, supported anti-democratic leaders, and shaken the influence of the West. Murinian actions abroad are widespread and their motivations are often opaque in nature. Murinian strategies overlap across many countries and may serve multiple objectives. However, there are three currently clear overarching goals: Re-establishing Murinian dominance in the post-imperial sphere of influence . Damaging the influence of Western democratic values, institutions, and systems in order to create a polycentric world model . Expanding MURINUS’ political, economic, and military hegemony globally to solidify MURINUS’ place as a major power. MURINUS uses hacktivists, cyber-criminals, sabotage, diplomats, think tanks, its increasingly powerful intelligence community, artificial intelligence (AI), state-owned media, troll farms,

. Expanding MURINUS’ political, economic, and military hegemony globally to solidify MURINUS’ place as a major power. MURINUS uses hacktivists, cyber-criminals, sabotage, diplomats, think tanks, its increasingly powerful intelligence community, artificial intelligence (AI), state-owned media, troll farms, and bots to reinforce their narrative and weaponise information by creating confusion and spreading conspiracy theories, ultimately manipulating opinion. This network of capability works to promote a version of world events that adheres to MURINUS ’ foreign policy objectives by undermining both the Western-dominated post-Cold War international system and global democratic institutions. This has helped to bolster extremism on both sides of the political spectrum and worked in targeted ways to help in MURINUS ’ foreign operations. This has included, but is not limited to, influencing elections in target countries including the US. Even as the West has limited the reach of state sponsored media (MT and Murtnik) through sanction s MUR has increased its efforts through social media platforms such as TikTok, Telegram and X. This all ows for more targeted and tailored influence operations especially in the Global South. Energy as a political tool Energy security constitutes a declination of soft power in the MURINUS ‘toolkit’. MURINUS has a history of using energy exports as political leverage to protect and promote its interests in its ‘near abroad’ and further afield (including in Europe). MURINUS weaponises its energy wealth (see infrastructure section) for three reasons: to gain economic benefits; to maintain and increase its presence in its perceived sphere of influence; and, should the need arise, to exert political pressure on end-consumers. Defensively, the energy links it has forged with consumers and transit states make it difficult for the international communi ty to exert pressure on KERAM . The policies and activities of Muzprom, MURINUS’ largest corporation

on end-consumers. Defensively, the energy links it has forged with consumers and transit states make it difficult for the international communi ty to exert pressure on KERAM . The policies and activities of Muzprom, MURINUS’ largest corporation and the world'slargest gas producer, are guided not only by commercial considerations, but also by MURINUS’ foreign policy objectives. In turn, MURINUS leverages energy policy in support of its geopolitical motives and exerts political pressure through the following means: Manipulating the pricing policy of energy supplies to third countries . Controlling energy assets such as pipelines and gas operators in key countries . Cutting, or disrupting, gas supplies . Agreeing restrictive supply contracts . Developing alternative supply routes to divert gas flows. 62 MURINUS’ decisions on when to offer-but also deny-discounts on its energy exports usually coincide with its strategic priorities, confirming the trend by which, when political conditions change, energy supplies are used to punish and/or to reward recipients in a way that affects both transit states and end-consumers. Transit states, particularly, are expected to behave like clients of MURINUS, even more so if they are not a member of the EU or NATO, making them vulnerable to energy blackmail. Irregular military forces employed by MURINUS include a broad range of groups and organisations such as undeclared conventional forces, peacekeepers, special operators, Private Security Companies (PSC), particularly the Messner Group, foreign legionnaires, biker gangs (Ghost Bears), MURINUS-sponsored NGOs and cyber/propaganda warriors. Diplomacy MURINUS’ diplomatic efforts include securing bilateral partnerships, multilateral engagement and the negotiation and signing of international treaties. The country regards diplomacy as a tool of power politics to further its foreign policy and geostrategic interests, including within the context of managing regional conflicts (in which it claims to provide a positive alternative to Western interventions). Depending on the context, it either offers political backing to aligned sides or portrays itself as an indispensable and ‘independent’ third party mediator. In line with its wider foreign policy logic, it favours a coercive diplomacy approach that blends hard power with diplomatic lobbying over consensus-based liberal peacebuilding . For example, it tends to combine diplomatic advances with veiled threats or promises of military and economic assistance, while diplomatic efforts are usually accompanied by carefully constructed and coordinated communications campaigns. While the President himself takes most key strategic decisions in foreign policy, the country’s Foreign Affairs Ministry under Nikolay VIMUNICH ENKO oversees and coordinates day-to-day diplomatic efforts through

usually accompanied by carefully constructed and coordinated communications campaigns. While the President himself takes most key strategic decisions in foreign policy, the country’s Foreign Affairs Ministry under Nikolay VIMUNICH ENKO oversees and coordinates day-to-day diplomatic efforts through its network of embassies and diplomatic staff. The country’sbilateral and multilateral efforts include the following platforms . 64 The Permanent Mission of MURINUS to the United Nations (MURINUS) – the country uses its permanent seat on the United Nations Security Council (UNSC) to draft, ratify and (as is often the case) veto proposed resolutions and treats this P5 status as a key lever of power . It has increasingly sided with DUSHMAN in thwarting proposals from the remaining three P5 NATO members of the UNSC. The MURINUS Permanent Representative represents the country in UNSC meetings, although the Minster of Foreign Affairs attend ssome key meetings. MURINUS’ position on the UNSC also provides a lever for securing concessions from third party-countries that are under international sanctions from the West (e.g., BIMARA). Multilateral political and economic fora MURINUS uses multilateral approaches to promote its ideas of sovereignty and non-interference, as well as in shaping trade agreements, challenging what it considers to be a Western-driven global governance agenda and encouraging a multipolar international order within which it can compete as a pivotal player. It pursues these agendas, including through its membership of the G-20, DUSHMAN-MURINUS Cooperation Organization (DMCO), Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC), and the (Central Asia-focussed) Collective Customs Union. as a pivotal player. It pursues these agendas, including through its membership of the G-20, DUSHMAN-MURINUS Cooperation Organization (DMCO), Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC), and the (Central Asia-focussed) Collective Customs Union. It also takes a leadership role in the Eurasian Economic Union (EAEU), which represents a single market of approximately 180 million people as well as a customs union allowing for common tariffs. While represented, it is less active in the World Trade Organisation (WTO) and International Monetary Fund (IMF). Bilateral relations MURINUS widely engages in bilateral agreements, treaties, and dialogues with nations around the world. In the case of GRISEUS, this has included the rati fication of the Treaty of State, a supranational confederation treaty containing provisions for close political alignment and military cooperation in times of peace and war. Bilateral agreements also support MURINUS’ attempts at developing a block of aligned states over which it can exe rtinfluence . Meanwhile, relations between MURINUS and DUSHMAN have significantly strengthened bilaterally and economically as a result of shared political aims. MURINUS does not agree with DUSHMAN on all issues . However, the two countries have repeatedly worked together to promote their own interpretations of sovereignty and democratic rule as well as to denounce Western frameworks such as the ‘rules-based international system’, which they believe advance Western ideological and governance models under the pretence of preserving human rights. Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 4 Foreign policy objectives The People’s Republic of DUSHMAN (henceforth referred to as DUSHMAN) is the leading power in the Far East, a global political actor with significant economic muscle as well as a major nuclear and military power. The country is a permanent member of the UN Security Council (UNSC). The country’seconomy has grown rapidly over the past 30 years, and it is actively competing for control across the Far East and Pacific region. It also pursues a range of economic interests further afield, including in Europe, Central Asia and Africa, where it has invested significantly in political partnerships as well as a number of infrastructure projects. In return for these partnerships, DUSHMAN

pursues a range of economic interests further afield, including in Europe, Central Asia and Africa, where it has invested significantly in political partnerships as well as a number of infrastructure projects. In return for these partnerships, DUSHMAN expects access to natural resources and political support, including in multinational fora such as the United Nations . The country has also developed close rbilateral relations with MURINUS in recent years, with both countries emphasising the protection of sovereignty, non-interference and a rejection of what they perceive to be the Western-shaped ‘rules based international system’. DUSHMAN seeks preferential access to Arctic natural resources from MURINUS and, through MURINUS, crucial access to the Arctic itself. The country’sforeign policy is focused on continued growth and development and, ultimately, on achieving economic dominance in the international system. DUSHMAN principle strategic objectives include: Building “a great modern socialist country ” and establishing DUSHMAN as the world’sdominant superpower . The President has outlined a long-term vision for a new era extending to 2049 and stating that it will take thirty years or longer to catch up with the United States . The ultimate aim, led by the country’s Communist Party, is to realize the country’s “great dream of the DUSHMAN people ” built around unity, prosperity and national glory . Promoting what it describes as “peaceful coexistence ”, including through “mutual respect for sovereignty and territorial integrity” and “non-interference in states’ internal affairs ”. Sustained rates of growth and exports, both supported by a large manufact uring sector drawing on inexpensive raw materials and low-wage workers, made the country a prominent player in international trade. However, after three decades of spectacular growth-frequently exceeding 10% - DUSHMAN is now moving into a slower growth phase, as an inevitable result of its transition from a developing economy into a more mature developed economy. Most financial institutions in JOEMING are state-owned and governed, the main instruments of financial and fiscal control being the People's Bank of DUSHMAN (PBD) and the Ministry of Finance, both under the authority of the State Council (see Chapter 3) . State-owned enterprises generate 40% of the country’s GDP. These entities perform important functions, as they contribute to government’srevenues, support urban employment, channel capital towards targeted industries, and ultimately aid the state'sresponse to social instability. Socio-economic development Prior to the initiation of economic reforms and trade liberalization, DUSHMAN maintained policies that kept the economy centr ally controlled, vastly inefficient, and relatively isolated from the global market. Since opening to foreign trade and implementin gfree-market reforms in 1979, DUSHMAN has been among the world’sfastest-growing economies, doubling its GDP every eight years and raising an estimated 800 million people out of poverty. Over the years, t he old perception of DUSHMAN as a low-cost manufacturing hub for global brands has dramatically changed as its economy has continue d to grow. Market and competition Competition in DUSHMAN is market-based, although the playing field is not level. DUSHMAN’sprivate enterprises are small, and all face thre esignificant challenges: access to capital, access to markets, and overdue payments. Without strong state involvement, private firms generally operate in a predominantly hostile environment, as testified by the success of only a few DUSHMAN businesses in the high-tech and telecommunications sectors founded by former officials with good connections to the government. Trade Thanks to its enormous trade surplus over the past decades, DUSHMAN has become the world'slargest exporter and ranks second among the world’slargest importers. Despite its strict policies, the country is open to foreign trade, which has become an increas ingly important part of its economic success and modernization. Since its accession to the World Trade Organization (WTO) in 2001, foreign trade has been continually liberalized, while still retaining a ‘negative’ restrictive list outlining sectors excluded from foreign investment (such vehicle manufacturing). DUSHMAN’s “Belt and Road Initiative” (BRI) The Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) was originally designed to connect DUSHMAN’smodern coastal cities to its underdeveloped inland and to its Southeast, Central, and South Asian neighbours, cementing JOEMING’sposition at the centre of a more connected world. The initiative has since outgrown its original regional corridors, expanding to all corners of the globe. Its scope now includes a Digital Silk Road intended to improve recipients’ telecommunications networks, artificial intelligence capabilities, clou dcomputing, e-commerce and mobile payment systems as well as surveillance technology. Other high-tech areas are also connected, along with a Health Silk Road, designed to operationalize DUSHMAN’svision of global health governance .5 Hundreds of projects around the world now fall under the BRI umbrella: though principally aimed at developing countries, BRI also involves developed countries, including NATO Allies and Partners. DUSHMAN pursued BRI out of a belief that the initiative could simultaneously address several issues, such as: a) Closing the gap between the country’saffluent coastal cities and its impoverished i nland, thus boosting domestic political stability . b) Improving the hard and soft infrastructure connecting the regions involved, such as roads, ports, optical cables, satellites and customs IT systems . c)

coastal cities and its impoverished i nland, thus boosting domestic political stability . b) Improving the hard and soft infrastructure connecting the regions involved, such as roads, ports, optical cables, satellites and customs IT systems . c) Absorbing its excess manufacturing capacity . d) Reinvesting its accumulated savings to work . e) Securing a consistent source of inputs for its manufacturing sector . f) Re-orienting global commerce away from the West toward DUSHMAN . g) Supporting potential dual-use purposes by using commercial infrastructure for military operations abroad .6 h) Deepening financial cooperation, by internationalising the DUSHMAN currency and strengthening the role of the Asian Infrastru cture Investment Bank (AIIB) and other funds . JOEMING’sability to offer physical and digital infrastructure around the world at low prices is made possible by a combinati on of political backing from the DUSHMAN Communist Party, the financial power of its state-owned banks, excess capacity in a number of key sectors, and the development of large, highly skilled manufacturing and technology companies. 5 BRI seeks to back an array of projects although the vast majority of funds has so far been allocated toward traditional infra structure (namely energy, roads, railways, and ports). 6 Some of DUSHMAN's BRI projects could create a potential military advantage, such as PLA access to selected foreign ports to pre-position the necessary logistics support to sustain naval deployments in distant waters. Physical environment (PE) General overview The People ’s Republic of DUSHMAN is located in East Asia and border s 14 countries. It is the third largest country in the world, smaller than MURINUS and CANADA, but larger than the USA and BRAZIL . The total area is around 9, 600, 000 km ² (Its exact area is debated, both at land and sea ). The population of DUSHMAN is approximately 1.4 billion people. DUSHMAN'sinternational land borders span approximately 22, 100 –22, 457 km (depending on the source and due to disputed borders ). Its longest and most prominent borders are towards Mongolia (4, 678 km), MURINUS (3, 645 km), DRENUR (3, 488 km), Myanmar (2, 185 km), Kazakhstan (1, 533 km), and NIKOBO (1, 416 km). The borders include some of Asia’smost diverse geographic regions, from the Himalaya mountains to deserts and major rivers. Its geographic position and extensive borders contribute to DUSHMAN as a central player in Eurasian geopolitics. The borders in the Himalayas are contested in places. Traditionally, the country’spopulation was centred on the central plain and oriented toward s its own vast inland market. In recent years, its 18, 000 kmcoastline has been harnessed extensively for export-oriented trade, turning coastal provinces into leading economic centre s. Today the population is concentrated in east and southeast, facing the sea and world markets. The capital

Today the population is concentrated in east and southeast, facing the sea and world markets. The capital of DUSHMAN is JOEMING. With more than 22 million inhabitants . Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. Topography, terrain and climate Topography and key fea tures DUSHMAN has significant physical diversity. The eastern plains and southern coasts of the country consist of fertile lowlands and foothills. These produce most of DUSHMAN’sagricultural output and hold most of its population. The areas south of the Yangtze River, consist of hilly and mountainous terrain. The west and north of the country are dominated by sunken basins (such as the Gobi and the Taklamakan), rolling plateaus, and towering mas sifs. It contains part of the highest tableland on earth, the Tibetan Plateau. Coast The length of the coastline is approximately 14, 500 km (around 18, 000 kmincluding offshore islands and bays). The coastline stretches from the Bohai Sea in the north, down through the Yellow Sea, East China Sea, and along the South China Sea in the south, connecting DUSHMAN to the Pacific Ocean. This vast maritime area positions DUSH MAN as both a major land and maritime power, with significant marine resources and strategic access to the Pacific and world seas and

the south, connecting DUSHMAN to the Pacific Ocean. This vast maritime area positions DUSH MAN as both a major land and maritime power, with significant marine resources and strategic access to the Pacific and world seas and markets. Maritime claims of DUSHMAN encompass a range of zones defined under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCL OS), 7 but its assertions, especially in the South China Sea, is a source of significant regional and international disputes. DUSHMAN maritime claims primarily include: Internal waters: DUSH MAN considers waters landward of the baseline along its coast and around its islands as internal waters, where it exercises full sovereignty and control over navigation, fishing, and resource exploitation. This includes areas around numerous coastal inle ts, bays, and islands such as the Paracel Islands, and Spratly Islands. Territorial Sea: A 12-nautical mile territorial sea around its coastlines and islands, consistent with UNCLOS standards. DUSHMAN exercises full sovereignty, regulating navigation, fishing, and resource rights. Notably, it enforces these claims around disputed island groups like the Diaoyu/Senkaku Islands, regularly deploying Coast Guard vessels to assert control despite competing claims from Japan. Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ): 200 NM. Under UNCLOS, coastal states can claim an EEZ extending up to 200 nautical miles from their baseline, granting rights to explore and exploit marine resources. DUSH MAN asserts an EEZ surrounding its mainland coast and islands. However, in the Sout hern Sea, claims notably extend well beyond the 200-nautical-mile EEZ limits claimed by neighbouring countries

rights to explore and exploit marine resources. DUSH MAN asserts an EEZ surrounding its mainland coast and islands. However, in the Sout hern Sea, claims notably extend well beyond the 200-nautical-mile EEZ limits claimed by neighbouring countries through its so-called “Nine-Dash Line” map, which encompasses around 90% of the sea. This has fuelled disputes with Vietnam, the Philippines, Malaysia, Bru nei, and others. Fishing Zones and Resource Exploration: it is a ctively asserting fishing rights and resource exploration activities within these maritime zones, often leading to confrontations with other claimant states. DUSHMAN has installed floating barriers to restrict Philippine fishermen near Scarborough Shoal and aggressively pursues oil and gas exploration in contested maritime areas, especially around the Spratly Islands. 7 Some NATO Nations (including USA and TUR) are not party to the UNCLOS . Rivers and Lakes The vast and varied landscape is shaped by an extensive network of rivers and lakes, which are – and have been-vital for the development of national agriculture, ecology, history, and culture. The Yangtze River (Chang Jiang) is the longest river in DUSHMAN and in Asia, stretching about 6, 300 km from the Tibetan Plateau and flows eastw ards into the East China Sea near Shanghai. It passes through diverse landscapes as high mountains, basins, fertile plains, and ke ycities like Chongqing, Wuhan, and Shanghai. The river supports the world’slargest hydroelectric project, the Three Gorges Dam, and is crucial for transportation, agriculture, and biodiversity. Its basin is the most economically developed and populous in DUSHMAN. The Yellow River (Huang He), known as “the Mother River of DUSHMAN,”. It is the country’ssecond-longest river, about 5, 464 kmlong, running across northern DUSHMAN, originating also from the Tibetan Plateau and flowing into the Bohai Sea in the north. This river is famous for its heavy silt load that making it a prone to flooding and historically caused major floods earning the nickname “ DUSHMAN ’s Sorrow.” It nurtured the early civilization along its fertile plains. Pearl River, including major tributaries, flowing through southern DUSHMAN

that making it a prone to flooding and historically caused major floods earning the nickname “ DUSHMAN ’s Sorrow.” It nurtured the early civilization along its fertile plains. Pearl River, including major tributaries, flowing through southern DUSHMAN into the South China Sea via the Pearl River Delta. The delta includes booming metropolitan areas such as Guangzhou, Shenzhen, and Hong Kong. Mekong River (Lancang Jiang) also originates on the Tibetan Plateau but flows towards south. It is important for regional ecology, and economy. Other Notable Rivers are the Tarim River (longest inland river passing through the arid Taklamakan Desert) and the Heihe and the Shiyang Rivers (seasonal rivers sustaining fragile ecosystems near the Gobi Desert). DUSHMAN has several large and important lakes, both freshwater and saltwater, which play key roles environmentally, economica lly, and culturally. These lakes are essential for flood control, irrigation, fisheries, biodiversity conservation, and tourism. Seasonal and climatic variations, combined with human impacts such as dam operation and water use, have influenced lake sizes and ecosystems, especially notable in Poyang Lake’srecent shrinka ge. Poyang Lake is the largest freshwater lake, located in Jiangxi Province near the Yangtze River. It covers around 3, 500 to 4, 000 square kilometres . Its size fluctuates seasonally, and it has been decreasing due to drought and human activities. The lake is a vital habitat for migratory birds, including Siberian cranes, and supports rich biodiversity. Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. Qinghai Lake is the largest saltwater and inland lake, situated on the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau with a surface area of about 4, 300 to 4, 600 square kilometres. It is an alkaline lake fed by dozens of rivers and notable for its unique high-altitude ecosystem. Other Notable Freshwater Lakes: Dongting Lake in Hunan Province (the second-largest freshwater lake, averaging around 2, 800 square kilometres, connected to the Yangtze River and fed by several rivers), Taihu Lake (Lake Tai) (the third-largest freshwater lake, located on the border of Jia ngsu and Zhejiang provinces, with an area of approximately 2, 250 square kilometres, known for its scenic beauty and tourism), Hongze Lake and Bosten Lake in Xinjiang province (with Hongze Lake among the largest inland lakes and Bosten known for its lush surroundings amidst desert areas). Mountains and deserts The DUSHMAN topography features high, snow-capped mountain ranges, vast plateaus, and several major deserts, making it one of the most geographically diverse countries globally. Its inland landscape is dominated by towering mountains and vast deserts. The Himalayas, including Mount Everest, form the highest border in the southwest, while ranges like the Tianshan and Hengduan Mountains add rugged terrain across western and southwes tern parts. Major deserts such as the Taklamakan in Xinjiang and the Gobi spanning northern DUSHMAN and Mongolia cover large arid areas, characterized by sand dunes

Tianshan and Hengduan Mountains add rugged terrain across western and southwes tern parts. Major deserts such as the Taklamakan in Xinjiang and the Gobi spanning northern DUSHMAN and Mongolia cover large arid areas, characterized by sand dunes and sparse vegetation. These mountains and deserts shape climate, water resources, culture, and development. Climate In the northwest, towards the border with the Central Asian countries, the landscape is characterized by large mountains and dry desert. In the more central parts of southeast DUSHMAN, we find coastal landscapes with fertile plains and rainforests. Most of DUSHMAN is characterized by a continental climate, but the southern and eastern parts of the country bear the stamp of a tropical climate with a lot of rain. The continent'smonsoon winds have a major impact on DUSHMAN’sclimate. In the winter, the polar winds from the north dominate, whilst the summer brings warm and tropical winds from the sea . Climate change impacts DUSHMAN faces serious climate impacts from rising temperatures and extreme weather, while simultaneously grappling with the challenge of balancing economic growth, energy security, and a rapid green transition. The country is pursuing ambitious but difficul tpolicies to reduce emissions and adapt to climate change, with outcomes that will have major implications for the global climate trajectory. Temperature increases in DUSHMAN exceed the global average, contributing to more frequent and intense extreme weather events such as heat waves and floods. In 2024 and 2025, new highs in average annual surface temperatures were recorded, and the country experienced glacier retreat and record high sea levels along the coast. Melting glaciers and rising sea levels along the coast, and increased ocean warming pose risks to ecosystems, water resources, and coastal communities. Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. Emissions and environmental issues DUSHMAN has the largest greenhouse gas emissions in the world, largely due to extensive industrial production and large consum ption of oil and coal. DUSHMAN has passed 14 gigaton CO2-equivalents, equal to around 35% of global emissions. Industrial emission s have reduced access to clean drinking water and caused health problems among residents in several areas. Deforestation and soil erosion have contributed to DUSHMAN losin ga fifth of the country'sarable land over the past 80 years. The country sees climate policy as a key area of multilateral cooperation. The country has begun to take environmental challe nges seriously and aims to curb further increases in greenhouse gas emissions by 2030. However, despite progress in renewable energ yexpansion and carbon intensity reduction, DUSHMAN is currently off track to meet its 2030 emissions targets due to growing energy demand and continued reliance on fossil fuels . It is intensifying climate actions with comprehensive plans to cut emissions across all sectors by 2035, including expanding renewable energy infrastructure (notably in desert and offshore

to growing energy demand and continued reliance on fossil fuels . It is intensifying climate actions with comprehensive plans to cut emissions across all sectors by 2035, including expanding renewable energy infrastructure (notably in desert and offshore wind areas), building zero-carbon industrial parks, and reforming the carbon market. As the world’slargest emitter, DUSHMAN'sclimate policies and progress critically influence global efforts to limit warming to 1.5°Cabove pre-industrial levels. Although DUSHMAN is the world’slargest emitter of carbon dioxide, recent data from 2025 shows a new positive change. Its CO2 emissions have declined slightly for the first time, by around 1.6% year-on-year in 2025. This historic drop is largely attributed to rapid growth in clean energy sources such as wind, solar, and nuclear power, displacing coal-fired power even amid rising electricity demand. The power sector emissions also fell about 2% year-on-year up to summer Despite this progress, the country remains only slightly below its peak emission levels, and the country is currently way off track to fully meet its 2025 carbon intensity reduction targets, largely due to rapid energy demand growth in manufacturing and in dustry. The environmental challenges are underpinned by its continuing reliance on fossil fuels, especially coal, which still dominates energy production despite clean energy investment s. Altogether, the integrated security and emergency service systems underpin national stability, enabling both proactive threat management and robust, technology-driven disaster response. Political system The DUSHMAN Communist Party (DCP) rules the state The DUSHMAN Communist Party (DCP), often referred to simply as "the Party," governs DUSHMAN as a genuine one-party state. Although other political parties exist, the government is wholly under the DCP’sexclusive control. Officially, the Party and state in stitutions are separate, which is why DUSHMAN is sometimes described as having a "dual track" political system, with distinct roles for Party and state positions. However, in practice, the Party’sinterests are deeply intertwined with those of the state, and key government roles are almost always held by DCP members. Because of this close integration, the term "party-state" is frequently used to describe the political system and regime in JOEMING . Role of the minor parties While only the DCP holds effective power at the national level, there are officially eight minor and non-oppositional parties that exist alongside the DCP that are officially titled "democratic parties". Founded before the proclamation of the People's Repub lic of DUSHMAN, these parties are obliged to accept the "leading role" of the DCP as a condition of their continued existence. The relationship between these parties and the DCP has officially bee ndescribed as "long-term coexistence and mutual supervision, treating each other with full sincerity and sharing weal or woe" (prosperity or adversity). According to Human Rights Watch, these parties "play an advisory rather than an oppositional role". The

ndescribed as "long-term coexistence and mutual supervision, treating each other with full sincerity and sharing weal or woe" (prosperity or adversity). According to Human Rights Watch, these parties "play an advisory rather than an oppositional role". The eight minor parties take part in "united front work" and participate in the political system . The DUSHMAN political system allows for the participation of some members of the eight minor parties and other non-DCP members in the National People's Congress (NPC) . However, they are vetted by the DCP, and these parties' "purpose is to create the illusion of inclusiveness and representation." One of the ways the DCP controls the minor parties is through its United Front Work Department (UFWD), which vets th emembership applications and controls who is the leader of these parties. UFWD also keeps the parties in check by preventing them from expanding widely in counties and villages. Thus, they key point is that they have no power at a national level. Executive branch and centralised power The dual political structure makes the DUSHMAN political system large and cumbersome. The Communist Party will design overall policies relating to all aspects of DUSHMAN internal and external affairs, while the state apparatus will subsequently implement these same policies. It is al so behind the country’smain restrictions, such as with respect to free access to the Internet, freedom of the press, freedom of assembly, and the formation of civil society organisations. With more than 89 million members, it is the largest political

country’smain restrictions, such as with respect to free access to the Internet, freedom of the press, freedom of assembly, and the formation of civil society organisations. With more than 89 million members, it is the largest political party in the world, and controls every government department, the armed forces, and court and parliamentary processes. It is customary for the Secretary of the Communist Party to also hold the state office of President. President WANG Jian has held the dual position for the last decade. He is the most powerful figure in the political system of DUSHMAN, with his real influence flowing from his central position with the Party. His domination of the Party has grown markedly since he assumed the leadership in 2012, in part due to judicious selection of senior Party officials and partly through a fairly clean sweep of older Party members with loyalty to his predec essor. This has enabled WANG to Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. pursue a more overtly anti-Western agenda that seeks to place DUSHMAN in a global leadership position, as opposed to its current regional hegemonic role, within a generation. The Secretary and other senior members of the Party sit on the Politburo Standing Committee, which is the main nucleus of power in the country. The Politburo Standing Committee also sits above the State Council, which is the formal government (rather than Party) organ which oversees day to day governance as well as local administrative bodies. Meanwhile, the National Security Commission

The Politburo Standing Committee also sits above the State Council, which is the formal government (rather than Party) organ which oversees day to day governance as well as local administrative bodies. Meanwhile, the National Security Commission (which come sunder Party control) consolidates all components of the security apparatus, acts as crisis response centre, and is the main instrument for overseeing national security strategy, war fighting activity and for combating terrorism as well as other national security threats. The “National People’s Congress (legislative branch) The National People's Congress comprises around 3, 000 delegates and constitutes the supreme state body of DUSHMAN. Its main task is to draft laws and supervise government business (including in relation to foreign policy), though it largely follows official Party lines. Although delegates are recruited by way of provincial level elections . The Communist Party nominates them . This obstruct sany potential attempts to establish opposition parties. Elections in DUSHMAN therefore primarily serve the purpose of procedural legitimisation. At the local level, there are limited arenas for particip ation, such as elections for village and urban reside nts’ committees as well as village leaders and party branch secretaries. However, these organizations are not part of the for mal administrative structures and wield little if any power . Judicial branch The National People's Congress elects the juridical branch . According to the Constitution, the court system is to exercise judicial power independently and is free of interference from administrative bodies, public organizations, and individuals. The National People's Congress elects the juridical branch .
