In effect, however, the DCP exercises dire ctcontrol over the entire court system. Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. Key personalities Role and name Background Beliefs and policy positions Relationships President and Secretary-General of the Communist Party WANG Jian Born 1954, in JOEMING. WANG has served as the President of the People's Republic of DUSHMAN since 2013. He has been General Secretary of the DUSHMAN Communist Party and chairman of the Central Military Commission, and thus is the paramount leader of DUSHMAN, since During the political turmoil of the 1960s, his family were forced to flee the capital to the interior. WANG grew up in a farming village. He joined the Communist Party (after several failed attempts) as a local party secretary. After his worker-peasant-soldier studies, he rose through the ranks politically. His lucky break came when appointed governor of Zhejiang in 1998. He held the position till 2006 and then joined the Politburo Standing Committee as first secretary in 2008. He was later appointed Vice President of DUSHMAN and vice chairman of the Central Military Commission. From there it was a relatively small step to the Presidency. During his time in growing up in the interior, he developed an affinity with the rural poor which seems to have stayed with him. Compared to many of his peers, he appears uncorrupted as far as money is concerned, while being ruthless in the pursuit of political power. On specific policy positions, most analysists say it is hard to identify common threads in his thinking. But his overall approach is to seek stronger regional hegemony and global influence for DUSHMAN, and to turn the country back towards its totalitarian roots. This means higher spending for the security apparatus and more power and influence for the Party, and less freedom for individual citizens. Nevertheless, in DUSHMAN, most citizens are supportive of this approach – so long as it comes with growing economic prosperity for a wider share of the population and narratives of DUSHMAN greatness in the world remain credible. Overview DUSHMAN has acquired a wide spectrum of levers of power, which it uses in various combinations in different contexts. These range from ‘hard’ military, cyber space and security capabilities and active political operations such as subversion to ‘softer’ influence activities using social, economic, diplomatic, infrastructure and information instruments. These levers of power can be used in different configurations, in line with the country’sconceptual notion of DUSHMAN as the dominant superpower in the world . Thus, conventional capabilities are doctrinally part of an integrated approach that typically includes non-linear and other asymmetric activities such as economic manipulation, extensive and vigorous disinformation and propaganda campaigns, strength ening alterna tives to Western ideas, and developing economic and infrastructure programs in support of “targeted” countries. In return, DUSHMAN expects that increased diplomatic dependencies will result in positive advocating and support in international forums such as the United Nations . Internal levers of power Domestic strategic planning The DUSHMAN government pursues social stability and economic development as its two main priorities, but global standing and reputation have also become a key strategic goal

the United Nations . Internal levers of power Domestic strategic planning The DUSHMAN government pursues social stability and economic development as its two main priorities, but global standing and reputation have also become a key strategic goal under the WANG administration. Transition towards democracy based on the rule of law, on the other hand, is not one of the government'slong-term strategic goals . Rather, the government follows a deliberate strategy to counter act the perceived threat of democratisation, alongside challenges to the Party’spolitical hegemony. Politics in JOEMING is characterized by long-term perspectives and subject to evidence-based impact assessment and strategic planning. The government uses guidelines as a framework for binding and non-binding political goals . Five-year plans specify social and economic targets, some of which are quantified and binding, while others are non-quantified and highlight results that local authorities should aim to achieve. Major policy changes, such as those inv olving innovation or education, take longer to deliver . The time is ext ended to 10 or sometimes even 15 years. The central government has prioritized investment s in the developmen t of A rtificial Intelligence (AI), a field in which it envisages that DUSHMAN will become the market leader by 2030 . The government also continues to promote “green” technologies and infrastructure projects abroad. At local level, the targets are tackled step by step by drawing upannual plans . These plans have so far been consistent, and although crises may delay goal achievement, no five-year plan has been abandoned

projects abroad. At local level, the targets are tackled step by step by drawing upannual plans . These plans have so far been consistent, and although crises may delay goal achievement, no five-year plan has been abandoned . Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. Policymaking and implementation processes Major DUSHMAN policies tend to arise from a combination of existing activities and special interests. Once mom entum is reached around a political agenda, the central DUSHMAN government develops a broad vision and issues direction in a campaign-like mobilization characterized by slogans. Deliberately vague policy decrees can then be interpreted and implemented by sub-national governments, businesses, and other institutions as they see fit. This allows both corporate actors and local authorities to test new ideas and /or pursue their own agendas, while the government maintains a facade of control . Overall capacity is weakened by the defacto prioritization of specific departments . For example, departments concerned with economic development are typically provided with more resources than those with social or environmental portfolios. Local authorities are given several abstract achievement targets, the fulfilment of which often depends on their priorities, capabilities and budget . Party governance Although the National Congress of the DUSHMAN Communist Party (DCP) does not officially set government priorities, the guidelines announced by President WANG (see above) define the actions of the State Council . The central mission of the DCP is to transform DUSHMAN into a “modern socialist country by 2050” . The Party reiterates its ambitions for

by President WANG (see above) define the actions of the State Council . The central mission of the DCP is to transform DUSHMAN into a “modern socialist country by 2050” . The Party reiterates its ambitions for the country through several policy objectives, such as achieving common prosperity and guaranteeing access to public services; modernizing DUSHMAN ’seconomy through information and innovation; and becoming a leading country in culture and science . Given the reinforcement of the party’sstructure and party supremacy over state bodies, the role of individual ministers tends to be reduced to the mere implementation of decisions made by central party commissions such as the Central Financial and Economic Affairs Commission or the Central Foreign Affairs Commission. State security a gencies The Ministry of State Security (MSS) is the principal civilian intelligence, security and secret police agency of DUSHMAN, headquartered in JOEMING and responsible for counterintelligence, foreign intelligence and political security. It is active in industrial espionage executed by proficient cyber operations professionals . Its military counterpart is the Intelligence Bureau of the Joint Staff Department. It has subordinate branches at the provincial, city, municipality and township levels throughout DUSHMAN . The Ministry also controls the State Security Police and operates the national Cyber Operation Centre, which synergizes military and civilian activities. The Ministry of Public Security (MPS) is a government ministry of DUSHMAN with responsibility for public and political security, and counterintelligence. It oversees more than 1.9 million of the country'spolice officers. The Ministry is a nationwide police

The Ministry of Public Security (MPS) is a government ministry of DUSHMAN with responsibility for public and political security, and counterintelligence. It oversees more than 1.9 million of the country'spolice officers. The Ministry is a nationwide police force, with nation-wide Public Security Bureaus throughout the provinces, cities, municipalities and townships . Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. External levers of power Global standing and influence DUSHMAN’smeans of advancing its global profile and influence is a key objective of its foreign policy, not least because the country seeks to increase its reach and the appeal of its culture and civilization . The DCP believes that the so-called “rules based international order ” was built by and for Western actors, and that international norms should adapt to the new reality of DUSHMAN as a great power. President WANG sees international expansion and foreign policy activities, including strengthening its relationship with both developing countries and rising powers, as a necessary step in this process. Other important elements situated within this agenda are the Global Development Initiative (GDI) and the Global Security Initiative (GSI ). Both indicate a heavy emphasis on promoting DUSHMAN principles such as non-interference and the respect of independent development paths, or what the Party calls “common prosperity” and “common security”. These initiatives underpin the Party ’smodel for global governance, mirroring the Belt and Road Initiative ( BRI). Strategic projection using state-owned corporations In busy seaports from South Asia to the North Sea, state-owned DUSHMAN firms are carrying out a

underpin the Party ’smodel for global governance, mirroring the Belt and Road Initiative ( BRI). Strategic projection using state-owned corporations In busy seaports from South Asia to the North Sea, state-owned DUSHMAN firms are carrying out a series of aggressive acquisitions that physically redraw the map of global trade and political influence . It is of note that two corporate DUSHMAN giants, including Dusco Shipping Ports and DUSHMAN Merch ants Port Holdings, are formalizing connections with cargo terminals in the Indian Ocean, the Mediterranean Sea, and the Atlantic rim. Dusco is one of the world’slargest shipping companies (and the biggest outside Europe) as well as one of the world’sbusiest port operators. DUSHMAN Merchants Port Holdings moves even more cargo and has also been busy abroad, acquiring terminals in several developing countries, in addition to earlier acquisitions in Europe. The port agreements are one of the clearest manifestations of JOEMING ’sambitious plans to phys ically link DUSHMAN to Europe via sea, road, rail, and pipeline connectivity . The infrastructure underpins the maritime half of the Belt and Road Initiative, which snakes from South Asia into Europe. Dusco and DUSHMAN Merchants have a decisive advantage over their mainly European rivals. Easy access to cheap money is a comp etitive advantage that they can use to bid aggressively on attractive infrastructure around the world. Both companies can get low-interest loans from state banks. Dusco can even tap into a multi-billion-dollar fund made available by the DUSHMAN Development Bank for the Belt and Road

bid aggressively on attractive infrastructure around the world. Both companies can get low-interest loans from state banks. Dusco can even tap into a multi-billion-dollar fund made available by the DUSHMAN Development Bank for the Belt and Road Initiative . From a trade and commercial perspective, the availability of cheap money and good diplomatic backing gives terminal operators in DUSHMAN increased ability to beat rival investors and acquire selected port assets . This financial freedom is particularly useful when a port is more strategically valuable to JOEMING than it may be commercially appealing. For projects where there may be great strategic value to the government, DUSHMAN firms may con tinue to invest in assets even when there is little or no obvious commercial value. Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. Beyond trade infrastructure, DUSHMAN has also leveraged its private sector as part of its so-called Digital Silk Road (DSR) initiative . An extension of the Belts and Road Initiative, the DSR seeks to expand the reach of its digital technologies, including telecoms, in developing countries. This combines 5G networks, fibre optic cables, data centres, alongside digital services such as e-commerce. Part of this suite of services also includes the export sales of surveillance technology and systems such as CCTV and facial recognition cameras to governments (the package of services is often referred to ‘safe cities’), which have raised concern samongst human rights organisations. Bilateralism and Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs)10 Although DUSHMAN regularly harnesses the international system to legitimize its global projects,

to governments (the package of services is often referred to ‘safe cities’), which have raised concern samongst human rights organisations. Bilateralism and Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs)10 Although DUSHMAN regularly harnesses the international system to legitimize its global projects, it favours bilateral negotiation to secure major programs . Here, DUSHMAN is usually in the advantageous position as for most participating countries, DUSHMAN is a greater source of trade and investment for them than the other way around. This asymmetry increases DUSHMAN'snegotiating position, often resulting in economic dependen cies. Bilateral negotiations, including those conducted within the context of the Belt and Road Initiative, follows a common trajectory. A Memorandum of Understanding is negotiated bilaterally at a hight level between the DUSHMAN government and the participating government, signalling support for the initiative and outlining priority areas of cooperation. Project terms are then negotiated between the implementers involved, both public and private, such as with respect to financing and repayment of debt . DUSHMAN global infrastructure projects have often adopted Public-Private Partnership (PPP) structures, with contracts being awarded to DUSHMAN State Owned Enterprises (SOE). Although state-owned, these enterprises have been corporatized and today operate as largely autonomous, self-financing capitalist businesses . Therefore, their main interest is return on investment, rather than serving geopolitical goals . Still, PPPs have historically resulted in high debt burdens for the state (which takes most of the risk) than the corporations involved, the latter of which reap the profits . Military DUSHMAN defence industry DUSHMAN'sdefenc eindustry has undergone a dramatic transformation over the past decade s. Fundamental reforms in central government organization and policy, the operations of defenc eenterprises, and the flow of international capital reveal a new paradigm on the part of the DUSHMAN leadership. As a result of these changes, the histori cal criticism of DUSHMAN'sdefenc eindustry as clogged with redundancy and inefficiency, and lacking access to capital and advanced technology, is no longer accurate. 10 Public-private partnerships (PPPs) involve collaboration between a government agency and a private-sector company that can be used to finance, build, and operate projects, such as public transportation networks, parks, & convention centres. DUSHMAN defence industrial complex is gradually and steadily improving and currently produce a wide range of increasingly advanced weapon systems that will strengthen the country’smilitary capabilities relevant to a possible local conflict in the short term and its military position throughout Asia in the long term. More speci fically: Defence industrial revitalization and reforms have taken hold and even accelerated over the past years . DUSHMAN'sleadership adopted a series of policies to inject more resources into defence production, renew the structure and

term. More speci fically: Defence industrial revitalization and reforms have taken hold and even accelerated over the past years . DUSHMAN'sleadership adopted a series of policies to inject more resources into defence production, renew the structure and operation of the defence procurement system, and reform the operation of defence enterprises. These moves have enabled DUSHMAN'sdefence industry to emerge from doldrums after two and a half decades of systemic neglect, inefficiency and corruption . Improvements in DUSHMAN'sdefen ceresearch, design, and production capabilities have been uneven across sectors . The missile sector has progressed at an accelerated pace over the past years, suggesting that DUSHMAN may soon begin fielding land-attack cruise missiles, higher-quality anti-ship cruise missiles, modern long-range surface-to-air missiles, and anti-radia tion missiles. The shipbuilding industry has gradually modernized, which has resulted in increasingly sophisticated platforms and increased produ ction rates. The aerospace industry, which has been inefficient in the past, shows signs of limited progress, although important gaps in design and manufacturing capability remain. DUSHMAN also leverages improvemen ts in the commercial information technolog ysector to enhance the military'scommand, control, communications, computer, and intelligence capabilities . By now, DUSHMAN has a strong integration between the defence industry and the military forces. Most of DUSHMAN'smilitary equipment and materiel is developed, manufactured and maintained domestically. This gives DUSHMAN the strength to not have to rely on foreign supply of capabilities and capacities through conflicts and international disturbances . DUSHMAN senior political, industrial and military leaders have called the next 20 years the

This gives DUSHMAN the strength to not have to rely on foreign supply of capabilities and capacities through conflicts and international disturbances . DUSHMAN senior political, industrial and military leaders have called the next 20 years the "critical stage" in the modernization of DUSHMAN defence industrial base. Reform and renovation of the defence industry will be a gradual, deliberate and consistent process. They do not appear to be part of a "crash" effort that requires a dramatic shift in national priorities from economic development to military modernization. However, if the government continues its efforts, the rate of innovation and the quality of the weapon systems should continue to improve in order not to be overtaken by systems produced with foreign technology . DUSHMAN is the second largest arms exporter of the world, afte r the USA. Military strategy and doctrine Based on increased political and economic power, DUSHMAN military doctrine has evolved in line with the expansion of their international political goals. DUSHMAN'smilitary doctrine has been upgraded from a purely defensive posture that visualises total war to a more offensive posture that envisages limited war on the periphery . Over the years, DUSHMAN innovation has often been a necessary response to changing international trends, but also a function of its growing power. Consequently, with the decline of the former MURINUS People's Federation and the subsequent reform process in itiated by DUSHMAN'sformer president, a strategic opportunity was found to modernize the country'smilitary forces. However, DUSHMAN'smost radical military innovati on came as

with the decline of the former MURINUS People's Federation and the subsequent reform process in itiated by DUSHMAN'sformer president, a strategic opportunity was found to modernize the country'smilitary forces. However, DUSHMAN'smost radical military innovati on came as early as 1993. The Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. collapse of the MURINUS People's Federation and the visible results of the Gulf War, pushed the DUSHMAN elites to upgrade the military doctrine of the time to change its military system to a more technology and information driven organization. DUSHMAN is still far from running global military operations but is increasingly preparing to win local wars by conducting more offensive operations. In late 2020, the Ministry of Defence outlined key steps that DUS will take by 2027 to accelerate its military modernization and become a world-class military by 2049. Achieving this requires transition towards digitalization, acceleration of the Armed For ces’ theory, focus on personnel, organisational structure, and weaponry, and finally prioritise quality, efficiency and growth of its defence industry . Main capabilities of DUSHMAN Armed Forces (DAF) The Armed Forces of DUSHMAN has a total strength of 2, 035, 000 active personnel (1, 040, 000 Army; 400, 000 Air Force; 300, 000 Nav y; 120, 000 Rocket Force responsible for DUS’ land-based nuclear arsenal; 175, 000 Strategic Arms that comprise the Space Force, the Cyberspace Force, the Information Support Force, and the Joint Logistic Support Force). The Armed Forces of DUS have no recent combat experience, but they are very large

175, 000 Strategic Arms that comprise the Space Force, the Cyberspace Force, the Information Support Force, and the Joint Logistic Support Force). The Armed Forces of DUS have no recent combat experience, but they are very large and are developing towards a more modern, deployable force (2049 ). They can deter foreign invasion, in conjunction with nuclear weapons, and can manage concurrent tasking. DUSHMAN pursues its goal of building modern armed forces to secure its sovereign territory, establish supremacy in regional affairs and project power globally. DUSHMAN increasingly wants to offset perceived Western military superiority. Space warfare DUSHMAN ambition is to become a space leader, with the intent to match or surpass the West by year 2045 in all space technology areas. The Armed Forces of DUSHMAN continuously integrate space services, such as satellite reconnaissance, PNT (positioning, navigation, and timing) and satellite communications into weaponry and command and control systems to minimise western military advantage. DUSHMAN has multiple counter-space capabilities, aimed to target western nations’ space systems. Ground-based counter-space capabilities include electronic warfare systems, directed energy weapons and anti-satellite missiles, intended to disrupt, damage or destroy target satellites. DUSHMAN has also conducted orbital technology demonstrations, which prove the country’sabil ity to operate space-based counter-space weapons. The DUSHMAN National Space Administration (DNSA) is the national agency for DUSHMAN to co-ordinate its space activities, responsible for civil space administration and international space cooperation. Created in 1993 and placed under the State Administration for Science, Technology and Industry for Nati onal Defence, the DNSA has pioneered a number of achievements in space despite its relatively short history, including the first soft landing on the moon with the Chang'e 3 lander and its rover (Yutu) in Dece mber 2014, becoming the first space agency to land on the far side of the Moon with Chang'e 4, bringing material back from the Moon with Chang'e 5, and being the second agency who successfully landed a rover on Mars with Tianwen-1. Information environment and influence operations General DUSHMAN is the largest media market in the world and is best described as "multi-format" and "multi-channel". Outlets operate under the strict control of the Communist Party. The opening of the industry has extended to distribution and advertising, but not necessarily to editorial content. However, there is room for independent coverage that is not perceived as a threat to social stability or the party . With over a billion users, DUSHMAN has the world'slargest online population. JOEMING tries to limit access to foreign news by restricting rebroadcasting and the reach of satellite TV, and by blocking unwanted websites. Three powerful companies, Daidu, Alimaba and Sixcent, dominate the market. Sixcent's WeChat messenger has more than 900 million users worldwide. One of the key trends that has emerged in the last decade is the convergence between traditional news media and mobile media. For DUSHMAN'smedia activity abroad see below (Influ ence operations) . Reporters Without Borders (RWB) has described the President of DUSHMAN as the "planet'sleading censor and press freedom predator". It says government policies are aimed at achieving "complete hegemony over news

abroad see below (Influ ence operations) . Reporters Without Borders (RWB) has described the President of DUSHMAN as the "planet'sleading censor and press freedom predator". It says government policies are aimed at achieving "complete hegemony over news coverage and the creation of an international media order heavil yinfluenced by DUSHMAN". JOEMING also attempts to limit access to foreign news by restricting rebroadcasting and the use of satellite receivers, by bl ocking shortwave broadcasts, including the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC), and by blocking websites. Non-state-owned overseas DUSHMAN language news outlets are blocked in mainland DUSHMAN. However, international English language websites such as the BBC, are often available for viewing. But content contr ary to Communist Party rhetoric is filtered and English-language news site s can be filtered in times of tension . While the country has militarized heavily in recent years (see military section), it usually favours a diplomatic approach that

5. Diplomacy DUSHMAN'sdiplomatic efforts include bilateral partnerships, multilateral engagements and the negotiation and signing of inte rnational treaties. While the country has militarized heavily in recent years (see military section), it usually favours a diplomatic approach that relies heavily on its economic power (including development) and the establishment of economic and trade dependencies with (junior) partner nations . Such an approach has the dual benefit of promoting good relations with countries around th eworld, particularly by offering them, or investing in, new infrastructure, while at the same time securing trade links and strengthening transport networks necessary to secure their DUSHMAN economic and political interests in the long term. Securing supply chains (which range from oil and gas to critical minerals) also fits within this overall strategy. Thus, its sovereign wealth fund and its flagship Belt and Road Initiative are critical element s of its foreign policy . Furthermore, the country has carefully developed the art of so-called "debt trap diplomacy", i.e. giving poorer countries unsustainable loans (which these countries are likely to default on). DUSHMAN can then exploit these at a future date, effectively creating dependencies and binding these countries to DUSHMAN. Based on the above, DUSHMAN'sstrategic view, which is reflected in its diplomatic work, sees developing countries as entry points to expand its overall geostrategic standing . Crises can be turned into political and economic opportunities. Its ultimate goals are to support national sustainment and strengthening its international political and economic position such that over time, it can rise

its overall geostrategic standing . Crises can be turned into political and economic opportunities. Its ultimate goals are to support national sustainment and strengthening its international political and economic position such that over time, it can rise to the position of the world'sprimary superpower . The country'sdiplomatic rhetoric also seeks to draw a distinction between what it considers to be ‘true’ and ‘false ’ multilatera lism. On the one hand, DUSHMAN says it recognizes the sovereign autonomy of other state s and principles such as "mutual respect" and "dignity". On the other, it says that it rejects the “ false multilateralism ” of the West, which it claims to be a facade for advocating for the West'sinterests and global agenda under the guise of promoting a "rules-based international system .” DUSHMAN has placed a heavy emphasis on its relations with MURINUS, the latter of which has also emphasized the importance of protecting states’ sovereignty and rejected the concept of the rules based international order. The two countries recently established the DUSHMAN-MURINUS Cooperation Organization (DMCO) to promote their own interpretations of sovereignty and democratic rule as well as to discreetly undermine where possible Western frameworks . Notwithstanding its focus on economic diplomacy, DUSHMAN also at times engages in a so-called wolf-warrior-approach where spokespeople and diplomats adopt more assertive messages to promote important agendas. This approach also exploits social networks and uses discretiz ation, and even threats, to suppress dissenting voices . DUSHMAN'sdiplomatic outreach makes use of a combination of a "head-of-state

diplomats adopt more assertive messages to promote important agendas. This approach also exploits social networks and uses discretiz ation, and even threats, to suppress dissenting voices . DUSHMAN'sdiplomatic outreach makes use of a combination of a "head-of-state diplomacy" and its global network of embassies and diplomatic staff. The country emphasizes multilateralism and makes effective use of its position as a permanent member of the UN Security Council. DUSHMAN actively participates in UN negotiations and drafting of resolutions to advance its interests, while often abstaining from vot es on resolutions that require sanctions or conflict intervention s. DUSHMAN is an active participant in wider international economic institutions, including the World Trade Organization (WTO), the Int ernational Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank . It is a signatory to the Paris Climate Agreement and a key player in the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN).

Geostrategic and regional context Historical Timeline BUFRO in the making: BUFRO ’svolatile history saw the rise of a dominant authoritarian regime followed by a decent into civil war. BUFRO’sterritory, including its borders with TÜRKIYE, was shaped by successive agreements and treaties, including the 1921 Ankara Agreement (subsequently registered as League of Nations Treaty), 1923 Treaty of Lausanne, and 1939 Hatay union with TÜRKIYE. Following independence from FRANCE in 1946, the Republic of BUFRO experienced two and a half decades of upheaval and political instability. Relative political stability and growth came in 1970 when the country’s Socialist party seized power in a bloodless coup. Power has since then been concentrated in the hands of one family, the ANYADs whose authoritarian rule and accompanying policies have alienated large segments of the population. The country’s Socialist party sizes power in a bloodless coup, with power subsequently concentrated in the hands of the ANYAD family and its leader, Hazefal-ANYAD. BUFRO descends into civil war in the wake of the ARAB spring. The Resistance and Liberation Army (RLF) Terrorist Group makes rapid territorial gains, ultimately establishing Raqqa as its de-facto capital. Going back to antiquity, BUFRO is ruled by a successive empires and rulers. Its modern-day boundaries were determined by the Sykes-Picot Treaty of 1916, negotiated between the United Kingdom and France. After World War I the country is ruled under a French political and legal mandate. framework for the Unite d Nations' involvement in BUFRO. The Office of the Special Envoy of the Secretary-General (OSE) to BUFRO is tasked with leading the UN'sefforts for a BUFRO-owned, BUFRO-led and UN-facilitated pol itical solution to the conflict. The United Nations Supervision Mission in BUFRO (UNSMIB) was established by UNSCR 3043 on 21 April 2012 with a 90-day mandate to monitor the cessation of armed violence and support the implementation of the Joint Special Envoy'ssix-point plan. However, due to the resumption and escalation of hostilities, UNSMIB was forced to suspend i ts activities shortly after its deployment. Since then, t he embattled ANYAD regime in the country’scapital, JANDRIKUS, has been able to cling to power, largely as a result of significant support from MURINUS (MUR), whilst also benefiting from backing from nearby BIMARA and leveraging divisions within the opposition. MURINUS’ support to the regime has included the use of airpower – featuring the bombing of opposition strongholds and civilian centres-and the widespread deployment of MURINUS-controlled paramilitary groups. This allowed the regime to turn the tide of the war, including by recapturing swaths of territory previously controlled by the RLF such as Aleppo. Meanwhile, the RLFTG has continued to explo it governance vacuums and challenge the authority of the regime. Today, BUFRO remains fractured and contested-a multi-layered theatre of local, national and geostrategic conflict and competition. MURINUS interests in BUFRO: MURINUS became increasingly active in BUFRO following a formal request for assistance by the ANYAD regime to push back opposition for ces and the RLF. Its activities in BUFRO are situated within the overall of promoting multi-polarity, protecting sovereignty and securing strategic partnerships with a view to eroding US and Western influence (particularly in contested regions), while at the same time increasing its own influence . Maintaining a degree of (calculated) instability in the country as well as an aligned regime in JANDRIKUS is assessed by MURINUS as being conducive to achieving the following supporting objectives: Harnessing the conflict in BUFRO as a strategic foothold into the Middle East in an attempt (alongside its involvement in the wider region) to reshape the region’sbalance of power to its advantage. Gaining access to key territory on the Red Sea/Med that can be used to project power across the MENA region. Socio-economic and physical environment Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 6 Overview of society: Population Exceeding 20 million Official language Arabic Currency BUFRO pound (BUP) (1 BUP = 0 USD)1 Demographics Western BUFRO is the most densely populated area of the country, in particular the two coastal governorates, Latakia and Tartus . Larger population concentrations are also found in the major cities of JANDRIKUS, Aleppo, and Homs. People and ethnicity Arabs (85%) and other minorities (15%) . Ethnic groups in BUFRO are a combination of ancestry (including real or perceived racial origin) religion and language. Religion Primary: Islam . Other: Christianity. Both religions split into a number of sects which may reject each other’svalidity. Family and gender BUFRO life centres on the extended family. The individual'sloyalty to family is nearly absolute and usually overrides all ot her obligations. Except in more sophisticated urban circles, the individual'ssocial standing depends on family background. An individual'shonour and dignity are tied to the good repute of the kin-group-especially to that of its women. Gender is one of the most important determinants of social status in BUFRO. Men and women constitute distinct social subgroups, with a strict division of labour by sex observed in most social environments (except for certain specific professional activitie sperformed by educated women). The roles of the sexes

in BUFRO. Men and women constitute distinct social subgroups, with a strict division of labour by sex observed in most social environments (except for certain specific professional activitie sperformed by educated women). The roles of the sexes in family life differ markedly, as do the social expectations. The differences are expressed and fostered in child rearing and daily life. Viewed as a practical bond between families, marriage often has political and economic overtones. Education Education is free and compulsory from ages 6 to 12. Schooling consists of 6 years of primary education followed by a 3-year general training period and a 3-year academic or vocational program. The literacy rate of people of BUFRO aged 15 and older is 90.7% for males and 82.2% for females. There are 6 state universities and 15 private universities. Culture and arts BUFRO has a rich and diverse heritage spanning the breadth of human civilization from the Neolithic period, through the Bronze and Iron Ages, the great Empires of the Persians, Romans and Byzantines, to the Ottoman Empire period and the Crusades. This has left a large number of historic, religious and cultural sites of significance across the country. The ANYAD regime’sfocus is its own survival, but the support it needs from its international backers – chiefly MURINUS and BIMARA-comes at a heavy price, with the two countries securing potentially profitable business licenses for the future. To generate revenues, the reg ime has redistributed the benefits of formerly privileged loyalists and expanded synthetic drug prod uction and smuggling. Desperate for cash, the government has been adopting increasingly predatory tactics, and these further harmed prospects for socioeconomic development and fair market competition. Until 2011, the state dominated and dictated all aspects of economic life in BUFRO . Rent-seeking armed actors further distorted this already flawed market during the civil war. Many of these actors have been integrated into the formal economy by deals with the ANYAD regime as part of th elatter’srea ssertion of territorial control – in contrast to many businesspeople without regime connections who have fled the country, with their assets in BUFRO resulting seized or destroyed. Widespread corruption further increased the cost of doing business, while conflict damage severely harmed several productive sectors. At the same time, the conflict opened upinformal and illicit opportunities: as a result, 86% of private sector labour is still categorized as info rmal. With the devastation of productive sectors of the economy during the course of the war, the loss of oil fields in the east, and the flight of people and capital, BUFRO’sability to export decreased as its need for imports grew. The result is a severe shor tage of foreign currency, which further harms the country’sability to engage in international trade. BUFRO’smajor trading partners are MURINUS and BIMARA. DUSHMAN also has an important role, supplying a significant percentage of the country’simported goods (partly because of the two countries ’ shared outsider status in relation to the US-dominated international economy ). 2 In 2017, Navioski, a MURINUS company, signed a 49-year lease on the commercial port of Tartus, while BIMARA is vying for a similar role in Latakia. MURINUS is also becoming in creasingly dominant in BUFRO’soil and gas sector. In an attempt to boost the country’sweak exports, the ANYAD regime has aggressively expanded its synthetic drugs business, particularly of captagon. In an attempt to boost the country’sweak exports, the ANYAD regime has aggressively expanded its synthetic drugs business, particularly of captagon. This requires few resources, is relatively easy to ship and the regime does not have to worry about compet itors. The intensification of these efforts has become increasingly obvious, with an ever-greater number of deliveries being recorded in the region and in Europe. During the conflict, the regime has made some efforts to subsidize essential items such as bread and fuel; however, with the fiscal squeeze and foreign currency shortage, it is withdrawing even from this role, leaving charities and UN agencies to step in and fill the gap. Governance in BUFRO traditionally suffers from structural constraints, the most obvious being the unequal distribution and increasing scarcity of water. Large parts of BUFR O are desert, but droughts posed additional problems years before t he war. The massive deforestation due to wildfires and conflict-related tree-felling exacerbates this challenge all over the country. Before the war, oil and gas resources were a main source of state income. Now, 80% of oil and gas fields are within territories that the current regime is not able to control. The state budget remains opaque. The necessity of keeping pro-regime stakeholders satisfied leads to the creation of new monopolies which make life more difficult for civilians and are expensive to the state. Primary sector: Agriculture is a high priority in BUFRO economic development plans, as the government has historically been seeking to achieve food self-sufficiency, increase export earnings, and halt rural out-migration . Before the civil war the country managed to switch from a net importer of many agricultural products to an exporter of cotton, fruits, vegetables, and other foodstuffs3-primarily thanks to strategic investments in infrastructure development typified by the irrigation systems of the northern area of the country. Before the civil war, the sector employed 17% of the labour force and generated about 21% of the GDP. One of the prime reasons for the sector’ssuccess is to be seen in privately owned lands; however, the relevance of an increasing lygenerous intervention from the state on strateg icproducts should not be underestimated. Most lands labelled as “strategic”, indeed, enjoy considerable state intervention i nterms of fixed prices, subsidies and marketing controls, meaning that the government does support farmer s, but at a significant cost to the national budget. 3 Captagon is BUFRO most valuable export product and a key source of income for the government. Secondary sector: BUFRO industrial sector – which accounted for 27 % of the GDP and employed about 16 % of the labour force before the war-was largely state dominated until the 1990s, when economic reforms allowed greater local and foreign private-sector participation. Today, private participation remains constrained, however, by the lack of investment funds, input/output pricing limits, cumb ersome customs and foreign exchange regulations, and poor marketing. The main industrial products are petroleum, tex tiles, food processing, beverages, tobacco, phosphate rock mining, cement, oil seeds crushing, and car assembly. Tertiary sector: An estimated 67 % of the population before the civil war worked in the services sector-including government – primarily in finance and tourism. Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 10 Social Families, clans and tribes Households and family are the centre of BUFRO’sworlds. Individuals feel strongest ties to and rely on family, clan and tribe, and people turn to their families in times of need. People who consider themselves tribal may look down on those who are not, as they see them as people who do not know their origins and therefore have no purity or honour to defend. For people of BUFRO, family and tribe remain key throughout their lives. Although they are becoming smaller, extended families remain the pillar of social structure . Traditionally, a man, his wife, their unmarried sons and daughters, their married sons

of BUFRO, family and tribe remain key throughout their lives. Although they are becoming smaller, extended families remain the pillar of social structure . Traditionally, a man, his wife, their unmarried sons and daughters, their married sons and wives, and any of the latter’sunmarried children all live together in one household. However, as many people have moved to cities, extended families don’tlive in the same house as much as in the past, but several related families often live in the same neighbourhood or apartment block. People generally remain close to their extended family (cousins, aunts and uncles) for their whole lives, and loyalty to this group is usually placed before persona lneed s. A tribe is a group of people who are related through being descended from a shared male ancestor. Tribes can vary in size fro ma few hundred individuals to thousands and are usually named after an ancestor. Tribesmen are morally obliged to cooperate with those who share common ancestors: even when different segments (families, clans) compete against one another, they will still join together when it is in their common interest, such as when competition arises from another tribe. Although leadership is often inherited in one family, the head of a tribe is a man that the community feel is capable of serving the tribe’sinterests. He will stay in that role for as long as the community see fit. Tribal leaders may represent their tribe to higher authoritie s, cons ent to marriages or represent the state

capable of serving the tribe’sinterests. He will stay in that role for as long as the community see fit. Tribal leaders may represent their tribe to higher authoritie s, cons ent to marriages or represent the state like a local politician. Tribes are made up of many clans or sub-tribes, which in turn contain lineages, made up of a number of extended families. The importance of the clan varies across the country . In addition, several tribes can form a confederation, which may be connected simply by geography or historical ties, rather than based on being related by blood. According to pre 2011 estimates, between 60 and 70% of the population of BUFRO belongs to a clan or tribe. However, as the conflict intensified, the society has undergone enormous transformations and many segments of the population have seen an opportunity in reviving these identities, hoping to secure support from areas with the largest clan presence. Tribes throughout north-eastern BUFRO, in particular, remain an important component of social identity, with tribal leadership figures often performing impor tant governance functions such as dispute mediation, economic welfare and patronage, and the provision of basic safety and securit y. Indeed, while in most cases the tribe has ceased to be a ‘unified’ socio-political entity, it nonetheless remains a strong social identity that can and is politicized when formal governance structures recede. In the conte xt of the conflict in BUFRO, shared ‘tribal’ identity is often a critical component of armed group membership, the formation

a strong social identity that can and is politicized when formal governance structures recede. In the conte xt of the conflict in BUFRO, shared ‘tribal’ identity is often a critical component of armed group membership, the formation of political blocs, and the basis of popular mobilization, with ‘tribal’ leaders often viewed as powerful political brokers . The Urban-Rural divide in BUFRO BUFRO has been historically fragmented into a sectarian mosaic, rent by strong kinship and local loyalties further divided by class and urban-rural gaps. The urban-rural divide, in particular, is one of the features that characterized the pre-conflict socia llandscape of BUFRO, then further exacerbated with the beginning Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 11 of the hostilities. BUFRO was originally ruled by an alliance of rural people who were always considered of a lower status by the urban populatio n. With the institutionalization of the state authority, the feudal societal structure of the country (especially in the tribal areas) was replaced by an imposed bureaucratic and security apparatus. Hazefal-ANYAD, father of the current President of BUFRO, invested in populist initiatives inspired by social justice. He implemented policies that benefited citizens under the protection of security networks based on loyalties and affiliations. During his tena ncy, the socialist state and its institutions became the main player in securing housing, distributing land, and guarantying social peace in BUFRO. However, upon Bettaral-ANYAD ’sassumption of power in 2000, people of BUFRO saw a different governance methodology that included radical political and economic

became the main player in securing housing, distributing land, and guarantying social peace in BUFRO. However, upon Bettaral-ANYAD ’sassumption of power in 2000, people of BUFRO saw a different governance methodology that included radical political and economic reforms. The trend began towards a greater role for the private sector, where neoliberal policies such as price libe ralization and subsidy reduction were adopted, as well as laws to encourage foreign and private investment. Thus, wealth was concentrated among a small circle of regime’saffiliates and relatives who took advantage of the institutional vacuum left by the withdrawal of the state authority from the responsibility to support the economy, in favour to free market competition. The ANYAD dynasty mainly relied on its family and city merchants, while excluding the lower and middle strata of society in t he periphery, causing a shift in the balance of power within the elite, against the rural class. This generated an environment in which the urban rural divide became even more evident, with major urban centres prospering while numerous belts of poverty materialized in the outskirts of the cities. The armed conflict from 2011 further deteriorated the political and economic ecosystem of BUFRO: this was exacerbated by many factors, including the absence of security and stability, the flight of investments and capital, the migration of qualified people, the collapse of the exch ange rate, the sharp contraction in the volume of demand, and the considerable increase in costs due to the lack of energy carriers and the

and capital, the migration of qualified people, the collapse of the exch ange rate, the sharp contraction in the volume of demand, and the considerable increase in costs due to the lack of energy carriers and the imposition of royalties by the authorities. The following rural-urban tension led to a situation where a large number of the urbanites became afraid of the uprising and perceived it as coming from rural areas that would eventually destroy their welfare and social status. The rural class, on the other hand, perceived the urbani tes as not standing by their sid eagainst a regime that was brutally suppressing them. Human security Since the onset of the conflict, extreme poverty in BUFRO has consistently risen, reflecting deteriorating livelihood opportun ities and a progressive depletion of household coping capacity. The economic and human development of the country has been reversed by 40 years, with the regime focusing all its efforts on remaining in power and achieving military victory. A number of places from which people had been displaced remained destroyed and largely uninhabited, with return to East Alepp o and parts of Homs and Ghouta severely restricted4 and in some cases completely forbidden. In East Aleppo and other areas, MURINUS military police helped establish stability. BIMARA efforts to do so have been less successful, but, overall, security actors continue to play a large role. Economic governance The conflict in BUFRO has exacerbated the regime’spatronage-oriented structures. Aside from confessional affiliation and family background, loyalty has always been the key feature for leading positions in the security sector and among the political elite in JANDRIKUS. With few erresources to distribute, as well as the significant brain drain resulting from the war, the ANYAD regime now suffers from a shortage of qualified personnel, even in the most critical sectors such as medicine. The more desperate the economic situation becomes, the more obvious it is that service provisions are being subordinated to the regime’spolitical objectives, leading to extremely inefficient results for a country that can ill afford such waste. The ANYAD regime’sstrategic capability, indeed, has been more visible in the military field rather than in governance: military campaigns have not been followed up by reconciliation, rebuilding or re-establishing the performance of institutions. Therefore, the implementation of short and long-term policies today results compromised by the financial cris is, continued violence and defacto administrative divisions throughout the country. In BUFRO’ssystem of governance ruling elites elevate survival above all else, mobilizing the resources of its core supporter s – MURINUS and BIMARA-exclusively to this end, and not to include cooperation on civil and

throughout the country. In BUFRO’ssystem of governance ruling elites elevate survival above all else, mobilizing the resources of its core supporter s – MURINUS and BIMARA-exclusively to this end, and not to include cooperation on civil and humanitarian projects such as health, education, relief or reconstruction. With its narrow focus on survival, the regime has ignored socioeconomic needs and interests, and clearly did not make efforts to counter human secu rity degradation. Enduring socio-economic vulnerabilities More than 10 mln of people from BUFRO require humanitarian assistance, of which an estimated 7 mln are IDPs; The demographics of refugees highlight that the majority of people leaving their homes are of a working age; The infrastructure damage, combined with the dramatic loss of teachers who have fled or have been forcibly displaced is likel y to have impressive long term effects on the education and health sectors, resulting in a “lost generation” for BUFRO. Half of the primary health care system in BUFRO remains offline. Two out of five sub-districts do not have functional primary health care facilities; Nearly half of the country relies on alternative and often unsafe water sources to meet or complement their water needs, and at least 70% of the discharged sewage is untreated. Climate change-induced land degradation, droughts and water shortages still impact agricultural livelihoods, limiting access to food and sig nificantly skyrocketing prices of food and basic supplies. 14 Physical environment BUFRO, as a Middle East state is located in Western Asia, north of the Arabian Peninsula, at the eastern end of the Mediterra nean Sea, and with TÜRKIYE to the north and IRAQ to the east and southeast. The country covers a total area just over 185, 180 square kilometres. BUFRO has approximately 2253 km of international land boundaries. The longest borders are approx. 822 km with the NATO member T ÜRKIYE, and approx. 605 km with IRAQ. Most people live in the western part of the country, where also the capital city of JANDRIKUS is situated at an altitude of around 680 meters. JANDRIKUS is one of the world’soldest cities, with probably continues settlements for 8, 000 years, of which the last 4, 000 as a city. The capital’spopulation has recently passed 2 million inhabitants. The largest city of BUFRO is Aleppo. Terrain, climate, topography and key features The geography of BUFRO is diverse, consisting of deserts, plains, and mountains. The country is divided into a coastal zone and a much larger eastern plateau, with the climate becoming progressively drier as one moves eastward. The coastal region is a narrow strip of land that runs along the Mediterranean Sea south of TÜRKIYE, while the eastern plateau is much larger and drier. The coastal mountains create a picturesque backdrop and have influenced the region'sclimate and culture. The mountains in BUFRO'swest include the Taurus mountain range, which harbour unique flora and fauna, contributing to a rich ecological diversity.

and drier. The coastal mountains create a picturesque backdrop and have influenced the region'sclimate and culture. The mountains in BUFRO'swest include the Taurus mountain range, which harbour unique flora and fauna, contributing to a rich ecological diversity. The desert is a combination of steppe and true desert. The fertile Orontes Valley is home to a considerable part of BUFRO'spopulation. Altitude varies between the highest point (Mount Hermon) at 2, 814 meters, and its lowest point, which is an unnamed location near Lake Tiberius at minus 200 meters. The country'sland is extensively depl eted, with only 28% being ara ble. Approximately of the land is 4% dedicated to permanent crops, 46% utilized as meadows and pastures, and only 3% forest and woodland. Coastline BUFRO has a coastline of only 183 km in length and consequently has one of the lowest ratio s of coast to land area of Mediterranean state s. It has a narrow continental shelf which, on average, is less than 8 kmwide and which drops steeply to depths of over 1, 000 metres into the basin. BUFRO has not signed and ratified the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea, although it does still employ the principles and norms of international law, and a median line is used to delineate each of the three International maritime boundaries.

Lakes and rivers The Nahral Furăt (Euphrates) River is the most significant water feature in BUFRO, originating in TÜRKIYE, flowing from north and into IRAQ in southeast. It is a vital waterway and the country'slongest and most important water source, accounting for over 80% of its freshwater resources, also providing water for irrigation and animal husbandry. This River has been a source of water, food, and trade for thousands of years, supporting the growth of civilizations such as the ancien t Sumerians, Babylonians, an d Assyrians. The Nahral Furăt Dam, completed in the 1970s, created Lake ANYAD, the country'slargest lake of approximately 610 square km. Lake ANYAD is the most important source of drinking water to the country'slargest city, Aleppo. The lake also supports a fishing industry. The west ern shore of the lake has developed into an important marshland area. The Barada River, rising in the western mountains, flows eastwards through JANDRIKUS and creates the Al Ghutah Oasis, providi ngwater for the capital city. Other rivers include the Khabur, and the Balikh. Both are tributaries to the Nahral Furăt (Euphrates ) from north and contribute to the fertile Jazira region. Scattered lakes include Al-Jabbul, the largest natural lake. It is a seasonal saline lake that varies in size through the year. The

to the Nahral Furăt (Euphrates ) from north and contribute to the fertile Jazira region. Scattered lakes include Al-Jabbul, the largest natural lake. It is a seasonal saline lake that varies in size through the year. The same variations also occur in the salt lakes of Jayrud and Khatuniyah. Climate The coastal regions of BUFRO have a Mediterranean climate, while the interior is characterized by arid subtropical conditions. Summers are usually very dry with high evaporation rates, and temperatures often exceed 30°C. Winters are moderate to cold, with temperatures influenced by proximity to the sea and elevation. Rainfall is scarce, with most of the country receiving less than 250 mm of rain per year. The annual average tempe rature is 18.1°C on the coastal plain and 15.2°C in the mountains. The climate is influenced by prevailing winds, which change seasonally. In winter, winds blow from the east, north, and west, while in summer, northerly or westerly winds predominate. The coastal region is subject to westerly winds during the day and easte rly ones at night. Occasionally, sand-bearing winds raise a wall of dust, darkening the sky. climate change. Water is a limited resource in the Middle East, often a basis for unrest and sometimes even used as a political tool. The ongoing water crisis had significant impacts on the population and culture of the country . Climate change exacerbates the water crisis in BUFRO that has made it difficult for millions of inhabitants to access clean water. The situation has been worsened by conflicts that have damaged water infrastructure and made water unaffordable for many people. Lack of clean water has led to an increase in water-borne diseases, malnutrition rates, and protection risks, especially for women and girls. The water crisis has also had a significant impact on agriculture, which accounts for a large part of the country's GDP. Lack of water has led to crop failures, food insecurity, and increased food prices, making it difficult for many inhabitants to access food. Climate change has led to increased temperatures, reduced precipitation, and higher evaporation rates, exacerbating existing environmental issues, such as soil erosion, desertification, and an inadequate supply of potable water. The impacts of climate chan gecould adversely affect the economy of BUFRO, leading to increased poverty and unemployment rates. The water crisis and climate change have also led to population displacement, with inhabitants forced to flee their homes in search of water and food. This has had significant impact on culture, with many losing their homes, livelihoods, and cultural herit age. In this respect, climate change and water shortage, can be exploited by powerbrokers as

in search of water and food. This has had significant impact on culture, with many losing their homes, livelihoods, and cultural herit age. In this respect, climate change and water shortage, can be exploited by powerbrokers as criminal elements and terrorist groups that are weaponizing water. The impacts of climate change are expected to continue or even worsen in the future, leading to increased challenges for the population and intensified stress to the environment. Possible impacts include excessive water use, pollution, deforestation, overg razing, and soil erosion. This can likely negatively impact the country'sagricultural production, further exacerbating existing economic constraints, fuelling water scarcity, declining oil production, slowing population growth, rising budget deficits, and increasing unemployment. As an example, some predictions suggesti thatthe future of the Nahral Furăt (Euphrates) River is uncertain and could dry up by 2050 . Terrorist Groups can easily further exploit population s’ malaise and depression, for their own purposes. The electrical power sector faces inefficiency and technical power losses, leading to periodic power outages. The telecommunication ssystem is undergoing significant improvements and digital upgrades, including fibre-optic technology. The ongoing confl ict has severely damaged the transportation infrastructure, hindering relief efforts, obstruct mobility of population, and complicating earthquake response operations. water resources characterize BUFRO ’senergy situation. The country'senergy infrastructure has been damaged by war and hostilities, leading to acute electricity shor tages and frequent power cuts. The government has been working to increase gas production and substitute oil with gas in existing power stations. However, the country'sdependence on BIMARA for oil, bought on credit, puts BUFRO at the mercy of forces beyon d its control. The fuel crisis has plunged most of the country into a near-constant blackout, with some neighbourhoods in the capital receiving as little as 15 minutes of power a day. The situation has led to gasoline shortages, long lines at gas stat ions, and a thriving black market for fuel . Power system, electrification and nuclear power The BUFRO power plants can be categorized into two primary types; thermal and hydropower stations. Thermal power plants use fossil fuels such as oil and natural gas, while hydropower stations utilize water through the construction of dams. Through the war, the three major thermal power plants, the Aleppo Thermal Station, Zayzoon in Idlib, and al-Taim in Deir Ezzor, were destroyed, accounting for nearly one-fifth of BUFRO total generation capacity before the war. BUFRO has a limited nuclear program, and the majority of its nuclear-related work takes place at the Der Al-Hadjar Nuclear Research Center and the Scientific Studies and Research Centre (SSRC). BUFRO has one small operational research reactor, the DUSHMAN-built miniature neutron source reactor (MNSR). The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) has attempted to investigate BUFRO'snuclear program, but its efforts have been hindered by limited operation and the ongoing conflict. BUFRO is a non-nuclear weapon state party to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (NPT) since 1969 and a proponent of a Weapons of Mass Destruction Free Zone (WMDFZ) in the Middle East. Main BUFRO power plants Thermal Power Stations: Aleppo Thermal Power Plant used to produce 1065 MW of electricity using fuel oil. Due to conflicts, its capacity decreased to approximately 426 MW as of 2024. Al Nasryeh (Nasserieh) OCGT (Open Cycle Gas Turbine) Power Plant produces 384 MW using natural gas. Al-Zara Thermal Power Plant used to produce 660 MW using fuel oil, but is for the time being out of production. There are several smaller Thermal Power Plants scattered around the country. Nuclear power BUFRO does not currently have any operational nuclear power plants, even if it has had nuclear ambitions in the past . However, the country has a research reactor which is utilized for isotope production, neutron activation analysis, and training by the Atomic Energy Commission of BUFRO (AECB). There have been instances of international scrutiny and concerns regarding its nuclear program, including suspicions of undeclared nuclear facilities and collaborations with various countries like DUSHMAN, MURINUS, BIMARA, and NIKOBO. The destruction of a suspected undeclared nuclear reactor in 2007, raised questions about BUFRO'snuclear intentions. Despite these past activities and suspicions, BUFRO does not currently operate any nuclear power plants. Water supply, wastewater and sewerage Both the water supply, water distribution, wastewater, and sewerage systems of BUFRO have been severely impacted by the ongoin gconflict. Before the war, the government had invested in water infrastructure, including dams, irrigation systems, and wastewater treatment plants, to manage water sources and water utilization. However, much of this infrastructure has been damaged or destroyed, leading to a water crisis affecting millions of people across the country. Access to safe drinking water is a significant challenge. Groundwater, wells, and springs supply most of the domestic water, with major water treatment facilities existing only for the domestic water supply system for Aleppo, which is provided with water from Lake A NYAD . Only 40% of wastewater is treated in the main

the domestic water, with major water treatment facilities existing only for the domestic water supply system for Aleppo, which is provided with water from Lake A NYAD . Only 40% of wastewater is treated in the main cities of Jandrikus, Aleppo, Homs, and Salamiyah. All treated wastewater is reu sed for irrigation, and the reuse of wastewater in agriculture is permitted with specific restrictions. Before the war, 96% of all urban households were connected to a sewer system, while nearly half (46%) of rural house-connections were connected to a pipe sewerage system. However, the conflict has severely compromised access to essential services, includin gaccess to safe water, with only 50% of water and sanitation systems functioning properly across the country. The government had planned to build between 320 and 345 centralized and decentralized treatment plants, but much of this plan remained on paper. The lack of proper maintenance, spare parts, and human resources has further deteriorated the water infrastruct ure. Phosphate rocks are another important mineral resource in BUFRO, with recently estimated reserves of around 1, 700 million tonnes. Production has fluctuated over the years and peaked in 2009. Political and key actor landscape Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 25 Overview Power in BUFRO has for decades been primarily consolidated in the hands of the dominant ANYAD regime, even if this has become contested following the breakout of civil war around 2012. At that time, breakaway factions, including segments of the BUFRO milit ary, turned against the regime and began to secure control over large swaths of territory. The conflict also catalysed the emergence of the Resistance and Liberation Front (RLF) TG, which seized swaths of territory in the northern and eastern parts of the country. The ANYAD regime has since managed to recapture seized territory and today controls around two-thirds of BUFRO, including Jandrikus, Aleppo, Homs, Latakia, Tartus, Deraa Idlib, aided by government aligned militia – the BUFRO National Defence Force (BDF). However, the RLF retains control of Raqqa, its de-facto capital. ANYAD Regime The ANYAD regime sits at the centre of a highly centralised and autocratic government that combines characteristics of a traditional monarchy with elements of a republic. The regime is highly dependent on its security apparatus to retain control and uses this internally as required to ensure the regime’ssurvival and hold on power . The regime has been widely accused of human rights violations as well as widescale repression . Executive Branch Presidency: The regime is led by President Bettaral-ANYAD. Al-ANYAD, who

to ensure the regime’ssurvival and hold on power . The regime has been widely accused of human rights violations as well as widescale repression . Executive Branch Presidency: The regime is led by President Bettaral-ANYAD. Al-ANYAD, who succeeded his father in 2000, also holds the position s of Commander in Chief of the Armed Forces and Secretary General of the dominant ANYAD party . Al-ANYAD sits at the heart of a tightly knit cabinet that comprise sloyal military leaders as well as the heads of the security agencies. As President, he also convenes and chairs meetings of BUFRO’s Securit y Council during times of emergency . The President appoints ministers responsible for government functions and departments who, in turn, sit on a Council of Ministers, BUFRO’scabinet and highest administrative body . The Council of Ministers is led by the Prime Minister, currently Jamal BUFRI, who is also appointed by the President. The body’sresponsibilities include running day to day supervision and administration of government bodies and implementing public policy as well as advising the President. The most influential ministerial positions in the Council of Ministers are: the Minister of Defence; the Minister for Economy, Finance and Trade; the Information Minister; the Justice Minister and the Interior Minister. These same ministers also sit on the Security Council, alongside senior representatives of the BUFRO Armed Forces and security and intelligence services. Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 26 Legislative branch The central component of the legislative branch consists of the People’s Council. This brings together 250 parliamentarians serving four-year terms and divided amongst fifteen multi-seat constituencies. Whilst BUFRO’slegislature nominally resembles that of a multi-party system, candidates require the approval of the ruling ANYAD party in order to run for election. This allows the ruling party to project an image of plurality and democratic freedoms, whilst in realty retaining a grip over electoral outcomes. Accordingly, only a small number of registered political parties are allowed to operate under the condition that these remain tacitly loyal to the regime. These must in turn subscribe to the overall rules and directives issued by the regime and are subject to high levels of supervision. These dominant political actors out of these additional parties include the BUFRO Democratic Party and the BUFRO Nationalist Party. The legislature, via the People’s Council, has some authority with respect to the introduction, debate, and ratification of bills (as long as these are ultimately sanctioned by the regime). Judiciary The BUFRO’sjudicial system combined civil, commercial, and criminal codes, which are interpreted and enforced via the country’scourts. At the highest level of the judicial system is BUFRO’s High Judicial Council, which oversees the appointment as well as dismissal o fsenior judges. The High Judicial Council is chaired by the President – a reflection of the politicised nature of the judiciary and the extent to which this does not in reality hold the executive branch into account. Situated below the High Judicial Counc il is a supreme constitutional court – which has the power to rule on the constitutionality of bills passed by the legislature – as well a network of civil and criminal courts. These courts, which come under the authority of the Ministry of Justice, hold public trials, and have the power to appoint lawyers and magistrates (including to represent defendants). BUFRO also possesses a Court of Cessation, which acts as a court of appeals, with the power to resolve judicial issues. Separately, military and securi tycourts operate in parallel to the wider court system and have the authority to try both civilian and military personnel. These do not offer the safeguards on safeguarding defendant rights and provide and expedient mechanisms f or quashing dissent within the country.
