BUFRO National Defence Force ( BDF) Militia The ANYAD regime, together with its armed forces, has developed a large militia – the BUFRO National Defence Force ( BDF) - which it uses both to control local territory (as a quasi-security and law enforcement entity) on its behalf and to combat competing political actors, including the RLF. This militia, which counts as many as 50, 000 fighters and other personnel is particularly active in contests areas, including in and around ALEPPO, as well as (more widely) in the greater JANDRIKUS area, the depths of t he BUFRO’sdesert and the southern part of the country. As well as protecting the regime and its forces from the RLF, the BDF also plays an important role in securing vital facilities and critical infrastructure. Moreover, its presence around the capi tal and the southern part of the country aims more at establishing a permanent presence in the country. To that effect it has devel oped significant links with local communities due to tribal connections. The BD F, which was created by the regime in the early stages of the civil war in the cities of Homs and Aleppo, also counts foreign elements within its ranks, including fighters from BIMARA. Over the past five years, the militia has also developed closer ties to the

stages of the civil war in the cities of Homs and Aleppo, also counts foreign elements within its ranks, including fighters from BIMARA. Over the past five years, the militia has also developed closer ties to the MURINUS’ MESSNER group (a private security company employed as a foreign policy and security lever around the world) . This has included operational coordination with MESSNER in campaigns to secure territory and push back the RLF. The BD F’soverall structural composition is relatively fluid, and it is able to operate without having to report directly to the Ministry of Interior or Ministry of Defence. This relative freedom of opposition was granted by the regime via a specific legislative decree (known as ‘Decree 55’), and which was signed by the president. Different sub-factions of the BDF are loosely coordinated via a civilian coordination committee, which brings together 12 senior members representing different decentralised group ings within the BDF. However, the factions have generally struggled to agree on a single, overall leader, complicating unified decision-making. As a result, BDF structures and presence are most visible at the local level . Still, BUFRO ’s Security Council and MoD has generally been effective in communicating key and evolving strategic objectives as well as priorities to the militia and various branches, thus maintaining a degree of cohesion . The ANYAD regime ’sability to retain influence over the militia is in no small part a result of the fact that the regime constitutes its main source of funding and

thus maintaining a degree of cohesion . The ANYAD regime ’sability to retain influence over the militia is in no small part a result of the fact that the regime constitutes its main source of funding and armament. Together with the 50, 000 local militia operatives, there are 60, 000 foreign militia fighters (split between those with extensive military experience and those without it that have joined either for ideological reasons or for profit). Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 29 The Resistance and Liberation Front (RLF) Terrorist Group (TG) The Resistance and Liberation Front (RLF) is the dominant terror (TG group operating in BUFRO, where it emerged as a splinter group of the BUFRO Liberation Front (BLF) – an organisation seeking the overthrow of the country’sregime. Unlike the BLF, whose aims were more limited to political control of BUFRO, the RLF developed much more ambitious international objectives, ultimat ely seeking to establish a new regional, radical order spanning from the Middle East across the Sahara to West Africa, alongside th e Horn of Africa. In order to do so, the group has secured strategic alliances with affiliated groups and ‘start-up’ cells in multiple countries, creating a franchised operation that allows it to project influence at scale, including RLF-Africa (active in the Sahel and IGAINER). The RLF has evolved from purely being a terrorist and insurgent organisation to a proto-state capable of de-facto rule over controlled territory. Its territorial control relies on political deals with other actors, primarily local

Sahel and IGAINER). The RLF has evolved from purely being a terrorist and insurgent organisation to a proto-state capable of de-facto rule over controlled territory. Its territorial control relies on political deals with other actors, primarily local tribes, as well as with form ermembers of the of the ANYAD regime. Through a combination of active recruitment and coercion, the RLF was able to expand its territorial control in in north-eastern BUFRO, establishing Raqqa as its de-facto capital. The terrorist group is able to dra w on a membership of around 160, 000, which includes administrative personnel as well as fighters (around 30, 000) . It has also recruited members and established cells in the West, including in NATO countries. The RLF has demonstrated a comprehensive approach to its territorial expansion and control, taking over weaker adversaries, a swell as areas that provide resources, such as oil fields, water resources, dams (for power and water) and grain silos. Its financ ial strategy has also involved the coerced taxation of the local population. The RLF strategy is the overt pursuit of physical control prior to gaining social and political acceptance. The key distinguishing element of the RLF in BUFRO lies in the fact that it is the first terrorist organization to have managed to position itself as a realistic alternative to the A NYAD regime both militarily and politically, and to establish an administrative entity that sustains, even if partially, a system of governance and a series of economic, social, and legal

itself as a realistic alternative to the A NYAD regime both militarily and politically, and to establish an administrative entity that sustains, even if partially, a system of governance and a series of economic, social, and legal services. This has far-reaching implications. The RLF in BUFRO is not just a local group seeking simply to rule the country. Rather, it hopes to turn the country into an entity with a worldview and an identity, interested in a far more than geographical area of BUFRO. One of the enabling factors that permitted the RLF to estab lish a presence swiftly in north-eastern BUFRO was the tribal structure of the population, traditionally very conservative. Tribes are an important source of power and support. RLF Political, governance and administrative structures The RLF’sgovernance structure is designed to be replicable and scalable, offering the potential to include territories further a field under its sphere of control. It is led by the RLF Leader, Abdel Yasko, who effectively holds the combined roles of ideological, political and military leader. The RLF Leader is advised by a central council, staffed with two Deputies and a Delegated Committee bringing together representatives of both the organisation’smai nfunctional committees (in effect administrative depar tments ) and provinces. The main committees are the financial committee (responsible for taxation and revenue); the leadership committee (responsible for the development of policy, including laws); the legal committee (responsible for judicial decisions and oversight); the military committee (responsible for war fighting and military strategy); the foreign

(responsible for taxation and revenue); the leadership committee (responsible for the development of policy, including laws); the legal committee (responsible for judicial decisions and oversight); the military committee (responsible for war fighting and military strategy); the foreign assistance committee (the main body for lia ising with new branches of the franchise and establishing cells in other territories); the media and information committee (responsible for communication and propaganda); and the intelligence and security committee (with responsibility for internal security, including policing, and for collecting intelligence ). The provision of security to the local population . Establishing governance structures. In accordance to this strategy, the RLF’smilitary operational approach is anchored in three principles: a) use of unyielding cruelty: b) psychological warfare: c) mobility and flexibility of its actions. Its military tactics include acts of terrorism, insurgency operations and more conve ntional military action. The RLF aims to ‘soften ’ targets through a campaign of terrorist attacks and infiltration of the population, before launching final assault s. Such an approach was witnessed within the context of the capture of Raqqa . At the strategic level, the RLF displays a high degree of command and control. At the operational and tactical level, the RLF follows district lines (corresponding to provinces), with individual district being relatively autonomous. Only some speciali sed units move between the different districts. RLF fighting forces have proved to be both militarily adept and adaptable. The organisation’sfighting forces are organised in Divisions, Brigades and Battalions. However, the size of each military unit varies from one district to another. Moreover, the RLF’smilitary units are composed of local recruits, with ties to the local tribes and clan social contract norms remaining intact.

Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 34 International Organisations, Civil Society and Non-Governmental Organisations BUFRO Arab Red Crescent (BARC) The BUFRO Arab Red Crescent (BARC) was established in 1942 and is an independent humanitarian organisation of public utility. As a national organisation of legal entity with financial and administrative independence it complies with the Geneva provisions and conventions of the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC), and other agreements related to International Humanitarian Law and Human rights approved by the Government of BUFRO. It is a member of the International Movement and the International Federa tion of the Red Cross and Crescent Societies in Geneva, and the General Secretariat of the Arab Red Crescent and Red Cross Organisations and committed to the Geneva Conventions and the seven basic principles. With an HQ in Jandrikus, it works with fourteen branches, 75 sub-branches and 12, 500 volunteers. Activities include community services, water & rehabilitation, medical care services, risk education, disaster management, humanitarian support, and livelihood. International Committee of

With an HQ in Jandrikus, it works with fourteen branches, 75 sub-branches and 12, 500 volunteers. Activities include community services, water & rehabilitation, medical care services, risk education, disaster management, humanitarian support, and livelihood. International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) The ICRC has been present in BUFRO since 1967 . The ICRC works to support the most vulnerable in BUFRO who have been affected by over a decade-long conflict. It carries out its activities in cooperation with the BUFRO Arab Red Crescent (BARC) to contribute to improved access to food, medical services, and to rehabilitate water and sanitations networks across the country, and to help people gradually restore their livelihoods . To conduct their operations, the ICRC needs sustained, non-politicized access from all actors involved in the conflict. ICRC activities in BUFRO include: ▪ water and habitat interventions across the country, ▪ provision of food parcels to households, ▪ local livelihood initiatives, such as the distribution of sheep, poultry, fodder, beekeeping, fertilizers, and agricultural tools, ▪ access to healthcare through 11 BARC polyclinics and 12 mobile health units supported by the ICRC, ▪ exchange of oral greetings and Red Cross messages between separated families, and more than 645 cases were opened by families in BUFRO to trace persons unaccounted for, ▪ ICRC visits to places of detention, and ICRC donations to prisons (essential drugs, fortified date bars, mattresses, blankets, water, and medical equipment), ▪ Forensics support, including the establishment Forensic Centres in Homs and Hasakeh, as well as a

visits to places of detention, and ICRC donations to prisons (essential drugs, fortified date bars, mattresses, blankets, water, and medical equipment), ▪ Forensics support, including the establishment Forensic Centres in Homs and Hasakeh, as well as a Forensic Human Identification Centre in Jandrikus, ▪ Introduction to International Humanitarian Law (IHL) workshops and Advanced IHL for officers from the MoD, MoI, diplomats, BA RC personnel and university students, ▪ Sessions on risks related to mines and explosive remnants of war and safer behaviour practices in weapon-contaminated areas, held by BARC’srisk education teams who were trained by the ICRC. EU funded programs and the provision of loans and technical assistance through the European Investment Bank (EIB) were ceased. In 2017, the EU Strategy for BUFRO was adopted, accompanied by three Foreign Affairs Council Conclusions and endorsed by the Europe an Council in October 2019. The EU also aims to mobilize the international community in support of a comprehensive and credible political solution to the BUFRO conflict, in line with UN Security Council Resolution 2254. Since the EU formally suspended its cooperation with the BUFRO regime, the logic of non-humanitarian programming inside BUFRO has been bottom-up and community driven interventions at local level with the objective to strengthen the resilience of the BUFRO population and paving the way for future recovery and return. Assistance is provided in sectors such as education, livelihood, civil society capacity building, health, accountability, and transitional justice. Assistance is delivered through United Nations (UN) agencies, international and BUFRO NGOs as well as EU Members State agencies, in complementarity to humanitarian aid. The European Commission’s Civil Protection and Humanitarian Aid Operations department (DGECHO) is the main provider of humanitarian assistance to BUFRO. Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 36 Civil Society Organisations and Non-Governmental Organisations (NGOs) The landscape of Civil Society Organizations and NGOs in BUFRO is highly complex. BUFRO NGOs and NGO networks have very differ ent ideas, motivations, and visions for the future of BUFRO. Before 2011, BUFRO NGOs had limited experience in working in networks, however, they began to develop and complement capacities, and meanwhile BUFRO counts about 12 networks comprised of approximately 200 local NGOs. Funding and fundraising remain a major challenge for these networks as opportunities are mostly geared toward sorganizations with direct implementation, such as emergency assistance programs. NGOs are also facing challenges in conducting security vetting as a requirement to obtain international funding. Therefore, UNOCHA implemented a vetting platform to ensure that organizations are humanitarian, not political. Meanwhile, UNOCHA works with 27 International NGOs (INGOs), as implementing partners, as well as with 108 (vetted) national NGOs (NNGOs). In recent years, Women-led Networks of BUFRO women’sorganizations were created to give space to organize and advocate towards broader civil partici pation. The main driver behind this movement is female solidarity and awareness of the absence of gender equ ity. The BUFRO Women’s Network (BWN) was the first network of this kind. and awareness of the absence of gender equ ity. The BUFRO Women’s Network (BWN) was the first network of this kind. White Helmets (BUFRO Civil Defence) The BUFRO White Helmets is a unique group of 3000 volunteers who work to save lives and strengthen communities in BUFRO. The White Helmets’ work started in 2012 as a grassroots team of volunteers across the country coming together in response to the aerial bombardment of neighbourhoods and the withdrawal of state essential services like firefighting and healthcare. Activities include search and rescue missions, essential services for more than four million civilians, including firefighting, removal of unexp loded weapons, community safety awareness, electrical and sewage maintenance and rehabilitation of neighbourhoods . The White Helmets claim that their work, in particular documenting war crimes, have made them a target for the BUFRO Regime and MURINUS . Since 2013, more than 280 volunteers have been killed, and White Helmet centres have been repeatedly attacked. Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 37 4. The active personnel fills the ranks of the Land Forces (200, 000), Air Force (15, 000), Air Defence ( 55, 000) and Navy (6500). fills the ranks of the Land Forces (200, 000), Air Force (15, 000), Air Defence ( 55, 000) and Navy (6500). MUR expeditionary forces (MEF-B) and BIMARA-connected elements augment the BUFRO military. MEF-Bprovide vital support to BFR’s Armed Forces in terms of intelligence, organisation and training, planning, integration of air defence, close air support. War fighting activity falls under the overall command of the chief of the general staff, who reports to the defence minister. Given BUFRO’spolitical climate, loyalty to the regime is considered the primary criterion for advan cement; senior Armed Forces leaders are selected and promoted only if they demonstrate unquestioned reliability and loyalty to the ANYAD regime. Defence structure The Armed Forces of BUFRO are comprised of the Land Forces, the Navy, the Air Force and the Air Defence Forces, and the National Defence Forces. Following the conclusion of the civil war, the Armed Forces face a period of consolidation and restructuring. Force structure, readines s, logistics, training and personnel management will likely be reviewed and optimised. It’scommand and control structures consist of the following: Supreme Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces: President ANYAD; Minister of Defence: Lt General Ali KARIM; Chief of Staff of the Armed Forces, Commander, Land Forces: Major General Ibrahim MAHMUD; Commander, Air Force: Major General Ahmet KABUL; Commander, Navy: Major General Hasan YASSER . President ANYAD, as supreme commander of the Armed Forces, directs defence policy, chairs the Republican Defence Council (RDC ) and appoints the minister of defence as well as the

KABUL; Commander, Navy: Major General Hasan YASSER . President ANYAD, as supreme commander of the Armed Forces, directs defence policy, chairs the Republican Defence Council (RDC ) and appoints the minister of defence as well as the commanders of main services. Members of the Alawite community occupy key post swithin the military structure. The Republican Guard is under the direct control of ANYAD and is responsible for the protection of the regime. Doctrine, Strategy and Capabilities The Armed Forces of BUFRO ensure the survival of the regime. Currently, their main mission is to restore the control of national territory. The Armed Forces are capable of holding territory, conducting sustained operations against lightly armed militia forces (COIN, counter-insurgency), but are less capable of large-scale operations against neighbouring state forces. Modernisation efforts are principally oriented towards MUR-manufactured equipment continuing the long ‐standing defence agreements between the two states. Spending on personnel and maintenance will remain the main part of the defence budget. Major procurements in the near-term are less likely, focusing instead upon addressing critical deficiencies. MUR security guarantees and presence has reduced the threat of foreign intervention. Counter-insurgency (COIN) efforts will continue to be a major factor of military operations in the foreseeable future. The Armed Forces’ capabilities in the areas of ground-based air defence ( AD), offensive air support, ground reconnaissance, and mechanised warfare and infantry operations are assessed as MEDIUM. Improved coordination between ground units and aircraft in close air support/ground attack missions has been evident during the civil

defence ( AD), offensive air support, ground reconnaissance, and mechanised warfare and infantry operations are assessed as MEDIUM. Improved coordination between ground units and aircraft in close air support/ground attack missions has been evident during the civil war . BUFRO ’snavy effectively cooperates with MUR Naval units based at TARTUS. Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 39 Critical capabilities, such as air-to-air warfare, indirect fire, combat engineering, air reconnaissance, maritime surveillance, airlift, maritime transportation and ASW/AS uW (both airborne and surface) are assessed as LOW. The Armed Forces are missing cri tical capabilities, such as submarines (in AS uW roles), naval surface fire support, aerial refuelling and maritime anti-air warfare. These gaps will significantly affect the Armed Forces to conduct and sustain multi-domain operations against regional near-peer rivals. The October 2016 ratification of a BUFRO-MURINUS defence treaty enabling MUR forces to remain in BUFRO indefinitely will likely ensure that BUFRO forces become increasingly familiar with operating alongside MUR air, ground and naval forces. Thus, MUR will likely become a de-facto defence force against state ‐ based threats until the BUFRO 'smilitary capabilities can be more substantively reconstituted. In July 2020, BIMARA and BUFRO formally signed a strategic defence agreement that stipulated a willingness i mprove defence co-operation. BUFRO 's Armed Forces are organised and equipped to engage and defeat regional states’ forces in a conventional conflict. BUFRO modified its doctrine to orient the force towards area denial through greater emphasis on developing its surface ‐to‐surface missile (SSM) and surface

organised and equipped to engage and defeat regional states’ forces in a conventional conflict. BUFRO modified its doctrine to orient the force towards area denial through greater emphasis on developing its surface ‐to‐surface missile (SSM) and surface ‐to‐air (SAM) capability. BUFRO sought to challenge potential adversary dominance of the air through a robust air ‐defence network, while possessing offensive missiles capable of striking the enemy rear, damaging or destroying its airbases, and disrupting its capacity for mobilisation. BUFRO 'sasymmetric warfare capabilities have increased, focusing upon the development of Special Forces (SF) to disrupt attacks by the adversary’sinfantry and armour, while utilising its own armour to confront invading forces under the cover of a robust air defence network. The requirements of the civil war refuted much of BUFRO ʼs established doctrinal practices, being replaced by ad-hoc planning and operations to seize key terrain in contested environments by any available means. In the conflict, territory was traded for the provision of a more robust defence of the state'skey infrastructure and urban centres. This strategy created the opportunity to provide the foundation for later counter ‐offensives when aided by MUR aircraft. Future defence doctrine will likely draw heavily from the conduct of the civil war, implemen ting lessons learned in COIN and asymmetric warfare. Land forces The Land Forces of BUFRO are organised into the Republican Guard (HQ Jandrikus), the1st Army Corps (HQ Jandrikus), the 2nd Army Corps (HQ Zabadani) and the 3rd Army Corps (HQ Aleppo), the 5th Assault Corps (Latakia), the Special Forces Command (HQ Jandrikus) and the Missile Force s Command (HQ Aleppo). Among the most capable and experienced army units are the Republican Guard, 4th Armoured Division and the 5th Assault Corps. An Army Corps is usually made up of 3-4 Divisions and numbers up to 50, 000 personnel. The Division is comprised of 5-6 Brigades and Regiments, and numbers between 5, 000 and 15, 000 personnel. There are two types of brigades: a mechanised brigade (3, 500 personnel, 105 IFVs and 41 tanks, organised in to three mechanised battalions, a tank battalion, an artillery battalion, an AD battalion and an engineer battalion) and an armoured brigade (2, 500 personnel, 105 tanks and 31 IFVs, organised into three armoured battalions, one mechanised battalion, an artillery battalion, an AD battalion and an engineer battalion). Security and Intelligence apparatus BUFRO has an extensive intelligence and security apparatus with four main independent intelligence organizations; two civilian and two military ones, as well as a smaller Presidential security service . A third, semi-independent naval intelligence agency reports to the Air Force intelligence agency (see below). Together with the Armed Forces, they form the backbone of ANYAD and the Socialist Party’spower. BUFRO’sintelligence community is particul arly close to the leadership. The four main organizations hav eoverlapping functions and skills, ensuring that the regime is not overly dependent on any one of them. The regime has histo rically promoted competition among them, so that no one organisation can dominate the others. Each of them runs its own prisons, detention, and interrogation facilities. Civilian Intelligence Organisations Directorate Of General Intelligence (B-DGI): The B-DGI is the main civilian intelligence service. Organically is within the Ministry of Interior (MoI), but it actually reports directly to the President through the Office of National Security of the Socialist Party (ONS). The B-DGI

The B-DGI is the main civilian intelligence service. Organically is within the Ministry of Interior (MoI), but it actually reports directly to the President through the Office of National Security of the Socialist Party (ONS). The B-DGI is split between operative and investigative units, its headquarters are in JANDRIKUS. It has 12 Central branches and, at least, one sub-branch in each province. The main tasks have traditionally been the fight against alpolitical opposition, dissidents and political partie s that could undermine the leadership of the Socialist Party, the control and censorship of the press and mass media and the control of foreigners on national soil. B-DGI, as most other intelligence organisations in the country, is divided in two main divi sions: Internal Security and External Security: Internal Security Division: its main task is the surveillance of the population and counterespionage. It targets opposition groups and foreign fighters . In addition, it also deals with drug trafficking and corruption. External Security Division: control and elimination of opposition groups and individuals abroad, protection of BUFRO’sdiplomatic offices, and espionage. Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 45 Directorate of Political Security (B-DPS): The B-DPS is organically subordinate to the MoI, but functionally it reports directly to the President through the ONS and has supervisory authority over the minister of interior, his offices and staff. The B-DPS focuses (as does the B-DGI) on organised political and opposition forces, political activities of anyone threatening the regime. the minister of interior, his offices and staff. The B-DPS focuses (as does the B-DGI) on organised political and opposition forces, political activities of anyone threatening the regime. It is divided between Internal Security Department and Externa l Security Department. The chiefs of the Directorate have almost always been from the ANYAD community. Military Intelligence Organisations Directorate of Military Intelligence (B-MIS): It is the largest military intelligence organization. Organically it falls under the ministry of defence (MoD), however it reports directly to the President. In fact, not only the MoD does not have authority over it, but it is the B-MIS who is involved in the appointment of the minister of defence, his deputies, and his chiefs of staff. B-MIS is tasked with military espionage and counter-espionage, it manages Military Attaches abroad and controls the foreign ones in BUFRO. In addition, B-MIS oversees other security services activities and monitors their behaviour to check their loyalty. Directorate of Air Force Intelligence (B-AFI): The B-AFI is considered the most powerful and loyal of the BUFRO’sintelligence community. It has the strongest manpower and best technical skills. Although organically within the MoD, it reports directly – without passing through the ONS – to the Presiden t. It is dedicated to the control of air force personnel and the protection of various airport infrastructure. It is also tasked with air space surveillance and thus responsible for IMINT, EW and SIGINT. In ad dition, B-AFI is in charge of wiretaps. B-AFI has six subordinate branches, its

the protection of various airport infrastructure. It is also tasked with air space surveillance and thus responsible for IMINT, EW and SIGINT. In ad dition, B-AFI is in charge of wiretaps. B-AFI has six subordinate branches, its own investigation branch, and six branches located across the country’sprovinces, coupled with one on each airbase. It conducts operations both inside and outside of BUFRO . Directorate of the Naval Intelligence (B-NID): The B-NID is the only intelligence organization that operates within its armed forces. It reports to the Commander of the Naval Forces, but also to B-AFI. The B-NID conducts military espionage and counterespionage, and is responsible for the security of naval units, ports and Navy infrastructures. The navy being the smallest of the Armed Forces, its service is somewhat limited. B-NID has its HQ in Latakia. It has a second branch in Tartus. The relationships of the military intelligence organisations, namely B-AFI and B-MIS, have become more oriented towards BIMARA. Whereas the civilian organisations, have closer ties with MURINUS. Additional security agencies Service for the Presidential Security (B-SPS): The B-SPS is responsible for guaranteeing the security of the President, its staff and, on a wider scale, dignitaries of the regime. It is considered a service within the intelligence organizations because its staff is embedded within intelligence-security structures, although the top echelons are always drawn from the B-AFI. within intelligence-security structures, although the top echelons are always drawn from the B-AFI. Chemical Warfare Capabilities BUFRO acceded to the 1925 Geneva Protocol unconditionally in 1968. For decades, BUFRO resisted being a state party or signatory state to the Chemical Weapons Convention (CWC). BUFRO’sgovernment has previously called for a WMD ‐free zone in the Middle East and stated it will consider joining the CWC only when other regional states agree to eliminate its undeclared CWs. BUFRO later became a signatory to the CWC in September 2013 following a Murinian ‐led deal resulting in BUFRO surrendering its chemical weapons (CW) capabilities to the international community in return for the avoidance of military action against the state. A range of CW delivery systems inclusive of air-dropped bombs, artillery munitions, and ballistic missile and rocket warheads, some with range of up to 600 km. Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 46 BUFRO once possessed significant chemical weapons (CW) arsenals in the region, but over a decade of civil war and international san ctions has severely eroded the country’s WMD capabilities. In 2014, the regime in JANDRIKUS was forced to stop its WMD development programmes and surrender all of its stockpiled CW to the international community as part of an agreement to avoid international military intervention. BUFRO’shistoric CW programme was centred at a research and development centre in Bazeh alongside two ammunition storage facilities in vicinity to Homs. BUFRO’shistoric CW programme was centred at a research and development centre in Bazeh alongside two ammunition storage facilities in vicinity to Homs. Biological capabilities In terms of biological weapons (BW), there has been no indications of that BUFRO has tried to produce a possible BW. BUFRO has stated that it does not feel bound by the limited commitments it made in acceding to the 1925 Geneva Protocol and by signing (but not ratifying) the Biolo gical and Toxin Weapons Convention in 1972. Currently, BUFRO 'sbiotechnology infrastructure may be capable of supporting limited agent development and production. The technology it has developed to produce weapons to disseminate chemical agents would provide it with the means to produce BWs, although there is no evidence to date that it has done so. Nuclear Overview BUFRO is a non-nuclear weapon state and party to the Treaty of the non-proliferation of nuclear weapons (NPT) since 1969. BUFRO is also a proponent of a Weapons of Mass Destruction Free Zone (WMDFZ) in the Middle East since 2003. In the 1970s, BUFRO sought the assistance of the IAEA, BIMARA, DUSHMAN, MURINUS and NIKOBO to develop its nuclear programme. There is no information to indicate the country’sambitions for nuclear weapons programme. BUFRO has not developed full nuclear fuel-cycle expertise and is not known to possess enrichment or reprocessing technologies. The national authority that directs BUFRO’slimited/research nuclear programme is the Atomic Energy Commission of BUFRO . not known to possess enrichment or reprocessing technologies. The national authority that directs BUFRO’slimited/research nuclear programme is the Atomic Energy Commission of BUFRO . JANDRIKUS possesses only one small operational research reactor, the DUSHMAN-built 30 KW t SRR-1 miniature neutron-source reactor (MNSR), primarily used to produce medical isotopes under IAEA safeguards. The majority of BUFRO’snuclear-related work takes place at the Der Al-Hadjar Nuclear Research Centre and the Scientific Studies and Research Centre (SSRC) in JANDRIKUS. Cyber capabilities BUFRO 'scyber capabilities are limited. Pro ‐government hacktivists, in particular the BUFRO Cyber Army (BCA) and the BUFRO Electronic Army (BEA) have historically used distributed denial ‐of‐service (DD oS) attacks, website defacement against social networking sites and the spreading of malware. A Trojan virus called DarkComet was reportedly used to log and monitor dissident activity, but was terminated following its exposure. There is also pot ential link between BUFRO and BIMARA, as one of the hacking tools utilised by the BEA was the BIMARA ‘Havij' Structured Query Language (SQL) exploitation tool. The BUFRO government is becoming increasingly cautious of the Internet’s its potential for inciting rebellion or the spread of radicali sation. It has routinely blocked certain sites critical of the regime. The government has also been active in promoting cyber secur ity within the state and improving its cyber protection of critical C2 systems and facilities, such as the land-based air defence system. Diplomacy The ANYAD regime has had complex and volatile relationships with its neighbours, from whom it has been increasingly political lyisolated since the outbreak of the civil war. It has nevertheless maintained (gradually improving) relations with a number of Arab League members as well as the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) and close ally MURINUS. Furthermore, the ANYAD regime has benefited from support of BIMARA, who has waged proxy war in BUFRO (fighting on the side of the regime) via affiliated militias. Moreo ver, the regime has established closer relations with DUSHMAN including through a high-profile Presidential visit. During the visit, the two countries declared a ‘strategic partnership’ and DUSHMAN committed to providing financial support, including within the context of reconstruction efforts. DUSHMAN’sposition is situated within its overall policy of promoting sovereignty and non-interference within states’ internal affairs (a policy that is in no small part aimed at countering Western foreign policy inf luence and promoting a post-Western international order). Accordingly, DUSHMAN also blocked Western-backed sanctions against the ANYAD regime in the United Nations Security Council. Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 49 MURINUS MURINUS levers of power and application of force MURINUS has adopted a multi-lever and integrated approach to secure strategic gains within BUFRO. Thus, diplomatic activity – both at the regional level and bilaterally – is used to shape the political ground, allowing for the deployment of forces via a num ber of bases in the country. Non-linear and other (semi) deniable capabilities are also brought to bear in support of conventional operations. At the same time, MURINUS has also enga ged in information and strategic communications campaigned at both legitim ising its presence and activities, including by framing these as humanitarian assistance and relief operations. Military forces and activities MURINUS’ presence in BUFRO includes around 10 bases, located in strategic locations around the country, as well as field outpo sts. Out of these, the two major ones are the Tartus naval base and the Hmeimim air base, both of which are located along the count ry’s Mediterranean coast. Moreover, both MURINUS and regime forces use jointly military bases and installations, including the al-Jarrah military air base located east of Aleppo in northern BUFRO, which was recaptured from the Revolutionary Liberation Front in 2017. MURINUS Air power MURINUS has made extensive use of air power (and, more specifically bombing raids) in order to target opposition strongholds and support offensives by the ANYAD regime. Most sorties are flown from the Hmeimim airbase, located in the vicinity of Bassel Al-ANYAD International Airport. The legal status of the base was established following a treaty signed in 2017 between the government of MURINUS and the regime in JANDRIKUS. Air support provided by MUR has played an important role in changing the course of the conflict and overturning early gains from opposition forces. Despite some losses (including fast jets) air power remains a relatively safe means of projecting power at scale, particularly given the lack of advanced air defence systems wit hin the oppositio n. The MURINUS-BIMARA Relationship in BUFRO MURINUS has established strategic bilateral relations with BIMARA, which also flow into operational and tactical level activi ty. Whilst BIMARA shares elements of MURINUS’ strategic outlook, including with respect to pushing back on Western Influence . More widely, BIMARA’sapproach has focussed on using its proxies to secure influence over regime and MURINUS-controlled areas, including in southern JANDRIKUS, the Abu Kamal border crossing and the Deir Zor rural region, including areas around Hama and Aleppo. Collaboration between and the two has included the focused application of MURINUS airpower coordinated with BIMARA-backed militias on the ground. This has proven effective in not only preventing the regime’scollapse but also allowing it to reclaim control over lost territory.
